Preface to the I3C Basic Specification
1
1 What is MIPI I3C Basic?
2
3 MIPI I3C Basic is a feature-reduced, lower-complexity version of the powerful, flexible, and efficient MIPI
4 I3C interface [MIPI08], suitable for a broad range of device interconnect applications including sensor and
5 memory interfacing.
2 Motivation
6
7 MIPI Alliance considers I3C technology to have significant advantages over the widely adopted legacy
8 interfaces currently used for similar applications, and wishes to see I3C technology broadly deployed in the
9 industry. Being aware that some advanced I3C v1.x features are not needed for many common applications,
10 and that some potential users might regard MIPI Alliance membership as a barrier, the MIPI Board of
11 Directors decided to make the I3C Basic Specification freely available without requiring a MIPI Alliance
12 membership, and to facilitate a royalty free licensing environment for all implementers, as described further
13 below.
3 IPR Status
14
15 MIPI Alliance’s regular intellectual property rights-related terms apply only by and among members. To
16 address this issue, MIPI Alliance created a set of supplemental patent licensing terms for the current and
17 future versions of the I3C Basic Specification, attached to this document as Annex G. MIPI required that all
18 members participating in the development of the I3C Basic Specification agree to these additional terms.
19 These terms include an obligation to license applicable patent claims to both member and non-member
20 implementers, for uses both in mobile devices and otherwise, on “RAND-Z” terms: that is, under royalty free
21 (the Z references zero royalty) and otherwise reasonable and non-discriminatory terms.
22 As described in Annex G, any implementer that wishes to benefit from the RAND-Z obligation must also
23 commit to license other implementers under the same RAND-Z terms. This reciprocity requirement is
24 intended to expand royalty free license obligations through the broader I3C Basic ecosystem.
25 The MIPI member companies that joined the I3C Basic Specification development effort and manifest
26 agreement to the terms in Annex G are listed in Annex H. Annex H also notes if and when any party
27 terminates their agreement to these terms (subject to certain ongoing license obligations, as described in the
28 terms).
29 The I3C Basic IPR terms are intended to create a robust royalty free environment for implementers. However,
30 no set of IPR terms can comprehensively address all potential risks. The terms apply only to those parties
31 that agree to them, and the scope of application is limited to what is described in the terms. Implementers
32 must ultimately make their own risk assessment, and the disclaimers described elsewhere in this document
33 remain in full force and effect.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 1
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 16 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4 Relationship to MIPI I3C v1.x Specifications
34
35 The MIPI I3C Basic Specification is a subset of the MIPI I3C Specification. In the I3C Basic Specification,
36 the terms “I3C,” “I3C Device,” and “I3C Bus” should be interpreted as referring to both I3C and I3C Basic.
37 In addition, I3C Basic:
38 • Addresses all issues addressed by all published Errata for previous versions of the I3C
39 Specification
40 • Includes numerous fixes, clarifications, and editorial improvements planned for inclusion in the
41 MIPI I3C Specification. In general, these are not new feature additions, but represent the latest
42 understanding and definition of I3C features and capabilities.
43 4.1 I3C v1.1.1 Functions Not Included in I3C Basic
44 The following functions of the I3C Specification have been removed from the I3C Basic Specification.
45 Companies interested in implementing these functions should join MIPI Alliance to enjoy member IPR
46 licensing.
47 • Timing Control (Section 6.6), namely the portions addressing:
48 • Synchronous Time Control
49 • Asynchronous Time Control (Modes 1–3)
50 • Monitoring Device Early Termination Capability (Section 6.8)
51 • Device to Device(s) Tunneling (Section 6.9)
52 • HDR Ternary Modes (HDR-TSP and HDR-TSL) (Section 6.3)
53 • Multi-Lane support for SDR Mode, HDR-DDR Mode, and HDR-TSP Mode (Section 6.7)
54 4.2 Organization of This Specification
55 The structure of this Specification, i.e., the naming and numbering of Section headings, is consistent with the
56 I3C v1.2 Specification. In order to preserve alignment with the I3C v1.2 Specification’s structure, numerous
57 references to I3C v1.2 functions and capabilities that were removed for I3C Basic v1.2 are retained. In many
58 cases the associated section headings are retained with a note that the content is only available in the full I3C
59 v1.2 Specification.
60 4.3 I3C Basic v1.2 as an Update of I3C Basic v1.1.1
61 I3C Basic v1.2 is an updated, clarified version of I3C Basic v1.1.1, and is also a strict subset of the full I3C
62 v1.2 Specification. As such, I3C Basic v1.2 includes numerous clarifications and corrections that were also
63 applied to the full I3C v1.2 Specification. Note that I3C Basic v1.2 does not include any new features as
64 compared with I3C Basic v1.1.1.
5 How I3C Basic Devices Work with I3C v1.x Devices
65
66 MIPI I3C Basic Devices, whether Primary Controller, Secondary Controller, or Target, are intended to be
67 interoperable with I3C v1.x Devices for the set of optional features that are mutually supported by the
68 connected Devices. It is easiest to view I3C Basic as a subset of I3C, with a specific set of additional, optional
69 features that are only offered to MIPI Alliance Members.
2 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.

71 elements.

1.1 Managing Optional Element Combinations
74 Because the number of possible combinations of I3C’s optional elements is large, this specification includes
75 measures intended to help implementers manage the resulting complexity:
76 • A table on the specification cover summarizes which separable blocks (i.e.: Section 4 Required
77 Elements and each Optional Feature) have been updated in this specification version.
78 • Throughout the specification, each separable block has its own independent version number and
79 change history.
80 • To help implementers determine whether a given I3C specification version requires revision of a
81 given I3C product, blocks that have not changed in this specification version are clearly indicated.
82 • Because some of the optional elements are mutually incompatible, each Optional Feature indicates
83 any other Optional Features with which it is technically incompatible.
84 • Annex B presents a concise syntax which is recommended for expressing which optional elements
85 an I3C product does and does not implement. This syntax is intended for use in product data sheets
86 and other technical marketing, to simplify comparison between products and matching application
87 requirements to I3C parts.
4 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 19 ---\nVersion 1.2 INTRODUCTION Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
2 Introduction
88 I2C was introduced many years ago to provide a low cost method for connecting a processor to a set of devices
89 to support the needs of consumer electronics; various flavors of I2C have continued to be widely used in
90 many industries where its low cost ability to connect a set of devices (AKA Multi-Drop) has served the needs
91 reasonably well, although often with the need for side-band pins, and often with interoperability challenges.
92 In mobile wireless devices the I2C tradition continued but changing requirements had made I2C more and
93 more problematic. With the increasing use of sensors, this started creating fragmentation in terms of choice
94 of bus, including I2C, SPI, and UART to address bandwidth and other issues. Further, none of those buses
95 addressed the pin count problems made far worse with more complex end-devices, including the need for
96 various per-device GPIOs in parallel to the bus to cover interrupt (Target Device notifying Controller), sleep
97 and reset signals, and so on. On top of that, the increasing number of devices with increasing amounts of data
98 to send/receive has put pressure on bus bandwidth and latency. Adding more buses adds more pins, more
99 traces, and more power.
100 The MIPI I3C interface has been developed to address these sensor integration concerns, as well as those
101 historically encountered while using I2C, SPI, and UART in any product, by providing a fast, low cost, low
102 power and managed, two-wire digital interface. MIPI worked to address a set of key issues across:
103 performance, power, extraneous pins (e.g., Targets interrupting and reset control), interoperability, bus
104 management, and error-handling/stuck-bus protections; it chose to do all of this while maintaining back-
105 wards compatibility with legacy I2C, to aid in transition and evolution.

Figure 1 I3C System Diagram
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 5
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 20 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic INTRODUCTION Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
2.1 Scope and Purpose (Informative)
107 The following topics are in scope for this Specification:
108 • I3C interface protocols and commands
109 • Electrical specifications, such as timing and voltage levels
110 • Support for specific classes of Devices (e.g., sensors)
111 The following topics are out of scope for this Specification:
112 • Mechanical, system, and implementation details within an I3C Device
113 • ESD (Electrostatic Discharge) structures
114 • System power management
115 • Use case specific data or format definitions besides Bus management command codes
116 The I3C interface is intended to improve upon the features of the I2C interface [NXP01], preserving backward
117 compatibility. This Specification defines a standard Multi-Drop interface between Host processors and
118 peripheral Devices (e.g., sensors).
119 Implementing the I3C Specification greatly increases the flexibility system designers have to supplant
120 incumbent interfaces (i.e., I2C, SPI, UART) and support a wide array of Devices of increasing complexity
121 and diversity in their system (e.g., sensor subsystem) and at as low a cost as possible.
2.2 I3C Key Features (Informative)
122 Two main concerns are paramount for the I3C interface: The use of as little energy as possible in transporting
123 data and control, while reducing the number of physical pins used by the interface.
124 Therefore, the I3C interface features:
125 • Two-wire serial interface up to 12.5 MHz using Push-Pull
126 • Legacy I2C Device co-existence on the same Bus (with some limitations)
127 • Dynamic Addressing while supporting Static Addressing for Legacy I2C Devices
128 • Legacy I2C messaging
129 • I2C-like Single Data Rate messaging (SDR)
130 • Optional High Data Rate messaging Modes (HDR):
131 • HDR-DDR for Double Data Rate
132 • NOT INCLUDED IN I3C BASIC: HDR-TSL for Ternary Symbol Legacy-inclusive-Bus (I2C
133 Devices allowed)
134 • NOT INCLUDED IN I3C BASIC:HDR-TSP for Ternary Symbol for Pure Bus (no I2C
135 Devices allowed)
136 • HDR-BT for Bulk Transport
137 • Multi-Drop a multitude of physically and virtually attached Devices
138 • Multi-Controller capability
139 • In-Band Interrupt support
140 • Hot-Join support
141 • PARTLY INCLUDED IN I3C BASIC: Synchronous Timing Support and Asynchronous Time
142 Stamping
143 • Grouped Addressing
144 • Target Reset without additional wires
145 • NOT INCLUDED IN I3C BASIC:Monitoring Device Early Termination
146 • NOT INCLUDED IN I3C BASIC:Device to Device(s) Tunneling
147 • PARTLY INCLUDED IN I3C BASIC:Multi-Lane Data Transfer
6 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 21 ---\nVersion 1.2 INTRODUCTION Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
148 The I3C interface provides improved efficiencies in Bus power while providing significant speed
149 improvements over I2C. Figure 2 through Figure 5 show different Bus Mode options that provide tradeoffs
150 for performance/power vs. target Device complexity. The following conditions were assumed for all
151 calculations:
152 • Devices are in close vicinity:
153 • Targets clustered close to Controller
154 • No delay on the wires
155 • Total leakage on each wire: 4 µA
156 • Driver’s internal resistance: 90 Ω
157 • Total Bus capacitance: 50 pF
158 • Bus main clock: 12.5 MHz
159 • V DD = 1.8 V
160 • Pull-Up resistor on Open-Drain: 2833 Ω
161 • Equal probability for 1 and 0 on data transmission
162 Figure 2 shows the Raw and Effective single-Lane bit rates of the I3C Modes compared to I2C FM+ (1 MHz).

2.3 Terminology
2.3.1 Use of Special Terms
184 The MIPI Alliance has adopted Section 13.1 of the IEEE Standards Style Manual, which dictates use of the
185 words ‘shall’, ‘should’, ‘may’, and ‘can’ in the development of documentation, as follows:
186 The word shall is used to indicate mandatory requirements strictly to be followed in order
187 to conform to the Specification and from which no deviation is permitted (shall equals is
188 required to).
189 The use of the word must is deprecated and shall not be used when stating mandatory
190 requirements; must is used only to describe unavoidable situations.
191 The use of the word will is deprecated and shall not be used when stating mandatory
192 requirements; will is only used in statements of fact.
193 The word should is used to indicate that among several possibilities one is recommended
194 as particularly suitable, without mentioning or excluding others; or that a certain course of
195 action is preferred but not necessarily required; or that (in the negative form) a certain
196 course of action is deprecated but not prohibited (should equals is recommended that).
197 The word may is used to indicate a course of action permissible within the limits of the
198 Specification (may equals is permitted to).
199 The word can is used for statements of possibility and capability, whether material,
200 physical, or causal (can equals is able to).
201 All sections are normative, unless they are explicitly indicated to be informative.
202 All quoted voltage and frequency values in the informative sections represent their typical values.
2.3.2 Definitions
203 1-Wire: A communication bus protocol.
204 ACK: Short for ‘acknowledge’. See also NACK.
205 Active Controller: The I3C Device that presently has control (i.e., the Controller Role) of the I3C Bus.
206 Address Arbitration: Process for determining arbitrated Addresses to resolve contention.
207 Address: A set of bits designating a Device or the location of a register.
208 Arbitrable: Subject to decision by Arbitration.
209 Arbitration: If two Devices start transmission at the same time, then Arbitration is required to determine
210 Bus control. Arbitration could also be required during a Target transmission if a Controller addresses multiple
211 Targets.
212 Bit Banged: When GPIOs are manually changed by software to follow a protocol. For example, software
213 could operate in I2C Controller mode quite easily by writing the SCL pin and reading/writing the SDA pin in
214 between.
215 Bridge Device: Device on the I3C Bus that allows conversion from the native I3C Bus protocol to another
216 protocol (such as SPI, UART, etc.).
217 Broadcast: A command intended for multiple Target Devices, using the Broadcast Address 7'h7E.
218 Bus: The physical and logical implementation of the SCL and SDA lines.
219 Bus Available Condition: A period during which the Bus Free Condition, after a STOP and before a START,
220 is sustained continuously for a duration of at least t AVAL (see Table 49 and Section 4.3.3.2.2).
221 Bus Free Condition: A period after a STOP and before a START with a duration of at least t CAS (see Table
222 48 and Table 49) for a Pure Bus, and at least t BUF (see Table 48 and Section 4.3.3.2) for a Mixed Bus.
10 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 25 ---\nVersion 1.2 INTRODUCTION Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
223 Bus Idle Condition: A period during which the Bus Free Condition, after a STOP and before a START, is
224 sustained continuously for a duration of at least t IDLE (see Table 49 and Section 4.3.3.2.3).
225 Bus Turnaround: When a transmitting Device sends a command, and then the receiving Device takes over
226 the I3C Bus in order to respond.
227 Characteristics: Quantification of a Device’s available features and capabilities.
228 Clock to Data Turnaround Time: The time duration between reception of an SCL edge and the start of
229 driving an SDA change. See t SCO in Table 50.
230 Coding: See Data Transfer Coding.
231 Controller: An I3C Device that is capable of controlling the I3C Bus.
232 Note:
233 In previous versions of the I3C Basic Specification, a Controller Device was called a “Master Device”.
234 This version of the I3C Basic Specification has deprecated the term “Master”, and now uses the
235 updated normative term “Controller.” Please note that the technical definition of such a Device, and
236 its Role on an I3C Bus, are unchanged.
237 Unless specified otherwise, a Controller as a Device (or its Role) is a generic term between I3C Basic
238 and I3C. Such a Device may comply with either this I3C Basic Specification or a compatible version
239 of the full I3C Specification [MIPI08] in cases where this distinction is not technically pertinent.
240 Controller Role: State in which the device assumes control of the Bus. See Active Controller.
241 Controller Role Request: A method whereby a Controller-capable Device a Secondary Controller) requests
242 to become the next Active Controller of the Bus, using a form of interrupt request (i.e., In-Band Interrupt).
243 CRC-5: Cyclic Redundancy Check with fifth-order polynomial length:
244 CRC-5 = X5 + X2 + X0
245 CRC-16: Cyclic Redundancy Check with sixteenth-order polynomial length, as defined by ANSI and used
246 in USB (also known as CRC-16-IBM):
247 CRC-16 = X16 + X15 + X2 + 1
248 CRC-32: Cyclic Redundancy Check with thirty-second-order polynomial length:
249 CRC-32 = X32 + X26 + X23 + X22 + X16 + X12 + X11 + X10 + X8 + X7 + X5 + X4 + X2 + X + 1
250 Data Transfer Coding: A specific contract for extending an I3C Mode to use additional Data Lanes (i.e.,
251 Multi-Lane). The contract may include changes to the framing, flow, and data bit/byte packing of the
252 structured protocol elements. In some sections of this Specification related to Multi-Lane, a Data Transfer
253 Coding may also be referred to as simply a “Coding”.
254 Device: A Controller or Target.
255 Device ID: Defines a Device’s characteristic or function within a sensor system.
256 Dynamic Address: A Device Address that is assigned or allocated during initialization of the Bus. Usually
257 occurs after power up.
258 Failsafe: An I3C Device Bus pad is considered Failsafe if its leakage does not increase when it is unpowered.
259 A pad may be unpowered because the Device is unpowered, because its IO rail is unpowered or clamped, or
260 both. Failsafe only matters for some Hot-Join Devices.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 11
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 26 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic INTRODUCTION Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
261 Flow Element: A structured protocol element, such as a Word or a Block, used as portion of the framing of
262 a given flow to indicate various phases of data transmission to define the Frame, as well as other necessary
263 conditions such as Bus Turnaround.
264 Frame: A Frame begins with a START, followed by the Address of the Target(s), Data, and finally a STOP.
265 High: Defines a signal level that is a logical ‘1’.
266 High Data Rate (HDR): High Data Rate Modes that achieve higher speed than SDR Mode.
267 High-Keeper: A weak Pull-Up type Device used to keep lines on High level when their drivers are on High-Z
268 condition.
269 Host: Hardware and software that provides the core functionality of a mobile device.
270 Hot-Join: Targets that join the Bus after it is already started, whether because they were not powered
271 previously or because they were physically inserted into the Bus; the Hot-Join mechanism allows the Target
272 to notify the Controller that it is ready to get a Dynamic Address.
273 Hot-Join Request: A method whereby a Target joins the Bus and requests a Dynamic Address from the
274 Controller, using a special Interrupt request with a reserved I3C Address.
275 Hot-Joining Device: A Device that is currently attempting to join the Bus (i.e., as a Target or Secondary
276 Controller) using the Hot-Join Request.
277 In-Band Interrupt (IBI): A method whereby a Target Device emits its Address into the arbitrated Address
278 header on the I3C Bus to notify the Controller of an interrupt.
279 I2C Device: A Controller or Target that meets the requirements of the I2C Specification [NXP01].
280 I3C Basic Device: A Controller or Target that meets the requirements of this I3C Basic Specification (not
281 the full I3C Specification [MIPI08]). That is, an I3C Basic Device supports only the I3C Basic core
282 capabilities plus any optional features and capabilities defined in this I3C Basic Specification; it does not
283 support any optional features defined only in the full I3C Specification.
284 Note:
285 An I3C Basic Device is defined to be interoperable with I3C with respect to the core capabilities that
286 I3C and I3C Basic share, and with respect to I3C Basic’s optional features and capabilities. See the
287 Preface for an overview of I3C v1.2 functions not included in I3C Basic and how I3C Basic Devices
288 work with I3C v1.x Devices; see also other sections in this Specification that define the specific
289 features.
290 I3C Controller: See Controller.
291 I3C Device: A Controller or Target that meets the requirements of either this I3C Basic Specification, or of
292 a version of the full I3C Specification that is interoperable with I3C Basic (e.g., I3C v1.2 [MIPI08]).
293 Note:
294 In this I3C Basic Specification, the term I3C Device typically describes compatibility with I3C in a
295 generic sense. An I3C Device does not always imply an I3C Basic Device, since an I3C Device can
296 support either I3C or I3C Basic where such distinctions are not technically pertinent. However, in
297 cases where an I3C Bus contains both I3C Basic Devices and other I3C Devices that comply with
298 the full I3C Specification and that also use features or capabilities that are not included in I3C Basic,
299 there might be restrictions on which features or capabilities can be used with such an I3C Basic
300 Device, and certain compatibility concerns should be understood and addressed. See the Preface
301 for an overview.
302 I3C Master: Deprecated term, see Controller.
303 I3C Slave: Deprecated term, see Target.
304 I3C Target: See Target.
305 Legacy I2C: I3C maintains the industry standard architecture of I2C and supports existing I2C Target Devices.
306 I3C does not support I2C Bus Controllers.
12 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 27 ---\nVersion 1.2 INTRODUCTION Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
307 Note:
308 A Legacy I2C “Target” Device is defined to have the same meaning as an I2C “Slave” Device, as
309 defined in the current version of the I2C Specification [NXP01].
310 Low: Defines a signal level that is a logical ‘0’.
311 Master: Deprecated term, see Controller.
312 Message: A packetized communication between Devices.
313 Minimal Bus: An I3C Bus with one Controller Device (potentially with reduced functionality) and one active
314 Target Device with a fixed and reserved Target Address value of 7'h01.
315 MIPI Manufacturer ID: A two-byte/16-bit unique identifier for a vendor of a MIPI compliant Device
316 [MIPI01].
317 Mixed Bus: An I3C Bus topology with both I2C and I3C Devices present on the I3C Bus. May be either a
318 Mixed Fast Bus or a Mixed Slow/Limited Bus.
319 Mixed Fast Bus: I3C Bus topology with both I2C and I3C Devices present on the I3C Bus, where the I2C
320 Devices have a true I2C 50 ns Spike Filter on the SCL line.
321 Mixed Slow/Limited Bus: I3C Bus topology with both I2C and I3C Devices present on the I3C Bus, where
322 the I2C Devices do not have a true I2C 50 ns Spike Filter on the SCL line.
323 Mode: Distinguishes different data transfer methods used in I3C and I3C Basic including Legacy I2C Mode,
324 Single Data Rate Mode (SDR), High Data Rate Mode (HDR), Dual Data Rate Mode (HDR-DDR), Ternary
325 Symbol Legacy Mode (HDR-TSL), Ternary Symbol for Pure Bus Mode (HDR-TSP), and HDR Bulk
326 Transport Mode (HDR-BT). Note that I3C Basic does not include support for the HDR Ternary Modes that
327 are defined in the full I3C Specification: Ternary Symbol Legacy Mode (HDR-TSL) and Ternary Symbol for
328 Pure Bus Mode (HDR-TSP).
329 Multi-Controller: Multiple Bus Controllers present on the Bus. Used when multiple nodes on the Bus need
330 to initiate a transfer.
331 Multi-Drop: A Bus that communicates through a process of Arbitration to determine which Device sends
332 information at any point. The other Devices listen for data they are intended to receive.
333 Multi-Lane: A method of using more physical wires (i.e., additional Data Lanes along with the SDA Lane)
334 to achieve higher data transfer rates in various I3C Modes.
335 NACK: Short for “not acknowledge”, which means No ACK was asserted. See also ACK.
336 Offline Capable: An Offline Capable Device is able to disconnect from the physical I3C Bus and/or is able
337 to ignore I3C traffic on the I3C Bus. A Device’s Offline capability is one of the capabilities reflected in its
338 Bus Characterization Register.
339 Open Drain: High-Z with an active Pull-Down. Typically used in conjunction with a passive Pull-Up.
340 Overflow: A state in which a Device or process is being fed with data at a faster speed than the data is being
341 consumed.
342 Park: Logic level High set by the Controller (or Target on Read) before turning around the Bus to allow the
343 other to drive to Logic level Low or not (will be held High by weak Pull-Up).
344 Peripheral: The portion of the logic of a Device that implements the I3C Bus protocol, usually including
345 any FIFOs or other buffers, and an interface to the rest of the system, such as an internal bus to a processor.
346 Preamble: The bits preceding the data Words in HDR-DDR.
347 Primary Controller: The Controller-capable I3C Device that initializes the I3C Bus and performs
348 configuration of all Target Devices. It acts as the authority for the Bus in its initial state, and becomes the
349 first Active Controller once the Bus is configured.
350 Pull-Down: Active mechanism used to pull the Bus to a logical Low state.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 13
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 28 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic INTRODUCTION Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
351 Pull-Up: Mechanism used to pull the Bus to a logical High state. The mechanism may be either active or
352 passive.
353 Pure Bus: A Bus topology with only I3C Devices present. No I2C Devices are permitted on a Pure Bus.
354 Push-Pull: Active Pull-Down and active Pull-Up on output driver.
355 Read Segment: A series of Flow Elements that comprise a Read transaction addressing a Target, within the
356 framing of a given HDR Mode. Usually part of a Direct SET CCC flow.
357 Repeated START: Two or more instances of a START in a row without an intervening STOP. A Repeated
358 START is used in circumstances where the Controller wishes to continue communicating on the I3C Bus
359 without having to first generate a STOP. In this Specification, a Repeated START is abbreviated as ‘Sr’. This
360 is equivalent to Repeated START in I2C [NXP01].
361 Route: Path through a Device connecting two different I3C Buses. A given Device may contain one or more
362 Routes.
363 Routing Device: Device on the I3C Bus that allows conversations between two or more different I3C Buses
364 through integrated logic on the given Device. The Routing Device (as distinct from a Bridge Device)
365 buffers/queues the transactions, causing the Device to be non-transparent.
366 SDR-Only: An SDR-Only Device supports only SDR Mode, i.e., does not support any HDR Mode.
367 Secondary Controller: A Controller-capable I3C Device that initially acts as a Target, but can also accept
368 the Controller Role from any Active Controller (including the Primary Controller). Once a Secondary
369 Controller accepts the Controller Role it becomes the new Active Controller, and may then pass the Controller
370 Role along: either back to the previous Active Controller, or on to any other Controller-capable Device
371 present on the I3C Bus.
372 Segment: A series of Flow Elements that comprise either a Read or Write transaction addressing a Target or
373 Group, within the framing of a given HDR Mode. Usually part of a Direct CCC flow.
374 Single Data Rate (SDR): Single Data Rate transfers data on only one edge of the clock.
375 Slave: Deprecated term, see Target.
376 Spike Filter: A filter that removes SCL (and SDA) spikes shorter than 50 ns in duration. See Input Filter (t SP
377 parameter) in the I2C Specification [NXP01]. Also known as a glitch filter.
378 Stall: The act of the I3C Controller holding the SCL line Low under specific transitory conditions.
379 START: START is the I3C Bus condition of a High to Low transition on the SDA line while the SCL line
380 remains High. In this Specification, a START is abbreviated as ‘S’.
381 START Request: A method for a Target to force the Controller to issue a START on an idled I3C Bus.
382 Static Address: A Device Address that is fixed and cannot be changed.
383 STOP: STOP is the I3C Bus condition of a Low to High transition on the SDA line while the SCL line
384 remains High. In this Specification, a STOP is abbreviated as ‘P’.
385 Swizzle: To swap or interleave bits.
386 T-Bit: Alternative use of the 9th bit.
387 Target: A Target Device can only respond to either Common or individual commands from a Controller.
388 Note:
389 In previous versions of the I3C Basic Specification (as well as some previous versions of the full I3C
390 Specification), a Target Device was called a “Slave” Device. This version of the I3C Basic
391 Specification has deprecated the term “Slave”, and now uses the updated normative term “Target.”
392 Please note that the technical definition of such a Device, and its Role on an I3C Bus, are unchanged.
393 Unless specified otherwise, a Target as a Device (or its Role) is a generic term between I3C Basic
394 and I3C. Such a Device may comply with either this I3C Basic Specification or a compatible version
395 of the full I3C Specification [MIPI08] in cases where this distinction is not technically pertinent.
14 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 29 ---\nVersion 1.2 INTRODUCTION Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
396 Target Reset: The Target Reset mechanism allows the Controller to request a reset of specific Target Devices,
397 including: reset of the I3C Peripheral, reset of the whole Device, and wake from deepest sleep. Target Reset
398 uses a specialized pattern of Bus activity (specifically, an extension of the HDR Exit Pattern) that cannot
399 occur in any other way in the I3C protocol.
400 Termination Marker: A Flow Element or other unique HDR Pattern that signifies the end of a specific Flow
401 Element, a Segment, or the data message sent by the Controller as part of a Broadcast CCC flow.
402 Ternary Mode: These Modes are defined in the full I3C Specification, and I3C Basic does not include
403 support for them: HDR-TSP (Ternary Symbol for Pure Bus) Mode and HDR-TSL (Ternary Symbol Legacy)
404 Mode.
405 Timestamping: The act of determining and assigning the time at which an event occurred.
406 Timing Control: Methods of exchanging and controlling system timing information, with the intent to
407 synchronize and/or Timestamp I3C Bus Devices and events. See Section 6.6.
408 Underrun: A state in which a Device or process is being fed with data at a slower speed than the data is
409 being consumed.
410 Virtual Target: A physical Device that represents multiple I3C Targets, often implemented as multiple Target
411 Devices sharing a common set of I3C pads. Additionally, there can be multiple Virtual Targets inside a single
412 Device or a Bridge/Hub that manages the Bus connection. See also Table 7, Section 4.3.2.1.2, and
413 Section 4.3.7.3.19.
414 Virtual Target Detect Operation: A method for determining which Virtual Targets share the same Peripheral
415 logic within a particular I3C Device.
416 Word: Transmission containing 16 payload bits and two parity bits.
417 Write Segment: A series of Flow Elements that comprise a Write transaction addressing a Target or Group,
418 within the framing of a given HDR Mode. Usually part of a Direct SET CCC flow.
2.3.3 Abbreviations
419 ACK Acknowledge
420 e.g. For example (Latin: exempli gratia)
421 HDR-TSx HDR-TSP and/or HDR-TSL
422 High-Z An output driver that is set to high impedance mode (cannot source or sink current)
423 i.e. That is (Latin: id est)
424 NACK Not Acknowledge
425 P STOP
426 PHY Physical Layer
427 S START
428 Sr Repeated START
429 T Transition Bit

430 AXI Advanced eXtensible Interface
431 BCR Bus Characteristics Register
432 CCC Common Command Code
433 CRC Cyclic Redundancy Check

438 ESD Electro Static Discharge

441 HDR-DDR HDR Double Data Rate Mode

3 Technical Overview (Informative)
493 This Section generally describes the I3C Bus, the I3C interface, and I3C Controller and Target Devices.
494 I3C is a two-wire bidirectional serial Bus, optimized for multiple Target Devices and controlled by only one
495 I3C Controller Device at a time. I3C is backward compatible with many Legacy I2C Devices, however I3C
496 Devices support significantly higher speeds, new communication Modes, new Device Roles, and an ability
497 to change Device Roles over time (i.e., the initial Controller can pass the Controller Role to another I3C
498 Device that supports the Secondary Controller feature).
499 I3C includes:
500 • Support for many Legacy I2C Target Devices and Messages
501 • I3C Single Data Rate (SDR) Mode: New I3C enhanced version of the I2C protocol supporting
502 private Messages, and adding two kinds of standard built-in Messages:
503 • Broadcast Messages, which are sent to all I3C Targets on the Bus
504 • Direct Messages, which are addressed to specific Targets
505 • I3C High Data Rate (HDR) Modes: Additional optional Modes that add significant functionality:
506 • Dual Data Rate (HDR-DDR) Mode: Uses same signaling as SDR Mode, but it transfers data on
507 both clock edges to achieve about twice the speed of SDR. Not significantly different from the
508 I2C protocol
509 • Bulk Transport (HDR-BT) Mode: Gives the highest possible throughput using a
510 Clock-and-Data transmission model leveraging dual data rate over single-, dual-, or quad-lane
511 configurations
512 Readers interested in a detailed, low-level introduction to the operation of the I3C Bus for each of the defined
513 I3C Modes are encouraged to study and compare the example waveforms shown in informative Annex F.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 19
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 34 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic TECHNICAL OVERVIEW Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
3.1 I3C Fundamental Principles
514 I3C supports several communication formats, all sharing a two-wire interface.
515 The two wires are designated SDA and SCL:
516 • SDA (Serial Data) is a bidirectional data pin
517 • SCL (Serial Clock) can be either a clock pin or a Bi-directional data pin while in certain HDR
518 Modes
519 An I3C Bus supports the mixing of various Message types:
520 1. I2C-like SDR Messages, with SCL clock speeds up to 12.5 MHz
521 2. Broadcast and Direct Common Command Code (CCC) Messages that allow the Controller to
522 communicate to all or one of the Targets on the I3C Bus, respectively
523 3. HDR Mode Messages, which achieve higher data rates than SDR Mode per equivalent clock
524 cycle
525 4. I2C Messages to Legacy I2C Targets
526 5. Target-initiated START Request to the Controller, for example to send an In-Band Interrupt or
527 to request the Controller Role
528 An example traffic pattern on the I3C Bus is shown in Figure 6.
SDR (I2C-Like) HDR: In-Band
One or more, R/W One or more Interrupt HDR
(Controller to Target) Commands (Initiated by Target)
529
Figure 6 Example of Data Traffic on the I3C Bus
20 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 35 ---\nVersion 1.2 TECHNICAL OVERVIEW Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
530 Figure 7 illustrates how I3C communication is initiated:
531 • All I3C communication occurs within a Frame. The Frame begins with a START, followed by one
532 or more transfers, and a STOP.
533 • For the HDR Modes:
534 • First the dedicated Broadcast I3C Address (7'h7E) is issued to all Targets on the I3C Bus.
535 • Then one of the Enter HDR CCCs is issued, indicating that the Controller is entering an HDR
536 Mode. Each HDR Mode has its own Enter HDR CCC.
537 • This is followed by one or more HDR transfers.
538 • HDR Mode is ended by using the HDR Exit Pattern protocol.

Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 21
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 36 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic TECHNICAL OVERVIEW Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
540 I3C is based on a Frame encapsulation approach. A Frame includes a Data Payload. The transfer protocol for
541 the Data Payload is either SDR or HDR. Frames are bordered by I2C-like Bus management.
542 The I3C Frame always includes at least the START, the Header, the Data, and the STOP.
543 The Header following a START allows for Bus Arbitration. The Controller uses the Header to address Target
544 Device(s). I3C Target Devices(s) may use the Header Arbitration for multiple purposes: for In-Band Interrupt,
545 for Hot-Join, and for Secondary Controller functionality.
546 ENTHDRx Common Command Codes (CCCs) are used to enter the High Data Rate (HDR) Modes. It is
547 important to understand that I3C Bus activity for the HDR Message does not follow the Legacy I2C format.
548 Supported HDR protocols use the SDA line and the SCL line to transfer commands, data, and other control
549 information, using alternate formats specified in Section 6.1.
550 I3C allows only one Controller to have control of the I3C Bus at a time. Mechanisms for handoff of the
551 Controller Role from one Device to another Device are provided.
22 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 37 ---\nVersion 1.2 TECHNICAL OVERVIEW Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
3.2 I3C Controller and Target Devices
552 A given I3C Bus always has one Controller and one or more Targets. This Section generally describes I3C
553 Controller Devices and I3C Target Devices.
554 A given I3C Device can be designed to function either solely as an I3C Controller, solely as an I3C Target,
555 or with both I3C Controller and I3C Target capabilities.
556 An I3C Device with both I3C Controller and I3C Target capabilities cannot function as both Controller and
557 Target at the same time, instead it must be configured either as an I3C Target Device or as an I3C Controller
558 Device. Such an I3C Device can be initially configured (initialized) on an I3C Bus either as the Controller of
559 that I3C Bus, or as a Target on that I3C Bus. However for that I3C Bus to function properly, only one of the
560 multiple I3C Devices on the Bus can be initially configured (initialized) as an I3C Controller Device. That
561 I3C Device will have the “Primary Controller” Device Role, and will be the first I3C Device on the Bus to
562 serve as Active Controller; all other I3C Devices and Legacy I2C Devices on the I3C Bus will be initially
563 configured (initialized) as Targets.
564 I3C introduces the concept of Active Controller, defined as the I3C Controller Device on the I3C Bus that is
565 functioning as Controller (i.e., the one that is controlling the Bus) at the present time. Only one I3C Device
566 on an I3C Bus can serve as Active Controller at a time. However after initial Bus configuration the Active
567 Controller function can be cooperatively passed from the Active Controller to any other I3C Device on the
568 Bus with I3C Controller Device capability, using provided I3C commands (CCCs).
569 I3C defines several Controller and Target Device Roles (see Table 2 and Table 4) to reflect the functional
570 capabilities of a given I3C Controller or Target Device. A given I3C Device must support at least one Device
571 Role, and can be designed to support multiple Device Roles. Every I3C Device exposes the Device Roles it
572 supports via its Bus Characteristics Register (BCR, see Section 4.3.1.2.1).
573 Table 2 Roles for I3C Compatible Devices
Device
Device Role Description
Type
I3C I3C Primary Controller Initially configures I3C Bus, has HDR support
Controller1
SDR-Only Primary Controller Initially configures I3C Bus, no HDR support
I3C Secondary Controller Can control the Bus but initially functioning as Target
Can control the Bus but initially functioning as Target,
SDR-Only Secondary Controller
no HDR support
I3C Target2 I3C Target Ordinary I3C Target, no Controller capability
Ordinary I3C Target, no Controller capability,
SDR-Only Target
no HDR support
I2C Target No I3C Controller or I3C Target capabilities
Note:
1) Applies to Controller-only Devices. In a Multi-Controller context, a Controller Device may also
implement functionality to join the Bus acting in a Target role.
2) Applies to Target-only Devices.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 23
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 38 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic TECHNICAL OVERVIEW Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
3.2.1 I3C Controller Device
574 An I3C Bus requires there to be exactly one I3C Device at a time functioning as an I3C Controller Device.
575 In I3C terms, this I3C Controller Device is the Active Controller at that time. In typical applications, the
576 Active Controller is the I3C Device on the Bus that sends the majority of the I3C Commands (CCC),
577 addressing either all Targets (Broadcast CCCs) or specific individual Targets (Directed CCCs). The Active
578 Controller is also the only Device on the I3C Bus allowed to send I2C Messages.
579 In addition to sending I3C Commands and I2C Messages, an I3C Controller Device also:
580 • Generates the Bus clock when in SDR Mode, HDR-DDR Mode, and HDR-BT Mode
581 • I3C Targets may optionally generate the Bus clock in HDR-BT Mode, for Read transfers.
582 • Note that there is no traditional clock in HDR Ternary Modes (which are not included in I3C
583 Basic).
584 • Manages Pull-Up structures
585 • Manages Dynamic Address Assignment while acting as the Active Controller.
586 • There are methods based on SETDASA, SETAASA, or ENTDAA (including Hot-Join events).
587 • Manages START Requests from I3C Target Devices on the Bus along with Address Arbitration
588 requests:
589 • For In-Band Interrupts
590 • For Hot-Join events
591 • To become Active Controller
592 • Supports I2C Legacy Target Devices
593 • Supports I3C SDR Mode
594 In addition, an I3C Controller Device can optionally support any combination of I3C’s defined HDR Modes.
595 Figure 8 is a block diagram of a typical generic I3C Controller Device.

3.2.1.1 I3C Controller Device Roles
597 All I3C Controller Devices support one of the two Primary Controller Device Roles, and may also support
598 one of the two Secondary Controller Device Roles.
599 Primary Controller Device Roles:
600 • Primary Controller: The I3C Controller Device on the I3C Bus that initially configures the I3C Bus
601 and serves as the first Active Controller. Only one I3C Device on a given I3C Bus can be the
602 Primary Controller, i.e., this responsibility cannot be passed on to any other I3C Device on the I3C
603 Bus. Supports both SDR Mode and at least one HDR Mode.
604 • SDR-Only Primary Controller: A Primary Controller that only supports I3C’s SDR Mode, i.e., does
605 not support any of the HDR Modes.
606 Secondary Controller Device Roles:
607 • I3C Secondary Controller: Any I3C Device on the I3C Bus, other than the Active Controller, with
608 I3C Controller Capability. There can be multiple Secondary Controllers on an I3C Bus at the same
609 time. By definition, a Secondary Controller functions as an I3C Target Device unless it becomes
610 Active Controller. Supports both SDR Mode and at least one HDR Mode.
611 • SDR-Only Secondary Controller: A Secondary Controller that only supports I3C’s SDR Mode, i.e.,
612 does not support any of the HDR Modes.
613 See also Table 2 and Table 3.
614 Note:
615 Active Controller is not formally defined as an I3C Device Role, and is not exposed in the I3C Device’s
616 Bus Characteristics Register (BCR, see Section 4.3.1.2.1).
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 25
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 40 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic TECHNICAL OVERVIEW Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
3.2.2 I3C Target Device
617 The maximum number of Devices that an I3C Bus supports will depend on trace length, capacitive load per
618 Device, and the types of Devices (I2C vs. I3C) present on the Bus, because these factors affect clock frequency
619 requirements.
620 Note:
621 Previous versions of the I3C Basic Specification listed a maximum number of 11 I3C Basic Target
622 Devices. This limit was calculated based on typical electrical parameters (see Section 4.3.1).
623 An I3C Target Device listens to the I3C Bus for relevant I3C Commands (CCCs) sent by the Active Controller
624 and responds accordingly. This includes all Broadcast Commands (CCC), and any Directed Commands
625 (CCC) addressed specifically to and supported by that I3C Target Device.
626 In addition to responding to I3C Commands, an I3C Target Device always supports I3C SDR Mode.
627 An I3C Target Device does not generate the Bus clock, and always follows the Bus clock generated by the
628 Active Controller, except during:
629 • Read transfers in HDR-BT Mode (optional, at implementer discretion)
630 For addressing, an I3C Target Device supports at least one of the Dynamic Address Assignment methods:
631 based on SETDASA, SETAASA, or ENTDAA.
632 In addition, an I3C Target Device can optionally:
633 • Request In-Band Interrupts
634 • Generate Hot-Join events
635 • Request to become Active Controller (If Target device is a Secondary Controller)
636 • Support any combination of I3C’s defined HDR Modes
637 While functioning as a Target, an I3C Target Device functions in one of the Target Device Roles detailed in
638 Section 3.2.2.1.
639 Figure 9 is a block diagram of a typical generic I3C Target Device.

3.2.2.1 I3C Target Device Roles
641 All I3C Target Devices support one of the two I3C Target Device Roles:
642 • I3C Target: An ordinary I3C Target Device without Controller capability. Supports both SDR Mode
643 and at least one HDR Mode.
644 • SDR-Only I3C Target: An I3C Target without Controller capability that only supports I3C’s SDR
645 Mode (i.e., does not support any of the HDR Modes).
646 Note:
647 An additional Target Device Role is defined for I2C Target, however this is not relevant for an I3C
648 Basic Target Device.
649 See also Table 2 and Table 4.

4 Required Elements
650 Every I3C Basic Device shall implement all features and functionality specified in Section 4.
651 All features and functionality specified in Section 4 are required for I3C Basic.
652 Table 3 Required Elements Status
Required Elements last updated in I3C Specification Version I3C v1.2
Required Elements is included in I3C Basic Yes
653 Changes in this Version
654 • See Annex A.
4.1 Scope and Purpose (Informative)
655 Every functioning I3C Basic Device must implement everything specified in Section 4. Optional elements
656 are out of scope for Section 4.
4.2 Technical Overview (Informative)
657 This Section specifies the communication protocols for Single Data Rate (SDR) Mode and required
658 tolerances for High Data Rate (HDR) Mode.
659 SDR Mode is the default Mode of the I3C Basic Bus. SDR Mode is used to enter other Modes, sub-Modes,
660 and states; and for built-in features such as CCCs, IBIs, and transitions from I2C to I3C Basic by assignment
661 of a Dynamic Address.
662 I3C SDR Mode is significantly similar to the I2C protocol [NXP01] in terms of procedures and conditions.
663 As a result, I3C Basic Devices and many Legacy I2C Target Devices (not Legacy I2C Controller Devices)
664 can coexist on the same I3C Basic Bus. However, SDR Mode also includes numerous new features not
665 present in I2C. The I3C Basic protocol is designed to allow I2C traffic to be properly ignored by all I3C Basic
666 Targets. I3C Basic traffic from an I3C Basic Controller to an I3C Basic Target will not be seen by most
667 Legacy I2C Target Devices because the I2C Spike Filter is opaque to I3C Basic’s higher clock speed.
668 HDR Mode is an optional Mode that allows for data speeds higher than SDR Mode. Although use of HDR
669 modes is optional, handling of entry and exit to these modes is required for all I3C Basic Devices. This
670 requirement allows for HDR capable devices and SDR devices to operate on the same Bus. After HDR Mode
671 entry, an SDR-Mode-only device will ignore all messages (HDR Mode messages) until an HDR Exit is
672 completed and the Bus returns to SDR Mode.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 31
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 46 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS
Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
This page intentionally left blank.
32 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 47 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4.3 Technical Specification
673 This Section specifies all the core elements of an I3C Basic Device. It is important to note that the I3C Basic
674 Bus is always initialized and configured in SDR Mode.
4.3.1 Bus Configuration
675 The I3C Basic Bus can be configured as the link among several clients, in a flexible and efficient manner. At
676 the system architecture level, seven roles are defined for I3C Basic compatible Devices (see Table 2).
677 An example block diagram of I3C Basic interconnections is shown in Figure 10. In this figure the color blue
678 indicates Devices with a Controller Role, the color pink indicates Devices with an I3C Basic Target role, and
679 the color purple indicates Devices with an I2C Target role.
680 Note:
681 I3C Secondary Controller Devices have the ability to function in either Controller or Target roles at
682 different times, using the same Dynamic Address. In Figure 10, the I3C Primary Controller is the
683 Active Controller of the Bus, so the I3C Secondary Controller acts as a Target (i.e., its Controller
684 capability is not active).
685 I3C Basic Devices may also present multiple simultaneous roles. In Figure 10, a typical I3C Basic
686 composite Device is shown that presents multiple Virtual Targets on the I3C Basic Bus, where each
687 Virtual Target has a separate Dynamic Address. Such a composite I3C Basic Device uses shared
688 Peripheral logic to handle transfers for its Virtual Targets, which are active simultaneously, per
689 Section 4.3.2.1.2. Other types of composite I3C Basic Devices are possible, and might include
690 support for other roles (i.e., Secondary Controller).
691 I3C Basic Devices may also enable connections to other buses, either as a Bridge Device (i.e., to a
692 different bus such as I2C or SPI) or as a Routing Device (to another I3C Basic Bus). Such Devices
693 might present one or more Virtual Targets on this I3C Basic Bus that may be used to communicate
694 through the Bridge Device or Routing Device.

Figure 11 I3C Basic Minimal Bus with I3C Basic Target Devices
700 I3C Basic compatible Devices may have diverse features, as appropriate for their function within the I3C
701 Basic Bus. Depending on the I3C Basic Bus’s system design, it might not be necessary for all features of a
702 given Device to be enabled for any particular Bus instantiation. However, the enabled features of every I3C
703 Basic compatible Device shall be described in Characteristics Registers associated with the Device and its
704 Roles, as described in Section 4.3.1.2. The I3C Basic Primary Controller shall obtain the Characteristics of
705 any Legacy I2C Devices on the I3C Basic Bus before power-up (e.g., the fixed Address of each Legacy I2C
706 Device present on the Bus).
707 At every start-up from a powered-down state, the Primary Controller shall assign a unique Dynamic Address
708 to every Device presenting an active role on the Bus, including itself. The Dynamic Address assignment
709 procedure is described in Section 4.3.4. Dynamic Addresses create a priority ranking of In-Band Interrupts.
710 Any Secondary Controllers present on the I3C Basic Bus shall be made aware of the Dynamic Address
711 assignments and Characteristic Registers associated with each I3C Basic compatible Device on the Bus via
712 Common Command Codes as described in Section 4.3.7.
4.3.1.1 I3C Basic Device Characteristics
713 The configuration of an I3C Basic Bus will depend upon the Characteristics of the I3C Basic Devices
714 intended to be active on that I3C Basic Bus. Therefore, an active I3C Basic Device playing a given Role in a
715 given I3C Basic Bus instantiation shall fulfill all responsibilities for that Role, as detailed in Table 4.

717 The I3C Basic protocol supports a subset of I2C Target features. For example, an I3C Basic Target can have
718 a Static Address but also support Dynamic Addressing. A Device shall not have a 50 ns Spike Filter enabled
719 when used in an I3C Basic Bus operating at full clock speed. These differences are summarized in Table 5.
720 When used in an I3C Basic system, I3C Basic Targets shall enable or disable appropriate I2C features as
721 shown in Table 5.

1) See Table 46 and Table 48
2) I3C Basic drive and timing requirements are different from I2C.
3) If an I3C Basic Target has I2C features intended for use on an I2C Bus, then they will not be used
on an I3C Bus. As stated in Section 4.3.2.1.1, once the Target sees a 7'h7E, it will disable I2C
features that are not used by I3C.
4) For a Mixed Bus, timing requirements when communicating with I2C-only Devices may depend on
the maximum SCL clock frequency supported by such I2C-only Devices. See Table 47 and Table
48.
5) I2C Targets are not allowed to use any of the addresses marked as reserved in the I3C
Specification as a Static Address.
723 Performance of an I3C Basic Bus is heavily dependent upon any I2C-only Devices that may be connected to
724 that Bus. Consequently, all I2C-only Devices permitted on any instantiation of an I3C Bus must be compliant
725 with one of the categories detailed in Table 6. Furthermore (and as referenced in Table 47), no I2C or I3C
726 Basic Device present on an I3C Basic Bus shall have an I2C Static Address that matches any of the Addresses
727 associated with Error Type TE0 (see Table 43).

4.3.1.2 I3C Characteristics Registers
729 I3C Characteristics Registers describe and define an I3C Basic compatible Device’s capabilities and
730 functions on the I3C Basic Bus, as the Device services a given system. Devices without I3C Characteristics
731 Registers shall not be connected to a common I3C Basic Bus.
732 There are three Characteristics Register types:
733 • Bus Characteristics Register (BCR, see Section 4.3.1.2.1)
734 • Device Characteristics Register (DCR, see Section 4.3.1.2.2)
735 • Legacy Virtual Register (LVR, see Section 4.3.1.2.3)
736 Every I3C compatible Device shall have associated Characteristics Registers, depending on the Device type:
737 • Every I3C Basic compliant Device (as defined in Table 4) that holds one active role shall have one
738 Bus Characteristics Register, and one Device Characteristics Register; these are associated with the
739 Dynamic Address.
740 • I3C Basic compliant Devices that hold multiple active roles might use a single shared Bus
741 Characteristics Register and Device Characteristics Register that applies to all such active roles; or
742 one pair of such registers for each active role, associated with each role’s Dynamic Address.
743 • Composite I3C Basic Devices that present multiple Virtual Targets should usually have
744 separate Bus Characteristics Registers and Device Characteristics Registers, i.e., one set for
745 each Virtual Target, associated with its Dynamic Address.
746 • Every Legacy I2C Device to be connected to an I3C Basic Bus shall have one associated Legacy
747 Virtual Register. Since these are Legacy Devices, it is understood that this register will exist
748 virtually, for example as part of the Device’s driver.
38 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.

4.3.1.2.1 Bus Characteristics Register (BCR)
749 Each I3C Basic Device that is connected to the I3C Basic Bus shall have an associated read-only Bus
750 Characteristics Register (BCR). This read-only register describes the I3C Basic compliant Device’s role and
751 capabilities for use in Dynamic Address assignment and Common Command Codes. The bits within the BCR
752 shall conform to the Descriptions presented in in Table 7.
753 Table 7 Bus Characteristics Register (BCR)
Bit Name Description Notes
2'b00: I3C Basic Target 1
BCR[7] Device Role[1] 2'b01: I3C Basic Controller-capable
2'b10: Reserved for future definition by MIPI
Alliance I3C WG
BCR[6] Device Role[0] 2'b11: Reserved for future definition by MIPI
Alliance I3C WG
1: Supports optional advanced capabilities. 2
Use GETCAPS CCC (Section 4.3.7.3.19) to
BCR[5] Advanced Capabilities
determine which ones.
0: Does not support optional advanced capabilities
0: Is not a Virtual Target and does not expose other 3
downstream Device(s)
BCR[4] Virtual Target Support
1: Is a Virtual Target, or exposes other downstream
Device(s)
0: Device will always respond to I3C Basic Bus 4
commands
BCR[3] Offline Capable
1: Device will not always respond to I3C Basic Bus
commands
0: No data bytes follow the accepted IBI –
1: One data byte (MDB) shall follow the accepted
IBI, and additional data bytes may follow; see
also the Set/Get Maximum Read Length CCC
BCR[2] IBI Payload
(Section 4.3.7.3.6). Data byte continuation is
indicated by T-Bit per Section 4.3.2.3.4.
See also Section 6.6 on use of IBI Payloads for
Timing Control.
0: Not Capable –
BCR[1] IBI Request Capable
1: Capable
0: No Limitation 5
BCR[0] Max Data Speed Limitation
1: Limitation
Note:
1) The BCR Device Role bits for any I3C Basic Device capable of acting as I3C Basic Controller (either Primary
Controller or Secondary Controller) are 2'b01.
2) In I3C v1.0, bit BCR[5] only indicated HDR support, and that the Controller should use the GETHDRCAPS CCC to
determine what HDR Modes were supported. In I3C v1.1 and above, bit BCR[5] now indicates that the Controller
should use the same CCC (now renamed GETCAPS) to determine what advanced features the I3C Device
supports, including (but no longer limited to) HDR. Note that the I3C v1.0 method is fully interoperable with the I3C
v1.1 and above method.
3). In I3C Basic v1.0, bit BCR[4] only indicated Bridge Devices required to comply with the I3C Basic Specification. In
I3C Basic v1.1 and above, bit BCR[4] now indicates that the Device is a Virtual Target or exposes other
downstream Devices, and that the Controller should use the GETCAPS CCC with Defining Byte VTCAPS
(Section 4.3.7.3.19) to determine what features and capabilities the I3C Device supports, including (but no longer
limited to) Bridge Devices.
4) Offline Capable Devices retain the Dynamic Address, and are specified in Section 2.3.2.
5) Controller shall use the GETMXDS CCC to interrogate the Target for specific limitation.

4.3.1.2.2 Device Characteristics Register (DCR)
754 Each I3C Basic Device that is connected to the I3C Bus shall have an associated read-only Device
755 Characteristics Register (DCR). This read-only register describes the I3C Basic compliant Device type (e.g.,
756 accelerometer, gyroscope, etc.) for use in Dynamic Address assignment and Common Command Codes. The
757 bits within the DCR shall conform to the Descriptions presented in Table 8.
758 MIPI Alliance maintains a public registry of approved and pending DCR value assignments [MIPI02].
759 Table 8 I3C Device Characteristics Register (DCR)

DCR[5] Device ID[5]

DCR[2] Device ID[2]
DCR[1] Device ID[1]
DCR[0] Device ID[0]
4.3.1.2.3 Legacy Virtual Register (LVR)
760 Each Legacy I2C Device that can be connected to the I3C Basic Bus shall have an associated read-only
761 Legacy Virtual Register (LVR) describing the Device’s significant features. Since these are Legacy I2C
762 Devices, it is understood that this register will exist virtually, for example as part of the Device’s driver. When
763 Legacy I2C Devices are present on an I3C Basic Bus, LVR data determines allowed Modes and maximum
764 SCL clock frequency. The bits within the LVR shall conform to the descriptions in Table 9.
765 All LVRs shall be established by the higher-level entity controlling the I3C Basic Bus, and shall be transferred
766 to the I3C Basic Bus Primary Controller prior to Bus configuration. The LVR content for all I2C Devices is
767 always known by the Primary Controller. The Primary Controller shall transfer the LVR content for all I2C
768 Devices to Secondary Controllers by using the DEFTGTS CCC (see Section 4.3.7.3.7).

4.3.2 Bus Communication
770 The primary protocol and Mode of I3C Basic is SDR (Single Data Rate) Mode. The SDR protocol is based
771 on the I2C standard protocol [NXP01], with a few notable variations:
772 • The I3C Basic START and STOP conditions (as shown in Figure 81 and Figure 84, respectively) are
773 identical to the I2C START and STOP in their signaling, but they may vary from I2C in their timing.
774 Compare Table 49 vs. Table 48.
775 Note:
776 In I3C Basic SDR (but not I3C HDR), a STOP or Repeated START is tolerated (i.e., may
777 appear) any time that SCL is High while the Controller controls SDA or SDA is Open-Drain.
778 This is unlike I2C, which wants STOP or Repeated START only after a NACK of an address, or
779 after ACK/NACK of data. Although I3C Basic SDR also prefers STOP and Repeated START to
780 be used only in those situations, and after an I3C Broadcast Address is ACKed, it does not
781 disallow them in any other location. However, appearance of a STOP or Repeated START in
782 the middle of an Address or in the middle of data is interpreted to cancel that Address or data.
783 • The I3C Basic Address Header is identical to the I2C Address Header in bit form and in signaling, but it
784 may vary from I2C in its timing. See Section 4.3.2.2, Table 49, and Table 50.
785 • The Data 9-bit Words use the same bit count as I2C, but differ in the ninth bit, as explained in
786 Section 4.3.2.3.
787 • In I3C, the SCL line is only driven by the Controller. Normally this drive is Push-Pull, but it can also be
788 Open Drain.
789 Because the bit count is the same for Address Header and Data Word, the I3C Basic Target only needs to
790 know whether a Message is an I3C Basic Message (vs. is an I2C Message) if the Message is addressed to that
791 Target (either directly or by Broadcast).
792 An I3C Basic Message is defined as everything from the initial START (or Repeated START) to the next
793 Repeated START or STOP.
794 An I3C Basic Message is an SDR Message if:
795 • The Address in the Address Header is 7'h7E (the I3C Broadcast Address). All I3C Targets shall match
796 Address value 7'h7E. No I2C Target will match Address 7'h7E, because that value is reserved and unused
797 in I2C.
798 • The Address in the Address Header matches the Target’s Dynamic Address (as assigned by the I3C
799 Controller per Section 4.3.4). All I3C Basic Targets shall match their own Dynamic Address. (A Target
800 may decide to NACK its own Address if needed.)
801 All I3C Basic Targets shall ignore all Messages with Addresses other than 7’h7E or the I3C Basic Controller
802 Assigned Address, and shall await either the Repeated START or the STOP. I3C Basic Targets shall not
803 transmit on the Bus in response to a non-matching Address.
804 Note:
805 Legacy I2C Targets will ignore any Message not addressed to them, and will await the next START
806 or STOP. Per Section 4.3.2.4, Legacy I2C Targets may also not see some or all I3C Basic Messages
807 and Modes due to the speed of SCL signaling.
