4.3.2.1 Role of I3C Basic Target
809 An I3C Basic Target is a functional role that is embodied or presented by an I3C Basic Device. Such a Device
810 may be a standalone physical Device that presents only one Target, or it may be a composite Device that
811 presents multiple Virtual Targets using shared Peripheral logic. In either case, each Target (which may be a
812 Virtual Target) presented on the I3C Basic Bus may be assigned its own unique Dynamic Address, and the
813 Controller can address it independently for transfers.
814 Note:
815 Many such I3C Basic Device implementations are possible that might embody or present one or
816 more active I3C Basic Target roles. For composite I3C Basic Devices that present multiple Virtual
817 Targets, the shared Peripheral logic might handle many of the underlying functions that would
818 normally be handled by internal logic for a single, standalone I3C Basic Device that only presented
819 a single Target on the I3C Basic Bus. For simplicity, the remainder of this Section treats both options
820 equally, referring to “Target” in a generic sense. See Section 4.3.2.1.2 for special exceptions.
821 The I3C Basic Target does not have to know whether it is on a Legacy I2C Bus or an I3C Basic Bus. If it has
822 an I2C Static Address, then it may participate as an I2C Device using that Address (per Section 4.3.2.1.1) up
823 until it is assigned a Dynamic Address. Once assigned a Dynamic Address, unless asked to Reset, it shall
824 only operate as an I3C Basic Target.
825 An I3C Basic Capable Target may act as an I2C Device before it gets its Dynamic Address (DA) assigned.
826 However, the Target shall ACK the START with Address 7'h7E. (The only exception would be if the Target
827 is choosing to remain an I2C-only Device on a given Bus or use, in which case it would leave its 50 ns Spike
828 Filter enabled.)
829 Before Receiving a Dynamic Address
830 Targets that do recognize START and the 7'h7E Address may see any CCC, not just ENTDAA (see
831 Section 4.3.7.3.4), and shall behave as follows:
832 • The Target shall appropriately process all required Broadcast CCCs, including ENTDAA,
833 RSTDAA (see Section 4.3.7.3.3), ENEC (see Section 4.3.7.3.1), and DISEC (see
834 Section 4.3.7.3.1).
835 Examples of appropriate processing:
836 • RSTDAA has no effect, since no Dynamic Address is assigned
837 • For a Target Device intended to be used on I3C Buses that only use SETAASA and/or
838 SETDASA to assign a Dynamic Address to it, ENTDAA support is optional.
42 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 57 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
839 • If the Target would never issue a Controller Role Request, then the DISEC CCC for the
840 Controller Role Request can be ignored.
841 • The Target shall recognize the CCCs ENTHDR0 through ENTHDR7 (see Section 4.3.7.3.9), and
842 then wait for the HDR Exit Pattern.
843 • The Target may choose to understand and process the SETDASA CCC (see Section 4.3.7.3.10)
844 when its Static Address matches.
845 • The Target may choose to understand and process the SETAASA CCC (see Section 4.3.7.3.21).
846 • The Target shall disregard all Directed CCC commands, but shall properly recognize the ends of
847 Directed CCCs (i.e., either Repeated START followed by 7'h7E, or STOP).
848 • When no Dynamic Address has been assigned yet, the Target may either support or ignore non-
849 Required and Conditionally Required Broadcast CCCs.
850 This is true even if the Target supports any of these CCCs after being assigned a Dynamic
851 Address. For example, the Target may choose to only support Test Mode only when no Dynamic
852 Address is assigned.
853 • The I3C Target shall ignore TE0 type errors related to incorrect Addresses only (see Table 43).
854 Address Header Matching
855 The role of an I3C Target shall be as follows:
856 1. Following a START or a Repeated START, at any speed conforming to Section 4.3.1 of this
857 Specification, the I3C Target shall attempt to match the Address to the I3C Broadcast Address
858 (7'h7E) or to its own Dynamic Address, once assigned.
859 If the Target also supports Group Address capabilities (per Section 4.3.4.4), then it shall also
860 attempt to match the Address to any of its assigned Group Addresses.
861 If any match is found, then the I3C Target shall treat that Message as I3C Basic SDR.
862 2. If the Message is addressed to the Target’s Dynamic Address, then the Target may ACK or
863 passively NACK the Address Header:
864 A. If the Target ACKs the Address Header, then the Target shall process the Message as I3C
865 Basic SDR, following all rules as outlined in this Section.
866 B. If the Target NACKs the Address Header (does not drive the ACK bit Low), then the
867 Target should disregard any bits that follow, up until the next Repeated START or STOP.
868 3. If the Message is addressed to any of the Target’s assigned Group Addresses, with a Write
869 (RnW bit is 0), then the Target may ACK or passively NACK the Address Header:
870 A. If the Target ACKs the Address Header, then the Target shall process the Message, as
871 described above (i.e., in the same manner as an I3C Basic SDR Write to its Dynamic
872 Address).
873 4. If the Message is addressed to the I3C Broadcast Address (7'h7E), with a Write (RnW bit is 0),
874 then the Target shall process that Message at least through the first byte of data (if any data is
875 present in the Message):
876 A. If there is a byte of data in a 7'h7E Broadcast Message, then the Message is a CCC
877 Command, per Section 4.3.7.
878 B. The I3C Basic Target shall process all applicable Required CCC commands, per
879 Section 4.3.7.3. A command may be either Always Required, or only Conditionally
880 Required, per Table 16.
881 For Required CCCs: The Target shall always remain ready to respond to certain Direct
882 CCCs sent to its Dynamic Address, such as Get Device Status (GETSTATUS, see
883 Section 4.3.7.3.15) even if the Target (or its inner system) is processing previously sent
884 Messages, and might not be able to process or ACK other CCCs or Messages. (See
885 Section 4.3.8.2.5)
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 43
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 58 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
886 C. If the CCC command changes the Mode of the I3C Basic Bus, then the I3C Target shall
887 handle the new Mode in one of two ways.
888 Either:
889 i. If the new Mode is Dynamic Address Assignment Mode (see Section 4.3.4), and
890 required for all I3C Basic Targets, then the Target shall participate if it does not have a
891 current Dynamic Address; otherwise, the Target shall await the STOP that indicates
892 exit from Dynamic Address Assignment Mode.
893 Or:
894 ii. If the new Mode is HDR (High Data Rate) Mode, then the Target may either enter into
895 HDR Mode if supported (per Section 6.1.4.1), or else enable its HDR Exit Pattern
896 detector (per Section 4.3.10.2 and Section 4.3.10.2.1) to await the exit from HDR
897 Mode.
898 5. If the Message is not addressed to the I3C Broadcast Address (7'h7E) or to any of the Target’s
899 currently assigned Addresses (i.e., Dynamic Address or Group Address), then the I3C Basic
900 Target shall await either a Repeated START or a STOP. The Target may record/monitor the bits
901 as they pass (if desired), but the only obligation is to wait for either a Repeated START or a
902 STOP:
903 Note:
904 Repeated START and STOP are specified in Section 2.3.2 and Section 4.3.11.2, and are
905 similar to I2C. In both cases, I3C Basic timing might not be the same as with I2C.
4.3.2.1.1 Role of I3C Basic Target Acting as an I2C Target with Static Address
906 As noted in other Sections, I3C Basic Targets are capable of acting as standard I2C Targets as long as they
907 have an I2C Static Address.
908 There are two situations:
909 1. They are on a Legacy I2C Bus, and so all messaging is coming from a Legacy I2C Controller.
910 2. They are on an I3C Basic Bus, but the I3C Basic Controller is not assigning them a Dynamic
911 Address, so they continue to operate as an I2C Target; Messages to/from its I2C Static Address
912 use the I2C protocol.
913 For operation on an I2C Bus, the Target may use an I2C Fm/Fm+ Spike Filter of 50 ns (or more).
914 For operation on an I3C Basic Bus, the Target may have an I2C Fm/Fm+ Spike Filter of 50 ns (or more),
915 which it shall disable once it sees a Message from an I3C Basic Controller, notably the first I3C Basic Address
916 Header (i.e., a START followed by 7'h7E and a RnW bit of 0; see Section 4.3.2.2). To support the disabling
917 of the Spike Filter, the I3C Basic Controller shall emit at least the first such I3C Basic Address Header with
918 FM/FM+ timing parameters (per Table 49) such that the High and Low periods are long enough to be seen
919 with the Spike Filter enabled (see Section 4.3.2.2.2).
920 Note:
921 If the Target has such a Spike Filter enabled, and sees the Controller send such an I3C Basic Address
922 Header with FM/FM+ timing parameters, then it shall disable the Spike Filter after the ACK (i.e., after
923 acknowledging the I3C Broadcast Address).
924 An I3C Basic Target acting as an I2C Target will operate correctly on an I3C Basic Bus with the Spike Filter
925 disabled, or without ever having a Spike Filter, because of two strict requirements on all conforming I3C
926 Basic Targets:
927 1. An I3C Basic Target shall recognize all required Broadcast CCCs and act appropriately for
928 each one, even if the Target has not yet received a Dynamic Address. This notably includes the
929 ENTHDRn CCCs (see Section 4.3.7.2 and Table 16).
930 2. An I3C Basic Target shall recognize the HDR Exit Pattern (see Section 4.3.10.2).
44 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 59 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
931 As a result of these two requirements, it is safe for an I3C Basic Target acting as an I2C Target to see the short
932 SCL High periods (unlike a Legacy I2C Device). As a result, it can operate correctly as an I2C Target on an
933 I3C Basic Bus without a Spike Filter (unlike Legacy I2C Targets).
4.3.2.1.2 Composite Devices and Virtual Targets
934 As noted above, a Virtual Target is presented by an I3C Basic Device that also presents other such Virtual
935 Targets on the I3C Basic Bus, and must manage the communications for all such Virtual Targets. In this
936 manner, a Virtual Target can be considered a function of such a composite I3C Basic Device that holds
937 multiple active Target roles simultaneously. The I3C Basic Device shall maintain separate configuration for
938 each Virtual Target, including the assigned Dynamic Address.
939 Note:
940 For additional information on Virtual Targets, refer to the MIPI Alliance I3C Application Note: Virtual
941 Devices and Virtual Targets [MIPI10].
942 During Dynamic Address Assignment (see Section 4.3.4.2) the shared Peripheral logic manages the
943 interaction with the I3C Basic Controller and other I3C Basic Targets on the Bus, for the ENTDAA procedure.
944 This includes managing Address Arbitration while providing the 48-bit Provisioned ID, and ensuring that all
945 active Virtual Targets are able to receive Dynamic Addresses. The shared Peripheral logic also initiates the
946 Hot-Join Request (per Section 4.3.5) during Bus Initialization or whenever necessary.
947 Note:
948 A Device that presents multiple Virtual Targets might also support the SETDASA and/or SETAASA
949 CCCs, instead of the ENTDAA CCC. If so, then each Virtual Target must have a unique Static
950 Address.
951 Once Dynamic Addresses are assigned, the shared Peripheral logic typically handles many of the underlying
952 communications with the I3C Basic Bus in SDR Mode, while each Virtual Target receives data for Private
953 Write transfers addressed to its Dynamic Address, and also provides data to the shared Peripheral logic for
954 responding to Private Read transfers and raising In-Band Interrupt requests.
955 The shared Peripheral logic also handles the signaling and framing for HDR Modes (if supported, per
956 Section 4.3.10) as well as participation in Broadcast and Direct CCC flows, both in SDR Mode (per
957 Section 4.3.7.1) as well as any HDR Modes in which CCCs might be supported (per Section 6.1.3).
958 The shared Peripheral logic also handles the reset flows involving the RSTACT CCC with the Target Reset
959 Pattern (see Section 4.3.9), and generates appropriate internal reset messaging to Virtual Target functions
960 based on the type of reset. In most cases, a Device that reports as Virtual Target capable should indicate this
961 via Bit[4] of the BCR (per Section 4.3.1.2.1) and also report additional capabilities using the GETCAPS
962 CCC with Defining Byte VTCAPS (see Section 4.3.7.3.19).
963 Depending on the implementation, certain actions or CCCs sent to one Virtual Target’s Dynamic Address
964 may cause side effects for other Virtual Targets presented by the same composite Device, or within the shared
965 Peripheral logic itself. In some cases, certain features and capabilities that are reported by Virtual Target’s
966 Dynamic Address (e.g., by the GETCAPS CCC) shall actually apply equally to the entire Device (i.e., to all
967 Virtual Targets and the shared Peripheral logic).
4.3.2.1.3 Targets Supporting Group Addresses
968 If a Target supports Group Addressing (per Section 4.3.4.4), then it will have a single set of capabilities or
969 other configurable parameters that pertain to either the Target, or, in special cases, the Device or its Peripheral
970 logic.
971 • If such capabilities are accessible via a Direct GET CCC (e.g., the GETCAPS CCC,
972 Section 4.3.7.3.19) then these shall be assumed to apply to the Target and all its assigned
973 Addresses, including any assigned Group Addresses, unless specified otherwise in the particular
974 Direct CCC’s definition.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 45
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 60 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
975 • If such configuration can be set via a Direct SET CCC (per Section 4.3.7.3) then these shall also
976 apply to the whole Target, with exceptions and limitations noted in Section 4.3.7.4.
977 For such capabilities or other configurable parameters, the Target should accept (i.e., should ACK) such
978 Direct SET CCCs that might be sent to any assigned Dynamic Addresses or assigned Group Addresses, unless
979 the Direct CCC format is not supported for a Group Address, or a particular Direct CCC defines a different
980 use or format that applies when sent to an assigned Group Address (e.g., the MLANE CCC, see
981 Section 6.7.3.6).
4.3.2.2 I3C Basic Address Header
982 The I3C Basic Address Header follows either a START, or a Repeated START. The format is the same as I2C:
983 7 bits of Address, 1 bit of RnW, and 1 bit of ACK/NACK.
984 The Address Header following a START is an Arbitrable Address Header, as explained in Section 4.3.2.2.1.
985 This means the START and at least the first Address bit and ACK/NACK are issued on SDA using Open
986 Drain Bus drive, similar to I2C. However, some of the Arbitrable Address Header may be driven on SDA
987 using Push-Pull and higher speed (see Section 4.3.2.2.2).
988 The Address Header following a Repeated START is always driven on SDA using Push-Pull, with the
989 exception of the ACK/NACK (see Section 4.3.2.2.4).
990 Using the I3C Basic Arbitrable Address Header, I3C Basic Targets may transmit any of three requests to the
991 I3C Basic Controller:
992 1. An In-Band Interrupt, per Section 4.3.6. This is equivalent to toggling a wire to get the
993 Controller’s attention. The In-Band Interrupt request shall be made using the Target’s Dynamic
994 Address with a RnW bit of 1.
995 2. A Controller Role Request, per Section 6.5.3. An I3C Device shall not make such a request
996 unless it is marked as a Controller-capable Device in its BCR register, per Section 4.3.1.2.1.
997 The Controller Role Request may be made by a Secondary Controller wishing to gain the
998 Controller Role from the Active Controller, or by the Primary Controller after it has
999 relinquished the Controller Role and now wishes to regain it from the Active Controller. The
1000 Controller Role Request shall include the Device’s Dynamic Address with a RnW bit of 0.
1001 3. A Hot-Join Request, per Section 4.3.5. An I3C Basic Target shall only make such a request
1002 when becoming available after the I3C Basic Bus is operational. The Hot-Join Request shall be
1003 made using the reserved Hot-Join Address (i.e., 7'h02).
1004 The I3C Basic Targets shall make these requests to the I3C Basic Controller in only two Bus conditions:
1005 1. A START (but not a Repeated START) is issued on the Bus following a Bus Free Condition,
1006 per Section 4.3.3. The Target may transmit its Dynamic Address or the Hot-Join Address
1007 (7'h02) following the START, by adhering to the I3C Basic Address Arbitration rules per
1008 Section 4.3.2.2.1.
1009 2. The Bus is in a Bus Available Condition, per Section 4.3.3.2.2, so the Target may issue a
1010 START by pulling the SDA Low.
1011 A. If the Target pulls SDA Low, then the Controller shall pull SCL Low within t CAS (see
1012 Table 49).
1013 B. The Controller shall also pull SDA Low (overlapping the Target pulling it Low).
1014 C. Once the Controller has pulled SCL Low, the Target shall control the SDA line in Open
1015 Drain mode (i.e., either pull Low, or release High).
1016 D. The Target may then issue its Address in the normal way (condition 1 above).
46 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 61 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4.3.2.2.1 I3C Basic Address Arbitration
1017 An Address Header following a START (but not a Repeated START) is subject to Arbitration, meaning both
1018 the Controller and one or more Targets may attempt to drive an Address onto the Bus, using SDA. Such
1019 Address Headers are defined as Arbitrable Address Headers.
1020 The Arbitration model follows the common Open Drain approach. All Devices (whether Controller or Target)
1021 that are transmitting an Address shall then follow the same rule:
1022 1. If the current bit to transmit is a 0, then the Device shall drive SDA Low after the falling edge
1023 of SCL and hold Low until the next falling edge of SCL.
1024 Note:
1025 Other Devices may also be driving SDA Low, but that is acceptable.
1026 2. If the current bit to transmit is a 1, then the Device shall not drive SDA, but rather shall High-Z
1027 SDA on the falling edge of SCL.
1028 A. Additionally, the Device shall monitor the SDA on the rising edge of SCL to determine
1029 whether another Device has driven SDA Low.
1030 B. If another Device has driven the SDA Low, then the Device has ‘lost’ the Arbitration and
1031 shall not further participate in this Address Header. That is, the Device shall not transmit
1032 any more bits, but may wait for a future START (but not a Repeated START).
4.3.2.2.2 I3C Basic Address Arbitration Optimization
1033 I3C Basic Address Arbitration may optionally be optimized, as detailed in this Section.
1034 The Controller shall not apply the optimizations described in this Section for the first I3C Basic Address
1035 Header (i.e., START followed by 7'h7E and an RnW bit of 0) after Bus initialization, unless the Controller
1036 knows that no I3C Basic Target on the Bus has a Spike Filter. This permits any I3C Basic Target with an I2C
1037 Spike Filter to detect that it is on an I3I3C Basic C Bus, and as a result disable the Spike Filter.
1038 As previously described in Section 4.3.2.2, an I3C Basic Controller Device assigns 7-bit Dynamic Addresses
1039 with values in the range 7'h03 to 7'h7B. However, because the I3C Basic Controller treats the entire 9-bit
1040 Arbitrable Address Header as Open Drain, it has no way to detect whether an I3C Basic Target Device might
1041 be transmitting its own Address during some (or all) of the Address Header.
1042 For I3C Secondary Controllers and I3C Basic Targets that can request In-Band Interrupts, the I3C Basic
1043 Controller is typically free to restrict assigned Dynamic Addresses to the lower half of the available range
1044 (7'h03 to 7'h3F), thus leaving the value of Address bit A6 (the first Address bit after the START) as 0 in all
1045 such assigned Dynamic Addresses. For other I3C Basic Targets that cannot request In-Band Interrupts, the
1046 I3C Basic Controller restricts assigned Dynamic Addresses to the upper half of the available range (7'h40 to
1047 7'h7B), thus leaving the value of Address bit A6 as 1 in all such assigned Dynamic Addresses.
1048 Note:
1049 This Dynamic Address Assignment strategy is not always possible, since some I3C Basic Targets
1050 might not support arbitrary Dynamic Address values in the available range (i.e., when an I3C Basic
1051 Target has an I2C Static Address and also does not support the SETNEWDA CCC; see
1052 Section 4.3.7.3.11).
1053 Having restricted Dynamic Addresses in this manner, the I3C Basic Controller may then optionally optimize
1054 the Arbitrable Address Header as follows:
1055 • If the I3C Basic Controller is transmitting a 1 value (i.e., High-Z on SDA), as it would when
1056 transmitting 7'h7E, then it can monitor SDA on the rising edge of SCL. If SDA has the value 1
1057 (i.e., if SDA is not being driven by any Target), then the I3C Basic Controller may optionally
1058 transmit the remainder of the Address Header (up until the ACK/NACK) in Push-Pull mode. See
1059 Figure 13, upper waveform.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 47
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 62 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
1060 • If the I3C Basic Controller is transmitting a 0 value (i.e., is driving SDA Low), or if SDA was
1061 driven Low by a Target, then the I3C Basic Controller shall transmit the remainder of the Address
1062 Header using Open Drain.
1063 • If the I3C Basic Controller intends to transmit solely to other I3C Basic Devices (i.e., not to any
1064 I2C Devices), then it may optionally maintain the SCL pulse width below 50 ns, with the result that
1065 any I2C Devices present on the Bus will see only a 0 value. This shorter pulse width produces a
1066 higher data rate, because for Open Drain Arbitration only the Low time is extended. See Figure 13,
1067 middle waveform.
1068 • If the I3C Basic Controller intends to transmit to any I2C Devices, then it must use the slower I2C
1069 timing. See Figure 13, lower waveform.
1070 The upper waveform of Figure 13 illustrates this optimization. When the I3C Basic Controller sees a value
1071 of 1 for Address bit A6, but previously made sure that all Dynamic Addresses assigned to such Targets have
1072 a 0 in bit A6, then the I3C Basic Controller knows that the line was not being driven by any such Target (i.e.,
1073 for an In-Band Interrupt request or a Controller Role Request).

4.3.2.2.3 Consequence of Controller Starting a Frame with I3C Basic Target Address
1075 The I3C Basic Controller normally should start a Frame with 7'h7E (for all I3C Basic Messages) or an I2C
1076 Static Address (when sending only to a Legacy I2C Target).
1077 In both cases the Address may be arbitrated, and so the Controller shall monitor to see whether an In-Band
1078 Interrupt request, a Controller Role Request (i.e., Secondary Controller requests to become the Active
1079 Controller), or a Hot-Join Request has been made.
1080 • If not, then the Controller may proceed normally.
1081 • If so, then the Controller may ACK or NACK that request and then proceed accordingly.
1082 If the Controller chooses to start an I3C Basic Message with an I3C Basic Dynamic Address, then special
1083 provisions shall be made because that same I3C Basic Target may be initiating an IBI or a Controller Role
1084 Request. So, one of three things may happen:
1085 1. The Addresses match, but the difference is caught on the RnW bit, and the Controller was
1086 initiating a Private Write (i.e., RnW = 0) while the Target was attempting to send an IBI request
1087 (i.e., RnW = 1).
1088 In this case, the Controller wins (i.e., an IBI with RnW = 1 loses), and the Target shall ACK or
1089 NACK the Private Write. The Target shall defer its IBI, and may retry at a later opportunity.
1090 2. The Addresses match, but the difference is caught on the RnW bit, and the Controller was
1091 initiating a Private Read (i.e., RnW = 1) while the Target was attempting to send a Controller
1092 Role Request (i.e., RnW = 0).
1093 In this case, the Target wins (i.e., a Private Read with RnW = 1 loses), and the Controller must
1094 ACK or NACK the Controller Role Request. The Controller shall defer its Private Read, and may
1095 retry at a later opportunity.
1096 3. The Addresses match and the RnW bits also match, and so neither Controller nor Target will
1097 ACK since both are expecting the other side to provide ACK. As a result, each side might think
1098 it had “won” arbitration, but neither side would continue, as each would subsequently see that
1099 the other did not provide ACK.
1100 A. If RnW = 0 (i.e., if the Controller was initiating a Private Write while the Target was
1101 attempting to send a Controller Role Request), then the Target shall defer its Controller
1102 Role Request, and may retry at a later opportunity.
1103 B. If RnW = 1 (i.e., if the Controller was initiating a Private Read while the Target was
1104 attempting to send an IBI request), then the Target shall defer its IBI request, and may
1105 retry at a later opportunity.
1106 C. For either value of RnW: Due to the NACK, the Controller shall defer the Private Write or
1107 Private Read, and should typically transmit the Target Address again after a Repeated
1108 START (i.e., the next one or any one prior to a STOP in the Frame). Since the Address
1109 Header following a Repeated START is not arbitrated, the Controller will always win (see
1110 Section 4.3.2.2.4).
1111 This allows the Controller to determine whether the Target intended to provide the same
1112 RnW bit (i.e., which may have led to this NACK situation), or whether it may have
1113 simply chosen not to support this transaction. In this manner, the Controller can avoid a
1114 ‘live-lock’ situation.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 49
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 64 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4.3.2.2.4 Address Header Following a Repeated START is Push-Pull
1115 The Address transmitted by the I3C Basic Controller following a Repeated START shall not be arbitrated.
1116 That is, no I3C Basic Target shall attempt to transmit its own Dynamic Address nor the reserved Hot-Join
1117 Address (i.e., 7'h02) following a Repeated START.
1118 As a result, the Address Header (i.e., the 7 bits of Address plus the RnW bit) shall be transmitted on SDA
1119 using Push-Pull mode when the Message is not to a Legacy I2C Target. The ACK/NACK bit that follows the
1120 RnW bit is always Open Drain to allow the Target to ACK or passively NACK its Address.
4.3.2.2.5 I3C Basic Target Address Restrictions
1121 The I3C Basic Target Address space is dependent on decisions by the Controller. That is, the Controller may
1122 choose the Dynamic Addresses from a set of values, observing optional and non-optional restrictions as
1123 follows. These restrictions are also illustrated in Table 10.
1124 • The I3C Basic Controller shall not use any of 7'h00, 7'h01, 7'h02, 7'h7E, 7'h7F. All are reserved for
1125 I3C.
1126 • The I3C Basic Controller shall not use any of 7'h3E, 7'h5E, 7'h6E, 7'h76, 7'h7A, 7'h7C, 7'h7F. All
1127 are prohibited since I3C Basic Targets will interpret an I3C Basic Address Header with any of
1128 these Addresses as a Broadcast Address (7'h7E) with a single-bit error. See Error Type TE0, in
1129 particular the Note associated with Table 43 (“In the ENTDAA mode, “7'h7E / R” is excluded
1130 from the TE0 error definition.”).
1131 • The I3C Basic Controller may choose to not use 7'h03, marked in I2C as reserved.
1132 • The I3C Basic Controller shall not use 7'h04, 7'h05, 7'h06, 7'h07 if any Legacy I2C Devices are
1133 present on the Bus that support I2C “High-Speed Mode”.
1134 • The I3C Basic Controller shall not use 7'h7C or 7'h7D if any Legacy I2C Devices are present on
1135 the Bus that support I2C Device ID Mode.
1136 • The I3C Basic Controller shall not use 7'h78, 7'h79, 7'h7A, 7'h7B if any Legacy I2C Devices are
1137 present on the Bus that support I2C Extended Address Mode and have an Extended Address or
1138 would be impacted by the Extended Address mechanism.

4.3.2.3 I3C Basic SDR Data Words
1140 In I3C Basic SDR, the Data Words match I2C only in the sense that they are both 9 bits long. I3C Basic SDR
1141 Data Words differ from I2C in three ways, as detailed in Sub-Sections 4.3.2.3.1, 4.3.2.3.3, and 4.3.2.3.4.
1142 In summary:
1143 1. Handoff from Address ACK to SDR Controller Write Data: When performing an SDR Write,
1144 the handoff from the Target’s Address Header ACK to the Controller’s first Data bit is different
1145 in I3C Basic. I2C is Open Drain, so overlap from the ACK Low into the first bit is harmless. By
1146 contrast, I3C Basic is Push-Pull and so this handoff is specified (see Section 4.3.2.3.1).
1147 2. Ninth Bit of SDR Controller Written Data as Parity: In I2C, the ninth Data bit written by the
1148 Controller is an ACK by the Target. By contrast, in I3C Basic the ninth Data bit written by the
1149 Controller is the Parity of the preceding eight Data bits. Therefore, in I3C Basic the Target
1150 shall not drive the SDA line for Data written by the Controller in SDR. In SDR terms, the ninth
1151 bit of Write data is referred to as the T-Bit (for ‘Transition’) (see Section 4.3.2.3.3).
1152 3. Ninth Bit of SDR Target Returned (Read) Data as End-of-Data: In I2C, the ninth Data bit
1153 from Target to Controller is an ACK by the Controller. By contrast, in I3C Basic this bit allows
1154 the Target to end a Read, and allows the Controller to Abort a Read. In SDR terms, the ninth bit
1155 of Read data is referred to as the T-Bit (for ‘Transition’) (see Section 4.3.2.3.4).
1156 Note:
1157 A Target should have an SDA Read detector that determines if the SCL clock has not changed
1158 for 150 µs or more, so that it can abandon the read by switching SDA to High-Z and waiting for
1159 Repeated START or STOP.
4.3.2.3.1 Transition from Address ACK to SDR Controller Write Data
1160 The end of any Address Header (whether Arbitrated or not) is an ACK or NACK by the one or more addressed
1161 Targets, using Open Drain on SDA:
1162 • If 7'h7E, then it is the ACK of all I3C Basic Targets on the Bus.
1163 • If a single Target Address, then it is the ACK (or NACK) of the addressed Target, or a NACK if no
1164 such Target is on the Bus.
1165 When the Address Header results in an ACK, and the Message is SDR Write from Controller, the SDA line
1166 has to be turned from Open Drain to Push-Pull for the first data bit. To do that safely, I3C Basic SDR specifies
1167 how the handoff is to occur. This is summarized below and shown in Figure 76.
1168 1. The I3C Basic Target shall hold the SDA line Low during the ACK (while SCL is Low).
1169 This shall be an Open Drain SCL Low period.
1170 2. After the I3C Basic Target sees the rising edge of SCL, it releases the SDA line to High-Z.
1171 The I3C Basic Target shall release the SDA line using normal (Push-Pull) timing (release
1172 the SDA line as soon as it sees SCL rising).
1173 3. After the rising edge of SCL, the I3C Basic Controller shall drive the SDA line Low.
1174 As a result, both Controller and Target will be driving the SDA line Low for a short
1175 overlap (which is safe).
1176 The SCL High period may be as short as the minimal t DIG_H , per Section 4.3.11.2.
1177 4. On the falling edge of SCL the I3C Basic Controller shall begin driving data on the SDA line,
1178 using Push-Pull drive as shown in Figure 76.
1179 When the Address Header results in a NACK, the Controller may choose to either:
1180 1. Continue the transaction, by generating a Repeated START
1181 or
1182 2. Relinquish the Bus, by generating a STOP as shown in Figure 77.
52 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 67 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4.3.2.3.2 Transition from Address ACK to Mandatory Byte during IBI
1183 The end of any IBI Address Header during an IBI request (whether the Address Header is Arbitrated or not)
1184 is either an ACK or a NACK by the Controller, using Open Drain on SDA.
1185 If the Controller detects one of the Target Addresses, and if the Controller chooses to receive the request, then
1186 the Controller will ACK the request per Section 4.3.6.2.
1187 • ACK: When the IBI Target Address Header results in an ACK, and the Target Device is capable of
1188 sending a Mandatory Byte, then the SDA line has to be turned from Open Drain to Push-Pull for
1189 the first data bit. To do that safely, I3C Basic SDR specifies how the handoff is to occur. This is
1190 summarized below and shown in Figure 14.
1191 1. The I3C Basic Controller shall hold the SDA line Low during the ACK (i.e., while the SCL line
1192 is Low). This shall be an Open Drain SCL Low period.
1193 2. After the rising edge of the SCL line, the I3C Basic Target shall drive the SDA line Low as
1194 soon as possible. As a result, both Controller and Target will be driving the SDA line Low for a
1195 short overlap (which is safe).
1196 3. After (A) a minimum of the t SCO time reported by the Target Device, (B) a predetermined safe
1197 time that could guarantee the takeover by all Targets, or (C) the Controller may optionally keep
1198 the SDA line Low until the next falling edge of the SCL line in order to be in full control of the
1199 SDA line and make sure it accommodates all t SCO values from different Targets, the I3C Basic
1200 Controller may release the SDA line. The SCL High period may be as short as the minimal
1201 t DIG_H , per Section 4.3.11.2.
1202 4. On the falling edge of SCL, the I3C Basic Target shall begin driving data on the SDA line,
1203 using Push-Pull drive as shown in Figure 14.
1204 • NACK: When the Address Header results in a NACK, as shown in Figure 15, the Controller may
1205 choose to either:
1206 • Continue the transaction, by generating a Repeated START, or
1207 • Relinquish the Bus by generating a STOP.

4.3.2.3.3 Ninth Bit of SDR Controller Written Data as Parity
1210 The ninth data bit of each SDR Data Word written by the I3C Basic Controller (also referred to as the T-Bit)
1211 is a Parity bit, calculated using odd parity. Parity can help in detecting noise-caused errors on the line. The
1212 value of this Parity bit shall be the XOR of the 8 Data bits with 1, i.e.: XOR( Data[7:0], 1 ).
1213 Examples
1214 • If all eight data bits are 1’s (0xFF), then the Parity bit value will be 1.
1215 • If all eight data bits are 0’s (0x00), then the Parity bit value will be 1.
1216 • If an odd number of bits in the Data Word are 1’s (e.g., 0xFE or 0x01), then the Parity bit value
1217 will be 0.
1218 T (Parity) bit writes shall always be kept valid through the SCL High period. In the case of a T-Bit
1219 representing the last data byte, the write is therefore kept valid through the SCL High period, and the next
1220 SCL Low can then be used to either change the SDA, or not change the SDA, in preparation for the Repeated
1221 START or STOP that follows.
4.3.2.3.4 Ninth Bit of SDR Target Returned (Read) Data as End-of-Data
1222 In I2C, Read from Target has the issue that only the Controller ends the Read, so the Target has no ability to
1223 control the amount of data it returns. In I3C Basic SDR, by contrast, the Target controls the number of data
1224 Words it returns; but it also allows the I3C Basic Controller to abort the Read prematurely when necessary.
1225 This mechanism is controlled purely by the ninth (T) Data bit of each SDR Data Word returned by the I3C
1226 Basic Target. The ninth bit is returned by the Target in one of three ways, as explained below.
1227 • The I3C Basic Target returns the ninth bit as 0 (SDA Low) to end the Message (i.e., when reaching
1228 the configured MRL value for read and IBI payload size for IBI payload as defined in
1229 Section 4.3.7.3.6):
1230 • The Target shall set SDA Low on the falling edge of SCL.
1231 • On the following rising edge of SCL, the Target shall set SDA to High-Z.
1232 • The I3C Basic Controller shall drive SDA Low on the rising edge of SCL, thereby overlapping
1233 with the Target.
1234 • The I3C Basic Controller then shall issue either a STOP as shown in Figure 84, or a Repeated
1235 START as shown in Figure 85 (on the next clock, or one after, per the normal I2C procedure
1236 for setting up SDA to issue a Repeated START).
1237 • The I3C Basic Target returns the ninth bit as 1 (SDA High) to continue the Message (and permit
1238 the Controller to abort the Message):
1239 • The Target shall set SDA High on the falling edge of SCL.
1240 • On the following rising edge of SCL, the Target shall set SDA to High-Z, thereby Parking the
1241 Bus for the SCL High period:
1242 • If the I3C Basic Controller is able to continue the reply from the Target, then it shall do
1243 nothing. The weak Pull-Up resistor on SDA will keep SDA High during the SCL High
1244 period, as shown in Figure 86.
1245 • If the I3C Basic Controller wants to abort the Message, then it shall drive SDA Low after
1246 the rising edge of SCL, thereby terminating the Message with a Repeated START. The I3C
1247 Basic Controller then takes control starting with the falling edge of SCL. The Controller
1248 shall ensure enough delay after SCL rising before driving SDA Low to ensure no
1249 contention. In order to achieve this delay, the Controller might have to extend the SCL High
1250 period.
1251 Since the transition of SDA Low when SCL is High is a Repeated START, the Controller
1252 may begin a new Address (see Figure 88), or it may issue a STOP in the next cycle (see
1253 Figure 87). However in a Mixed Bus the Controller should extend the SCL Low period, in
1254 order to ensure that any Spike Filters for Legacy I2C Devices properly reset.
56 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 71 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
1255 • The Target shall monitor the SDA on the falling edge of SCL:
1256 • If SDA is High, then the Target shall continue with the next data value.
1257 • If SDA is Low (i.e., if there has been a Repeated START), then the Message has been
1258 aborted, and the Target shall not drive SDA after that.
4.3.2.4 Use of Clock Speed to Prevent Legacy I2C Devices from Seeing I3C Basic
Traffic
1259 In a system, there are three possible I3C Basic Bus Configurations:
1260 1. Pure Bus: Only I3C Basic Devices are present on the Bus.
1261 2. Mixed Fast Bus: Both I3C Basic Devices and Legacy I2C Devices are present on the Bus, such
1262 that the Legacy I2C Devices are restricted to ones that are generally permissible (i.e., Target-
1263 only, and no Target clock stretching), and that have a true I2C 50 ns Spike Filter on SCL. (I.e.,
1264 I2C Devices that do not ‘see’ the SCL line as High when the High duration is less than 50 ns,
1265 across all temperatures and processes.)
1266 3. Mixed Slow/Limited Bus: Both I3C Basic Devices and Legacy I2C Devices are present on the
1267 Bus, such that the Legacy I2C Devices are restricted to ones that are generally permissible (i.e.,
1268 Target-only, and no Target clock stretching), but that do not have a true I2C 50 ns Spike Filter
1269 on SCL.
1270 The Bus designer’s choice of Bus Configuration affects what speed options are available for I3C Basic SDR,
1271 as well as what HDR Modes are possible at various clock speeds. Table 11 shows the possible options for
1272 each Bus Configuration.
Table 11 Available Options for Bus Operating Parameters, Per I3C Bus Configuration
Available Options Per Bus Configuration

Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 57
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 72 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4.3.2.4.1 Use of Duty Cycle to Achieve Lower Effective Speed in a Mixed Fast Bus
1273 An I3C Pure Bus may simply change the clock speed to any frequency in the allowed speed range, as outlined
1274 in Section 4.3.1. By contrast, a Mixed Fast Bus wanting to clock faster than the slowest Legacy I2C Device
1275 needs to take advantage of the Spike Filter, i.e., shall ensure that the SCL High period is shorter than the
1276 Spike Filter, in order to prevent the Legacy I2C Devices from seeing the SCL High period (see t DIG_H_MIXED
1277 in Table 50). As a result, the Legacy I2C Device ‘sees’ the SCL as staying Low the whole period.
1278 However, a Mixed Fast Bus I3C Basic Controller can change the effective Bus frequency by varying the
1279 duty-cycle of SCL. This allows running the data rate slower, for example to accommodate Targets that need
1280 a lower rate as defined by GETMXDS CCC (see Section 4.3.7.3.18). It may also be necessary to
1281 accommodate Legacy I2C Devices with Spike Filters that need a longer Low period in order to function
1282 properly.
1283 In this model the SCL High period shall never exceed t DIG_H_MIXED , thus staying below the 50 ns required by
1284 the I2C Spike Filter; however, the Low period is free to be any length permitted by I3C Basic’s allowed clock
1285 frequency range. For example, depending on the clock generation capability of the I3C Basic Controller, the
1286 SCL Low period may be a multiple of the High period, or it may be any multiple of some higher frequency
1287 clock capable of providing a High period less than or equal to t DIG_H_MIXED , but greater than the minimum
1288 SCL High period as defined in Table 48.
1289 Example 1: An SCL High period of 40 ns, plus a Low period of 280 ns, yields a total clock period of 320 ns
1290 which corresponds to a frequency of 3.125 MHz.
1291 Example 2: In order to ensure that the Legacy I2C Device Spike Filters continue tracking SCL as Low, an
1292 SCL High period of 40 ns could be used with a Low period of 80 ns, yielding a total clock period of 120 ns
1293 which corresponds to a frequency of 8.3 MHz.
1294 Such adjustment of the clock Duty Cycle brings three benefits:
1295 • It remains hidden from Legacy I2C Devices, while still significantly increasing the SDR,
1296 HDR-DDR, and HDR-BT data rate.
1297 • It ensures a longer Low period, so that Legacy I2C Device Spike Filters continue to track SCL as
1298 Low.
1299 • It is easy for a Controller to generate, and has no impact on I3C Basic Targets (which just react to
1300 clock edges).
1301 This model works because all I3C Basic Targets must be able to accommodate 12.5 MHz as a clock rate, so
1302 the shorter High period will not impact them.
1303 Note that this Duty Cycle technique does not resolve issues of long Buses with high line capacitance. This is
1304 because the short High period may be insufficient for SDA propagation, even if the Low period is long
1305 enough.
58 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 73 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4.3.3 Bus Conditions
1306 This Specification defines Open Drain Pull-Up and High-Keeper, as well as three distinct conditions in which
1307 the I3C Basic Bus shall be considered inactive: Bus Free, Bus Available, and Bus Idle.
4.3.3.1 Open Drain Pull-Up and High-Keeper
1308 I3C Basic Controller Devices shall provide an active Open Drain class Pull-Up, to be engaged whenever the
1309 Bus is in Open Drain mode (with exceptions, detailed below, where a weak Pull-Up may be used).
1310 This active Pull-Up shall be implemented either:
1311 1. As a passive resistance from V DD , or
1312 2. As a passive resistance from a current source, or
1313 3. In any other method that both:
1314 A. Balances its current sourcing in order to ensure that SDA rises within t rDA (see Table 48),
1315 and
1316 B. Is not so strong as to prevent a Target with the minimum I OL driver (see Table 46) from
1317 driving SDA Low within t rDA (see Table 48).
1318 In addition to the active Open Drain class Pull-Up, a High-Keeper is also required on the Bus. A High-Keeper
1319 is generally used for a Controller-to-Target or Target-to-Controller handoff, and for optional termination uses
1320 where the Controller can signal a termination by driving SDA Low while Parked High.
1321 The High-Keeper on the Bus shall be strong enough to prevent system leakage (i.e., the sum of the leakage
1322 of all Devices on the Bus) from pulling SDA, and sometimes SCL, Low. The High-Keeper on the Bus shall
1323 also be weak enough that a Target with the minimum I OL driver (see Table 46) is able to pull SDA, SCL, or
1324 both Low within the Minimum t DIG_L period.
1325 The Controller may provide the High-Keeper on the Bus. If the Controller does so, then the Controller shall
1326 also provide a way to disable its High-Keeper. Reasons to disable a Controller-provided High-Keeper might
1327 include that the High-Keeper is not strong enough for the present system leakage, or other reasons.
1328 A Controller-provided High-Keeper may optionally be implemented using a single, common Pull-Up Device
1329 capable of supporting both the active Open Drain class Pull-Up function and the weak High-Keeper class
1330 Pull-Up function.
1331 Whether implemented as a single combined Pull-Up, or as two separate Pull-Ups, the Controller should
1332 switch SCL and SDA, each independently, between three Pull-Up states as needed, based on Bus state:
1333 1. No Pull-Up (High-Z)
1334 2. High-Keeper Pull-Up
1335 3. Open Drain Pull-Up
1336 The Bus shall have High-Keepers on SDA and SCL. When adequate High-Keepers cannot be provided by
1337 the Controller, then the system designer is required to handle this externally. Reasons why the Controller
1338 would be unable to provide adequate High-Keepers might include that the Controller does not support High-
1339 Keepers, that the Controller-provided High-Keepers are not strong enough for present needs, or that the Bus
1340 is too long to use the Controller-provided High-Keepers.
1341 The system High-Keepers on SCL and SDA may be implemented as one or more passive resistors tied to
1342 V DD , or they may be active Bus-Keeper Devices that turn off when the respective line is pulled below some
1343 threshold. The system High-Keepers on SCL and SDA shall be sized so as to balance between system leakage
1344 and the requirement that I3C Basic Devices be able to pull the corresponding line Low within the t DIG_L
1345 period.
1346 Note:
1347 The Controller may choose to not use the Open Drain class Pull-Up in cases of Open Drain mode
1348 where the SDA happens to be already High (i.e., is coming into the SCL Falling edge). In that case,
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 59
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 74 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
1349 the Controller may choose to rely solely upon the High-Keeper, in order to use less power if and when
1350 a Target drives SDA Low.
4.3.3.2 Bus Condition Timing
1351 After a STOP, three defined timing conditions are used to create permission for a Controller or a Target to
1352 take defined actions leading to a START: the Bus Free Condition, the Bus Available Condition, and the Bus
1353 Idle Condition. Figure 16 illustrates timing for each condition, and each condition is detailed in a sub-section
1354 below.

1355
Figure 16 Bus Condition Timing
4.3.3.2.1 Bus Free Condition
1356 The Bus Free Condition is defined as a period occurring after a STOP and before a START, and with the
1357 following duration:
1358 • For Pure Bus: A duration of at least t CAS (see Table 49).
1359 • For Mixed Bus (i.e., at least one Legacy I2C Device is present on the I3C Basic Bus): A duration
1360 of at least t BUF (see Table 48). Note that the value of t BUF depends upon whether any I2C FM
1361 Devices vs. I2C FM+ Devices are present on the Mixed Bus.
4.3.3.2.2 Bus Available Condition
1362 The Bus Available Condition is defined as a period during which the Bus Free Condition is sustained
1363 continuously for a duration of at least t AVAL (see Table 49). A Target may only issue a START Request (e.g.,
1364 for an In-Band Interrupt, or for a Controller Role Request) after a Bus Available Condition.
4.3.3.2.3 Bus Idle Condition
1365 The I3C Basic Bus Idle Condition is defined in order to help ensure Bus stability during Hot-Join events, and
1366 is defined as a period during which the SDA and SCL lines both sustain High level for a duration of at least
1367 t IDLE (see Table 49).
60 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 75 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4.3.3.3 Activity States
1368 I3C Basic provides a mechanism for the Controller to inform Targets about expected upcoming levels of
1369 activity on the I3C Basic Bus, in order to help the Targets better manage their internal states. Four Activity
1370 State levels from 0 through 3 are defined (see Table 12).
Table 12 Activity States
Activity State Activity Interval CCC
0 1 µs ENTAS0
1 100 µs ENTAS1
2 2 ms ENTAS2
3 50 ms ENTAS3
1371 The Activity State number serves as a hint to the Target, indicating how long it will be before the Controller
1372 directs Bus activity to that Target, and the likely latencies to expect in response to the Target pulling SDA
1373 Low (i.e., for the START Request to then generate an In-Band Interrupt Request or a Controller Role
1374 Request).
1375 The Controller uses CCC commands ENTAS0, ENTAS1, ENTAS2, and ENTAS3 (see Section 4.3.7.3.2) to
1376 communicate the four expected Bus Activity states to Targets. Each ENTASx CCC has both a Broadcast
1377 version and a Directed (per-Target) version. The Controller may switch to a different Activity State in any
1378 way, and at any time, by issuing the appropriate ENTASx CCC command.
1379 The Target may use the received Activity State hint to adjust internal settings such as power savings, FIFO
1380 trigger levels, timestamp counters and clock rates, and other suitable operating parameters. However Targets
1381 are not required to support the Activity States CCCs (ENTASx), and as a result could even ignore them
1382 completely.
1383 The Activity States mechanism is a basis for a general agreement between Controller and Target, i.e., that the
1384 Target may NACK any access occurring sooner than the general time factor. For example: If the Controller
1385 sends the ENTAS2 CCC, meaning the Controller is unlikely to initiate a request sooner than 2 ms from the
1386 time the CCC is sent, then a request arriving only 1 ms later could result in a NACK (although it will be
1387 ACKed if the request is repeated after the Target re-awakens). As a result, the Target shall wake up either
1388 upon any Bus activity, or upon matching 7'h7E and its own Dynamic Address.
1389 The Activity State CCCs also adjust the maximum value for I3C Basic Bus timing parameter t CAS (Clock
1390 after START; see Table 49), the maximum amount of time that the Controller may take to generate the SCL
1391 clock (drive SCL Low) in response to the Target pulling SDA Low. Note that t CAS is only a worst-case
1392 number; it does not indicate whether or not the Controller will ACK the In-Band Interrupt Request or
1393 Controller Role Request. Further, the selected Activity State does not necessarily indicate the time at which
1394 the Controller will read additional data from a Target in response to an In-Band Interrupt; that is a private
1395 contract between Controller and Target. A Target that does not support the ENTASx CCCs shall have a t CAS
1396 maximum value of 50 ms (the ENTAS3 value).
1397 Activity States are not intended as a substitute for a more precise power mode, nor for any other mechanism
1398 that might be supported by private contract between Controller and Target. For example, if a Target has a
1399 Device power mode setting, then the Controller should use that mechanism to put the Target into the desired
1400 state. Likewise, a Target could provide a FIFO trigger level setting, relating to the amount of time that the
1401 Controller will have to read the FIFO contents in response to an In-Band Interrupt Request; if so, then the
1402 Controller should use that setting to match its internal latencies.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 61
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 76 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4.3.4 Bus Initialization and Dynamic Address Assignment Mode
1403 The Controller-capable Device with the job of initializing the I3C Basic Bus holds the special role of
1404 “Primary Controller”. The Primary Controller acts as the authority for the initial configuration of the Bus and
1405 all Devices, including any Legacy I2C Devices. Since it acts as the Bus’s first Active Controller, during Bus
1406 initialization it also performs the Dynamic Address Assignment procedure as part of configuring all I3C Basic
1407 Devices that require a Dynamic Address.
1408 Additionally, either the Primary Controller or any Secondary Controller that supports Hot-Join may also
1409 subsequently perform the Dynamic Address Assignment procedure while acting as Active Controller, i.e.,
1410 when a Device needs to be reset or when a new Device is connected to an already-configured I3C Basic Bus
1411 and performs a Hot-Join Request.
1412 As detailed in this Section, a Controller must be responsible for performing a Dynamic Address Assignment
1413 procedure, in order to provide a unique Dynamic Address to each I3C Basic Device (i.e., Targets or Secondary
1414 Controllers) that is connected to the Bus.
1415 Note:
1416 For the remainder of this Section, the term ‘Controller’ applies to any capable Controller, including
1417 the Primary Controller, performing the Dynamic Address Assignment procedure for any reason.
1418 Once a Target or Secondary Controller receives a Dynamic Address, that Dynamic Address shall be used in
1419 all subsequent transactions on the I3C Basic Bus, until and unless the Controller changes the Dynamic
1420 Address (if applicable). The only way for the Controller to change the Dynamic Address is by using either
1421 the RSTDAA CCC command (see Section 4.3.7.3.3) or the SETNEWDA CCC command (if applicable; see
1422 Section 4.3.7.3.11). The Controller might choose to change the Dynamic Address due to re-prioritization,
1423 unless the Target does not support the SETNEWDA CCC.
1424 The Controller controls the Dynamic Address Assignment process. This process includes an Address
1425 Arbitration procedure similar to I2C’s (see [NXP01] at Sections 3.1 and 3.1.8). The I3C Basic Arbitration
1426 procedure differs from I2C by using the values of the 48-bit Provisioned ID and the Device’s I3C Basic
1427 Characteristic Registers (that is, BCR and DCR), concatenated. The Device on the I3C Basic Bus with the
1428 lowest concatenated value wins each Arbitration round in turn, and the Controller assigns a unique Dynamic
1429 Address to each winning Device.
62 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 77 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4.3.4.1 Device Requirements for Dynamic Address Assignment
1430 This Section describes the requirements for Target Devices to be identified by the Controller for assignment
1431 of a Dynamic Address.
4.3.4.1.1 Target Device 48-bit Provisioned ID
1432 A Device that supports the Broadcast Command Code Enter Dynamic Address Assignment (ENTDAA, see
1433 Section 4.3.4.2) shall have a 48-bit Provisioned ID. The Controller shall use this 48-bit Provisioned ID, unless
1434 the Device has a Static Address and the Controller uses the Static Address.
1435 The 48-bit Provisioned ID is composed of three parts:
1436 1. Bits[47:33]: MIPI Manufacturer ID [MIPI01] (15 bits)
1437 Note: The Most Significant Bit of the 16-bit MIPI Manufacturer ID is discarded,
1438 i.e., only the 15 Least Significant Bits are used.
1439 2. Bit[32]: Provisioned ID Type Selector (One bit, 1'b1: Random Value, 1'b0: Vendor Fixed
1440 Value)
1441 3. Bits[31:0]: 32 bits containing either a Vendor Fixed Value or a Random Value, depending on
1442 the value of Bit[32]:
1443 If the value of Bit[32] is 1'b0: Vendor Fixed Value, then:
1444 • Bits[31:16]: Part ID: The meaning of this 16-bit field is left to the Device vendor to define.
1445 • Bits[15:12]: Instance ID: The value in this 4-bit field should identify the individual Device,
1446 using a method selected by the system designer. For example: straps, fuses, non-volatile
1447 memory, or another appropriate method.
1448 • Bits[11:0]: The meaning of this 12-bit field is left for definition with additional meaning.
1449 For example: deeper Device Characteristics, which could optionally include Device
1450 Characteristic Register values.
1451 If the value of Bit[32] is 1'b1: Random Value, then:
1452 • Bits[31:0]: 32-bit value randomly generated by the Device. This value can be queried via
1453 General Test Mode, using the Command Code Enter Test Mode (ENTTM) (see
1454 Section 4.3.7.3.8).
1455 Note:
1456 Under Vendor Test Mode, the Device may provide a fully random or pseudo-random 32-bit value in
1457 Bits[31:0] of its Provisioned ID (see Section 4.3.7.3.8). Bits[47:33] shall not be randomized.
4.3.4.1.2 Unique Identifiability Methods
1458 In order to support the Dynamic Address Assignment procedure, each I3C Basic Device to be connected to
1459 an I3C Basic Bus shall be uniquely identifiable before starting the procedure. Two mechanisms are defined:
1460 1. The Device shall have a 48-bit Provisioned ID (see Section 4.3.4.1.1).
1461 2. In addition, the Device may also optionally have a Static Address, in which case the Controller
1462 may use that Static Address (presuming it is known to the Controller). For example, an Address
1463 similar to what I2C specifies [NXP01].
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 63
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 78 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4.3.4.2 Bus Initialization Sequence with Dynamic Address Assignment
1464 Bus Initialization and Dynamic Address Assignment shall be executed according to the following sequence.
1465 See also Figure 183, Dynamic Address Assignment FSM.
1466 The Dynamic Address Assignment process shall be performed in Open Drain mode, except that the Repeated
1467 START and 7'h7E/R may be either Open Drain or Push-Pull. For Open Drain the Controller shall drive the
1468 SCL line with clocks at the appropriate Open Drain speed for the Devices present on the I3C Basic Bus. For
1469 Repeated START, the Primary Controller may optionally choose to actively drive the SDA line High, but
1470 only after the Target has safely released the SDA line.
1471 In the procedure below, steps 1 and 2 shall be performed only by the Primary Controller, and only during Bus
1472 initialization. Subsequent steps may be performed by any capable Controller that is the Active Controller for
1473 subsequent Dynamic Address Assignment as needed.
1474 1. The Primary Controller shall begin in an appropriately configured state, and shall have, either
1475 in its own non-volatile memory or as a result of having received it from the Host, the following
1476 data:
1477 A. The number of I3C Basic compliant Devices that need to receive a Dynamic Address,
1478 B. The data for any I3C Basic Devices resident on the I3C Basic Bus that already have I2C
1479 Static Addresses, and
1480 C. The data for any Legacy I2C Devices resident on the I3C Basic Bus.
1481 2. The Primary Controller should assign Dynamic Addresses to any I3C Basic Devices with a
1482 known Static Address, using the Command Code Set Dynamic Address from Static Address
1483 (SETDASA, see Section 4.3.7.3.10), or by assigning all I3C Basic Devices their known I2C
1484 Static Address using the Command Code Set All Addresses to Static Address (SETAASA)
1485 (see Section 4.3.7.3.21).
1486 Under these pre-conditions, Targets supporting the SETAASA CCC and Targets only
1487 supporting the SETDASA CCC (see Section 4.3.7.3.10) can be mixed on the I3C Basic
1488 Bus. Via SETDASA, the Controller will individually assign a Dynamic Address to each
1489 Target not supporting SETAASA.
1490 In a system that mixes I2C-capable Devices and non-I2C-capable Devices, the Controller
1491 should send the SETAASA CCC before sending the ENTDAA CCC (see
1492 Section 4.3.7.3.4), in order to assign Dynamic Addresses to the I3C Basic-only Targets. In
1493 addition, any Target having a restricted Address (see Table 10) shall not support
1494 SETAASA.
1495 All I3C Basic Target Devices shall support at least one of the aforementioned Dynamic
1496 Address assignment methods.
1497 3. If the Active Controller knows it has already assigned all Targets their Dynamic Address(es) in
1498 the preceding Bus initialization steps, then it may skip the remaining steps in this procedure
1499 (i.e., is permitted to not use the ENTDAA CCC). Otherwise, the Active Controller shall send
1500 the Broadcast Command Code Enter Dynamic Address Assignment (ENTDAA, see
1501 Section 4.3.7.3.4).
1502 Note:
1503 A Target Device that does not implement the ENTDAA Broadcast Command Code
1504 CANNOT BE RELIED ON TO BE ASSIGNED A DYNAMIC ADDRESS on a generic I3C
1505 Basic Bus. DAA is an essential basic function of the generic I3C Basic Bus, and ENTDAA
1506 should not be omitted without careful consideration.
1507 4. The Active Controller shall send a Repeated START, and the I3C Basic Broadcast Address
1508 7'h7E with RnW bit High (i.e., Read). Every I3C Basic Device on the I3C Basic Bus that
1509 supports Dynamic Address Assignment with ENTDAA and does not yet have an assigned
1510 Dynamic Address shall acknowledge the I3C Broadcast Address, except for Hot-Joining
1511 Devices that are not eligible to participate (per Section 4.3.5).
64 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 79 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
1512 At least one I3C Basic Device on the I3C Basic Bus should acknowledge the I3C
1513 Broadcast Address in this step, if a Hot-Join Device had previously initiated a Hot-Join
1514 Request to signal that it needed a Dynamic Address and was eligible to participate in
1515 Dynamic Address Assignment with ENTDAA.
1516 Note:
1517 Per Section 4.3.5, a Hot-Joining Device must first emit the Hot-Join Request at least
1518 once, before it may ACK the I3C Broadcast Address 7'h7E with RnW bit High and
1519 participate in a Dynamic Address Assignment procedure with ENTDAA. For example, a
1520 Hot-Joining Device that uses the standard Hot-Join method must wait to determine that
1521 the Bus is Idle (see Section 4.3.3.2.3), before it may request a START as part of a Hot-
1522 Join Request. If such a Device has not waited for the minimum time (i.e., Bus Idle
1523 Condition) to determine whether the Bus is Idle, then it shall not emit the Hot-Join
1524 Request, and hence it cannot participate in Dynamic Address Assignment with ENTDAA.
1525 This use of the I3C Broadcast Address (7'h7E) with RnW bit High (i.e. Read) is specific to
1526 Dynamic Address Assignment Mode. It is also acceptable per the I2C Specification
1527 [NXP01].
1528 5. The Active Controller shall drive only the SCL line. The Active Controller shall release the
1529 SDA line to a High-Z state, allowing SDA to go to High level via the Bus Pull-Up resistor.
1530 6. Every I3C Basic Device that is eligible to participate and responds to the I3C Broadcast
1531 Address sent in Step 4 shall drive the SDA line with its own 48-bit Provisioned ID (using Big
1532 Endian bit order), until it loses Dynamic Address Arbitration.
1533 The 48-bit Provisioned ID shall be transferred continuously, starting with the most significant
1534 bit (bit[47]), with no delimitation or ACK/NACK pulse.
1535 Note:
1536 As mentioned above, a Hot-Joining Device shall only participate in the Dynamic Address
1537 Assignment procedure after emitting the Hot-Join Request (per Section 4.3.5).
1538 7. The Active Controller shall continue to drive the SCL line with the same clock, while still
1539 releasing the SDA line. The I3C Basic Device that did not yet lose the Arbitration shall then
1540 transfer its Bus Characteristics Register (BCR) and Device Characteristic Register(s) (DCR)
1541 per Section 4.3.1.2.2, until it eventually loses Dynamic Address Arbitration. See also
1542 Section 4.3.4.3.
1543 8. The Device whose concatenated Provisioned ID, BCR, and DCR has the lowest value will win
1544 the Arbitration round, due to the nature of Arbitration.
1545 Note:
1546 It is possible for multiple Devices to have the same concatenated value, although this is
1547 highly improbable. See Section 4.3.4.3.
1548 Per Section 6.10.3.4, the Controller can stall SCL after receiving the last bit of the DCR, if
1549 it needs time to determine the correct Dynamic Address for the specific Device that has
1550 won this Arbitration round.
1551 9. The Active Controller shall transfer a 7-bit wide Dynamic Address for the winning Device, in
1552 Open Drain Mode. This Dynamic Address shall incorporate the priority level that the Active
1553 Controller assigns to the Device, per Section 4.3.6.2. The Arbitration-winning Device shall
1554 acknowledge the assigned Dynamic Address. This Dynamic Address transfer procedure shall
1555 have the following steps:
1556 • The Active Controller shall drive the 7-bit Dynamic Address, followed by a parity bit (PAR),
1557 which is calculated as odd parity. Odd parity is the inverse of the XOR of the 7 bits.
1558 Therefore ~XOR( dynamic_address[7:1] ) is placed in position 0.
1559 • If the parity is valid, then the Target shall acknowledge receipt of the Dynamic Address on
1560 the next SCL clock. If the parity is invalid, then the Target shall passively NACK on the next
1561 SCL.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 65
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 80 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
1562 10. The Active Controller shall repeat this procedure, jumping back to step 4 until there is no ACK
1563 from any Device present on the I3C Basic Bus.
1564 11. This Dynamic Address Assignment procedure shall be ended by the Active Controller issuing a
1565 STOP.
1566 Figure 17 shows a typical Dynamic Address Assignment transaction using the flow that is entered with the
1567 ENTDAA CCC, including two iterations of steps 4–9 above. This figure shows how the Controller may send
1568 the ENTDAA CCC after either a START or a Repeated START.
