Figure 17 Dynamic Address Assignment Transaction
1570 Regarding this Dynamic Address Assignment procedure, note that:
1571 • The Active Controller may end the Dynamic Address Assignment procedure at any time, even if
1572 some of the pre-established I3C Basic Devices have not yet received their Dynamic Addresses.
1573 • The Dynamic Address Assignment procedure can be started again any time the I3C Basic Bus is in
1574 Bus Free Condition, using the Command Code Enter Dynamic Address Assignment (ENTDAA)
1575 (see Section 4.3.7.3.4).
1576 • If a given Target does not acknowledge its assigned Dynamic Address, then the procedure requires
1577 the Active Controller to continue from step 4. The Target will then participate in the Address
1578 Arbitration using the same 48-bit Provisioned ID, and as a result the Target will win the Arbitration
1579 round. If the Target does not ACK the Dynamic Address a second time, then the Active Controller
1580 shall exit the Dynamic Address Assignment procedure and execute an error management procedure
1581 provided by the I3C Basic Bus designer.\n--- Page 81 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
1582 • Any Secondary Controllers wishing to receive the Dynamic Addresses assigned to all I3C Basic
1583 Devices present on the I3C Basic Bus can do one, or both, of the following:
1584 • Follow the Dynamic Address Allocation procedure. If a Secondary Controller has been
1585 connected to the Bus since the Bus was initialized by the Primary Controller, then it will have
1586 received all the same data for all other I3C Basic Devices that received a Dynamic Address.
1587 • Monitor the Bus and wait for the Active Controller to send the Command Code Define List of
1588 Targets (DEFTGTS, see Section 4.3.7.3.7), then acquire the Addresses and Characteristics of
1589 all Devices on the I3C Basic Bus from the Broadcasted data.
1590 After the Dynamic Address Assignment process is complete, the Active Controller knows which Devices on
1591 the I3C Basic Bus are Secondary Controllers. The Active Controller then addresses the Secondary Controllers
1592 with the Command Code Define List of Targets (DEFTGTS, see Section 4.3.7.3.7) and transfers the data
1593 for all Devices on the I3C Basic Bus.
4.3.4.3 Provisioned ID Collision Detection and Correction
1594 In the Dynamic Address Assignment procedure described in Section 4.3.4.2, multiple I3C Basic-compliant
1595 Devices will receive the same Dynamic Address if they provide the Active Controller with both identical 48-
1596 bit Provisioned IDs, and identical Device Characteristic Register values. Although the probability of such a
1597 coincidence is quite small the potential does exist, and the I3C Basic Bus will not operate properly under
1598 such conditions (at least the Instance IDs must be different, see Section 4.3.4.1.1). As a result the Primary
1599 Controller must test for this collision condition for any Devices present during Bus initialization, and resolve
1600 it if necessary, before the I3C Basic Devices present on the I3C Basic Bus can all be safely used together.
1601 During Bus initialization the Primary Controller knows the number of Devices resident on the I3C Basic Bus
1602 requiring Dynamic Address Assignment (per procedure step 1.a. in Section 4.3.4.2), which enables detection
1603 of collision errors. At completion of the Dynamic Address Assignment procedure the Primary Controller shall
1604 compare this expected number to the final count of Static Addresses and Dynamic Addresses that were
1605 actually assigned. If fewer Dynamic Addresses were assigned than expected, then multiple Devices must
1606 have received the same Dynamic Address, i.e., a collision has occurred.
1607 If this collision condition is detected, then the Primary Controller shall resolve it using the following method:
1608 • The Primary Controller resets the Dynamic Address Assignment process by using the Broadcast
1609 Command Code Reset Dynamic Address Assignment (RSTDAA) (per Section 4.3.7.3.3), and
1610 then proceeding from step 3 of the procedure in Section 4.3.4.2 from step 8, until the sooner of
1611 either:
1612 A. The expected number of I3C Basic Devices is discovered, or
1613 B. A set maximum number of attempts fail. If this limit is reached, then a system error
1614 message shall be sent to the Host. To avoid freezing the entire system, a limit of three (3)
1615 attempts is recommended.
1616 A Secondary Controller might not be able to test for this collision without also knowing how many Devices
1617 are expected to require Dynamic Address Assignment. Additionally, for Devices that are connected to the
1618 Bus after it is configured, a Controller might not be able to detect a collision if the Devices both join at the
1619 same time and have identical Provisioned IDs.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 67
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 82 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4.3.4.4 Group Address Assignment Procedure
1620 As an optional feature, multiple I3C Basic Target Devices can share a single Group Address, allowing a
1621 Controller Device to send a given I3C Basic Message to all Target Devices in the Group at once rather than
1622 one at a time. In a Target that supports the Group Address capability, a Group Address is stored and exists
1623 alongside the Target’s unique Dynamic Address; i.e., both Addresses are in effect simultaneously. A Target
1624 can optionally be designed to support multiple Groups; such Targets store one Group Address for each Group
1625 in which the Target is included, up to the maximum number of supported Groups; all of the Group Addresses
1626 are in effect simultaneously.
1627 The Active Controller can write to a Group Address just as it would any I3C Basic Dynamic Address.
1628 However, the Active Controller shall not attempt to read data from I3C Basic Devices using a Group Address.
1629 Both rules apply for SDR and HDR Messages.
1630 An I3C Basic Device with active Target role that has been assigned a Group Address shall not issue an In-
1631 Band Interrupt (IBI) using that Group Address.
1632 The Active Controller can configure the Group Address function using the following CCC commands:
1633 • SETGRPA (see Section 4.3.7.3.24): Assigns a Group Address to one or more I3C Basic Targets,
1634 each selected via its Dynamic Address. (As a result, each such I3C Basic Device must be assigned
1635 a Dynamic Address first, before the Group Address is assigned via SETGRPA.)
1636 • GETCAPS (see Section 4.3.7.3.19): Gets the various capabilities of the I3C Basic Targets on the
1637 I3C Basic Bus, in particular whether the Group Address function is supported.
1638 • DEFTGTS (see Section 4.3.7.3.7): Informs any Secondary Controllers about what Addresses,
1639 including any Group Addresses, have been assigned to the I3C Basic Targets on the I3C Basic Bus.
1640 • DEFGRPA (see Section 4.3.7.3.26): Informs any Secondary Controllers that support the Group
1641 Address feature about what I3C Basic Targets on the I3C Basic Bus are included in each Group
1642 Address.
1643 • RSTGRPA (see Section 4.3.7.3.25): Removes one or more I3C Basic Targets from a Group by
1644 resetting their assigned Group Address, disbands a single Group, or removes all Groups and Group
1645 Addresses.
1646 To use the Group Address feature, the Active Controller must support the assignment of Group Addresses.
1647 The Active Controller shall use the following procedure to assign one or more Group Addresses to each
1648 desired I3C Basic Device on the I3C Basic Bus:
1649 1. After the Active Controller assigns Dynamic Addresses to the I3C Basic Devices on the I3C
1650 Basic Bus (via the ENTDAA and/or SETDASA CCC), the Active Controller shall determine
1651 the capabilities of the I3C Basic Devices on the I3C Basic Bus via the GETCAPS CCC.
1652 GETCAPS allows the Active Controller to determine, among other things, which I3C
1653 Basic Targets support the Group Address function.
1654 If an I3C Basic Device joins the I3C Basic Bus via Hot-Join, then the Active Controller
1655 shall determine that Device’s capabilities via the GETCAPS CCC following completion
1656 of the Hot-Join (i.e., only after assignment of a Dynamic Address via the ENTDAA
1657 CCC).
1658 2. If any of the I3C Basic Targets on the I3C Basic Bus support the Group Address feature, then
1659 the Active Controller shall assign the desired Group Address(es) to the desired I3C Basic
1660 Targets via the SETGRPA CCC.
1661 The act of assigning a Group Address to a Target includes that Target in the indicated
1662 Group (i.e., as a member).
1663 If the Active Controller wishes to have multiple Groups, then it would continue to issue
1664 the SETGRPA CCC for as many additional times as needed to complete configuration of
1665 the desired Groups. An I3C Basic Target capable of belonging to multiple Groups can be
68 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 83 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
1666 included in as many Groups as the Target is capable of supporting, if the Active
1667 Controller so desires.
1668 3. After the Active Controller assigns Group Addresses, the Active Controller shall use the
1669 DEFTGTS CCC to inform any Secondary Controllers about the Dynamic Addresses and Group
1670 Address(es) that were assigned.
1671 4. If any Secondary Controllers support Group Addressing, then the Active Controller shall use
1672 the DEFGRPA CCC to inform them about the Group memberships that were assigned.
1673 If the Active Controller detects the disconnection of any I3C Basic Devices from the I3C Basic Bus, then the
1674 Active Controller shall execute step 4 of the above procedure.
1675 The Active Controller could use the RSTGRPA CCC to manage Group membership of Targets, and to modify
1676 the existing Groups, or reset all Groups and Group Addresses.
1677 In addition to resetting the Target’s Dynamic Address, the RSTDAA CCC shall also reset all of the Target’s
1678 Group Addresses if the Target supports the Group Address function.
1679 Note:
1680 Controllers should use the RSTDAA CCC with caution, and re-assign any Group Addresses as
1681 needed after resetting all Dynamic Addresses. Additionally, any reset operation (such as a Target
1682 Reset) that causes a Target to lose or reset its assigned Dynamic Address, will also reset any
1683 assigned Group Addresses.
1684 Group Addresses may also be used for WRITE transactions that use Multi-Lane formatting, if supported by
1685 all Targets assigned to a Group Address (see Section 6.7.3.1.1).
1686 If an I3C Basic Target supports the Group Address feature, and is presented by a composite Device that
1687 presents multiple Virtual Targets on the I3C Basic Bus using shared Peripheral logic (per Section 4.3.2.1.2),
1688 then each Virtual Target shall report its Group Address capabilities using the GETCAPS CCC. Each such
1689 Virtual Target may optionally support multiple Groups, and the Device shall store all assigned Group
1690 Addresses for each Virtual Target. The Controller may assign any or all such Virtual Targets to as many
1691 Groups as each Virtual Target is capable of supporting, if the Active Controller so desires. The Device’s
1692 shared Peripheral logic shall match incoming Private Write transfers addressed to any such Group Addresses,
1693 and deliver them internally to appropriate logic for each Virtual Target function that has been assigned to that
1694 Group Address.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 69
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 84 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4.3.5 Hot-Join Mechanism
1695 The I3C Basic protocol supports a Hot-Join mechanism, to allow Targets to join the I3C Basic Bus after it is
1696 already configured.
1697 Note:
1698 Hot-Join does not allow Targets to join the I3C Basic Bus before the I3C Basic Bus has been
1699 configured.
1700 Hot-Join may be used for:
1701 • I3C Basic Devices mounted on the same board, but de-powered until needed. Such Devices shall
1702 not violate electrical limits for Targets when de-powered (or while transitioning) as defined in
1703 Section 4.3.1, including capacitive load.
1704 • I3C Basic Devices mounted on a module/board that is physically inserted after the I3C Basic Bus
1705 has already been configured. This specification does not attempt to address how that physical
1706 insertion is handled, however such insertion shall not disrupt the SCL and SDA lines, including
1707 respecting all electrical limits defined in Section 4.3.1.
1708 Hot-Joining Devices (i.e., Hot-Joining Targets) may include any valid Device type that supports the I3C Basic
1709 Target role, such as a Secondary Controller. After a Hot-Join, the Active Controller shall use the DEFTGTS
1710 CCC as described in Section 4.3.7.3.7. To ensure that any Secondary Controllers are aware of all of the
1711 available Targets, the Controller shall immediately notify the I3C Basic Bus if it discovers that any previously
1712 joined Targets are no longer present on the Bus (e.g., due either to non-response, or to mechanisms outside
1713 of the Bus).
1714 Flow and Procedure
1715 Since the Controller is not inherently aware when new Targets join the I3C Basic Bus, the Hot-Join
1716 mechanism provides the following procedure for informing the Controller about new Targets. Each Target
1717 shall determine its eligibility to emit the special Hot-Join Request on the I3C Basic Bus, per the method that
1718 it supports.
1719 1. As each Target connects to the I3C Basic Bus, and when the Target determines that it is eligible
1720 to do so (per the Target Hot-Join Requirements below), the Target shall issue an In-Band
1721 Interrupt using the reserved Target Address (i.e., 7'h02) as an IBI, using W (write) after the
1722 START.
1723 This is a Hot-Join Request, and the Target shall issue this request at the appropriate time,
1724 based on whether it is using the standard Hot-Join method (i.e., based on the Bus Idle
1725 Condition) or a passive Hot-Join method (i.e., waiting for an SDR Frame ending with a
1726 STOP, per Section 4.3.5.3).
1727 Note:
1728 Unlike a typical In-Band Interrupt request, a Hot-Join Request does not have a data
1729 payload or a Mandatory Data Byte.
1730 This requests a Dynamic Address Assignment process using the ENTDAA CCC (see
1731 Section 4.3.4.2).
1732 2. The Active Controller shall either ACK the request, to indicate that it has seen the Hot-Join and
1733 will assign a Dynamic Address at some point in the future; or NACK the request, to reject the
1734 Hot-Join and defer processing.
1735 A. If the Active Controller NACKs the request, then the Target attempting to Hot-Join shall
1736 try again, following the I3C Basic Target Interrupt Request procedure (see
1737 Section 4.3.6.2).
1738 B. If the Active Controller ACKs the request, then the Target knows that the Hot-Join was
1739 accepted, and it shall wait for the Active Controller to initiate the Dynamic Address
1740 Assignment procedure with ENTDAA (see Section 4.3.4.2). Once the Target has seen the
1741 ACK of the Hot-Join Request, it shall not send another Hot-Join Request.
70 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 85 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
1742 C. The Active Controller may choose to issue a Broadcast CCC to disable Hot-Join by setting
1743 the DISHJ bit in the Command Code Disable Target Events Command (DISEC, see
1744 Section 4.3.7.3.1). The Active Controller may do so after either a NACK or an ACK of the
1745 Hot-Join Request, although this typically happens after a NACK. If another Target
1746 attempts to Hot-Join before the Controller is ready to assign Dynamic Addresses to the
1747 Targets, then the Controller might need to repeat this procedure.
1748 D. Likewise, the Active Controller may choose to re-enable Hot-Join at a later time (i.e., after
1749 using the DISEC CCC with DISHJ bit set, to disable Hot-Join). To do this, the Active
1750 Controller may issue a Broadcast CCC to enable Hot-Join by setting the ENHJ bit in the
1751 Command Code Enable Target Events Command (ENEC, see Section 4.3.7.3.1).
1752 If any Hot-Joining Targets had previously received the DISEC CCC with DISHJ bit
1753 set, then such Targets may choose to re-send the Hot-Join Request after receiving the
1754 ENEC CCC with ENHJ bit set.
1755 3. The Active Controller should eventually issue a Broadcast Command Code Enter Dynamic
1756 Address Assignment (ENTDAA, see Section 4.3.7.3.4) to start the Dynamic Address
1757 Assignment process. Since only eligible Targets with no Dynamic Address shall participate,
1758 this allows the newly joined Target to receive its Dynamic Address.
1759 A. If the Active Controller has limited capabilities (e.g., is a reduced functionality Secondary
1760 Controller Device, per Section 6.5.3.3.4), then it may choose to not process the Hot-Join
1761 Request, by using the NACK followed by the DISEC CCC (as listed above), and then it
1762 may pass the Controller Role to a more capable Controller (per Section 6.5.3.3.6) at a later
1763 time.
1764 B. A Target that supports Hot-Join (either via the standard method, or the passive method per
1765 Section 4.3.5.3) is eligible to participate in Dynamic Address Assignment, if and only if it
1766 has issued the Hot-Join Request (i.e., using the reserved Target Address, 7'h02).
1767 Note:
1768 Controller ACK in a Hot-Join Request does not imply that the Dynamic Address
1769 Assignment will necessarily start immediately. The Controller may wait for a
1770 potentially prolonged period before issuing the ENTDAA CCC.
1771 Note:
1772 Hot-Join is only compatible with ENTDAA. Targets that do not support ENTDAA shall
1773 not use Hot-Join.
1774 If an Enter Dynamic Address Assignment (ENTDAA) Command Code is issued as
1775 described in Section 4.3.7.3.4, then the Target shall transmit its 48-bit Provisioned ID per
1776 the normal mechanism. Hot-Join Targets required to receive the same assigned Dynamic
1777 Address every time they join can use a fixed-value ID, per Section 4.3.4.1.1.
1778 Figure 184 in Annex E illustrates the Hot-Join flow for both standard and passive Hot-Joining Targets. This
1779 flow diagram is strictly informative and does not provide every possible combination of paths.
1780 If a Target drops off the I3C Basic Bus due to being detached or depowered, then the Controller might not be
1781 aware of this. The Controller may determine this condition by repeated attempts to contact the Target using
1782 a safe Command Code (i.e., a CCC that the Target is required to always respond to), such as Get Device
1783 Status (GETSTATUS, see Section 4.3.7.3.15). If the Controller determines that the Target is no longer part
1784 of the I3C Basic Bus, then it shall either recycle that Target’s Dynamic Address, or else reserve that Target’s
1785 Dynamic Address in case that Target later re-joins the Bus.
1786 Note:
1787 If the Controller’s first attempt to emit the first I3C Basic Address Header (i.e., emitted slowly to allow
1788 for I3C Basic Targets to disable Spike Filters) is converted into a Hot-Join, then the Controller needs
1789 to ACK it, then emit STOP and attempt to emit the I3C Basic Address Header again, per
1790 Section 4.3.2.1.1.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 71
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 86 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
1791 A Hot-Joining Target may power-up at the same time as the Primary Controller (e.g., may be
1792 connected to the I3C Basic Bus when power is applied to the system). In that case, the Hot-Joining
1793 Target might pull SDA Low even before the I3C Basic Bus has been started, assuming that SCL and
1794 SDA are being pulled up. If a Primary Controller needs 1 ms or more in order to start acting on the
1795 I3C Basic Bus, then that Primary Controller shall tolerate the situation of SDA being held Low when
1796 it is ready to initialize the I3C Basic Bus, or it may hold SDA and/or SCL Low until ready, keeping the
1797 Hot-Joining Target from seeing the Bus Idle Condition.
1798 Hot-Joining Targets should implement some method that allows the system designer to prevent the
1799 Device from attempting to Hot-Join when the Device is not being used as a Hot-Joining Target. For
1800 example: either NVMEM or a pin strap could be used when such a Device is soldered onto the board
1801 and powered with the rest of the I3C Basic Bus.
1802 Target Hot-Join Requirements
1803 A Hot-Joining Target (i.e., a Hot-Joining Device attempting to join with active Target role) should be ‘single-
1804 minded’ in nature, focusing exclusively on emitting the Hot-Join Request, until the Active Controller either
1805 ACKs the request (thereby indicating that it will eventually start the process of Dynamic Address
1806 Assignment) or NACKs the request (thereby indicating that the Target should continue providing Hot-Join
1807 Requests until the Active Controller either ACKs such a request, or sends the Broadcast DISEC CCC to
1808 disable Hot-Join, as mentioned above).
1809 1. The first time the Hot-Joining Target connects to the I3C Basic Bus, the Target shall wait for
1810 the appropriate opportunity to send the Hot-Join Request, based on its eligibility:
1811 A. A Hot-Joining Target using the standard method shall become eligible after waiting for a
1812 period of at least Bus Idle Condition (t IDLE , see Table 49) and ensuring that the Bus
1813 remains Idle (i.e., that SDA and SCL are both High, per Section 4.3.3.2.3).
1814 B. A Hot-Joining Target using a passive method shall follow the conditions of eligibility
1815 defined in Section 4.3.5.3.
1816 C. If multiple Hot-Joining Targets become eligible at the same time, then they could all
1817 attempt to emit the Hot-Join Request together (i.e., at the same time). This could apply to
1818 Hot-Joining Targets using any combination of standard or passive Hot-Join methods.
1819 2. The Hot-Joining Target shall, when eligible, send the Hot-Join Request on the I3C Basic Bus,
1820 following a START.
1821 A. The Hot-Join Request is an IBI from the reserved Target Address (i.e., 7'h02), with the
1822 RnW bit Low (i.e., Write).
1823 B. The Target shall send the Hot-Join Request, by either waiting for a START, or requesting a
1824 START by pulling SDA Low (for the standard method only). After the START, the Target
1825 shall drive the Address of 7'h02 / W into the Arbitrable Address Header.
1826 Note:
1827 To issue a START, a Hot-Joining Target using the standard method must pull SDA Low;
1828 the Target shall then wait for the Controller to drive SCL Low. This applies to the standard
1829 Hot-Joining method only; a passive Hot-Joining Target that does not have a timer must
1830 wait for another Device to drive a START Request, per Section 4.3.5.3.
1831 In the case where multiple Hot-Joining Targets successfully emit the Hot-Join Request at
1832 the same time, it would not be necessary for each of these eligible Targets to emit
1833 separate Hot-Join Requests in succession, with delays between each request. If a Hot-
1834 Join Request was successfully emitted, and if the Active Controller responds to that Hot-
1835 Join Request, then each such Target shall participate in a subsequent Dynamic Address
1836 Assignment procedure with ENTDAA that would be initiated by the Active Controller.
1837 C. The Target shall watch for the Active Controller to either ACK or NACK the Hot-Join
1838 Request.
1839 3. The Hot-Joining Target shall conditionally continue sending the Hot-Join Request on each
1840 subsequent START (i.e., at the next appropriate opportunity) until and unless the Active
1841 Controller provides an ACK for the Hot-Join Request.
72 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 87 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
1842 Once such an eligible Target sees the Active Controller ACK the Hot-Join Request (i.e., one that it
1843 initiates or that it is eligible to initiate, per step 1), then it shall stop sending further Hot-Join
1844 Requests.
1845 4. Once the Hot-Joining Target sees the Active Controller ACK or NACK the Hot-Join Request, it
1846 shall follow all normative requirements for the Role of an I3C Basic Target per Section 4.3.2.1
1847 (i.e., one that has not yet received its Dynamic Address).
1848 Note:
1849 A Hot-Joining Target that uses the standard Hot-Join method (i.e., based on the Bus Idle Condition)
1850 shall not follow these normative requirements (i.e., as a Target that has not yet received its Dynamic
1851 Address) until and unless it emits the first Hot-Join Request, and then receives an ACK or NACK
1852 from the Active Controller. This is because the Bus Idle Condition is related to the minimum time
1853 necessary to determine that the Bus is free and not in any HDR Mode (i.e., no transfer is in progress).
1854 A. This shall include receiving and appropriately processing all required Broadcast CCCs.
1855 B. This may also include the Broadcast ENEC CCC with the ENHJ bit set, if the Target
1856 chooses to support re-sending Hot-Join Requests as a response to this Broadcast CCC, and
1857 if it previously received the Broadcast DISEC CCC with the DISHJ bit set. Note that this
1858 shall not affect whether the Target supports (or remains ready to respond to) the Broadcast
1859 ENTDAA CCC.
1860 C. The Target shall also remember that it has seen an ACK/NACK response to at least one
1861 Hot-Join Request, and it shall remain ready to respond to the Broadcast ENTDAA CCC
1862 for subsequent Dynamic Address Assignment. Note that this may follow immediately after
1863 any of the following:
1864 i. An ACK or a NACK, followed by a Repeated START;
1865 ii. A STOP, followed by a delay (i.e., Bus Free Condition, or longer); or
1866 iii. A Repeated START that is followed by other valid Bus traffic, such as Private Reads,
1867 Private Writes, and/or other CCCs.
1868 Note:
1869 Once the Target has emitted the Hot-Join Request, the Target shall remain ready to respond to the
1870 Broadcast ENTDAA CCC, even if it sees a NACK to a Hot-Join Request, or a NACK followed by the
1871 Broadcast DISEC CCC with the DISHJ bit set.
1872 If a Hot-Joining Target has not yet emitted the Hot-Join request, then it shall not be eligible to
1873 participate in Dynamic Address Assignment with the ENTDAA CCC.
1874 A Hot-Join Request shall always follow a START, which means that this uses an Arbitrable Address
1875 Header. Hot-Joining Targets should attempt to arbitrate the reserved Target Address (i.e., 7'h02), per
1876 the conditions of eligibility to emit the Hot-Join Request (i.e., standard vs. passive Hot-Join method).
1877 Since this reserved Target Address has a very low value, it will almost always have higher priority
1878 over other I3C Basic Target Addresses, which means it is very likely to win Arbitration.
4.3.5.1 Failsafe Device Pads
1879 Hot-Joining Devices shall be Failsafe, unless there is a protection method outside of the pad. Failsafe means
1880 that when the SCL and SDA pads are unpowered but wire-connected to the I3C Basic Bus, they must not
1881 draw extra leakage current from the Bus.
1882 The Device SCL and SDA pads shall be designed so as to avoid drawing current from the system SCL and
1883 SDA lines, both when they are being held High and when they are being held Low. Such leakage may be
1884 avoided by using special connectors, or other isolation methods (e.g., physical connection order in a plug).
1885 This excess current should be avoided whether due to diode effect, rail tie for ESD, or clamps. The leakage
1886 current variation between unpowered and powered shall not exceed the normal variation range of an active
1887 pad, I i , as specified in Section 4.3.1, Table 46.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 73
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 88 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4.3.5.2 Legacy I2C Buses and Hot-Join
1888 Hot-Join is not compatible with Legacy I2C. A Target shall not attempt a Hot-Join when on a Legacy I2C Bus.
1889 This may be accomplished, with a Hot-Join capable Target, in one of two ways:
1890 1. The Target may use a pin or NVMEM to allow the system designer to turn the feature off when
1891 it is not appropriate for the system, as noted in Section 4.3.5.
1892 2. The Target may use a passive Hot-Join method per Section 4.3.5.3. This prevents it trying to
1893 Hot-Join until it is sure it is on an I3C Basic Bus.
4.3.5.3 Passive Hot-Join
1894 The passive Hot-Join method works the same basic way as outlined in Section 4.3.5, except with a
1895 modification to the conditions of eligibility for a Hot-Joining Target.
1896 A standard Hot-Joining Target must first wait for at least the Bus Idle Condition, and then it may pull SDA
1897 Low, or it may wait for a START. A passive Hot-Joining Target will first wait for an SDR Frame addressed
1898 to the Broadcast Address (7'h7E/W) ending in STOP, and then wait for the next START, which must be issued
1899 by either the Active Controller or any other Target that can request a START. This ensures that the Target is
1900 on an I3C Basic Bus.
1901 A passive Hot-Joining Target does not necessarily use or enable an internal timer that would otherwise be
1902 used to wait for the Bus Idle Condition (i.e., to emit the Hot-Join Request). If it does, then it must clearly
1903 recognize a START with the Broadcast Address (7'h7E) that another Device might issue (i.e., as opposed to
1904 a Repeated START).
1905 In practice, this means that a passive Hot-Joining Target will first determine that it is indeed on an I3C Basic
1906 Bus in SDR Mode (per the conditions above), and then it will:
1907 Either:
1908 • Wait for at least Bus Free Condition, if the Active Controller issues the next START (i.e., for the
1909 next I3C Basic transaction in SDR Mode);
1910 Or:
1911 • Wait for at least Bus Available Condition, if another Target issues the next START (i.e., for an
1912 In-Band Interrupt Request or a Controller Role Request).
1913 Once a passive Hot-Joining Target recognizes the next START, it shall then issue the Hot-Join Address (i.e.,
1914 the reserved Target Address, 7'h02) into the Arbitrable Address Header to emit its Hot-Join Request, in the
1915 same manner as a standard Hot-Joining Target.
1916 Note:
1917 Because a passive Hot-Join Target waits for a recognizable I3C Basic Address Header, on some
1918 Buses this could lead to a long delay before the Target is in fact Hot-Joined to the Bus.
1919 A Hot-Joining Target that uses such a passive Hot-Join method should still follow all normative
1920 requirements for the Role of an I3C Basic Target in Section 4.3.2.1 (i.e., one that has not yet received
1921 its Dynamic Address), since it would have already seen an SDR Frame ending in STOP. In this case,
1922 such a Target should also process any Broadcast CCCs that it might receive before it emits the Hot-
1923 Join Request (such as ENEC/DISEC), except for the ENTDAA CCC since it would not be eligible to
1924 participate in Dynamic Address Assignment before it emits the Hot-Join Request.
1925 If a Hot-Joining Target supports both the passive and standard Hot-Join methods, then it may use
1926 either method or both methods to emit the Hot-Join Request. For example, such a Target may
1927 opportunistically attempt to use a passive Hot-Join method if it clearly recognizes a START that
1928 another Device has issued; or it may wait for at least the Bus Idle Condition and then issue its own
1929 START, if the Bus remains Idle and no other Devices have issued a START during that period.
74 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 89 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4.3.6 In-Band Interrupt
1930 This Section specifies I3C Basic’s priority-based In-Band Interrupt mechanism.
4.3.6.1 Priority Level
1931 In I3C Basic, Priority Level controls the order in which In-Band Interrupt requests and Controller Role
1932 Requests from I3C Basic Targets are processed. The Priority Level of each Target (or Secondary Controller)
1933 in an I3C Basic Bus instantiation is encoded in its Address, with lower Addresses having higher Priority. That
1934 is, Targets with lower value Addresses and higher Priority Levels have their In-Band Interrupts and Controller
1935 Role Requests processed sooner than Targets with higher value Addresses and lower Priority Levels. This
1936 Priority Level ordering is a natural outcome of the I3C Basic Address Arbitration behavior specified at
1937 Section 4.3.2.2.1, where Address bits with value 0 prevail over bits with value 1.
1938 As a result, during each Dynamic Address Assignment operation (see Section 4.3.4) the Active Controller
1939 should assign lower Addresses to Targets for higher Priority In-Band Interrupt requests.
4.3.6.2 I3C Basic Target Interrupt Request
1940 In order to request an interrupt, an I3C Basic Target shall emit its Dynamic Address into the arbitrated Address
1941 header following a START (but not following a Repeated START). If no START is forthcoming within the
1942 Bus Available Condition, then the I3C Basic Target may issue a START by pulling the SDA line Low, and
1943 then wait for the Active Controller to pull the SCL Low.
1944 If the Active Controller sets the SCL line Low, thus completing the START, and then provides clocks on the
1945 SCL line, then the Target shall drive the SDA line with its own Address, followed by an RnW bit with value
1946 1'b1. If more than one Target has issued an IBI request after the same START, then the Active Controller shall
1947 process those IBIs in Priority Level order, per Section 4.3.6.1. The Target(s) that lost the Arbitration may
1948 issue another IBI request, but shall not do so until after the Bus Available Condition.
1949 At that point, the Active Controller shall perform one of the following three actions:
1950 Either:
1951 1. Accept the IBI by providing the ACK bit. The actions available to the Active Controller depend
1952 upon the value of the Target’s BCR[2] bit (in the Target’s BCR register):
1953 A. If the I3C Basic Target’s BCR[2] bit is set to 1, then per Section 4.3.1.2.1 the Active
1954 Controller shall read the Mandatory Data Byte that follows the accepted IBI request at any
1955 ‘read’ clock speed allowable by the Target. This operation is similar to a ‘read’ from the
1956 Target and all the related rules apply. Note that the Active Controller cannot avoid
1957 receiving the Mandatory Data Byte, since it is transmitted in Push-Pull mode.
1958 After sending the Mandatory Data Byte, the Target may optionally send (i.e., or not send)
1959 additional IBI data bytes, up to the IBI payload size limit per Section 4.3.7.3.6 (the
1960 Set/Get Max Read Length CCC) indicating end of data by setting T bit to 0 per
1961 Section 4.3.2.3.4. If the Target sends any such additional IBI data bytes, then the Active
1962 Controller may either accept or terminate them, depending upon (a) any private contract
1963 between Controller and Target regarding the meaning of these bytes, and (b) the
1964 Controller’s current willingness and/or ability to take them. After the Target sends any
1965 such additional IBI data bytes, the Active Controller may issue either a STOP, or a
1966 Repeated START, per the normal I3C Basic protocol.
1967 If the Active Controller completes this transfer before all additional Data Bytes are read,
1968 then the Target shall not repeat the as-yet unserviced IBI request; the Active Controller
1969 has assessed the request, and will service it at a later time. Depending upon the character
1970 of the data, the Controller can either:
1971 i. Attempt to read either the full data (e.g., for short data packets), or
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 75
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 90 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
1972 ii. Continue from the position at which the transfer was interrupted (e.g., for FIFO
1973 transfers), or
1974 iii. Abort the IBI service (e.g., for Timing Control information).
1975 In all cases the specific follow-up actions shall be established by private contract between
1976 the Devices, bearing in mind the possibility for data to be corrupted in the meantime.
1977 One conceptual time diagram of this sequence is shown in Figure 18.

Figure 18 IBI Sequence with Mandatory Data Byte
1978 B. If the I3C Basic Target’s BCR[2] bit is set to 0, then the Active Controller may take any
1979 other valid I3C Basic action to terminate the frame after providing the ACK bit; i.e., issue
1980 either a STOP or a Repeated START.
1981 Or:
1982 2. Refuse the IBI without disabling interrupts. To do this the Active Controller simply passively
1983 NACKs, thus denying the IBI. The Target will normally try again after the next Bus Available
1984 Condition, or on the next START.
1985 Or:
1986 3. Refuse the IBI and disable interrupts. To do this, the Active Controller NACKs to deny the IBI,
1987 then sends a Repeated START, and finally sets the DISINT bit in the Command Code Disable
1988 Target Events Command (DISEC) to the interrupting Target as described in
1989 Section 4.3.7.3.1. (The Active Controller can set the ENINT bit in the Command Code Enable
1990 Target Events Command (ENEC) at a later time.)
1991 Note:
1992 If the Controller chooses to set the DISINT bit in the Command Code Disable Target
1993 Events Command (DISEC) to a Target, then it should clear any pending ACKed interrupts
1994 before doing so.
1995 In cases where the Active Controller anticipates that the I3C Basic Bus might be needed for a higher Priority
1996 Level transaction before the newly requested transaction would complete, the Active Controller could either
1997 deny the access (by not acknowledging the request), or else drive the higher-priority Device Address instead
1998 of the Address of the Device sending the IBI request. The Active Controller would then hold the SCL line
1999 Low until the expected higher-priority transaction begins.
2000 Bus contention occurs during Address evaluation. As a result, if multiple I3C Basic Devices simultaneously
2001 attempt to win the Bus, then all but one will lose the Arbitration. These Devices will have the opportunity,
2002 but are not required, to repeat the attempt upon the next Bus Available Condition. Note that Target Devices
2003 have the option to choose to not try again.
76 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 91 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4.3.6.2.1 Mandatory Data Byte
2004 The Mandatory Data Byte of the IBI payload is the data that follows the Dynamic Address when a Device
2005 sends an IBI request. The availability of a Mandatory Data Byte, together with the payload information that
2006 follows it, is indicated by bit BCR[2] in the Bus Characteristics Register (see Section 4.3.1.2.1).
2007 It is called a Mandatory Data Byte because the Target Device takes over the line when the Controller ACKs
2008 the IBI request and the Target continues to send data; the Controller cannot decline the reception of the first
2009 byte, and must wait for the T-Bit to terminate any subsequent data.
2010 The Mandatory Data Byte provides the Controller additional information about the event that has happened,
2011 and is divided in two fields as shown in Table 13:
2012 • Interrupt Group Identifier: The three most significant bits: MDB[7:5]
2013 Values for the Interrupt Group Identifier are defined by MIPI Alliance I3C WG for
2014 different use cases.
2015 • Specific Interrupt Identifier: The five least significant bits: MDB[4:0]
2016 Example: D2DT is a MIPI Alliance I3C WG function. It uses the value 3'b001 for the Interrupt Group
2017 Identifier field, and the value 5'b10111 (5'h17) for the Specific Interrupt Identifier field.
2018 MIPI Alliance maintains a web-based registry of defined MDB values [MIPI05].
2019 Table 13 Mandatory Data Byte Field Format
Interrupt Group Identifier Field Specific Interrupt Identifier Field
MDB[7] MDB[6] MDB[5] MDB[4] MDB[3] MDB[2] MDB[1] MDB[0]

4.3.6.2.2 Pending Read Notification
2021 Target Devices that support In-Band Interrupt requests may also implement support for a particular Pending
2022 Read Notification contract, by which they may inform the Controller that there is data to be read for a specific
2023 purpose. Such a Target that expects that the Controller should also support this contract shall indicate its
2024 support by returning a value of 1'b1 in bit 6 of GETCAP3 byte (the third returned data byte, when the
2025 GETCAPS Format 1 CCC is sent (see Section 4.3.7.3.19).
2026 To signal a Pending Read Notification, the Target shall send an IBI with a Mandatory Data Byte value (see
2027 Section 4.3.6.2.1) for which the first three bits (i.e., Interrupt Group Identifier) matches 3'b101. The
2028 remaining five bits in the Mandatory Data Byte value (i.e., the Specific Interrupt Identifier) may be specific
2029 for a particular type of Target, as assigned by MIPI Alliance I3C WG, or it may be any other value in the
2030 region reserved for a Vendor device.
2031 If the Controller accepts the IBI and reads the Mandatory Data Byte from the Target, then it shall signal
2032 acceptance of the Controller’s obligation to read the data, per this contract. The Target shall note that the
2033 MDB has been read, and treat the Pending Read Notification as active: i.e., it shall make the data available
2034 on the next Private Read request received from the Controller. However, if the Controller terminates the IBI
2035 before reading the MDB, or if the Controller NACKs the IBI, then it shall not signal acceptance, and the
2036 Target may try again at a later time.
2037 The Controller should also read any additional data bytes as part of the IBI payload (see Section 4.3.6.2), as
2038 the Target may add additional bytes specific to the type of Target or its function (per the Specific Interrupt
2039 Identifier) to provide context or further describe or classify the data that the Target will return on the next
2040 Private Read request. If present, the format and length of any additional bytes in the IBI payload shall depend
2041 on the Specific Interrupt Identifier.
2042 Once the IBI and its MDB has been serviced and accepted, the Controller is obligated to initiate a subsequent
2043 Private Read request to the Target, to read the data that has been made available and ready for this Private
2044 Read request. The Controller may do this immediately after the IBI has been serviced, or it may do this at a
2045 later time. However, the Controller should initiate this Private Read request before taking any actions that
2046 would either reset the Target Device (thereby causing the data to be lost), or before any other queued
2047 commands or actions initiated by its Host can initiate a Private Read for another purpose (thereby reading
2048 the data and associating it with another transaction). The Controller may queue up several Private Read
2049 Notifications from several Targets and process them in any order.
2050 The Private Read request may be done in SDR Mode, or in any supported HDR Mode using any HDR Read
2051 command value specific to that HDR Mode. The choice of whether to use an HDR Mode may be a private
2052 contract between the Controller and the Target, or it may be indicated in the Specific Interrupt Identifier or
2053 any additional data bytes in the IBI payload.
2054 Note:
2055 If the Target also supports any HDR Modes, then the Target may choose to support certain HDR
2056 Read command values for the active Pending Read Notification, by private contract or by assignment.
2057 The Target may also continue to support HDR Reads for other purposes, using other HDR Read
2058 command values, while it waits for the Controller to initiate the HDR Read for the active Pending
2059 Read Notification.
2060 Once the Controller has initiated the Private Read request, it should read the data from the Target, queue it
2061 for processing or transfer to its Host, and retain the association of the data with the IBI notification, including
2062 the specific MDB value as well as any additional bytes in the IBI payload.
2063 A Target may hold only one active Pending Read Notification at any time, while it is waiting for the Controller
2064 to read the data:
2065 • While the Target is waiting for the Controller to initiate a Private Read request for its active
2066 Pending Read Notification, it shall not send an IBI with a Mandatory Data Byte value to indicate
2067 another Pending Read Notification until after the Controller has initiated the expected Private Read
2068 request to consume the data for the active Pending Read Notification.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 79
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 94 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
2069 • However, the Target may send an IBI with another Mandatory Data Byte value (i.e., one that does
2070 not signal a Pending Read Notification) for other reasons, such as reporting error conditions or
2071 other types of interrupt events.
2072 • If the Controller has serviced and accepted the IBI and its MDB, but has not initiated the expected
2073 Private Read request in a timely manner, then the Target may re-transmit the IBI with the same
2074 MDB (with additional data bytes, as described above) as a reminder.
2075 • The Controller need not initiate multiple Private Read requests (i.e., one for each additional
2076 IBI with MDB for a Pending Read Notification); the most recent IBI shall be the notification to
2077 consume the data for the active Pending Read Notification.
2078 • However, if the Controller does initiate additional Private Read requests due to additional IBIs
2079 as reminders, then the Target shall not accept such requests.
2080 • The Target shall keep the data available for the Controller, to satisfy the expected Private Read
2081 request, unless it encounters an error (e.g., due to delayed read or other conditions).
2082 • If the Target encounters an error or is unable to return the data, then it shall not accept the
2083 Private Read request (i.e., the Target shall NACK the read), and should also report this as an
2084 error (i.e., an IBI with another MDB value).
2085 By default, a Target should not attempt to send an IBI with a Mandatory Data Byte value to indicate a Pending
2086 Read Notification until it has been enabled to do so by the Controller.
2087 Note:
2088 The method for configuring a Target to enable or disable IBIs with Pending Read Notifications is not
2089 defined by the I3C Basic Specification. This method might be a private contract between the
2090 Controller and the Target, or it might be defined for specific assigned MDB values in Table 14 (i.e.,
2091 with Interrupt Group Identifier value of 3'b101).
4.3.6.3 I3C Secondary Controller Requests to be Active Controller
2092 When the I3C Secondary Controller requests to be an Active Controller:
2093 1. To perform the request, the I3C Secondary Controller shall wait for either a START (not a
2094 Repeated START), or a Bus Available Condition and issue its own START.
2095 2. After a START, the I3C Secondary Controller shall issue its own Dynamic Address on the I3C
2096 Bus, followed by the RnW bit of 0 to request to become Active Controller.
2097 3. If the I3C Secondary Controller wins the Arbitration by having the lower Dynamic Address,
2098 and if the Active Controller is currently willing to relinquish the Controller Role to the
2099 requester, then the Active Controller shall respond with ACK.
2100 Figure 185 shows the Controller Role Request flow if the Secondary Controller wins the Arbitration. Any
2101 Controller-capable Device shall use this flow to request the Controller Role, including when a former Active
2102 Controller needs to regain the Controller Role after acting as a Target.
2103 After acknowledging the Controller Role Request, the Active Controller should prepare for Handoff in a
2104 timely manner (see Section 6.5.3.1). The Secondary Controller should also remain ready to accept the
2105 Controller Role (i.e., not transition into a lower-power mode or a “deep sleep” state) and respond to all
2106 relevant CCCs that the Active Controller may use, as part of the process of preparing for Handoff.
2107 Section 6.5.3.2 specifies the process for passing the Controller Role.
80 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 95 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4.3.6.4 I3C Basic Active Controller Initiating a Transaction
2108 In order to ensure that any other I3C Basic Device can initiate a Target Interrupt Request or a Controller Role
2109 Request, the I3C Basic Active Controller may choose to initiate new Frames with a START (not a Repeated
2110 START) followed by the I3C Basic Broadcast Address (7'h7E). This allows another Device to emit its
2111 Address into the arbitrated Address header, when the Bus would otherwise be busy and a Device might not
2112 be able to wait for the minimum Bus Available period to initiate its own START by pulling SDA Low.
2113 If the other Device wins the Arbitration, then it may drive its IBI Request, and the Active Controller may
2114 either ACK or NACK the request. However if no other Device attempts to emit its Address into the arbitrated
2115 Address header, then the Active Controller may follow with a Repeated START, or any other valid Bus
2116 activity.
2117 If the Active Controller instead drives the same Dynamic Address and RnW bit as a Target issuing an IBI or
2118 a Controller Role Request with its own Dynamic Address, then the Arbitration might not have a clear winner,
2119 and as a result a retry might be necessary in order to give the Target a chance to request an IBI or the Controller
2120 Role (see Section 4.3.2.2.3).
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 81
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 96 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4.3.7 Common Command Codes (CCC)
2121 Common Command Codes (CCCs) are I3C Basic’s standardized command set. In SDR Mode, Target support
2122 for a number of CCCs is Required, some CCCs are Conditionally required, and others are entirely Optional
2123 to support (see Table 16). This Section specifies how SDR Mode CCCs are formatted and transmitted on the
2124 I3C Basic Bus, how each CCC functions, and which CCCs are Required, Conditional, and Optional for
2125 Targets to support in SDR Mode.
2126 Note:
2127 In the HDR Modes, Targets are not required to support any of the CCC Commands listed in Table
2128 16. Each manufacturer is free to choose to support any, all, or none of them in each supported HDR
2129 Mode.
2130 As detailed below, there are two main kinds of CCC:
2131 • Broadcast CCCs:
2132 • Are addressed to all I3C Basic Target Devices on the I3C Basic Bus.
2133 • All Broadcast CCCs are Write CCCs.
2134 • Direct CCCs:
2135 • Are typically directed to a single, specifically-addressed Target on the I3C Basic Bus, selected
2136 via its Dynamic Address. For some Direct Write CCCs a Group Address may also be used, per
2137 Section 4.3.7.4.
2138 • If the Direct CCC definition requires a Target response, then only the single, specifically-
2139 addressed Target will reply.
2140 • There are three types of Direct CCCs: Read, Write, and Read/Write.
4.3.7.1 CCC Command Formats
2141 In SDR Mode, the CCC Command always starts with the I3C Basic Broadcast Address, 7'h7E. That is, after
2142 a START or Repeated START, the Address field of a CCC Command shall always contain the value 7'h7E
2143 and the RnW bitfield shall always contain the value 1'b0 (Write).
2144 All I3C Basic Targets shall recognize:
2145 • The 7'h7E Broadcast Address,
2146 • Their own Dynamic Address (once it has been assigned), and
2147 • Optionally supported Group Addresses (once they have been assigned)
2148 Note:
2149 The 7'h7E value of I3C’s Broadcast Address is selected to avoid collision with any Legacy I2C Devices
2150 that may potentially be present on the I3C Basic Bus: Per the I2C specification (Section 3.1.2 of
2151 [NXP01]), no I2C Device will respond to the Address 7'h7E.
2152 Note:
2153 Formats for HDR Command Frames are different from the SDR Mode CCC Command format
2154 described in this Section, and are specified separately for the HDR Modes (see Section 6.1.3).
2155 CCC Commands are always available in SDR Mode and some CCC Commands are available in the HDR
2156 Modes, per Table 16. Generally, CCCs that assign Dynamic Addresses, alter I3C Basic Bus Modes, or transfer
2157 the I3C Basic Bus Controller Role are not allowed within HDR Modes.
2158 The I3C Basic Controller shall issue a CCC Command both before and after I3C Basic Dynamic Addresses
2159 are assigned.
2160 There are four categories of CCC Command:
2161 1. Broadcast Write: A Broadcast Write CCC is seen by all I3C Basic Targets. All Targets shall
2162 inspect every received Broadcast command, even if the Target then ignores the Broadcast
2163 command.
2164 Every Broadcast Write CCC Command ends with a Repeated START or a STOP.
82 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 97 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
2165 Note:
2166 If the CCC Command enters a Mode, then the new Mode ends according to its own rules.
2167 2. Direct Read/Write: A Direct Read/Write CCC may alternatively read or write data from/to one
2168 or more specific I3C Basic Targets, one Target at a time, selected by the Target Dynamic
2169 Address(es). For example, it may Write to, and then Read back from, the same Target to verify
2170 that a change was accepted.
2171 Every Direct Read/Write CCC Command ends with a STOP or a Repeated START followed by the
2172 I3C Broadcast Address (7'h7E).
2173 Note:
2174 This type may also have data both provided with the CCC (code), as well as with each write.
2175 3. Direct Write: A Direct Write CCC is directed to one or more specific I3C Basic Targets,
2176 selected by the Target Dynamic Address(es). This CCC type is only permitted to Write.
2177 Generally, only legacy I3C Basic v1.0 CCCs will be of this type.
2178 Every Direct Write CCC Command ends with either a STOP, or a Repeated START followed by
2179 the I3C Broadcast Address (7'h7E).
2180 4. Direct Read: A Direct Read CCC reads data from one or more specific I3C Basic Targets, one
2181 Target at a time, selected by the Target Dynamic Address(es). This CCC type is only permitted
2182 to Read. Generally, only legacy I3C Basic v1.0 CCCs will be of this type.
2183 Every Direct Read CCC Command ends with either a STOP, or a Repeated START followed by
2184 the I3C Broadcast Address (7'h7E).
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 83
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 98 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
2185 All CCC Commands share the same general Frame format, which is shown in Figure 19 for Broadcast CCCs, and in Figure 20 for Direct CCCs. Each CCC
2186 Command has a unique Command Code. The fields within this Frame format are detailed in Table 15.

2189 Table 15 CCC Frame Field Definitions
Field Definition
S or Sr A CCC always begins with either START or Repeated START.
I3C Basic Address This field has three parts:
Header
7'h7E The CCC Frame starts with the global Broadcast Address, so that all
I3C Basic Targets on the Bus will see the CCC Code that follows.
W The RnW Bit is set to value 1'b0, indicating that the Controller is writing
a Message to the Targets.
This Message always includes the CCC Code, and may optionally
include further Data, depending upon the value of the CCC Code.
ACK Acknowledgement (SDA driven Low) by all I3C Basic Targets.
Command Code / T An 8-bit value indicating which command is being sent, followed by a T-Bit.
All defined Command Code values are specified in Section 4.3.7.3.
Defining Byte / T This field is used with Broadcast CCC Messages, and for some Direct
(Per CCC) Read/Write CCC Messages. It is followed by a T-Bit.
Section 4.3.7.3 specifies, for each defined CCC Code, the use of a Defining
Byte as a selector or Sub-Command, extending CCC capability or an action
associated with the CCC.
When the Defining Byte is used for a Direct CCC, it applies to all subsequent
Direct CCC Messages (i.e., segments) until the Controller sends a new Direct
CCC. For some Direct CCCs, a Defining Byte is optional; for others, it is always
required.
Sub-Command This field is used with some Direct CCCs. It is followed by a T-Bit.
Byte / T In Direct CCC framing, this field always follows the Target Address (or Group
(Per CCC) Address) for a specific Direct Write/SET CCC Message (i.e., segment) and
applies only to that Message.
Section 4.3.7.3 specifies, for each defined CCC Code, whether a Sub-
Command Byte is required or optional.
Note:
Sub-Command Bytes are not typically supported for Broadcast CCCs.
Data / T This field is used with Broadcast CCC Messages, and for some Direct
(Per CCC) Read/Write CCC Messages per Target (or, for some Write CCCs, per Group). It
is followed by a T-Bit.
Section 4.3.7.3 specifies, for each defined CCC Code, how much Data (if any)
appears here.
Note:
For Direct Read/GET CCCs, the Target shall use the T-Bit in the same
manner as a typical SDR Read transfer, per Section 4.3.2.3.4.
Sr / Broadcast A CCC always ends with either a STOP or a Repeated START and Broadcast
Address Address.
or
P
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 85
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 100 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4.3.7.2 Broadcast CCCs vs Direct CCCs
2190 The Command Code space is divided into Broadcast Commands and Direct Commands:
2191 • Broadcast Commands are Command Codes 0x00 to 0x7F
2192 • Direct Commands are Command Codes from 0x80 to 0xFE
2193 • Command Code 0xFF is reserved.
2194 As a result, a Target can easily determine whether a received Command is being Broadcast to all Targets on the
2195 I3C Basic Bus (1'b0), or is a Direct Command (1'b1) intended only for the particular Target, by inspecting Bit 7
2196 (MSb) of the Command Code.
4.3.7.2.1 End of a CCC Command
2197 A CCC Command shall end in one of the following three I3C Basic Bus conditions:
2198 • A STOP after the Command or Data
2199 • For a Broadcast Command, a Repeated START (for any Address value)
2200 • For a Direct Command, a Repeated START followed by 7'h7E which may be
2201 • The start of a new CCC
2202 • Followed by a Repeated START
2203 • Followed by a STOP, as per Figure 20
2204 If the CCC Command enters a Mode, then the Mode ends according to its own rules.
2205 A 7'h7E following a Repeated START to end a Direct CCC may start another CCC, or may be followed by a
2206 Repeated START.
2207 Although not a normal use, the Controller may terminate a Direct CCC Command without addressing any Target.
2208 If the Controller invalidly terminates the data associated with a CCC prematurely, then the Target shall use best
2209 efforts to handle the termination and ascertain the proper course of action. That is, the Target may choose to
2210 disregard the event with no effect, or the Target may attempt to process the incomplete data.
4.3.7.2.2 Framing Model for Direct CCC Commands
2211 In Direct CCC Commands the Frame is formatted to contain the following, see Figure 20 for reference:
2212 • The Command Code, then
2213 • One or more blocks of:
2214 • Repeated STARTs, then
2215 • The Address of the Target,
2216 • Followed by Data
2217 • Finally, either a STOP or a Repeated START and 7'h7E.
2218 A single Direct CCC Command may also optionally address more than one Target Device. A Group Address may
2219 also be used in the place of a Target Address for those Direct Write or Direct SET CCCs that can also be sent to an
2220 assigned Group.
2221 With Direct Read/Write Direct CCC Commands, the Command Code may be followed by data, such as a Defining
2222 Byte. This will be explained in the details for each CCC. Further, each Target access may be a Write with zero or
2223 more data bytes following from the Controller, or it may be a Read with one or more data bytes following from
2224 the Target, if it has ACKed the Read. The CCC Command Definition for each Direct CCC (see Section 4.3.7.3)
2225 will explain whether and how it uses the Write and Read.
2226 Each addressed Target Device may either ACK or NACK the Direct CCC Command. For a Read request, the
2227 Target Device then returns the requested data in accordance with the SDR Read model, using the T-Bit to end the
2228 Read (see Section 4.3.2.3.4).
2229 Note:
2230 Future I3C Basic CCC definitions could be extended so as to include additional data bytes beyond the
2231 ones specified in this version of the I3C Basic Specification. For this reason, all I3C Basic Controller and
86 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 101 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
2232 Target Devices shall ignore any additional, unrecognized data bytes (i.e., more data bytes than this
2233 Specification defines for the given CCC Command).
2234 Target Handling of Unsupported Direct CCCs
2235 If the Target detects an unsupported Direct CCC, then the Target shall generate NACK after its own matched (i.e.,
2236 assigned) Address in the Direct CCC framing, and shall then wait for STOP or Repeated START.
2237 A CCC is considered unsupported in the following cases:
2238 • If the Target detects a Command Code it does not support
2239 • If the Target detects a Defining Byte it does not support, for a Command Code that it does support
2240 • If the Target receives a matching Address (i.e., Dynamic or Group) with Write when it expects (based on
2241 that CCC’s definition) a Direct Read or Direct GET
2242 • If the Target receives a matching Dynamic Address with Read when it expects (based on that CCC’s
2243 definition) a Direct Write or Direct SET
2244 Note:
2245 Handling an unsupported CCC is not necessarily associated with Error Type TE5 (see Section 4.3.8.1.6),
2246 which defines how a Target handles illegally formatted CCCs. The effect of a Target generating NACK for
2247 unsupported CCCs could seem identical to the I3C Basic Controller.
2248 Optional Defining Bytes
2249 Starting with I3C v1.1 and I3C Basic v1.1.1, some Direct GET CCCs that earlier I3C Basic versions defined as
2250 not having Defining Bytes may now optionally use Defining Bytes. This mechanism allows Target behavior to be
2251 extended, or for the Target to indicate alternate status or capabilities, based on the Defining Byte value.
2252 Note:
2253 This Section does not apply to Direct CCCs that show the Defining Byte field as Required in Table 16 as
2254 well as the CCC’s definition in this Specification.
2255 In these cases, an I3C Basic Target that supports Defining Bytes for such a CCC shall generally treat a Defining
2256 Byte value of zero as though the Defining Byte were not present; i.e., the same behavior as if the same CCC had
2257 been transmitted with no Defining Byte.
2258 In order to properly distinguish a Direct CCC sent without a Defining Byte from one sent with a Defining Byte,
2259 each Target shall track the Command Code value as well as the Defining Byte value (if present), and then respond
2260 appropriately when it sees a Repeated START before its own matched Address. In effect, the Target can regard
2261 such a Direct CCC either as an 8-bit Command Code, or as a 16-bit Command Code that includes the optional
2262 Defining Byte.
2263 If the Target supports a Direct CCC without a Defining Byte, but does not support that same Direct CCC when
2264 used with a particular Defining Byte value, then it shall NACK the Direct CCC Command after the Target Address.
2265 The Controller shall then assume that the Target does not support that particular Defining Byte for that Direct
2266 CCC; however, the Controller shall not infer the presence or absence of any particular Target feature or capability
2267 based solely upon the fact that the Target NACKed that particular combination of CCC and Defining Byte. The
2268 Controller should also not assume that the Target does not generally support the Direct CCC.
2269 Note:
2270 The Controller is responsible for determining whether the Target supports any given Defining Bytes for a
2271 given CCC. Depending on the particular CCC, this can usually be determined by checking other status
2272 bits or capabilities advertised by the Target, such as by reading a value returned from a Direct GET CCC
2273 without a Defining Byte.
2274 The Controller must also determine whether the Target supports Defining Bytes at all (i.e., whether the
2275 Target complies with v1.1 or higher of the I3C Basic Specification). If the Controller attempts to send a
2276 Direct CCC with a Defining Byte to a Target that does not comply with I3C Basic v1.1 or higher, then the
2277 results may be undefined.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 87
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 102 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4.3.7.2.3 Retry Model for Direct GET CCC Commands
2278 I3C Basic mandates a single-retry model for Direct GET CCC Commands.
2279 Recall that a Direct GET CCC Command first sends an overall GET command to all Targets using the Broadcast
2280 Address 7'h7E, and then sends individual Dynamic Address(es) for each Target, in order to stimulate per-Target
2281 response(s). This requires the Target to be in a state allowing an immediate response if its Address is transmitted,
2282 but also to be prepared for its Address not to be transmitted (and therefore for the Target not to have to actually
2283 respond). For Direct GET CCC Commands that the Target supports in hardware, such as those dealing with
2284 information locally known within the Target (like GETBCR), this requirement generally presents no issues. But
2285 for Direct GET CCCs supported by the Target’s system, or those supported by software in the Target, it’s possible
2286 that the Target might not be able to respond to the Direct GET CCC in time.
2287 Such situations are handled with the following single-retry model, which applies to Direct GET CCC Commands
2288 only.
2289 If a Target cannot provide a response to a Direct GET CCC Command in time, then:
2290 1. The Target shall NACK its Address.
2291 2. The Controller shall then emit a Repeated START, followed by the Target Address. Note that this is
2292 a second transmission of the Target Address. This gives the Target extra time to prepare its response.
2293 As a further measure to give the Target even more time to prepare its response, the Controller may
2294 optionally also employ a clock delay (per Section 6.10) after the Target’s NACK and before the
2295 Controller’s Repeated START.
2296 3. The Target should respond to the retry attempt in step 2 with an ACK, and then transmit the results
2297 requested by the Direct GET CCC.
2298 This Retry Model is limited to a single retry. Any Target still unable (for any reason) to respond to the retry attempt
2299 from step 2 will NACK it. If a Target NACKs the second attempt in step 2, then the Controller shall not attempt
2300 further retries to that Target.
2301 Note that step 2 only occurs if the Target NACKs the original Direct GET CCC request. As a result, this retry
2302 model never imposes a time penalty, except in cases where the Target actually needs the extra time.
88 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 103 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4.3.7.3 CCC Command Definitions
2303 Table 16 lists all defined I3C Basic Common Command Codes (CCCs). See also Table 75 and Table 76, which
2304 specify the HDR Modes in which each CCC can be used. Below Table 16, a sub-section details each defined CCC.
2305 In Table 16:
2306 • The CCC is Required / Conditional / Optional column indicates:
2307 • R: The CCC is Required. It shall be supported by all I3C Basic Controllers and by all I3C Basic
2308 Targets.
2309 • C: The CCC is Conditionally required. It shall be supported if the I3C Basic Controller or I3C
2310 Basic Target meets the “Required If:” condition shown in the Description column.
2311 • O: The CCC is Optional. It may be supported by an I3C Basic Controller or I3C Basic Target at
2312 the discretion of the manufacturer.
2313 • N: The CCC is Not included in I3C Basic. It shall not be supported by or used by any I3C Basic
2314 Devices.
2315 Note:
2316 Other I3C Controller Devices that comply with the full I3C Specification should not expect to use
2317 CCCs indicated as Not included with an I3C Basic Target Device, and must properly mitigate any
2318 resulting interoperability issues.
2319 When a non-Required CCC is supported by an I3C Basic Device, it shall be implemented as
2320 defined in this Specification.
2321 • The CCC Framing columns indicate, for CCC fields Defining Byte and Sub-Command Byte:
2322 • R: The field is Required for this CCC (i.e., the CCC’s framing always includes the field).
2323 • O: The field is Optional for this CCC (i.e., the CCC’s framing allows the field to be either
2324 provided, or not provided).
2325 • N: The field is Not permitted for this CCC (i.e., the CCC’s framing never includes the field).
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 89

Note:
1) Target Devices may self-power-manage based on this information.
2) Applies to Target Devices that support a variable limit on the maximum number of data bytes per Message, where that limit may exceed 16 bytes.
3) All I3C Basic compliant Targets shall recognize that the Bus is entering an HDR Mode, and detect the HDR Exit Pattern.
4) This CCC shall be supported by Target Devices with Bus Control Register (BCR) Bit[0] set to 1'b1.
5) This CCC shall be supported by Target Devices that support any HDR Mode.
6) See Section 4.3.7.3.5 Set/Get Max Write Length (SETMWL/GETMWL).
7) See Section 4.3.7.3.6 Set/Get Max Read Length (SETMRL/GETMRL).
8) This CCC shall be supported by I3C Basic Devices that comply with I3C Basic version 1.1 and greater.
9) A Target Device that will only ever be used on special-purpose I3C Basic Buses that do not require Enter DAA may choose to not implement support for the ENTDAA CCC.
(Such special-purposes I3C Basic Buses use static addresses via SETAASA/SETDASA instead of ENTDAA). Such a Target may also choose not to implement support for the
SETNEWDA CCC, per Section 4.3.7.3.11; in this case, the Target’s Static Address effectively becomes its fixed Dynamic Address
10) Standards use of Vendor CCC’s controlled by Bus Context. (See Section 4.3.7.3.27 and public registry at https://www.mipi.org/MIPI_I3C_bus_context_byte_values_public.html
[MIPI07].

4.3.7.3.1 Enable/Disable Target Events Command (ENEC/DISEC)
2327 These four Direct (Figure 21) or Broadcast (Figure 22) CCCs allows the Controller to control when Target-
2328 initiated traffic is allowed (i.e., Enabled) vs. is not allowed (i.e., Disabled) on the I3C Basic Bus. This control
2329 governs a Target’s attempts to request an Interrupt (ENINT/DISINT), to request the Controller Role
2330 (ENCR/DISCR), or to signify a Hot-Join event (ENHJ/DISHJ).
2331 Table 17 Enable/Disable Target Events Command Codes (ENEC/DISEC)
Command Codes
Command Support Purpose
Broadcast Direct
ENEC Required 0x00 0x80 Enable Target Events
DISEC Required 0x01 0x81 Disable Target Events

Figure 22 ENEC/DISEC Format 2: Broadcast
2334 Table 18 and Table 19 show the bits to set in the Target Events Byte to enable and disable, respectively, the four
2335 supported I3C Basic Target/Secondary Controller event types. Reserved bits are for future MIPI use.
2336 Table 18 Enable Target Events Command Byte Format
Bit 7 Bit 6 Bit 5 Bit 4 Bit 3 Bit 2 Bit 1 Bit 0
Reserved ENHJ Reserved ENCR ENINT
2337 Table 19 Disable Target Events Command Byte Format
Bit 7 Bit 6 Bit 5 Bit 4 Bit 3 Bit 2 Bit 1 Bit 0
Reserved DISHJ Reserved DISCR DISINT\n--- Page 112 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
2338 The supported I3C Basic Target/Secondary Controller event types are:
2339 • Target Interrupt Requests: Enable (ENINT) / Disable (DISINT)
2340 These bits allow the Controller to control when Target-initiated (i.e., In-Band) Interrupts are (ENINT)
2341 and are not (DISINT) allowed on the I3C Basic Bus.
2342 Note that this capability is enabled by default in Targets that support In-Band Interrupt capability.
2343 • Controller Role Requests: Enable (ENCR) / Disable (DISCR)
2344 These bits allow the Active Controller to control when Controller Role Requests from Secondary
2345 Controllers are (ENCR) and are not (DISCR) allowed on the I3C Basic Bus.
2346 This capability shall be enabled by default in Secondary Controllers that support Controller Role
2347 Request capability.
2348 • Hot-Join Event: Enable (ENHJ) / Disable (DISHJ)
2349 These bits allow the Controller to control when Target-initiated Hot-Join is (ENHJ) and is not (DISHJ)
2350 allowed on the I3C Basic Bus. The Controller can Broadcast this CCC in order to command Devices to
2351 refrain from making Dynamic Address Assignment requests until later authorized by the Controller, in
2352 case the Controller isn’t ready to service the Hot-Joining Device(s). (Hot-Join events are asynchronous.)
2353 This capability shall be enabled by default in Targets that support Hot-Join capability.
98 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 113 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4.3.7.3.2 Enter Activity State 0–3 (ENTAS0–ENTAS3)
2354 These four Direct (Figure 23) and four Broadcast (Figure 24) CCCs allow the Active Controller to inform one or
2355 all Target Devices that it will not be active on the I3C Basic Bus for an approximated amount of time, so that the
2356 Target Devices may use a lower power state during that period (see Section 4.3.3.3). There is one Direct CCC and
2357 one Broadcast CCC per Activity State.
2358 Note:
2359 These CCCs do not place any requirements on Target Devices; they merely signal the Active Controller’s
2360 intent to be inactive, i.e., a lower power state. However, all I3C Basic Target Devices shall maintain I3C
2361 Basic communications capabilities in all Activity States, regardless of the Active Controller’s state.
2362 Table 20 Enter Activity State 0–3 Command Codes (ENTAS0–ENTAS3)
Command Codes
Command Support Purpose
Broadcast Direct
ENTAS0 Conditional 0x02 0x82 Enter Activity State 0
ENTAS1 Optional 0x03 0x83 Enter Activity State 1
ENTAS2 Optional 0x04 0x84 Enter Activity State 2
ENTAS3 Optional 0x05 0x85 Enter Activity State 3

4.3.7.3.3 Reset Dynamic Address Assignment (RSTDAA)
2366 This Broadcast CCC (Figure 25) indicates to all I3C Basic Devices that the Controller requires them to clear/reset
2367 their Controller-assigned Dynamic Address. If the I3C Basic Device supports the Group Address feature, then
2368 receipt of an RSTDAA CCC shall also reset (clear) all of its assigned Group Addresses. After clearing their
2369 Dynamic Address, the Devices shall be ready to participate in an I3C Basic procedure such as Dynamic Address
2370 Assignment (Section 4.3.4), SETDASA (Section 4.3.7.3.10), or SETAASA (Section 4.3.7.3.21), or is configured
2371 to communicate via I2C (though without a Spike Filter).
2372 The Command Code for the RSTDAA Broadcast CCC is 0x06. Support for this CCC is required.

4.3.7.3.4 Enter Dynamic Address Assignment (ENTDAA)
2374 This Broadcast CCC (Figure 26) indicates to all I3C Basic Devices that the Controller requires them to enter the
2375 Dynamic Address Assignment procedure described in Section 4.3.4. Target Devices that already have a Dynamic
2376 Address assigned shall not respond to this command.
2377 The Command Code for the ENTDAA Broadcast CCC is 0x07. Support for this CCC is required, unless this I3C
2378 Basic Device has an I2C Static Address.

4.3.7.3.5 Set/Get Max Write Length (SETMWL/GETMWL)
2380 These Direct and Broadcast CCCs (Direct Set or Get in Figure 27, Broadcast Set in Figure 28) allow the I3C
2381 Basic Controller to Set or Get a maximum data write length in bytes for one or all Target Devices. This Max Write
2382 Length does not affect data write lengths for Broadcast CCCs, since their data payloads are defined for each CCC.
2383 The Set/Get Max Write Length value is transmitted over two bytes, with the most significant byte (MSB)
2384 transmitted first. The Target should define the minimum possible value that can be set as Max Write Length.
2385 Note:
2386 If the Controller sends a MWL value (via SETMWL) that the Target does not support, then the Target
2387 should leave the MWL value unchanged, so that it would return that value on the next use of GETMWL.
2388 This CCC is required if (and only if) any private Write Message(s) and/or any extended Write CCC(s) implemented
2389 by the Target Device support a variable limit on the maximum number of data bytes per Message. This allows the
2390 Target to limit the number of bytes the Controller sends. A Target Device with no such settable limit may optionally
2391 support this CCC, but is not expected to do so.
2392 Table 22 Set/Get Max Write Length Command Codes (SETMWL/GETMWL)
Command Codes
Command Support Purpose
Broadcast Direct
SETMWL See above 0x09 0x89 Set Max Write Length
GETMWL See above – 0x8B Get Max Write Length
