4.3.7.3.6 Set/Get Max Read Length (SETMRL/GETMRL)
2395 These Direct and Broadcast CCCs (Direct Set or Get in Figure 29, Broadcast Set in Figure 30) allow the I3C
2396 Basic Controller to Set or Get a maximum data read length, and optionally a maximum IBI payload size.
2397 The Set/Get Max Read Length value is transmitted over the first two bytes, with most significant byte (MSB)
2398 transmitted first. The Target should define the minimum possible value that can be set as the Max Read Length.
2399 Note:
2400 If the Controller sends a MRL value (via SETMRL) that the Target does not support, then the Target should
2401 leave the MRL value unchanged, so that it would return that value on the next use of GETMRL.
2402 For Devices with BCR bit 2 set to 1'b1, the Max IBI payload size value is added as a third byte, where a value of
2403 0 indicates an unlimited payload size. If Timing Control is used, then the minimum IBI payload size is either four
2404 bytes or five bytes, per the Timing Control method that is supported. If Timing Control (see Section 6.6) is not
2405 used, then the minimum IBI payload size is one (one byte).
2406 This CCC is optional for the Target, with two exceptions:
2407 1. This CCC is required if both (a) any private Read Request Message(s) and/or any extended Read
2408 Request CCC(s) implemented by the Target support a variable limit on the maximum number of data
2409 bytes that the Target may return per Message, and (b) this limit is greater than 16 bytes.
2410 2. This CCC is required if the Target both (a) supports an IBI Payload (as indicated with BCR bit 2),
2411 and (b) will transmit more than one byte of private payload (not counting Timing Control bytes,
2412 when Timing Control used).
2413 Table 23 Set/Get Max Read Length Command Codes (SETMRL/GETMRL)
Command Codes
Command Support Purpose
Broadcast Direct
SETMRL See above 0x0A 0x8A Set Max Read Length
GETMRL See above – 0x8C Get Max Read Length

4.3.7.3.7 Define List of Targets (DEFTGTS)
2416 Note:
2417 In previous versions of the I3C Basic Specification (as well as some versions of the full I3C Specification),
2418 this CCC was named “DEFSLVS”. The usage and data format for this CCC are unchanged.
2419 This Broadcast CCC (Figure 31) is only relevant to Secondary Controllers. This CCC tells Secondary Controller
2420 Devices which Targets (and Groups) are present on the I3C Basic Bus, via four consecutive Data Bytes per Target
2421 (or Group). First the Active Controller identifies itself by transmitting its own data in the first set of four data bytes,
2422 using the value 7'h7E as the Static Address. Then each additional Target or Group on the I3C Basic Bus is
2423 represented by a further set of four data bytes.
2424 If the Active Controller supports the Group Address function, then the Active Controller shall send the Secondary
2425 Controllers any configured Group Address information, using the DEFTGTS CCC in the same manner used to
2426 transmit the Dynamic Address information. Each Group is treated as a virtual I3C Basic Device on the I3C Basic
2427 Bus, described by: Group Address, reserved DCR, BCR, and Static Address as defined below. The Group Address
2428 bytes may be transmitted within the same DEFTGTS CCC message as the Target Devices.
2429 The Command Code for the DEFTGTS Broadcast CCC is 0x08. All Secondary Controllers are required to support
2430 this CCC.
104 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.

2431
Figure 31 DEFTGTS Format
2432 Count is the number of Targets and Groups present on the I3C Basic Bus. Each Target or Group is represented by
2433 a set of four data bytes, as specified in Table 24.
2434 Note:
2435 For each configured Group Address, the DEFGRPA CCC (see Section 4.3.7.3.26) is used to provide more
2436 information about the Group, such as the list of I3C Basic Targets that have been assigned to that Group
2437 Address.\n--- Page 119 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic

4.3.7.3.8 Enter Test Mode (ENTTM)
2442 This Broadcast CCC (Figure 32) informs all I3C Basic Devices that the Controller is entering a specified Test
2443 Mode during manufacturing or Device test. The Enter Test Mode command Frame format includes a byte that
2444 specifies which Test Mode to enter. Supporting I3C Basic Devices shall enter the indicated Test Mode upon receipt
2445 of the Enter Test Mode CCC. Table 25 lists the defined Test Mode byte values.
2446 The Command Code for the ENTTM Broadcast CCC is 0x0B. Support for this CCC is optional.
End of this Broadcast CCC

4.3.7.3.9 Enter HDR Mode 0–7 (ENTHDR0–ENTHDR7)
2449 These eight Broadcast CCCs inform all I3C Basic Devices that the Bus is being switched into the indicated HDR
2450 Mode. See the indicated Section of this Specification for further details on each HDR Mode.
2451 Support for each CCC shall depend on whether the I3C Basic Device supports the associated HDR Mode (see
2452 Section 4.3.10).
2453 Note:
2454 Of the eight possible HDR Modes, only HDR Mode 0 (HDR-DDR) and HDR Mode 3 (HDR-BT) are included
2455 in I3C Basic. To gain access to the capabilities of the other HDR Modes, please contact MIPI Alliance.

4.3.7.3.10 Set Dynamic Address from Static Address (SETDASA)
2456 This CCC (Figure 33 and Figure 34 illustrate the two distinct command formats) allows the Controller to assign
2457 a Dynamic Address to one Target using the Target’s Static Address. This is faster than the ENTDAA Dynamic
2458 Address Assignment procedure (see Section 4.3.7.3.4). The SETDASA CCC should be used before the ENTDAA
2459 CCC is used; all Targets without assigned Dynamic Addresses will respond to the ENTDAA CCC.
2460 Note:
2461 Per Section 5.1.4, only the SETNEWDA and RSTDAA CCCs will cause a Target that already has an
2462 assigned Dynamic Address to change or lose its address. Targets that support the SETDASA CCC shall
2463 not respond to this CCC if a Dynamic Address has already been assigned (i.e., by any of the CCCs that
2464 set or assign a Dynamic Address). If a Dynamic Address has already been assigned, then the Target shall
2465 NACK this CCC and ignore the command.
2466 Note:
2467 The SETDASA Command Code is unusual in that it addresses the desired I3C Basic Target by its I2C
2468 Static Address, rather than by its assigned Dynamic Address. As a result, this CCC can only be sent to a
2469 Target that has an I2C Static Address.
2470 Per Section 4.3.4.2, SETDASA is intended as an optimization. An I3C Basic Target that supports
2471 SETDASA and does not support ENTDAA cannot be relied on to be assigned a Dynamic Address on a
2472 generic I3C Basic Bus, and also will not support Hot-Join (per Section 4.3.5). Use of SETDASA requires
2473 the I3C Basic Controller to have prior knowledge of the I3C Basic Target. Dynamic Address Assignment
2474 with ENTDAA is generally recommended for most use cases.
2475 The newly assigned Address is transmitted in the Dynamic Address Byte shown in Figure 33, where the 7 most
2476 significant bits (Bits[7:1]) contain the 7-bit Dynamic Address, and the least significant bit (Bit[0]) is filled with
2477 the value 1'b0.
2478 The Command Code for the SETDASA Direct CCC is 0x87. Support for this CCC is optional; however, the I3C
2479 Basic Target that supports this CCC must have an I2C Static Address.
Describes First Target
End of this Direct CCC
P
S 7-bit
Direct

4.3.7.3.10.1 SETDASA Minimal Bus Point-to-Point Communication
2481 The SETDASA CCC may also be specially used for simple point-to-point communication in I3C Basic Minimal
2482 Bus use cases (per Section 4.3.1 and Figure 11). An I3C Basic Minimal Bus is an I3C Basic Bus with one I3C
2483 Basic Controller Device (potentially with reduced functionality), and one I3C Basic Target Device. In this special
2484 usage of the SETDASA CCC, the Controller Device both uses the fixed value 7'h01 as the Static Address, and uses
2485 the fixed (and reserved) value of 7'h01 as the Dynamic Address (see Figure 34). This special usage of the
2486 SETDASA CCC allows for simpler Controller Devices, and optionally simpler Target Devices intended for use
2487 specifically in Minimal Bus configurations.
2488 I3C Basic Target Devices should support this special usage of the SETDASA CCC unless such support would
2489 make the Target Device unusable in a Minimal Bus use case. A Target Device supporting this special Minimal Bus
2490 usage of the SETDASA CCC shall match the Static Address 7'h01, and then accept 7'h01 as its new Dynamic
2491 Address (same as the natural result of SETDASA). The Target Device may choose to behave differently after
2492 receiving its Dynamic Address in this manner.
2493 An I3C Basic Controller shall not use this special form of the SETDASA CCC unless the I3C Basic Bus is known
2494 to have precisely one Controller and either:
2495 • One active I3C Basic Target Device that is capable of supporting this special usage of the SETDASA
2496 CCC, or
2497 • One or more I3C Basic Target Devices that act strictly as receivers, i.e., that do not use In-Band
2498 Interrupt and that will not be sent Read requests. This arrangement permits a single Controller Device to,
2499 in effect, Broadcast to the Target Devices.
End of this Direct CCC
P
S
Direct

4.3.7.3.11 Set New Dynamic Address (SETNEWDA)
2501 This Direct CCC (Figure 35) allows the I3C Basic Controller to assign a new Dynamic Address to one I3C Basic
2502 Target or Secondary Controller. In the Dynamic Address field, the 7 most significant bits (Bits[7:1]) contain the 7-
2503 bit Dynamic Address, and the least significant bit (Bit[0]) is filled with the value 1'b0.
2504 If an I3C Basic Target (or Secondary Controller) supports this CCC and accepts the new 7-bit Dynamic Address
2505 from the Controller via this CCC, then this shall change its current Dynamic Address. After accepting the new
2506 Dynamic Address, the Target (or Secondary Controller) shall respond to subsequent transactions that are directly
2507 addressed to this new Dynamic Address (per Section 4.3.2.1) and not to the previous Dynamic Address.
2508 Note:
2509 This CCC may only be used to change the currently assigned Dynamic Address, which must be initially
2510 assigned via any supported method (per Section 4.3.4.2). If an I3C Basic Target (or Secondary Controller)
2511 has not received its Dynamic Address via the ENTDAA CCC (i.e., Dynamic Address Assignment) or via
2512 the SETAASA and/or SETDASA CCCs (i.e., from an I2C Static Address), then it cannot respond to the
2513 SETNEWDA CCC.
2514 In version 1.0 of the I3C Basic Specification, all I3C Basic Targets were required to support this CCC.
2515 Starting with I3C Basic version 1.1.1, this CCC is now required only for Targets that also implement support
2516 for Dynamic Address Assignment via the ENTDAA CCC (see Section 4.3.7.3.4). This CCC is now
2517 optional for Targets that have I2C Static Addresses and do not support the ENTDAA CCC; therefore, such
2518 Targets must only receive Dynamic Addresses via the SETAASA and/or SETDASA CCCs (per
2519 Section 4.3.4.2). For most Targets with I2C Static Addresses and that do not support the ENTDAA CCC,
2520 support for the SETNEWDA CCC is recommended; however, if such a Target does not support the
2521 SETNEWDA CCC, then it shall not respond (i.e., shall NACK its current Dynamic Address).
2522 The Command Code for the SETNEWDA Direct CCC is 0x88. Support for this CCC is required, unless the I3C
2523 Basic Target has an I2C Static Address and does not support the ENTDAA CCC.

4.3.7.3.12 Get Provisioned ID (GETPID)
2525 This Direct CCC (see Figure 17 and Figure 36) is a Get request for one I3C Basic Target Device to return its 48-

2529 Assignment with the ENTDAA CCC, then it shall also support this CCC. If an I3C Basic Target does not support
2530 Dynamic Address Assignment with the ENTDAA CCC, then support for this CCC is optional.

4.3.7.3.15 Get Device Status (GETSTATUS)
2546 This Direct CCC is a Get request for one I3C Basic Target Device to return its current Status.
2547 It has two formats:
2548 • GETSTATUS Format 1 (Figure 39) returns the two-byte format detailed in Table 27.
2549 • The optional GETSTATUS Format 2 (Figure 40) adds a Defining Byte (Table 28) and returns a variable
2550 number of data bytes. The number of data bytes returned and their format depend upon the Defining
2551 Byte value. GETSTATUS Format 2, including its Defining Byte value PRECR, is detailed below.
2552 The Command Code for the GETSTATUS Direct CCC is 0x90. Support for this CCC is required.
Describes First Target

Table 27 GETSTATUS MSB-LSB Format 1
Byte Bits Field Description
MSB 15:8 Vendor Reserved for vendor-specific meaning.
Reserved
LSB 7:6 Activity Contains the two-bit ID of the Target Device’s current Activity Mode (i.e., its
Mode readiness to support data read of sensor or related information).
A Controller-capable Device (i.e., Secondary Controller) must use this field to
indicate when it is unable to participate in any of the steps to prepare for
Handoff of the Controller Role (see Section 6.5.3.1). If such a Device is
currently unable to participate, then it shall return a value of 2'b11. Any other
value shall indicate that the Device may be ready to participate.
For all other Target Devices, the meanings of the four possible values are not
defined by this Specification; instead, they depend upon a private contract
between the Controller and each Target.
5 Protocol 1'b1: The Target detected a protocol error since the last Status read. The Target
Error might or might not be able to check for such errors. Note that this value
self-clears upon every successful completion of a Controller read of the
Target’s Status.
4 Reserved Reserved for future definition by MIPI Alliance I3C WG.
3:0 Pending Contains the interrupt number of any pending interrupt, or 0 if no interrupts are
Interrupt pending. This encoding allows for up to 15 numbered interrupts. If more than
one interrupt is set, then the highest priority interrupt shall be returned.
This field indicates whether interrupts are pending (i.e., are enqueued and
waiting to be sent) and does not depend on whether the Target was previously
told to not send interrupts (i.e., if the Controller had sent the DISEC CCC with
the ‘DISINT’ bit set). This value can still be read even when IBI is disabled for
the Target with DISEC CCC.
114 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 129 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4.3.7.3.15.1 GETSTATUS Format 2
2554 The optional GETSTATUS Format 2 (Figure 40) adds a Defining Byte (Table 28) and returns a variable number
2555 of data bytes. The number of data bytes returned and their format depend upon the Defining Byte value.
2556 Support for the GETSTATUS Format 2 CCC is optional, typically depending upon the Device’s role, capabilities,
2557 or other extended behavior. Before using GETSTATUS Format 2 for a given Target, a Controller shall determine
2558 whether that Target supports it. One way is to send the GETCAPS Format 1 CCC (no Defining Byte) and find that
2559 in Byte 3 (GETCAP3) of the data that the Target returns, bit 4 contains 1'b1 (see Table 63)
2560 GETSTATUS Format 2 conforms to the CCC Direct General Frame Format (Figure 20):
2561 • If the addressed Target Device does not support the given Defining Byte value for GETSTATUS
2562 Format 2: Then the Target shall NACK its Target Address, thus terminating the Direct CCC.
2563 • If the addressed Target Device does support the given Defining Byte value for GETSTATUS
2564 Format 2: Then the Target shall ACK its Target Address and return the requested data byte(s). The
2565 number of data bytes returned shall depend on the particular Defining Byte and its associated
2566 capabilities, as detailed below.
2567 • If the addressed Target Device is unable to immediately respond to the GETSTATUS Format 2
2568 CCC with the given Defining Byte: Then, per the Retry Model for Direct GET CCC Commands
2569 (Section 4.3.7.2.3), the Target shall NACK the first attempt, and then the Controller shall attempt a
2570 single retry. If the Controller receives a NACK after a retry, then the Controller shall assume that the
2571 Target does not support the GETSTATUS Format 2 CCC with the given Defining Byte, and hence
2572 cannot return data describing any capabilities corresponding to that Defining Byte value. In most cases
2573 the Controller can proceed as though it had read a value equivalent to all zeros as a default value.
2574 However, a NACK after a retry does not guarantee that the Target does not possess any capability or
2575 feature set associated with the given Defining Byte value.
2576 A Target that supports the GETSTATUS Format 2 CCC (i.e., GETSTATUS with any optional Defining Bytes) shall
2577 return a value of 1'b1 in bit 4 of Byte 3 (GETCAP3), when the GETCAPS CCC is sent without a Defining Byte.
2578 A Controller that sees that bit set to 1'b1 will know that the Target is capable of supporting at least one Defining
2579 Byte with the GETSTATUS Format 2 CCC. The ACK/NACK method explained above allows the Target to indicate
2580 which Defining Byte values it actually supports.
Describes First Target
End of this Direct CCC
GETSTATUS Format 2

Length Depends Upon Defining Byte

16-Dec-2024
4.3.7.3.15.2 Get Secondary Controller Status (GETSTATUS Format 2, Defining Byte PRECR)
2583 Note:
2584 This Section describes the GETSTATUS Format 2 CCC when the value of the Defining Byte is 0x91
2585 (PRECR)
2586 Support for the GETSTATUS Format 2 CCC with the PRECR Defining Byte is optional. All Controller-capable
2587 Devices should support the GETSTATUS Format 2 CCC with the PRECR Defining Byte while in Target mode, if
2588 they might enter such a deep sleep state, or might require significant time to recover and/or re-synchronize their
2589 internal state based on Broadcast data sent by the Active Controller.
2590 This Direct CCC (Figure 41) allows the Active Controller to query a Controller-capable (i.e., Secondary
2591 Controller) I3C Basic Device, to read the current state of the Device (Table 29) in order to determine whether it
2592 entered a “deep sleep” state during which it might have missed any Broadcast DEFTGTS CCCs or DEFGRPA
2593 CCCs sent by the Active Controller, or is currently processing data from these Broadcast CCCs and is unable to
2594 safely accept the Controller Role until it has finished processing that data and updated its internal state.
2595 It is anticipated that some classes of Controller-capable Devices operating as a Secondary Controller (i.e., in Target
2596 mode) may enter a deep sleep state, and hence would not only miss these Broadcast CCCs while in that state, but
2597 would also require a significant amount of time (from the perspective of the Bus and the Active Controller) to
2598 process these Broadcast CCCs sent by the Active Controller in an attempt to re-synchronize all Secondary
2599 Controller Devices, in preparation for a Controller Role handoff. If such a Device has entered a deep sleep state,
2600 then the Device shall indicate this state until it has received at least one Broadcast DEFTGTS CCC from the Active
2601 Controller. It is also expected that such a Device will remain active for a sufficient amount of time (i.e., after
2602 indicating its current Secondary Controller Status and processing the Broadcast CCC data received from the Active
2603 Controller) to ensure that it does not re-enter a deep sleep state which would invalidate any efforts to bring it back
2604 online and up to date.
2605 When receiving the GETSTATUS Format 2 CCC with the PRECR Defining Byte, the Device returns its Status
2606 using the two-byte format detailed in Table 29.
2607 A Device that is not Controller-capable shall NACK its Target Address, to indicate that it does not support the
2608 GETSTATUS Format 2 CCC with the PRECR Defining Byte.
2609 It is the responsibility of the Active Controller to determine whether a Controller-capable Device supports the
2610 GETSTATUS Format 2 CCC with the PRECR Defining Byte, and whether the deep sleep handling and re-
2611 synchronization is necessary before a Controller Role handoff.
2612 Resynchronization: A Secondary Controller that may require re-synchronization due to a returning from a deep
2613 sleep state shall indicate this capability when the GETCAPS CCC is sent with a Defining Byte value of CRCAPS
2614 (see Section 4.3.7.3.19). A Controller that sees that this capability is indicated knows that it then needs to send the
2615 GETSTATUS Format 2 CCC with Defining Byte value PRECR to read the current Secondary Controller Status
2616 from this Device, in order to determine its need to re-synchronize from a deep sleep state, or check to see whether
2617 it is processing the Broadcast CCC data, before expecting this Device to successfully ACK the GETACCCR CCC
2618 as part of the Controller Role handoff.
2619 Additional Time: A Secondary Controller requiring additional time to process data from these Broadcast CCCs
2620 sent by the Active Controller shall indicate this capability when the GETCAPS CCC is sent with a Defining Byte
2621 value of CRCAPS (see Section 4.3.7.3.19). If the Controller sees that capability is indicated, then it shall send this
2622 CCC with Defining Byte value PRECR to poll the Device, in order to frequently check its Status and determine
2623 when it has finished processing this data. This Secondary Controller shall indicate its current processing Status,
2624 by returning a value of 1'b1 in Bit[1] in the alternate Status two-byte format detailed below, until it has finished
2625 processing the data and can accept the Controller Role.

Figure 41 GETSTATUS Format 2 with Defining Byte PRECR
2627 Table 29 Status Bytes for GETSTATUS Format 2 with Defining Byte PRECR
Byte Bits Field Description
MSB 15:8 Vendor Reserved for vendor-specific meaning.
Reserved
7:2 Reserved Reserved for future definition by MIPI Alliance I3C WG.
1 Handoff This bit indicates whether this Device is currently processing any DEFTGTS
Delay NACK CCC (and DEFGRPA CCC, if supported) Broadcasts that may have been sent
by the Active Controller. It is expected that this Device might take significant
time to process the data in such Broadcasts.
1'b1: The Active Controller may wait to send GETACCCR CCC to this Device,
or should at least be aware that any attempts to send GETACCCR
CCC to this Device will be met with a NACK response, until this bit is
read again later with a value of 1'b0, to indicate that the Device has
finished processing the data in these Broadcasts and has updated its
internal state.
1'b0: This Device is not currently processing Broadcast data, or has finished
LSB processing this data; in either case, it can safely accept the Controller
Role.
0 Deep Sleep This bit indicates whether this Device has entered a “deep sleep” state, in
Detected which it might have missed any DEFTGTS CCC (and DEFGRPA CCC, if
supported) Broadcasts sent by the Active Controller. Consequently, this
Device’s internal state of known Target Devices (and known Group
Addresses, if applicable) should be considered outdated.
1'b1: The Active Controller is obligated to send a fresh Broadcast of
DEFTGTS CCC (and DEFGRPA CCC, if applicable and supported) to
update this Device’s internal state before sending GETACCCR CCC to
this Device.
1'b0: This Device has not entered a “deep sleep” state, or is not capable of
doing so.\n--- Page 133 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4.3.7.3.16 Get Accept Controller Role (GETACCCR)
2628 This Direct GET CCC (Figure 42) is used both to verify a Controller Role Request, if received earlier; and to
2629 allow the Active Controller to offer (i.e., to pass) the Controller Role to an I3C Secondary Controller, even if it
2630 was not previously requested. The GET is used to confirm acceptance; to do so, the Secondary Controller returns
2631 its 7-bit Dynamic Address as shown in Figure 42. The value of the 7-bit Dynamic Address will be the same as the
2632 value of the Target Address. If the Secondary Controller does not accept, then it shall NACK the Get request as
2633 shown in Figure 43.
2634 A Secondary Controller requesting the Controller Role will gain the Controller Role only if all three of the
2635 following steps occur in the indicated order. The Active Controller may offer the Controller Role after preparing
2636 for handoff as detailed in Section 6.5.3.1.
2637 1. The Active Controller transmits a GETACCCR command to the Secondary Controller.
2638 2. The Secondary Controller correctly replies with its 7-bit Dynamic Address. The value of the 7-bit
2639 Dynamic Address will be the same as the value of the Target Address.
2640 If the Secondary Controller instead responds with NACK (Figure 43), or with an incorrect 7-
2641 bit Address (Figure 44), then:
2642 • The Secondary Controller will not acquire the Controller Role
2643 • The GETACCCR command is canceled, and
2644 • The Active Controller can then either (a) Retry the CCC, or (b) Simply issue a Repeated START
2645 followed by 7'h7E to cancel (Figure 44).
2646 3. The Active Controller issues a STOP and then begins the Controller to Controller Handoff Procedure
2647 per Section 6.5.3.2.
2648 If the Active Controller instead issues a Repeated START, then:
2649 • The Secondary Controller will not gain the Controller Role
2650 • The GETACCCR command is canceled (Figure 44), and
2651 • The Active Controller can then either (a) Retry the CCC, or (b) End the transaction by providing
2652 a STOP.
2653 The Command Code for the GETACCCR Direct CCC is 0x91. If a Controller-capable Device (e.g., a Primary
2654 Controller or Secondary Controller) intends to pass the Controller Role to another Controller-capable Device, then
2655 it shall support this CCC. All Secondary Controller Devices (i.e., those that initialize as Secondary Controllers)
2656 should support this CCC.

2660 odd parity: ~XOR( Target Address[7:1] ).

4.3.7.3.17 Set Bridge Targets (SETBRGTGT)
2663 This Direct CCC (Figure 45) is only relevant to Bridge Devices. An I3C Basic Controller shall only send this CCC
2664 to known Bridge Devices that accept it.
2665 Note:
2666 For additional information on Bridge Devices, refer to the MIPI Alliance I3C Application Note: Virtual
2667 Devices and Virtual Targets [MIPI10].
2668 The Command Code for the SETBRGTGT Direct CCC is 0x93. Support for this CCC is optional.
Describes One Bridged Target
k = Bridge index (values 0 through Count–1)
End of this Direct CCC
P
S
Direct Dynamic ID k ID k P
7’h7E SETBRGTGT Sr Target Addr Count Address k MSB LSB
/ W / ACK C / C T C / W / ACK / T / 1 / ' T b0 / T / T Sr / W 7’ h / 7 A E CK Sr T / a R r n g W et / A A d C d K r
Sr
Next CCC
Repeat for all Targets
2669
connected to this Bridge
Figure 45 SETBRGTGT Format
2670 Target Addr is the I3C Basic Dynamic Address of the Bridge Device.
2671 Count is the number of bridged endpoints that will be exposed as Targets.
2672 Each described bridged Target is represented by two fields:
2673 • Dynamic Address: The 7 most significant bits (Bits[7:1]) contain the Target’s assigned 7-bit Dynamic
2674 Address, and the least significant bit (Bit[0]) is filled with the value 1'b0. For Legacy I2C Devices, the
2675 value of the Dynamic Address shall be 7'h00.
2676 • ID: A 16-bit unambiguous identifier for the bridged Target (two bytes)
2677 The meaning of the ID field is not defined by this Specification, and should be a contract between the Bridge and
2678 the Controller (or any other entity that supplies such information to the Controller). For example, the upper nibble
2679 of the MSB could indicate the communication type (e.g., I2C, SPI, UART, LIN, etc.), and the remaining 12 bits
2680 (i.e., lower nibble of MSB plus full LSB) could be the port (as port number and Device selector).
2681 Note:
2682 1. If the Controller needs to change the Dynamic Address of a bridged Target that was configured using
2683 SETBRGTGT, then the change shall be performed using an updated SETBRGTGT, and not SETNEWDA.
2684 2. The Controller shall not rely on each bridged Target having a unique BCR, DCR, or PID; so, use of
2685 GETBCR, GETDCR, and GETPID may return the same result for each.
2686 3. The Controller shall not rely on each bridged Target handling directed CCCs as unique. So, for example,
2687 SETMRL to one bridged Target may affect all Targets accessed via the same bridge.
2688 4. Use of GETMXDS is recommended for bridged Targets, such that each may return its own appropriate
2689 values based on protocol being bridged to.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 121
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 136 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4.3.7.3.18 Get Max Data Speed (GETMXDS)
2690 The Controller uses this Direct CCC (there are three formats: Figure 46, Figure 47, and Figure 48) to determine
2691 the SDR Mode data speed limitations of one Target Device. The Controller is required to use this CCC only when
2692 Bit[0] of the addressed Target Device’s Bus Control Register (BCR) is set to 1'b1 (see Table 7). A Target Device
2693 is required to support this CCC only when Bit[0] of its Bus Control Register (BCR) is set to 1'b1 (see Table 7).
2694 Bit[0] of the Bus Characteristics Register shall be set in any Target Device with a Clock-to-Data turnaround time
2695 greater than 20 ns. Target Devices within the t SCO delay range should not set the limitation bit (i.e., Bit[0]) unless
2696 other limitations exist. But Devices should support this CCC to allow better factoring of each number when
2697 computing the maximum effective frequency for reads, since the Controller/System-designer can be told the t SCO
2698 parameter along with line length (propagation time), line capacitance, number of Targets, and stubs if present.
2699 Bit[6] of the maxRd byte indicates whether the Controller may insert a STOP between the Write (index) and the
2700 Read. If the value of Bit[6] is 1'b1, then the Controller can use the extra time for other communications or end the
2701 communication and re-initiate it at the appropriate time. If the value of Bit[6] is 1'b0, then the Controller shall keep
2702 within one Frame. If the Maximum Read Turnaround time is more than a few micro-seconds, then the Target
2703 should permit STOP between the Write and Read.
2704 Note:
2705 This CCC relates to bit data rates and Read Turnaround times, not data sizes. Data sizes are the subject
2706 of the CCCs Get Max Read Length (GETMRL, see Section 4.3.7.3.6) and Get Max Write Length
2707 (GETMWL, see Section 4.3.7.3.5).
2708 The Target Device shall return either:
2709 • GETMXDS Format 1: Two data bytes containing the Target Device’s Maximum Write Speed and
2710 Maximum Read Speed (see Figure 46), or
2711 • GETMXDS Format 2: Five data bytes containing the Target Device’s Maximum Write Speed, Maximum
2712 Read Speed, and a three-byte Maximum Read Turnaround Time (see Figure 47).
2713 The Target signals which Format it is returning via the number of returned data bytes: Two bytes indicates Format
2714 1, and five bytes indicates Format 2.
2715 The Target’s selection of Format 1 vs. Format 2 should be based upon whether Maximum Read Turnaround Time
2716 needs to be communicated to the Controller Device.
2717 Interpretation of the returned fields maxWr, maxRd, and (for Format 2 only) maxRdTurn is shown in Table 30,
2718 Table 31, and Table 32 respectively.
2719 When reading the GETMXDS value from a Target, the SCL clock rate should be slowed (by frequency or duty
2720 cycle) to accommodate a possible slow Clock-to-Data turnaround (t SCO ). This is only needed if the BCR bit has
2721 indicated a limitation. If BCR is not known (e.g., if SETDASA or SETAASA was used), then either read GETBCR
2722 slowly, or simply read GETMXDS slowly no matter what. See also Section 5.2.5 BCR Use in the MIPI Alliance
2723 I3C Application Note: General Topics [MIPI03].
2724 The Command Code for the GETMXDS Direct CCC is 0x94. Support for this CCC is required if Bit[0] of the I3C
2725 Basic Target’s Bus Control Register (BCR) is set to 1'b1.

4.3.7.3.18.1 GETMXDS Format 3
2731 Additionally, some I3C Basic Devices may support this CCC with an optional Defining Byte (Figure 48), to return
2732 other capabilities based on the value of the Defining Byte. This support may be required as part of the Device’s
2733 role, capabilities, or other extended behavior. It is the Controller’s responsibility to determine whether the Device
2734 supports this CCC with an optional Defining Byte. The Controller may determine whether the Target has such
2735 support, by first sending this CCC without a Defining Byte, and checking for a value of 1'b1 in bit 3 of Byte 1
2736 (maxWr) in the data read from the Target (see Table 31).

Figure 48 GETMXDS Format 3: With Defining Byte
2738 To read the other Device capabilities accessed via a Defining Byte, the Controller shall send this CCC with a
2739 Defining Byte, according to the CCC Direct General Frame Format (Figure 20). If the Device identified by the
2740 Target Address does not support a particular Defining Byte value for this CCC, then it shall NACK its Target
2741 Address to terminate the Direct CCC. If the Device does support a particular Defining Byte, then it shall ACK its
2742 Target Address and return additional bytes of data. The length of the data returned shall depend on the particular
2743 Defining Byte and its associated capabilities (see Table 33 for supported Defining Byte values; see subsections
2744 below for specifics per Defining Byte value).
2745 Table 33 GETMXDS Defining Byte Values
Value Encoding Description
0x00 WRRDTURN Describes standard (Target) Write/Read speed parameters, and
optional Maximum Read Turnaround time. Equivalent to the
GETMXDS CCC without a Defining Byte.
0x01 – 0x90 Reserved Reserved for future definition by MIPI Alliance I3C WG
0x91 CRHDLY Describes delay parameters for a Controller-capable (i.e., Secondary
Controller) Device, and its expected Activity State during the
Controller handoff (see sub-section below).
Recommended if an I3C Basic Device is compliant with version 1.1
or higher of the I3C Basic Specification, and if it advertises itself as
Controller-capable (i.e., if BCR bits 7:6 contain the value 2'b01).
0x92 – 0xBF Reserved Reserved for future definition by MIPI Alliance I3C WG
0xC0 – 0xDF Reserved Reserved for future definition by other MIPI Alliance WGs
0xE0 – 0xFE Vendor Extensions For Vendor use
0xFF Reserved Reserved for future definition by MIPI Alliance I3C WG
2746 According to the Retry Model for Direct GET CCC Commands, if a Device cannot provide an immediate response
2747 to this CCC with a particular Defining Byte, then it shall NACK the first attempt, and then the Controller shall
2748 attempt a single retry (see Section 4.3.7.2.3). If the Controller receives a NACK after a retry, then the Controller
2749 shall assume that the Device does not support this CCC with this particular Defining Byte, and hence has no data\n--- Page 141 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
2750 that would be returned for any capabilities relating to that Defining Byte value. In most cases, the Controller may
2751 proceed as though it read a value equivalent to all zeros as a default value. However, a NACK after a retry should
2752 not be interpreted as meaning that the Device does not possess a capability or feature set associated with that
2753 Defining Byte value.
2754 A Target that supports this CCC with any optional Defining Bytes shall return a value of 1'b1 in bit 3 of Byte 1
2755 (maxWr) when this CCC is sent without a Defining Byte. If the Controller sees that bit set to 1'b1, then it knows
2756 that the Target is capable of supporting at least one Defining Byte with this CCC. The ACK/NACK method
2757 explained above allows the Target to indicate which Defining Byte values it actually supports.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 127
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 142 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4.3.7.3.18.2 Get Controller Handoff Delay Parameters (GETMXDS Format 3, Defining Byte
CRHDLY)
2758 This Direct CCC with Defining Byte value CRHDLY (Figure 49) allows the Active Controller to query a
2759 Controller-capable (i.e., Secondary Controller) I3C Basic Device, to read what optional Controller handoff delays
2760 or other timing parameters that Device will utilize. It is anticipated that a Device may advertise these parameters,
2761 to indicate whether it will enter an Activity State that requires the Active Controller to wait for an appropriate
2762 delay, before confirming its Controller Role or attempting the CE3 Error Recovery flow.
2763 It is recommended that all Controller-capable Devices should support this CCC with this Defining Byte in Target
2764 mode. If such a Device does not follow the default delay settings, or if it indicates other non-default timing
2765 parameters, then the Device shall support this Defining Byte.
2766 A Controller-capable Device may return one data byte, labeled CRHDLY1 per Figure 49.
2767 A Device that is not Controller-capable shall NACK its Target Address, to indicate that it does not support this
2768 CCC with this particular Defining Byte.
2769 Fields within the CRHDLY1 Byte are detailed in Table 34.

2771 Table 34 CRHDLY1 Byte Format
Bits Field Description
7:3 Reserved Reserved for future definition by MIPI Alliance I3C WG
2 Set Bus This bit indicates whether the Active Controller should set the Bus to the Activity State
Activity State indicated in bits 1:0 before handing the Controller Role off to this I3C Basic Device as
part of the normal flow.
Values:
0: The Active Controller should not set the Bus to any Activity State before handoff to
this Device.
1: The Active Controller should set the Bus to the Activity State indicated by bits 1:0
before handoff to this Device.
For the Activity States and their delay intervals, see Table 12.
1:0 Controller This 2-bit integer indicates whether this I3C Device initially acts with a given Activity
Handoff State, after becoming Active Controller on the Bus according to the Controller handoff
Activity State flow. The indicated Activity State implies that the Device may have a delayed
response to Bus activity, and so the former Controller should wait the specified delay
time for the indicated Activity State before testing this Device, to confirm that it is
controlling the Bus before initiating the CE3 error recovery flow.
Values:
0: Acts according to Activity State 0.
1: Acts according to Activity State 1.
2: Acts according to Activity State 2.
3: Acts according to Activity State 3.
For the Activity States and their delay intervals, see Table 12.
For the Error Type CE3 recovery flow, see Section 4.3.8.2.4.

16-Dec-2024
4.3.7.3.19 Get Optional Feature Capabilities (GETCAPS)
2772 This Direct CCC (Figure 50) allows the Controller to query an I3C Basic Target to determine what optional I3C
2773 Basic features that Target supports. It replaces the GETHDRCAP CCC, which was defined in version 1.0 of the
2774 I3C Basic Specification.
2775 This CCC has two formats:
2776 • For GETCAPS Format 1, the I3C Basic Target may return the first 2, 3, or all 4 of the GETCAP1 through
2777 GETCAP4 Bytes as detailed in Section 5.4.
2778 • The optional GETCAPS Format 2 adds a Defining Byte and returns a variable number of data bytes.
2779 GETCAPS Format 2 is detailed further in Section 5.4.

Figure 50 GETCAPS CCC Format Overview
2781 Note:
2782 It is possible for an I3C Basic Controller that supports I3C Basic version 1.1.1 or greater to be used with
2783 an I3C Basic Target Device that supports version 1.0 of the I3C Basic Specification. In such a case, the
2784 Target might only support the previous GETHDRCAP CCC format, and as a result might respond to the
2785 GETCAPS CCC by returning only one byte (i.e., the GETCAP1 Byte). Therefore, an I3C Basic v1.1.1 or
2786 greater Controller must recognize this as a valid configuration ( even though the I3C Basic Target returns
2787 just a single byte and as a result has limited capabilities).
2788 However, note that all I3C Basic Targets that support I3C Basic version 1.1.1 or greater are required to
2789 respond to the GETCAPS CCC by returning at least the first 2 bytes.
2790 For the optional GETCAPS Format 2, the variable number of data bytes returned depends upon the Defining Byte
2791 value. A Controller shall not use the GETCAPS Format 2 CCC with any given Target unless it first determines that
2792 the Target supports Format 2 of the CCC. A Target that supports the GETCAPS Format 2 CCC shall return a value
2793 of 1'b1 in bit 4 of Byte 3 (GETCAP3), when the GETCAPS CCC is sent without a Defining Byte. If the Controller
2794 sees that bit set to 1'b1, then the Controller will know that the Target is capable of supporting at least one Defining
2795 Byte with the GETCAPS Format 2 CCC.\n--- Page 145 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic

4.3.7.3.20 Set Route (SETROUTE)
2796 This Direct CCC (Figure 51) is used by an I3C Basic Controller to configure a Routing Device (Routing Devices
2797 are described below). Because this CCC is only relevant to Routing Devices, a Controller shall only send the
2798 SETROUTE CCC to a known Routing Device that accepts it.
2799 Note:
2800 For additional information on Routing Devices, refer to the MIPI Alliance I3C Application Note: Virtual
2801 Devices and Virtual Targets [MIPI10].
2802 The Command Code for the SETROUTE Direct CCC is 0x96. Support for this CCC is optional.
Describes One Route to Other Network
k = Route index (values from 0 to Count–1)
End of this Direct CCC
P
S
Direct Dynamic Start Trans P
/ W 7’ h / 7 A E CK SET C R / C O T C UTE Sr Ta / r W ge / t A A C d K dr Co / u T nt Add / 1 / r e ' T b s 0 s k ID / T k Cou / T nt k Sr / W 7’ h / 7 A E CK Sr T / a R r n g W et / A A d C d K r

2803
Figure 51 SETROUTE Format
2804 Target Addr is the Address of the Routing Device: either the Target’s original 7-bit static I2C Address, or else the
2805 current value of the Target’s assigned 7-bit Dynamic Address. This Target Address is used both for the Routing
2806 Device itself, and the other operations of the Device into which the routing behavior and logic is integrated.
2807 Count is the number of configured Routes to be defined/assigned with this CCC (not the number of Routes the
2808 Routing Device provides).
2809 Each Route is represented by three fields:
2810 • Dynamic Address: The 7 most significant bits (Bits[7:1]) contain the current value of the Target’s
2811 assigned 7-bit Dynamic Address, and the least significant bit (Bit[0]) is filled with the value 1'b0. This
2812 Address (not the Target Addr) is used when sending transactions to the associated I3C Basic Bus.
2813 • ID: An 8-bit identifier, implementation-specific to the Routing Device, identifying the specific Route to
2814 the I3C Basic Bus endpoint provided by the Routing Device.
2815 • Start Trans Count: An 8-bit value to which the Routing Device shall reset the transaction counter when
2816 initializing the Route.
2817 The method by which the Controller is informed about the identifiers for the various Routes provided by the
2818 Routing Device is not defined by this Specification; the Controller shall have prior knowledge of the identifiers,
2819 through either discovery registers or a priori knowledge. Although the identifiers used by the Routing Device are
2820 arbitrary, they shall be unique per I3C Basic Bus endpoint.
2821 Only one Controller using the SETROUTE CCC shall configure a set of Routes at any given time. Any Controller
2822 accessing the Routing Device on any of the I3C Basic Buses shall configure the Route(s). Only one Controller and
2823 its associated configured Routes shall be active at any given time. Each time a different Controller desires to
2824 configure its Route(s) through the Routing Device, it shall negotiate for ownership of the I3C Basic Bus first.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 131
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 146 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4.3.7.3.20.1 Routing Devices
2825 A Routing Device enables an I3C Basic Controller on a given I3C Basic Bus to communicate to one or more other
2826 I3C Basic Buses (see Figure 52). That is, a Routing Device enables a connection (also known as a Route), or
2827 multiple distinct connections, to exist between the connected I3C Basic Buses. These I3C Basic Buses are
2828 independent, and may have different sets of Dynamic Addresses.
2829 A Routing Device is distinguished from a Bridge Device, in that a Routing Device is not transparent; the
2830 transactions are relayed between the I3C Basic Buses. Since the Routing Device inevitably incurs delays on the
2831 transactions, it shall handle transactions through buffers/queues in order to meet the transmit/receive requirements
2832 of the I3C Basic protocol on each connected I3C Basic Bus. Transactions from the Controller on its I3C Basic Bus
2833 are queued by the Routing Device, and then sent to the other I3C Basic Buses configured by the Controller. Each
2834 Route should have its own separate queue, in order to handle differences in timing and clocking.

Figure 52 Example Routing Device
2836 Routes through the Routing Device will work either in a single direction, or in multiple directions, depending on
2837 the capabilities of each I3C Basic Bus endpoint.
2838 Examples:
2839 • An I3C Basic Bus endpoint that is both a Target and a Secondary Controller supports Routes in both
2840 directions through that endpoint.
2841 • An I3C Basic Bus endpoint that is only a Target, and not a Secondary Controller, only supports single-
2842 direction Routes (i.e., inward to the Routing Device).
2843 Responses are queued and tagged with a transaction count, so that the original sender of the transaction can
2844 associate the responses with the original transaction. Each Route shall have its own counter. The initial value of
2845 the counter should be configurable, to allow the Controller to reset the counter to a previous value after a change
2846 in ownership.\n--- Page 147 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4.3.7.3.21 Set All Addresses to Static Address (SETAASA)
2847 This CCC (Figure 53) allows the Controller to request that all connected Targets that have I2C Static Addresses
2848 use their I2C Static Address as their Dynamic Address. This is the fastest method to assign I3C Basic Dynamic
2849 Addresses to all I3C Basic Targets with I2C Static Addresses (see Section 4.3.4.2).
2850 Note:
2851 Per Section 5.1.4, only the SETNEWDA and RSTDAA CCCs will cause a Target that already has an
2852 assigned Dynamic Address to change or lose its address. Targets that support the SETAASA CCC shall
2853 not respond to this CCC if a Dynamic Address has already been assigned (i.e., by any of the CCCs that
2854 set or assign a Dynamic Address). If a Dynamic Address has already been assigned, then the Target shall
2855 ignore this command.
2856 Note:
2857 Per Section 4.3.4.2, SETAASA is intended as an optimization. An I3C Basic Target that supports
2858 SETAASA and does not support ENTDAA cannot be relied on to be assigned a Dynamic Address on a
2859 generic I3C Basic Bus, and also will not support Hot-Join (per Section 4.3.5). Use of SETAASA requires
2860 the I3C Basic Controller to have prior knowledge of the I3C Basic Target, as well as all other I3C Basic
2861 Targets that support SETAASA. Dynamic Address Assignment with ENTDAA is recommended for most
2862 use cases.
2863 The Command Code for the SETAASA Broadcast CCC is 0x29. Support for this CCC is optional; however, any
2864 I3C Basic Target that supports this CCC shall have an I2C Static Address.

4.3.7.3.22 Data Transfer Ending Procedure Control (ENDXFER)
2866 The ENDXFER CCC provides the framework for Controllers and Targets to exchange set-up parameters for ending
2867 data transfers in HDR Modes, and for Monitoring Devices to request early termination of running transactions.
2868 The ENDXFER CCC is available as both a Broadcast Command (Figure 54) and a Direct Command (Figure 55).
2869 The ENDXFER CCC always includes the one-byte Defining Byte field, which acts as a sub-command identifier
2870 defining the specific function of the CCC. For some Sub-Commands (i.e., for some values of the Defining Byte
2871 field), additional Data Bytes follow. Interpretation of these additional Data Bytes depends upon the particular
2872 Defining Byte value, as detailed in Table 36.

4.3.7.3.23 Target Reset Action (RSTACT)
2877 This Broadcast (Figure 56), Direct Write (Figure 57), and Direct Read (Figure 58) CCC is used to configure the
2878 next Target Reset action, and may be used to retrieve a Target’s reset recovery timing. The RSTACT CCC is used
2879 in conjunction with the Target Reset Pattern (Section 4.3.9.3), i.e., the reset action previously configured in a
2880 Target via the RSTACT CCC is triggered when the immediately following Target Reset Pattern is received.
2881 The RSTACT CCC uses a Defining Byte (Table 38).
2882 • For the Broadcast and Direct Write formats, the Defining Byte indicates which Target Reset action
2883 (including to take no action) is to be configured (values 0x00 through 0x7F).
2884 • Defining Bytes 0x00 and 0x01 are required: A Target shall support these operations, and shall ACK
2885 its Target Address if issued as a Direct CCC.
2886 • Support for other Defining Bytes (values 0x02 through 0x7F) is optional, and depends on other
2887 conditions or support for other capabilities. If a Target does not support such an operation, then it
2888 shall NACK its Target Address for such a Defining Byte, as well as any related Defining Bytes
2889 defined for the Direct Read format (values 0x82 through 0xFF) if issued as a Direct CCC.
2890 • For the Direct Read format, the Defining Byte may also indicate the Controller’s desire to read back the
2891 Target’s reset recovering timing or other parameters for the operation (values 0x81 through 0xFF).
2892 • If the CCC is NACKed and the related reset operation is supported, then the Controller should
2893 assume the default reset return times of 1 ms to reset the Peripheral (i.e., the reset operation for
2894 Defining Byte 0x01) and 1 second to reset the whole Target Device (i.e., the reset operation for
2895 Defining Byte 0x02).

16-Dec-2024
2901 Note:
2902 1. The Target shall clear its reset action (as configured via the RSTACT CCC) upon receipt of the next
2903 START (but not Repeated START).
2904 2. Reset operation Reset the Whole Target (Defining Byte 0x02) is optional, but support for this reset
2905 operation is recommended; see also Section 4.3.9.4. A Target Device that does not support this reset
2906 operation shall NACK its Target Address for this Defining Byte (if issued as a Direct CCC) or ignore the
2907 RSTACT CCC (if issued as a Broadcast CCC).
4.3.7.3.23.1 Debug Network Adaptor Reset
2908 Reset operation Debug Network Adaptor Reset (Defining Byte 0x03) is intended for Target Devices that support
2909 the MIPI Debug Over I3C specification [MIPI04]. Support for this reset operation is strongly recommended for
2910 any Debug-capable I3C Basic Device that supports MIPI Debug Over I3C as a Debug-capable Target System (TS).
2911 The Debug Network Adaptor Reset operation (Defining Byte 0x03) does not reset the I3C Basic Peripheral or its
2912 Dynamic Address; it only resets the Device’s Debug Over I3C Network Adaptors and all attached Debug Function
2913 logic. If issued as a Broadcast CCC, then it shall only affect Targets that support a Debug Network Adaptor reset
2914 operation.
2915 A Target Device that does not support the Debug Network Adaptor Reset operation:
2916 • Shall NACK its Dynamic Address for this Defining Byte if issued as a Direct CCC
2917 • Shall ignore the RSTACT CCC if issued as a Broadcast CCC.
4.3.7.3.23.2 Virtual Target Detect
2918 Reset operation Virtual Target Detect (Defining Byte 0x04) is intended for composite Devices that support multiple
2919 Virtual Targets that share the same Peripheral logic (see Section 4.3.2.1.2). Support for this Defining Byte is
2920 required for any such Device that reports Virtual Target capabilities; i.e.,is required if BCR bit[4] is set to 1'b1 and
2921 if the Device supports the GETCAPS CCC with Defining Byte VTCAPS to indicate multiple Virtual Targets using
2922 shared Peripheral logic (see Section 5.4.5).
2923 Additional requirements apply:
2924 • This operation does not configure a Target or its shared Peripheral logic for any defined Target Reset
2925 action. I.e., it is only used for identification and association of any linked Virtual Targets.
2926 • This Defining Byte is only supported with Direct CCCs, not Broadcast CCCs.
2927 • The flag that resides in the shared Peripheral logic and which is set by Defining Byte 0x04 shall not be
2928 accessible or writeable from any application-specific logic connected to any Virtual Target. The state of
2929 this flag shall not be accessed by any Controller by any method other than using this CCC (RSTACT) with
2930 this Defining Byte(0x04), and this flag shall not be cleared by any Controller by any method other than
2931 using this CCC (RSTACT) with Defining Byte 0x00.
2932 Additionally, if a shared Peripheral is implemented in a manner that passes incoming RSTACT CCC events as
2933 messages up to application-specific queues, or to buffers for any Virtual Targets, then the shared Peripheral shall
2934 not pass any RSTACT CCC events as messages if they contain Defining Byte 0x04 or 0x84 or otherwise change
2935 the flag. This is because these messages might notify a Virtual Target that the flag is being set or accessed by a
2936 Controller.
2937 Note:
2938 For additional information on Virtual Targets, refer to the MIPI Alliance I3C Application Note: Virtual Devices
2939 and Virtual Targets [MIPI10].
2940 A Target Device that does not support the Virtual Target Detect reset operation shall NACK its Dynamic Address
2941 for this Defining Byte (if issued as a Direct CCC).
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 139
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 154 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4.3.7.3.23.3 Return Reset Times
2942 If the Target supports the Direct Read format of the RSTACT CCC, and provides ACK to the RSTACT CCC with
2943 any Defining Byte value in the range from 0x81 through 0x83 (inclusive) to return the reset time, then the data
2944 byte value returned by the Target shall have the data format indicated in Table 39.
2945 Table 39 RSTACT Format 3: Direct Read CCC Byte Read Data Format
Bits Description Value
7 Indicates the time unit for the value in Bits[6:0] 1'b0: time unit of 10 ms
1'b1: time unit of 1 second
6:0 Amount of time in units 7'h00: 1 ms always (regardless of unit)
7'h01 – 7'h7F: 1 thru 127 units
Exceptions: 8'hFF: Return time is either beyond the maximum value, or is
indeterminate
2946 Examples:
2947 • Values 8'h00 and 8'h80 indicate 1 ms return time.
2948 • Value 8'h01 indicates 10 ms return time.
2949 • Value 8'h02 indicates 20 ms return time.
2950 • Value 8'h7F indicates 1270 ms (i.e., 1.27 seconds) return time.
2951 • Value 8'h81 indicates 1 second return time.
2952 • Value 8'h82 indicates 2 seconds return time.
2953 • Value 8'hFE indicates 126 seconds return time.
2954 • Value 8'hFF indicates that the return time is either beyond the maximum value (i.e., reported by private
2955 contract), or is indeterminate.
2956 In this case, the Controller should either:
2957 • Wait for an appropriate event based on the reset type, which could be either a Hot-Join Request or some
2958 other notification (e.g., an In-Band Interrupt Request defined by the I3C Basic content protocol); or
2959 • Use some other polling mechanism (i.e., defined by private contract) to determine when the reset is
2960 complete.
2961 Note:
2962 In earlier versions of the I3C Basic Specification, the unit values were not clearly defined. Targets that
2963 comply with I3C Basic Specification v1.1.1 or earlier should return single-byte values assuming legacy
2964 units of 1 ms for Defining Byte 0x81; 1 second for Defining Byte 0x82; and 1 ms for Defining Byte 0x83.
2965 If the Target resets quickly, i.e., if its return time is less than 1 ms, then the Target should either NACK its
2966 Dynamic Address (i.e., to indicate the default return time) or ACK its Dynamic Address and return a value
2967 of 1 ms (i.e., 8'h00 or 8'h80).
2968 Before using the RSTACT Format 3 Direct Read CCC to read the return time, the Controller should also
2969 use the GETCAPS CCC to confirm that the Target complies with v1.2 or newer of the I3C Basic
2970 Specification. If so, then the Controller will know whether the Target uses the data format defined in Table
2971 39. However, if this is not the case, then the Controller should use the legacy units to interpret the data
2972 byte value.
2973 If implementers choose to support any of the available RSTACT Defining Byte values (i.e., in the range of 0x40
2974 thru 0x7F) to reset any other logic, functions or domains within the chip, then implementers should also support
2975 the corresponding RSTACT Defining Byte values (i.e., in the range of 0xC0 thru 0xFF) to indicate the expected
2976 return times for such reset operations, using the same data format defined in Table 39.
140 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 155 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4.3.7.3.24 Set Group Address (SETGRPA)
2977 This Direct CCC (Figure 59) allows the Active Controller to assign a Group Address to an I3C Basic Target
2978 supporting the Group Address feature. The Target will then respond to both the configured Group Address and its
2979 Dynamic Address. If multiple I3C Basic Targets are configured with the same Group Address, then a single
2980 Message sent by the Active Controller to that Group Address will be received by all of those I3C Basic Targets
2981 simultaneously.
2982 Note:
2983 In this CCC, an I3C Basic Target to be assigned a Group Address is indicated by its Dynamic Address. As
2984 a result, this CCC cannot be used until the Target has been assigned a Dynamic Address.
2985 If the Target supports multiple Group Addresses, then the same Dynamic Address can be used in multiple
2986 SETGRPA CCCs (up to the maximum number of Group Addresses supported by the I3C Basic Device), supplying
2987 a different Group Address in each instance. The Target will then respond to any of the configured Group Addresses,
2988 in addition to its Dynamic Address.
2989 When a Target is assigned to the maximum number of groups that it is capable of supporting, the Target shall
2990 inform the Controller that this maximum capability has been reached by NACKing the Target Address for this
2991 Direct CCC.
2992 The Group Address to be assigned is transmitted in the Group Address byte shown in Figure 59, where the 7 most
2993 significant bits (Bits[7:1]) contain the 7-bit Group Address, and the least significant bit (Bit[0]) is filled with the
2994 value 1'b0.
2995 The Command Code for the SETGRPA Direct CCC is 0x9B. Support for this CCC is required if the I3C Basic
2996 Target supports Group Addressing (per Section 4.3.4.4).

4.3.7.3.25 Reset Group Address (RSTGRPA)
2998 This CCC is available in Direct and Broadcast versions:
2999 • Direct (Figure 60): This CCC allows the Active Controller to remove one or more I3C Basic Target
3000 Devices from a Group by resetting their assigned Group Address. If a Group Address is used instead of a
3001 Target Address (Figure 61), then this CCC also allows the Active Controller to disband the indicated
3002 Group (i.e., the assigned Group Address is reset for all Devices in the Group, with the result that no
3003 Devices remain in the Group).
3004 • Broadcast (Figure 62): This CCC allows the Active Controller to disband all Groups, by resetting the
3005 assigned Group Address for all Devices with the result that no Devices remain in any Group.

4.3.7.3.26 Define List of Group Addresses (DEFGRPA)
3010 This Broadcast CCC (Figure 63) is only relevant to Secondary Controllers (as with the DEFTGTS CCC; see
3011 Section 4.3.7.3.7). The DEFGRPA CCC tells the Secondary Controller more details about a particular Group,
3012 including the list of I3C Basic Targets that are members of the Group. If multiple Groups have been configured,
3013 then the Active Controller should issue one DEFGRPA CCC for each configured Group. Each use of the DEFGRPA
3014 CCC shall include the Group Address that uniquely identifies the Group, as well as the Dynamic Addresses of its
3015 I3C Basic Targets.
3016 The Command Code for the DEFGRPA Broadcast CCC is 0x2B. Support for this CCC is required if an I3C Basic
3017 Secondary Controller supports Group Addressing (per Section 4.3.4.4) as well as handoff and/or management of
3018 Group Addresses, as part of Controller Role Handoff.

3020 • Group Descriptor: Identifier for this Group, using the same format and the same MIPI-defined values
3021 as the DCR (see Section 4.3.1.2.2 and MIPI-maintained web-based registry of defined values
3022 [MIPI02]).
3023 Examples: 8'd0 for Generic v1.0 Device, 8'd33 for Touch, 8'd129 for Proximity, etc.
3024 • Count: The number of the I3C Basic Targets that are members of this Group. This is the same as the
3025 number of Dynamic Addr bytes that follow.
3026 • Dynamic Address: For each instance, the 7 most significant bits (Bits[7:1]) contain a member Target’s
3027 current 7-bit Dynamic Address. The least significant bit (Bit[0]) has the value 1'b0.\n--- Page 159 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic

4.3.7.3.27 Set Bus Context (SETBUSCON)
3028 This optional Broadcast CCC (Figure 64) allows the I3C Basic Controller to specify that a particular context
3029 (Table 41) is being used on the I3C Basic Bus, i.e., allows the Controller to set the Bus context. The context will
3030 usually be a higher-level protocol specification published by a standards-developing organization that relies upon
3031 MIPI I3C Basic for the communication, but it may also indicate which version of the MIPI I3C Basic Specification
3032 is being used.
3033 This CCC allows Targets to activate any special functions needed to support the selected higher-level protocol on
3034 this I3C Basic Bus. This activation includes the definition of Vendor-specific or Standards-specific CCCs, and
3035 might include initiating any preparations required before starting the selected higher-level protocol, or potentially
3036 control of any other Device capabilities.
3037 Example: A given Target might need to reduce, expand, or modify its support for the given Bus, based
3038 upon the requirements of the selected higher-level protocol specification.
3039 The SETBUSCON CCC is normally emitted only once during Bus initialization; however, the Controller might
3040 need to emit SETBUSCON again after a Hot-Join, a Target Reset, or any other situation that might cause a Target
3041 to lose its context.
3042 Note:
3043 In the SETBUSCON model, Targets only react to Bus context byte values that they recognize. For
3044 example, a Debug-capable Target (or Debug part of a Target) will react only to the Debug context byte
3045 value, whereas the normal application Target will react only to the context byte value representing the main
3046 context.
3047 The Command Code for the SETBUSCON Broadcast CCC is 0x0C. Support for this CCC is optional, and shall
3048 depend on the supported context(s).
3049 Layered Protocol Contexts
3050 On a given Bus, SETBUSCON CCC is typically used with only one context value. However in some situations it
3051 can be used more than once, with different context byte values, in order to support a layered protocol context.
3052 Examples:
3053 1. Emitting both the MIPI I3C Basic Specification revision context, and the context of a given higher-
3054 level protocol. The order would be: first emit the I3C Basic Specification revision context value, and
3055 then emit the higher-level protocol context value.
3056 2. Adding an isolated, mixed-channel use, for example Debug. This allows a normal higher-level
3057 protocol to be used alongside Debug-specific Messages, in an intermixed manner. In such cases both
3058 contexts may be emitted.
3059 3. Setting Bus context for JEDEC SPD changes rules for Dynamic Address Assignment, and defining a
3060 set of Vendor-specific / Standards-specific CCCs.
End of this Broadcast CCC
P

4.3.7.4 Direct CCCs and Group Addresses
3063 If a Target supports Group Address capabilities (see Section 4.3.4.4) and has been assigned a Group Address,
3064 then it may receive and acknowledge certain Direct SET or Direct Write CCCs sent to an assigned Group
3065 Address, for Direct CCCs that are either defined in Table 16, or otherwise reserved for other MIPI WGs or
3066 custom definition. The Controller may send a Direct CCC as a Write to a Group Address, such that all Targets
3067 in the Group may receive and acknowledge such Direct CCCs (if supported). Each such Target that supports
3068 such CCCs shall ACK the Direct CCC as a Write sent to an assigned Group Address, as indicated in this
3069 section, using the same manner for acknowledging a Direct CCC as a Write that would be sent to its assigned
3070 Dynamic Address (per Section 4.3.7.2.2).
3071 Note:
3072 If no such Targets ACK a Direct SET or Direct Write CCC that is sent to an assigned Group Address,
3073 then the Controller shall detect this as a NACK (i.e., a failure to ACK the CCC).
3074 Table 42 specifies which Direct CCCs are either recommended for use (with possible limitations) or not
3075 supported for use with a Group Address.
3076 • A Target that supports any of these CCCs marked as ‘Not supported’ that might otherwise be
3077 supported (i.e., when sent to its assigned Dynamic Address) shall not acknowledge the same CCC
3078 when sent to a Group Address to which it is currently assigned.
3079 • A Target that supports any of these CCCs marked as ‘May use as multicast’ may choose to
3080 acknowledge the CCC when sent to either its Dynamic Address or any assigned Group Address,
3081 with identical meaning. Such ‘multicast’ writes may be used for convenience of configuration.
3082 • The Controller should follow up with a Direct GET form of the same CCC (if applicable) to
3083 each Target’s Dynamic Address to confirm that the Direct SET to the Group Address was
3084 accepted.
3085 • If a Target does not support such a CCC when sent to a Group Address, then the Controller
3086 might not see its lack of acknowledgement.
3087 • A Target that supports any of these CCCs marked as ‘Limited support’ or ‘Not recommended’
3088 might support Group Addresses for certain formats or when used with certain Defining Bytes, for
3089 particular use cases only. In such cases, the Target might not support the CCC in the same manner
3090 as though it were sent to its Dynamic Address.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 147
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 162 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
3091 Table 42 Direct CCC Support for Group Addresses
Command
Status Notes and Limitations
Name
ENEC, May use as multicast Note that the event conditions that are controlled by these CCCs relate to Target
DISEC Interrupt Requests, which cannot be sent from a Group Address.
ENTAS0, May use as multicast Activity State configuration generally applies to the whole Device. Note that many
ENTAS1, uses of these CCCs are to communicate expected latencies to expect in response to
ENTAS2, pulling SDA Low, which do not apply to an assigned Group Address, since a Target
ENTAS3 Interrupt Request cannot be generated from a Group Address.
SETMWL May use as multicast Maximum write length applies to the whole Device, not an individual Group Address.
No standard method is defined to read the maximum write length for only a Group
Address, as opposed to a Dynamic Address.
SETMRL Not recommended Multicast configuration not recommended. Maximum read length does not usually
apply, since Controller cannot read from a Group Address.
SETDASA, Not supported CCCs that assign or change a Dynamic Address cannot be sent to any Group
SETNEWDA Address.
SETBRGTGT Not supported Group Addressing is not defined for a Bridge Device.
SETROUTE Not supported Group Addressing is not defined for a Routing Device.
SETXTIME May use as multicast Timing Control settings apply to the Device as a whole. Note that any Timing Control
settings sent to a Group Address should be used for multicast configuration as an
alternative to the Broadcast SETXTIME CCC. Controllers should first confirm that all
Targets in a Group support the same Timing Control Mode before using the
SETXTIME CCC with a Group Address.
ENDXFER May use as multicast Controller should confirm that applied settings are accepted, using Direct GET CCC to
each Target’s Dynamic Address; see Section 6.2.3.4.4.
D2DXFER Not supported Device to Device Transfers are addressed to a Receiver Address, not a Group
Address. See Section 6.9.
RSTACT Limited support, for Target Reset Actions are not defined for a Group Address. Specific Defining Bytes
defined uses only. might be used with Group Addresses, for some special use cases. Recommend using
Dynamic Addresses, unless specific situations require Group Addresses.
SETGRPA Not supported SETGRPA CCC may only be sent to a Target’s Dynamic Address.
RSTGRPA Limited support, for In one form, removes all Targets from this Group Address; see Section 4.3.7.3.25.
defined uses only.
MLANE Limited support, for Sets the ML Frame format for subsequent transfers addressed to the Group Address
defined uses only. for an I3C Mode. May only be sent if all Targets in the Group support separate ML
configurations (per Section 6.7.3.1).
MIPI WG Not yet defined Reserved for use by other MIPI Alliance Working Groups. Support for Group
Reserved Addressing might be defined in another MIPI specification.
Vendor / For Vendor or Generally available for use (see Table 16), but MIPI Alliance recommends careful
Standards Standards use consideration based on the intended use case. Contact MIPI Alliance I3C Working
Extension Group for guidance and specific recommendations.

Public Release Edition\n--- Page 163 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
3092 Note:
3093 For any Direct CCCs listed in Table 42 with either full or partial support for a Group Address, such
3094 CCC definitions in the respective sub-sections of Section 4.3.7.3 that only have a Direct SET or
3095 Direct Write CCC form using “Target Addr” in the Direct CCC framing (i.e., after the Repeated START,
3096 or “Sr”) should also be interpreted as supporting a valid Group Address with that particular form,
3097 where a Group Address may be used in place of the “Target Addr” in the framing (see
3098 Section 4.3.2.1.3).
3099 However, any CCCs that are listed as ‘Limited support’ or ‘Not recommended’ should generally not
3100 be interpreted as supporting a Group Address in place of the “Target Addr” (per notes and limitations).
3101 Additionally, any CCCs that are listed as ‘Not supported’ do not support a Group Address in place of
3102 the “Target Addr”.
3103 The Controller shall not send any Direct GET or Direct Read CCCs to a Group Address, and Targets shall
3104 not ACK any Direct CCCs that would attempt to read from an assigned Group Address.

4.3.8 Error Detection and Recovery Methods for SDR
3105 The error detection and recovery methods specified in this Section are provided in order to avoid fatal
3106 conditions when errors occur. A set of required methods is specified for I3C Basic Target Devices, and a
3107 separate set of required methods is specified for I3C Basic Controller Devices. Origins for all of these SDR
3108 Error Types are shown in Figure 177 through Figure 180.
4.3.8.1 SDR Error Detection and Recovery Methods for I3C Basic Target Devices
3109 The Error Types summarized in Table 43 shall be supported for all I3C Basic Target Devices. Each Error
3110 Type is further explained in a sub-section below the table.
3111 Note:
3112 In previous versions of the I3C Basic Specification, these Error Types for I3C Basic Target Devices
3113 were named “S0”, “S1”, etc. The purpose, detection methods, and recovery methods for these Error
3114 Types are unchanged.
3115 Table 43 SDR Target Error Types
