Public Release Edition\n--- Page 165 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
3116 Note:
1. In the ENTDAA mode, “7'h7E / R” is excluded from the TE0 error definition.
4.3.8.1.1 Error Type TE0
3117 If an error occurs during Broadcast Address/W or Dynamic Address/RnW after a Dynamic Address is
3118 assigned, then the Target will be unable to distinguish whether the transfer is a CCC transfer or a Private
3119 RnW transfer. For example, in the case of an ENTHDR CCC transfer the Target is not able to know that the
3120 I3C Basic Bus has changed to HDR Mode. A potentially fatal situation could ensue if this case is not detected
3121 and handled, because the Target might become confused by seeing many STARTs and STOPs as illustrated
3122 in Figure 65, and might attempt to interpret the HDR transfer as though the I3C Basic Bus were still in SDR
3123 Mode.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 151
Public Release Edition
S
S
D
C
A
L
S T O P S T A R T S T O P S T O P S T A R T S T A R T
3124
3125 Figure 65 Example Waveform for Error Type TE0
3126 In order to avoid this situation, the Controller shall not use any of the possible error case Addresses: 7'h7F,
3127 7'h7C, 7'h7A, 7'h76, 7'h6E, 7'h5E and 7'h3E.
3128 • The Controller shall not assign any of these Addresses to any Target as a Dynamic Address (or a
3129 Group Address, if supported) per the I3C Basic Target Address restrictions in Table 10 (see
3130 Section 4.3.2.2.5).
3131 • The Controller shall not use any of these Addresses in an I3C Basic Address Header with the RnW
3132 bit of 1'b0, because the Target cannot distinguish such an I3C Basic Address Header from a write
3133 to the Broadcast Address (i.e., 7'h7E/W) that has a single bit error.
3134 Except during a Dynamic Address Arbitration procedure, the Target shall consider receipt of any of these
3135 restricted Addresses (i.e., 7'h7F/W, 7'h7C/W, 7'h7A/W, 7'h76/W, 7'h6E/W, 7'h5E/W and 7'h3E/W), or the
3136 receipt of 7'h7E/R, as an error per the following conditions:
3137 • The Target shall always use its Error Type TE0 detector after a Dynamic Address has been
3138 assigned, to monitor the I3C Basic Address Header for SDR Mode transfers.
3139 • This generally applies to any I3C Basic Address Header that follows either a START or a
3140 Repeated START (see Figure 177, Figure 178, and Figure 179).
3141 • However, this shall not apply during the Dynamic Address Assignment procedure, if the
3142 Controller uses the ENTDAA CCC (per Section 4.3.4.2). If this occurs, then the Target shall
3143 use its Error Type TE4 detector instead (per Section 4.3.8.1.5) until the end of the Dynamic
3144 Address Assignment procedure (see Figure 180).
3145 If the Target’s Error Type TE0 detector detects such a single bit error in an I3C Basic Address Header, then
3146 the Target shall ignore the rest of the signal until either:
3147 • The Target detects the HDR Exit Pattern; or
3148 • Optional: The Target detects that both SCL and SDA stay High for a period greater than 60 µs (see
3149 Section 4.3.8.1.9).
3150 Note:
3151 The second method is optional, i.e., Target Devices are not required to support it. If this
3152 method is supported, then the Target shall enable its STOP or START detector to exit from the
3153 Error Type TE0 state. If supported, then this method may also be used for Error Type TE1.\n--- Page 166 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4.3.8.1.2 Error Type TE1
3154 If the Target detects a parity error during a CCC code, then the Target will not be able to know that the I3C
3155 Basic Bus has changed to HDR Mode if the CCC is ENTHDR. This is similar to the situation in Error Type
3156 TE0.
3157 In order to avoid this situation, if the Target detects a parity error during a CCC code, then the Target shall
3158 ignore the rest of the signal, until either:
3159 • The Target detects the HDR Exit Pattern, or
3160 • Optional: The Target detects that both SCL and SDA stay High for a period greater than 60 µs (see
3161 Section 4.3.8.1.9).
3162 Note:
3163 The second method is optional, i.e., Target Devices are not required to support it. If this
3164 method is supported, then the Target shall enable its STOP or START detector to exit from the
3165 Error Type TE0 state. If supported, then this method may also be used for Error Type TE0.
4.3.8.1.3 Error Type TE2
3166 If the Target detects a parity error during Write Data, then the Target shall wait for STOP or Repeated START.
3167 If the Target detects this error after receiving a CCC, then the Target shall either:
3168 1. Retain the CCC state until the Target detects the end of CCC command (i.e., when the Target is
3169 recovered by the Repeated START), or else
3170 2. Disregard everything until the STOP.
4.3.8.1.4 Error Type TE3
3171 If the Target detects a parity error in the PAR Bit of the Assigned Address during a Dynamic Address
3172 Arbitration procedure, then the Target shall generate NACK (after PAR) and then wait for another Repeated
3173 START and 7E/R to re-transmit the Provisioned ID.
4.3.8.1.5 Error Type TE4
3174 During a Dynamic Address Arbitration procedure, if the Target detects any value other than 7'h7E/R
3175 following Repeated START, then the Target shall generate NACK (after 7'h7E/R) and then wait for STOP to
3176 exit the ENTDAA mode.
4.3.8.1.6 Error Type TE5
3177 If the Target detects an illegally formatted CCC, then the Target shall generate NACK (after the Dynamic
3178 Address) and then wait for STOP or Repeated START.
3179 Examples of an illegally formatted CCC include:
3180 • If the Target receives a matching Dynamic Address with Write during a Direct CCC that only has a
3181 Direct Read or Direct GET form (e.g., the GETBCR CCC)
3182 • If the Target receives a matching Dynamic Address with Read during a Direct CCC that only has a
3183 Direct Write or Direct SET form (e.g., the SETGRPA CCC)
3184 If the Target detects this error after receiving a CCC, then the Target shall either:
3185 1. Retain the CCC state until the Target detects the end of CCC command (i.e., when the Target is
3186 recovered by the Repeated START), or else
3187 2. Stop the CCC when the Target is recovered by the STOP.
3188 Note:
3189 This Section does not provide all examples of illegally formatted CCCs.
152 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 167 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
3190 Note:
3191 If the Target detects a Direct CCC that is formatted correctly, but that is not supported, then the
3192 Target shall handle such CCCs, or unsupported Defining Bytes, as described in Section 4.3.7.2.2.
3193 In an HDR Mode CCC, the Target would wait for the CCC to end as described in Section 6.1.3.1,
3194 Figure 101.
4.3.8.1.7 Error Type TE6 (Optional)
3195 If an error occurs in the RnW Bit of the Address Header, then the Target might believe that it is responding
3196 to a Read, when what the Controller actually intends to initiate is a Write. If this happens, then the Write Data
3197 from the Controller might conflict with the Read Data from the Target.
3198 The Target should always monitor the Data it transmits for Read transactions. If the Target does so, then if
3199 the monitored Data differs from the Data the Target intended to transmit, the Target shall consider this
3200 condition to be an error.
3201 Note:
3202 The Controller should also monitor the Data it transmits. If this condition happens due to a Target’s
3203 misinterpretation of the RnW bit during an intended Write transfer, then the Controller should also
3204 detect this condition as Error Type CE1 (see Section 4.3.8.2.2).
3205 The Target shall not consider this condition to be an example of Error Type TE6 during the Arbitration
3206 round of a Dynamic Address Assignment procedure (i.e., when the Controller has sent the ENTDAA
3207 CCC) while the Target is attempting to drive its Provisioned ID, BCR and DCR (see Section 4.3.4.2.
3208 If the Target detects such an error during an intended Private Write transfer (i.e., when the Target believes it
3209 is responding to a Private Read transfer), then it shall stop the transmission, allow the Controller to finish,
3210 and then wait for STOP or Repeated START.
3211 If the Target detects such an error during an intended Direct SET or Direct Write CCC (i.e., when the Target
3212 believes that it is responding to a Direct GET or Direct Read CCC), then the Target shall stop the transmission,
3213 allow the Controller to finish, and then either:
3214 1. Retain the CCC state until the Target detects the end of CCC command (i.e., when the Target is
3215 recovered by the Repeated START), or else
3216 2. Stop the CCC when the Target is recovered by the STOP.
4.3.8.1.8 Error Type DBR (Optional)
3217 This error allows a Controller-capable Device that is acting as a Target (i.e., a Secondary Controller) to regain
3218 control of a dead Bus (i.e., where the Active Controller has stopped working for whatever reason). This works
3219 both for a Primary Controller acting as Target (i.e., while a Device that initialized as a Secondary Controller
3220 is acting as Active Controller), and for a Secondary Controller acting as a Target.
3221 The general model is that when the Controller acting as Target pulls SDA Low to request an IBI or CRR, it
3222 may measure the time before SCL goes Low (i.e., in response to this START Request). If SCL does not go
3223 Low in 50 ms (t CAS maximum for Activity State 3), then the Controller acting as a Target may take action to
3224 take over control of the Bus and become the new Active Controller.
3225 The steps are as follows:
3226 1. The Controller acting as Target pulls SDA Low to initiate an IBI or CRR.
3227 A. It starts a timer.
3228 2. If 50 ms has elapsed without SCL going Low, then the Controller acting as a Target assumes
3229 that the former Active Controller is not operational.
3230 A. If SCL is pulled Low before 50 ms, then all is well and Error Type DBR shall not apply.
3231 B. Otherwise, Error Type DBR applies and as a result the Target acting as Controller may
3232 start a transition to Controller mode.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 153
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 168 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
3233 3. Once in Controller mode, the Device shall verify that SCL is still High, and if so, pull SCL
3234 Low to complete a START.
3235 A. If SCL is not High by the time the Device is a Controller, then the Device must switch
3236 back to Target mode, as Error Type DBR no longer applies. This might occur due to
3237 another Controller-capable Device (i.e., a Secondary Controller) having taken control of
3238 the Bus first.
3239 4. After the START, the Controller may wish to check the status of the Targets.
3240 A. It may also initiate an ENTDAA sequence to see whether any Devices need a Dynamic
3241 Address to be assigned (see Section 4.3.4.2).
3242 B. If available, the last DEFTGTS information that might have been sent by a previous
3243 Active Controller (i.e., as a Broadcast CCC) should be used for proper Dynamic Address
3244 assignment.
3245 Note:
3246 A Controller-capable Device acting as Target may choose to monitor the Bus at all times. In the event
3247 that SDA is pulled Low by any Target, the Device may then measure the time waiting for SCL to be
3248 pulled Low. If 50 ms elapses without SCL being pulled Low, then it may start the Dead Bus Recovery
3249 process above at step 3.
3250 If the former Active Controller eventually resumes operation and senses activity on SCL and/or SDA,
3251 then it must assume that it has lost the Controller Role to the new Active Controller.
3252 Per Section 4.3.9.4.1, the Primary Controller shall use a similar procedure after receiving a Whole
3253 Target reset, to determine whether it should be the Active Controller of the Bus.
4.3.8.1.9 Optional Recovery Method for Error Types TE0 and TE1
3254 An I3C Basic Target can recover from an Error Type TE0 or Error Type TE1 situation not only by detecting
3255 the HDR Exit Pattern, but also by monitoring the SCL and SDA lines. If the Target detects that both lines
3256 stay at High level for a period exceeding 60 µs, then the I3C Basic Target can regard the Bus as operating in
3257 non-HDR mode. The I3C Basic Target can then recover from the TE0 or TE1 situation, and wait for a STOP
3258 or a START to resume normal operation.
3259 Note:
3260 Regarding timing, HDR’s slowest clock rate is 10 kHz (100 µs total cycle). The period 60 µs is derived
3261 by assuming that HDR (i.e., HDR-DDR Mode) will always keep an approximately even duty cycle,
3262 especially at very slow clock rates (since the only reason to go so slow is for long lines and/or large
3263 capacitive load on Bus lines). 60 µs represents 60% of the duty cycle, and therefore is a safe duration
3264 to wait when seeing both lines High.
3265 The Target can start measuring the period whenever it detects that both SCL and SDA are at High
3266 level. There is no need to start timing this period at any particular signal pattern (for example, at the
3267 STOP).
154 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 169 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4.3.8.2 SDR Error Detection and Recovery Methods for I3C Basic Controller Devices
3268 Table 44 summarizes the defined Error Types for I3C Basic Controller Devices. All I3C Basic Controller
3269 Devices shall support Error Types CE0, CE2, and CE3, and should support Error Type CE1. Each Error Type
3270 is further explained in a sub-section below the table. Escalation and recovery are explained in
3271 Section 4.3.8.2.5 and Section 4.3.8.2.6.
3272 Note:
3273 In previous versions of the I3C Basic Specification, these Error Types for I3C Basic Controller /
3274 Controller Devices were named “M0”, “M1”, etc. The purpose, detection methods, and recovery
3275 methods for these Error Types are unchanged.
3276 Table 44 SDR Controller Error Types
Error
Description Error Detection Method Error Recovery Method
Type
CE0 Transaction after sending CCC Detect illegally formatted CCC Stop the transmission, then send
STOP and retry the transmission.
CE1 Monitoring Error Controller detects (through Stop the transmission, then send
(optional) monitoring) transmitted Data STOP and retry the transmission.
different from what it intended to
transmit (Does not apply during
Dynamic Address Arbitration)
CE2 No response to Broadcast Controller detects NACK after Upon detection of NACK,
Address (7'h7E) Broadcast Address (7'h7E) Controller transmits HDR Exit
transmission Pattern followed by STOP
CE3 Failed Controller Handoff Active Controller detects New Active Controller regains the
Controller does not drive Bus after Controller Role and drives Bus to
handoff assert its Controller Role
4.3.8.2.1 Error Type CE0
3277 If the Controller detects an illegally formatted CCC, then the Controller shall stop the transmission, send
3278 STOP, and retry the transmission. An example of an illegally formatted CCC would be the Controller
3279 receiving just one byte from the Target in a GETMWL CCC code, since the Controller expects two bytes.
4.3.8.2.2 Error Type CE1 (Optional)
3280 Error Type CE1 occurs when the I3C Basic Controller detects that the transmitted data differs from what the
3281 Controller intended to transmit. This might happen when the I3C Basic Controller or I3C Basic Targets
3282 misinterpret the RnW bit or the ACK/NACK during transactions (including IBI) defined by the I3C Basic
3283 specification. This Section describes only two kinds of occurrence conditions for Error Type CE1, and the
3284 recovery method for each kind. Note that Type CE1 errors are not an expected behavior.
3285 If an error occurs in a RnW Bit in a Private Write transfer, then the Write Data from the Controller might
3286 conflict with the Read Data from the Target. For example, the Target could misinterpret a Private Write
3287 transfer as a Read transfer if there is an error in the RnW Bit, and as a result Write Data from the Controller
3288 would conflict with Read Data from the Target.
3289 The Controller should always monitor the Data it transmits. If the Controller does so, then if the monitored
3290 Data differs from the Data the Controller intended to transmit (except for Data transferred during a Dynamic
3291 Address Arbitration procedure), the Controller shall consider that to be an error. If the Controller detects this
3292 error, then it shall stop the transmission, then send STOP and retry the transmission.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 155
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 170 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4.3.8.2.3 Error Type CE2
3293 If the Controller does not receive an ACK of a transmitted Broadcast Address (7'h7E), then it shall transmit
3294 the HDR Exit Pattern followed by STOP in order to recover any Target after TE0, TE1, TE2, TE5, and TE6
3295 errors.
4.3.8.2.4 Error Type CE3
3296 After the Controller to Controller Handoff procedure (see Section 4.3.6.3), the former Active Controller shall
3297 release SCL to High-Z and disable its Open Drain class Pull-Up on SDA, setting SDA to High-Z. However,
3298 the former Active Controller shall monitor the Bus to ensure that the new Controller has successfully asserted
3299 its Controller Role.
3300 If the former Active Controller does not detect a START within a specified Handoff period, then it shall test
3301 the New Controller to determine whether it actually controls the Bus, and it shall regain control of the Bus if
3302 the New Controller has not asserted its Controller Role. The specified Handoff period shall be at least 100 μs,
3303 but may be longer if the new Controller indicates that it requires a longer Handoff period.
3304 Before initiating the Controller to Controller Handoff procedure, the Active Controller shall first determine
3305 whether the Secondary Controller that is about to receive the Controller Role supports the GETMXDS CCC
3306 with Defining Byte value CRHDLY (see Section 4.3.7.3.18). If the Secondary Controller needs a longer
3307 Handoff period to assert the Controller Role, then it shall do so by indicating an Activity State. The Active
3308 Controller shall wait for the time period associated with the indicated Activity State, or 100 μs (whichever is
3309 greater) before attempting to test the new Controller.
3310 Once the Handoff period has elapsed, if the former Active Controller has not detected a START, then it should
3311 use the following steps to test the new Controller:
3312 5. The former Active Controller shall pull SDA Low. This may be unnecessary if another I3C
3313 Basic Target also pulls SDA Low (since the Handoff period is longer than the Bus Available
3314 Condition), but the effect is the same.
3315 6. After SDA is pulled Low, the former Active Controller shall wait for another 100 μs, or the
3316 time indicated by the new Controller’s indicated Activity State (as above, read with the
3317 GETMXDS CCC with Defining Byte value CRHDLY, if supported), whichever is greater.
3318 A. If the new Controller responds by pulling SCL Low within that period, then all is well: the
3319 new Controller has successfully asserted its Controller Role of the Bus. This is equivalent
3320 to a START, so this flow has a successful outcome. The former Active Controller shall
3321 release SDA and allow the new Controller to proceed.
3322 B. If the new Controller does not respond by pulling SCL Low within that period, then the
3323 former Active Controller shall pull SCL Low to regain the Bus Controller Role. This is
3324 equivalent to a START, but initiated in this case by the former Active Controller.
3325 Note:
3326 This outcome is an error condition on the part of the new Controller, which has not
3327 responded per its obligations. As a result, the new Controller shall return to being a Target,
3328 or remain as a Target.
3329 In either case, the Controller that eventually ‘wins’ shall become the Active Controller, and shall drive a valid
3330 Bus action to assert its Bus Controller Role. Since the START has been driven, the next step is the I3C Basic
3331 Address Header (see Section 4.3.2.2). The Active Controller shall attempt to drive its own Address (7'h7E).
3332 If another I3C Basic Target drives its own Address and wins Arbitration, then that Target may attempt to issue
3333 a Target Interrupt Request. However, if the Active Controller wins Address Arbitration but does not have any
3334 actions to take at that time, then it may follow with a STOP. Either is sufficient for the Active Controller to
3335 assert its Bus Controller Role.
3336 If the former Active Controller regains the Bus Controller Role due to inactivity by the new Controller, then
3337 it is responsible for taking any necessary actions to determine the reason why the handoff failed. It may use
156 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 171 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
3338 the GETSTATUS CCC or other commands to test for the presence of the new Controller, or to attempt to
3339 read its current status.
4.3.8.2.5 Controller Error Detection and Escalation Handling
3340 If SDA is stuck Low, then see Section 4.3.8.2.6. This Section addresses situations in which the Target is not
3341 responding.
3342 If the Controller does not receive an ACK of a transmitted private Message to a Target, and if the following
3343 conditions are all true:
3344 • Activity State is 0
3345 • Either the Target Device has not indicated read-turnaround delays via GETMXDS, or the Target
3346 Device has indicated a GETMXDS period and a period longer than GETMXDS has elapsed
3347 • The Target Device has not notified the Controller that it will be going into a lower Activity State
3348 via a private contract In-Band Interrupt (and either GETSTATUS or a private activity state status),
3349 then the Controller has the following escalation options to recover the system:
3350 1. If the Controller is aware that the Target might sometimes need extra time, then the Controller
3351 can choose to try again after a short delay.
3352 2. If that fails, then the Controller shall check whether the Target is responsive by issuing
3353 GETSTATUS to the Target. The idea here is that the Target might be forced to NACK a private
3354 Message due to its inner system being unready, whereas GETSTATUS is a lightweight request
3355 that does not necessarily involve the inner system.
3356 3. If that fails, then the Controller shall use CE2 error handling (see Section 4.3.8.2.3) to emit the
3357 sequence:
S | 7'h7E (W) | ACK/NACK* | HDR Exit Pattern | STOP
3358 Note:
3359 In order to avoid any conflict with an incoming IBI from Target Devices on the Bus, the
3360 Controller should send the Broadcast address before the HDR-Exit Pattern. The I3C Basic
3361 Controller doesn’t care whether the I3C Basic Targets’ response is ACK vs. NACK.
3362 This ensures that the failure is not due to the Target thinking that the I3C Basic Bus is in HDR
3363 Mode.
3364 4. If that fails, and if the Target is still not responsive, then the next step depends on the value of
3365 the BCR “Offline Capable” bit (Bit[3]):
3366 A. If the Target is not Offline Capable, then:
3367 i. The Controller can try slowing the SCL clock rate, or try slowing its effective rate by
3368 using duty cycle.
3369 ii. If that fails, then the Target Reset mechanism can be used. A first attempt can be tried
3370 to recover via the Peripheral, and if that fails, then a second attempt will cause a chip
3371 reset. The Target Reset mechanism is explained in Section 4.3.9.
3372 iii. If that fails, then any further escalation is outside the scope of the I3C Basic
3373 Specification.
3374 B. If the Target is Offline Capable, then:
3375 i. If the Target goes offline and is to be awoken via Target Reset, then the Target Reset
3376 approach should be used (see Section 4.3.9).
3377 ii. If the Target is known (i.e., via a private contract) to have a long wake period, and
3378 also known to monitor the I3C Basic Bus during that wake period, then the Controller
3379 simply delays and tries later, after a delay based on the known or expected wake up
3380 time. Escalation and the GETSTATUS ensure that the Target’s Dynamic Address was
3381 issued; that should serve as the wake trigger. Either the Target may issue an In-Band
3382 Interrupt at a later time to notify the Controller that it is ready, or else the Controller
3383 may initiate the retry.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 157
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 172 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
3384 iii. If Target is not known to have a long wake period and to always monitor the I3C
3385 Basic Bus, then the Controller marks the Target as offline. The Controller may then
3386 either retain the Target’s Dynamic Address for re-use, or else discard it. The next
3387 action will be for the Target to issue a Hot-Join Request, in order to be re-joined to the
3388 I3C Basic Bus. In the Hot-Join operation the Controller will assign the Target a
3389 Dynamic Address; the new Address may be either the same Address that the Target
3390 used before being marked as offline, or a different Address.
4.3.8.2.6 Controller Stuck SDA Handling
3391 If SDA is not stuck, but the Target is not responding, then see Section 4.3.8.2.5. If the Controller has
3392 recovered from a crash or unexpected reset, then see Section 4.3.8.2.7.
3393 The steps to attempt a recovery from a stuck SDA are as follows:
3394 1. If reading from an I2C Device, and it is holding SDA Low, then:
3395 A. Pulse out nine (9) SCL clocks, to get it to see the NACK on 9th bit to get it to let go.
3396 2. If reading from an I3C Basic Device in SDR Mode, and it is holding SDA Low, then:
3397 A. Pulse out one clock at a time (up to 8), as it is required to drive SDA High for the T-Bit (9th
3398 bit).
3399 B. Watch for SDA going High, and abort the read by driving SDA Low when SCL is High.
3400 3. If reading from an I3C Basic Device in SDR Mode, and SDA is being held Low, and the
3401 approach in step 2 did not work, then:
3402 A. Hold SCL level (High or Low) for 150 µs. The Target might support the 150 µs read-abort
3403 approach (per Section 4.3.2.3), and if so, it will release SDA.
3404 4. If reading from an I3C Basic Device in SDR Mode, and SDA is High, and the Device does not
3405 seem to abort when the Controller drove SDA Low on the T bit (i.e., when SCL was High),
3406 then:
3407 A. First try a 150 µs SCL hold (as explained in step 3).
3408 B. If that does not work, then do the same drive SDA Low for each SCL High, for up to 8
3409 more times. If there is a framing misalignment, then this will ensure that the T-Bit is
3410 finally caught and the read aborted.
3411 Note:
3412 This risks contrary drive, so Controller could lower its drive strength to reduce current, if
3413 wanted.
3414 5. If reading from an I3C Device in HDR-DDR Mode, and it goes wrong, then see
3415 Section 6.2.3.5.
3416 Note:
3417 The steps defined in this section do not constitute any form of Target reset procedure. There is no
3418 expectation that un-sticking the SDA line with these steps will induce a non-responding Target to
3419 reset any of its logic or other state. However, the I3C Basic Specification does already define an
3420 existing Target Reset mechanism; see Section 4.3.9.
158 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 173 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4.3.8.2.7 Controller Recovery After a Crash or Unexpected Reset
3421 If the Controller is recovering from a crash or unexpected reset, and it does not know the context of the Bus,
3422 then the Controller must determine the state of the Bus. It is assumed that the Controller has tried to put the
3423 Bus into Bus Free condition (i.e., SCL High, SDA held High with a Pull-Up), and that enough time has
3424 elapsed for the Pull-Up to pull SDA High, if possible.
3425 The status may be one of:
3426 • SDA is High and controlled by the Controller alone. This is normal Bus Free Condition. In this
3427 case the Controller should emit an HDR Exit Pattern (to be safe), and then work to recover the Bus
3428 in the usual manner (e.g., RSTDAA, etc.).
3429 • SDA is High, but a Target is holding it High from a previous Read. See below for detection.
3430 • SDA is Low. This might be a Target still holding Low from a previous Read, or it might be a Target
3431 pulling SDA Low for an In-Band Interrupt, a Controller Role Request, or a Hot-Join.
3432 If SDA is High, and the Controller needs to determine whether it is held High, then the Controller may use
3433 one of three ways to do so:
3434 1. The Controller uses a weak Pull-Down resistor in its pad for SDA and turns off any active Pull-
3435 Up. If the line goes Low (after a reasonable time), then it is controlled by the Controller, and
3436 the Controller may proceed normally.
3437 2. The Controller may drive SDA Low, risking contrary drive (e.g., 4 mA current), to determine
3438 whether the SDA is held High.
3439 3. The Controller may lower the drive current of the SDA pad and then drive Low, to minimize
3440 contrary drive effects.
3441 If the Controller determines SDA to be held High, then follow the instructions in Section 4.3.8.2.6, step 4.
3442 This method can be used for both an SDR Read and an HDR-DDR Read, however an HDR-DDR Read will
3443 need 16 clocks (10 for data preamble, 16 in case last data into CRC).
3444 If SDA is Low, then the Controller needs to determine why it is held Low. The Controller can use the
3445 following steps to do so:
3446 1. The Controller drives SCL Low. If SDA is released (i.e., strong Pull-Up on SDA causes it to go
3447 High), then a Target was trying to perform an In-Band Interrupt, a Controller Role Request, or
3448 a Hot-Join. The Controller may pull SDA Low again and continue with a START, or emit a
3449 STOP (i.e., drive SCL High, then pull SDA High), and then proceed to restart the Bus by
3450 NACKing any In-Band Interrupt, Controller Role Request, or Hot-Join, and then using the
3451 RSTDAA CCC.
3452 2. If SDA is not released, then the Controller should emit 19 or more SCL clocks. If SDA goes
3453 High at some point, then it should emit more SCL clocks to ensure 19 clocks were used with
3454 SDA High (in case it was in HDR-DDR Mode). If SDA is now High, then the Bus is not in
3455 HDR-DDR Read and also not in an I2C Read. The Controller should test whether SDA is stuck
3456 High, as explained above (starting at “If SDA is High”). If SDA is not stuck High, then the
3457 Controller should issue an HDR Exit Pattern in case it had been in HDR-DDR Mode.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 159
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 174 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4.3.9 Target Reset
3458 This Section specifies the Target Reset mechanism.
3459 The Target Reset mechanism:
3460 • Allows the Controller to Reset one or more selected Targets, and avoid resetting any others
3461 • Supports different levels of reset, ranging from resetting only the I3C Basic Peripheral within a
3462 Target, to resetting the whole Target Device
3463 • Supports I3C Basic’s error escalation mechanism (see Section 4.3.8), including reset of an errant
3464 Target
3465 The Target Reset mechanism is simple, relying on these principles:
3466 • The uniqueness of the HDR Exit Pattern (i.e., cannot be confused with any other I3C Basic Bus
3467 traffic)
3468 • Use of an in-band Target Reset Pattern to trigger the actual reset action. (In contrast to a reset/break
3469 hold as used in UART, one-wire [R], etc., which would require timing.)
3470 • A defined default reset action that all Targets will use until and unless a different reset action is
3471 configured via the RSTACT CCC (Broadcast and Directed formats)
3472 • An error recovery escalation mechanism, for use when needed (e.g., Error Types TE0 and TE1).
3473 The Target Reset mechanism applies these principles to address three distinct reset cases:
3474 1. Recovery from error/hang in the Target I3C Basic Peripheral
3475 2. Recovery from more serious errors in the chip containing the I3C Basic Peripheral
3476 3. Wake from deepest sleep, while minimizing the number of gates used in the always-on domain
4.3.9.1 Theory of Operation
3477 The Target Reset mechanism uses a simple model (Figure 66). In normal operation the Controller directs the
3478 Targets in their actions, and the Targets take those actions. When the Target is in a deep enough sleep, or is
3479 broken, then the Target Reset performs an appropriate action. The Controller uses the RSTACT CCC
3480 (Broadcast and/or Directed formats, as needed) to configure which Targets are to be reset, the level(s) of reset
3481 to be used, and which Targets are not to be reset.
3482 Controller: To trigger the Reset action, the Controller shall emit the following sequence:
3483 • START
3484 • Zero or more Message components, including RSTACT CCCs (Broadcast and/or Directed, as
3485 needed) each optionally ending in Repeated START
3486 • At this point in the sequence the Controller shall not emit a STOP; the Controller shall instead keep
3487 the sequence within a single Frame (see Note below).
3488 • The Target Reset Pattern, including the final Repeated START and STOP that trigger the actual
3489 reset action
3490 • At this point in the sequence the Controller shall leave the Bus free for as long as needed (or
3491 desired) for the Targets to complete their reset cycles.
3492 • START, with an I3C Basic Address Header (directed to either 7'h7E or any other Address)
3493 Note:
3494 It is mandatory to emit the RSTACT CCCs and the Target Reset Pattern together in a single Frame
3495 (i.e., before emitting a STOP) for several reasons:
3496 • To avoid the risk of the RSTACT getting disconnected from the Target Reset action, which
3497 would be dangerous. A properly operating Target will cancel an RSTACT CCC when it sees an
3498 SCL falling edge following a START (but not a Repeated START).
3499 • Because neither I3C Basic nor I2C define the Bus condition of SCL falling from Bus Free, and
3500 as a result some Devices might exhibit unknown or unexpected behavior.
160 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 175 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
3501 • Because the Target Reset Pattern includes STOP, in order to preserve a clean Bus state for all
3502 Targets (i.e., both Targets that are being reset, and Targets that are not being reset).
3503 • A Target could pull SDA Low after a STOP.
3504 Target: A Target shall react to receipt of the Target Reset Pattern based on its state at the time of reception:
3505 • If the Target had been configured via the RSTACT CCC to take a particular reset action (including
3506 taking no action) and was able to process that RSTACT CCC (i.e., not broken), then the Target
3507 shall perform the reset action as configured.
3508 • If the Target had been in a deepest-sleep state, then it may Wake up (e.g., power up, etc.). This
3509 Wake operation itself may either reset the Device or not, per Section 4.3.9.5.
3510 • If a Target had been unable to recognize a RSTACT CCC (i.e., was broken), or if no RSTACT
3511 CCC had been sent to the Target, then the Target shall either:
3512 • If this is the first time that the Target has seen the Target Reset Pattern: Then the Target
3513 shall reset the I3C Basic Peripheral. If a processor is in use, then it will notify the application
3514 via internal interrupt, so that it can reconfigure the I3C Basic Peripheral).
3515 or
3516 • If the Target has previously reset the I3C Basic Peripheral and is seeing a new Target Reset
3517 Pattern with no intervening RSTACT or GETSTATUS CCC: Then the Target shall reset the
3518 whole chip, i.e., equivalent to an SRSTn pin being asserted (see Section 4.3.9.4).
3519 Note:
3520 Any reset action (including inaction) configured via the RSTACT CCC shall be cleared by the next
3521 SCL falling edge following a START (but not by the next Repeated START).
3522 This specification is silent regarding which Target internal memory is retained vs. cleared in a
3523 Peripheral reset (where ‘memory’ could be either volatile or non-volatile, and could be implemented
3524 in any number of ways). See Table 45 for more details. In particular, the Target’s Dynamic Address
3525 may be either retained or cleared. If the Target clears its Dynamic Address, then it shall participate
3526 in an ENTDAA that follows the Target Reset Pattern; if the Target retains its Dynamic Address, then
3527 it shall not participate.

4.3.9.2 RSTACT CCC
3529 The RSTACT CCC (fully detailed in Section 4.3.7.3.23) has three formats with different uses:
3530 • Broadcast to configure a Target Reset action for all Targets
3531 • Directed Write to configure a Target Reset action for one or more specified Targets
3532 • Directed Read to read the Reset recovery timing from each Target, including for reset of the
3533 Peripheral, Wakeup, and reset of the whole Device.
4.3.9.3 Target Reset Pattern
3534 The Target Reset Pattern (Figure 67) is used to trigger the default or configured reset action. The Target
3535 Reset Pattern begins with fourteen SDA transitions while SCL is kept Low, and ends with a Repeated START
3536 followed by a STOP while SCL is kept high which triggers the actual Reset action. The Target Reset Pattern
3537 is easily distinguished both from the shorter HDR Exit Pattern on which it is based (see Section 4.3.10.2),
3538 and from the HDR Restart Pattern.
3539 Note:
3540 • Similar to the HDR Exit Pattern and the HDR Restart Pattern, the Controller shall observe the
3541 minimum timing while sending the Target Reset Pattern, as defined by tDIG_H minimum value
3542 (see Section 4.3.10.2 and Section 6.1.2). This shall apply to all transitions on both SDA and
3543 SCL.
3544 • If the Target pulls SDA to Low first, before the Controller can pull SCL to Low to initiate the Target
3545 Reset Pattern, then the Controller is obligated to handle the situation by cancelling the Target
3546 Reset Pattern and driving transitions on SCL as part of the Arbitrable Address Header.
3547 Subsequently, the Controller may choose to send the DISEC CCC to disable Target requests
3548 before sending the next Target Reset Pattern.
3549 • However, if the Controller pulls SCL to Low first, then the Target shall not pull SDA to Low, and
3550 must release SDA and wait until the Controller finishes the Target Reset Pattern.
162 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 177 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
3551 • If the Controller is sending the Target Reset Pattern from the Idle state, then the Controller shall
3552 monitor the SDA line after pulling SCL Low to make sure that there is no SDA pull down currently
3553 initiated by the Target. This could take some time for the Controller to process in the case that
3554 an IBI is being initiated at the same time that the SCL line is pulled Low. The controller should
3555 add additional delay between the SCL Low and the first SDA transition in order to accommodate
3556 propagation delays in the bus. In this scenario, the controller shall cancel the Target Reset
3557 Pattern and handle the IBI.

Figure 67 Target Reset Pattern: General Case
3559 If the Target pulls SDA to Low to initiate IBI, Hot-Join, or CRR, then the Controller must cancel and service
3560 this request, and then should drive a STOP to end the SDR frame before sending the Target Reset Pattern.
3561 Once the I3C Basic Bus is idle, the Controller can send the Target Reset Pattern. The Controller can also
3562 drive the Target Reset Pattern at any time, to reset all Targets. Figure 68 shows how the Target Reset Pattern
3563 is initiated when the I3C Basic Bus is idle.

Note:
In previous versions of the I3C Basic Specification, these requirements were not defined as clearly. I3C Basic
Controllers should be aware that older Targets (i.e., those conforming to I3C Basic Specification v1.1.1 and
earlier) might have different reset behaviors. However, all new implementations should use the requirements
defined in the I3C Basic Specification v1.2 or later.
Additional requirements may apply to Virtual Targets that are part of a composite Device (i.e., one that has
multiple Dynamic Addresses), especially when the Broadcast RSTACT CCC is used. See also Section 5.2.2.3
in the MIPI Alliance I3C Application Note: Virtual Devices and Virtual Targets [MIPI10].
Table Notes:
1) If the Target also supports Group Addressing, then resetting the Dynamic Address shall also reset any
assigned Group Addresses.
2) This includes all I3C Basic Target configuration that is set by MIPI-defined CCCs, including ENEC/DISEC,
ENTAS0-ENTAS3, SETMRL, SETMWL, ENTTM and ENDXFER. For an I3C Basic Peripheral reset, the
implementer may determine how many of these are reset to defaults. For a Whole Target reset, all of these
should be reset to defaults.
3) Targets are not required to support the Whole Target reset; it is optional. However, if a Target does support
the Whole Target reset, then it shall reset all I3C Basic Target logic.
4.3.9.4 Whole Target Reset Behavior
3568 Reset of the Whole Target can be caused either by escalation of default (per Figure 66), or by a configured
3569 reset action via the RSTACT CCC with Defining Byte value 0x02 (per Table 38). An I3C Basic Target that
3570 receives a Whole Target reset shall clear its entire I3C Basic Target configuration, as though it was fully reset
3571 back to its default configuration.
3572 Additionally, implementers may choose to include a full/chip reset as part of the Whole Target reset. In
3573 general, a full/chip reset could include other logic within the chip/SoC (i.e., different domains or functions
3574 outside the I3C Basic Target logic) which would be reset at the same time as the I3C Basic Target logic.
3575 This Specification does not strictly define behavior for a full/chip reset, however it is assumed to be similar
3576 to the behavior upon assertion of an SRSTn pin:
3577 • Since this is a warm reset (i.e., power was not cut), the chip’s precise behavior as it comes back up
3578 is the responsibility of the implementation.
3579 • In order to receive a Dynamic Address, the Target will need to participate in the Dynamic Address
3580 Assignment process (e.g., with the ENTDAA CCC), since all I3C Basic Target logic would be reset
3581 by the Whole Target reset.
3582 • Any further steps after Dynamic Address Assignment are the responsibility of the driver
3583 controlling the Controller and Target. Such steps might be dictated by rules imposed by higher-
164 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 179 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
3584 level standardized protocols (e.g., Camera, Touch, Debug, etc.), and/or by other a priori driver
3585 knowledge of the specific Target type.
3586 Examples
3587 • For a RAM-Based Target: Determining whether another firmware download is needed
3588 • For a Sensor: Determining whether a full calibration vs. a push of stored calibration data is
3589 needed
3590 However, implementers are not required to trigger a full/chip reset when performing a Whole Target reset.
3591 Implementers may choose to support any of the available RSTACT Defining Byte values (i.e., 0x40 thru
3592 0x7F) that can optionally be used for resetting other logic, domains, or functions within the chip; and may
3593 also choose to use one of these available Defining Byte values as a dedicated full/chip reset (i.e., instead of
3594 Defining Byte value 0x02).
4.3.9.4.1 Primary Controller Behavior After Whole Target Reset
3595 There is a special condition where the Primary Controller acting as a Target (i.e., a Secondary Controller) is
3596 reset by the Active Controller.
3597 When the Primary Controller wakes up from the reset and it does not know why it was reset, it then needs to
3598 determine whether it was the Active Controller before the reset, or was acting as a Target (i.e., a Secondary
3599 Controller) and some other Controller-capable Device was the Active Controller.
3600 In order to do this, the Primary Controller shall monitor the Bus for activity on SCL for t IDLE . If the Bus is
3601 active, then the Device assumes it is not the Active Controller. If there is no activity, then the Device shall
3602 drive the SDA Low (if it wasn’t already Low) and wait for 50 ms (i.e., t CAS maximum for Activity State 3),
3603 and if an Active Controller that initiated the Reset does not respond by pulling down the SCL line, then the
3604 Primary Controller can assume that it is the Active Controller and start driving the SCL line. Then the Primary
3605 Controller shall broadcast the RSTDAA CCC and continue with the Bus initialization.
3606 Note:
3607 This procedure is similar to the Dead Bus Recovery procedure for Error Type DBR (see
3608 Section 4.3.8.1.8).
4.3.9.5 Wake from Target Reset Behavior
3609 This Specification does not mandate any particular implementation or behavior for Wake from Target Reset
3610 (i.e., the arc starting with “Target into Deepest Sleep” in Figure 66). Waking from deepest-sleep/standby
3611 might also involve a reset (in whole or part); however, this will usually depend upon how the deepest-
3612 sleep/standby is implemented.
3613 Common types of Wake from Target Reset behavior include:
3614 • Wake Performs Reset: The Target wakes from “power down” state with little or no restoration of
3615 saved information. In particular, the Target’s Dynamic Address is not saved. As a result, this case
3616 behaves like a normal reset:
3617 • Following the reset operation, in order to receive its assigned DA the Target will need to
3618 participate in the Controller-initiated ENTDAA Dynamic Address Assignment process.
3619 • If Hot-Join is supported, then the Target should also issue the Hot-Join Request after the reset.
3620 • Wake with State Restoration: Some amount of critical state information was previously saved
3621 somewhere outside the Target (e.g., always-on flops or the RTC domain), and is restored upon
3622 Target wake. This case works the same as the offline wake model. This usually means saving and
3623 restoring the Target’s Dynamic Address, and can also involve additional state data.
3624 • If the Target’s DA is restored in this manner, then the Controller will not need to take further
3625 steps to re-assign it (i.e., no need to then perform the ENTDAA CCC Dynamic Address
3626 Assignment procedure).
3627 • In this case, the Target is not required to issue a Hot-Join Request.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 165
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 180 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
3628 • Wake with State Preserved: In this case, Target state information does not need to be explicitly
3629 restored because it is preserved in place during the reset-wake interval. This case behaves the same
3630 as a wake from light sleep. Data preservation might be achieved via state-retention flops (SRFF),
3631 or simply by not actually cutting power during the reset-wake interval.
166 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 181 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4.3.10 HDR Pattern Tolerance
3632 This Section describes the required HDR communications for all I3C Basic Devices.
3633 Although support for HDR Modes is optional in I3C, all I3C Basic Targets must understand the following,
3634 even if such Targets do not support a particular HDR Mode (or any HDR Modes):
3635 • When the Controller enters an HDR Mode (i.e., by sending any ENTHDRx CCC); and
3636 • When the Controller sends the HDR Exit Pattern (i.e., to exit the HDR Mode and return to SDR
3637 Mode).
3638 This ensures device compatibility on the Bus.
4.3.10.1 HDR Entry
3639 Each HDR Mode is separate from all other HDR Modes:
3640 • I3C Basic Targets may choose to support any desired combination of the HDR Modes, including no
3641 HDR Modes. If the I3C Bus enters an HDR Mode that an I3C Basic Target does not support, then
3642 that Target can simply wait and watch for the common HDR Exit Pattern.
3643 • I3C Basic Controllers may choose to support any desired combination of the HDR Modes, including
3644 no HDR Modes. However, manufacturers are generally encouraged to support all of the defined
3645 HDR Modes.
3646 • I3C Basic Devices that support HDR-BT may choose to support DUAL Lane or QUAD Lane
3647 configurations, in addition to SINGLE Lane configuration.
3648 HDR Modes have Bus-wide effect. That is, the whole I3C Bus can be put into a given HDR Mode, and once
3649 entered that HDR Mode shall remain in effect until the end of that transaction.
3650 HDR Modes may support CCCs as indicated by Table 75. Generally, CCCs that assign Dynamic Addresses,
3651 alter I3C Bus Modes, or transfer the I3C Bus Controller Role are not allowed within HDR Modes.
3652 An HDR Mode period on the I3C Bus involves five steps:
3653 1. The Controller Broadcasts an Enter HDR Mode CCC, indicating which particular HDR Mode
3654 to enter. (See the Command Codes Enter HDR Mode 0 through Enter HDR Mode 7
3655 [ENTHDR0–ENTHDR7] in Section 4.3.7.3.9).
3656 2. The I3C Bus switches from SDR Mode to the requested HDR Mode (see Section 6.1.4).
3657 3. The Controller issues the first structured protocol element per the HDR Mode framing.
3658 Typically, this is either a Command or Header, followed by optional Data sent by the
3659 Controller or Target Device, etc.
3660 4. An HDR Restart Pattern (see Section 6.1) or HDR Exit Pattern is sent.
3661 • If an HDR Restart Pattern, then the Controller issues the first structured protocol element for
3662 the new HDR Mode transfer (i.e., another Command or Header, followed by optional Data,
3663 etc.; see Section 6.1.4.2).
3664 The Controller may repeat this process to remain in the HDR Mode, or send an HDR Exit
3665 Pattern to exit the HDR Mode.
3666 5. If the Controller sends an HDR Exit Pattern, then it is always followed by I3C STOP, which
3667 ends with the Bus Free Condition.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 167
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 182 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4.3.10.2 HDR Exit Pattern
3668 Once an HDR Mode is entered, the HDR Exit Pattern is used to leave it, always exiting back to SDR Mode.
3669 The same HDR Exit Pattern is used to exit all HDR Protocols; that Pattern does not appear in any HDR
3670 Protocol’s normal data or command flow. All I3C Basic Targets shall detect and respond to the HDR Exit
3671 Pattern, irrespective of whether the Target supports any particular HDR Mode. The HDR Exit Pattern
3672 Detector is specified in Section 4.3.10.2.1.
3673 The HDR Exit Pattern is defined thus:
3674 • SDA starts High, SCL starts Low
3675 • SDA falls (from High to Low) 4 times, while SCL remains Low for the whole time
3676 • Each transition on SDA and/or SCL is separated by a time interval of at least t DIG_H (see
3677 Section 4.3.11.2)
3678 If necessary, then the HDR Exit Pattern shall be preceded by one additional edge on SCL and/or SDA to set
3679 each line up into the correct state (i.e., “Set Up SDA / Set Up SCL”).
3680 A normal I3C Basic STOP (i.e., SCL being High while SDA rises) always follows the HDR Exit Pattern, as
3681 shown in Figure 69.

3683 Note:
3684 After the fourth SDA falling edge, the Controller shall drive SCL High in order to set up SCL to prepare
3685 for the I3C Basic STOP.
3686 The Controller can also drive the HDR Exit Pattern at any time, specifically to recover the I3C Basic Bus
3687 from certain Target Error Types. Figure 70 shows how the HDR Exit Pattern is initiated when the I3C Basic
3688 Bus is idle.

4.3.10.2.1 HDR Exit Pattern Detector
3690 All I3C Targets shall include an HDR Exit Pattern Detector.
3691 The Target shall enable its HDR Exit Pattern Detector either:
3692 • (A) When any HDR Mode is entered (i.e., when the ENTHDRx CCC is seen), or
3693 • (B) When the Target enters any Target Error state that can be recovered by the HDR Exit Pattern (see
3694 Section 4.3.8.1).
3695 Once the Detector is enabled in these scenarios, the Target shall act on the HDR Exit Pattern once it is seen.
3696 The Detector may be implemented either in digital logic, or in software (Bit Banged).
3697 Note:
3698 The HDR Exit Pattern Detector does not strictly need to be disabled under other circumstances (i.e.,
3699 no HDR Mode entered and not in a Target Error state) or once the HDR Exit Pattern has already
3700 been seen. However, the Target must ensure that the Detector does not initiate any special actions
3701 when an HDR Exit Pattern is not expected to be seen.
3702 The remainder of this Section presents an example digital logic implementation in both schematic view and
3703 in RTL code.
3704 The basic logic model is that SCL is held Low (0), and so SCL High (1) will reset the detector. The Detector
3705 also only uses falling edges of SDA, hence SDA is treated as a clock. An example Detector schematic is
3706 shown in Figure 71.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 169
Public Release Edition

3707
Figure 71 Example HDR Exit Pattern Detector (Schematic)
3708 The Detector uses an inverted version of SDA as a clock (so positive-edge logic, but can use negative-edge
3709 logic), and is reset either when SCL is High (1), or when the block is not enabled (i.e., when the Target should
3710 not act on the HDR Exit Pattern, as defined above). The asynchronous nature of the reset makes this safe,
3711 even in the presence of HDR Ternary Modes which change SCL and SDA at the same time. This is shown in
3712 Figure 72. Due to the nature of SCL vs. SDA changing at the same time with HDR Ternary Modes, the Bus
3713 Target could see SCL change after one SDA and before the next. If the Detector were only using clocking
3714 logic, then it would not see any change at all (SDA posedge would always see SCL Low in this example).
3715 Because the Detector uses an asynchronous reset on SCL, a change in SCL will impact the counter, even in
3716 case (b) above. Note that there will still be approximately 50 ns between SCL and SDA changes. So, as
3717 shown, if SCL rises at any time before the end of the HDR Exit Pattern (as defined above), then the Detector
3718 will be reset, and therefore will not mistakenly signal a false Exit.
SDA SDA
SCL SCL
a) As Emitted by HDR b) As Seen by Target
3719
Figure 72 Metastable Changes on SCL and SDA Do Not Break the Exit Pattern Detector\n--- Page 184 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
3720 An example RTL implementation of the Figure 71 schematic is shown in Listing 1.
3721 Listing 1 Example RTL Code for HDR Exit Pattern Generator
3722 wire scl_rst_n;
3723 wire SDA_clk_n; // ~SDA as clock
3724 reg[3:0] stp_cnt; // HDR STOP counter (shift chain)
3725
3726 assign scl_rst_n = ~iSCL & iIsInHDR; // SCL=1 resets
3727 // next uses glitch free XOR for SDA clock. Only inverts
3728 // SDA as clock if not in scan. Could add a clock mux
3729 // so uses one common clock in scan.
3730 SAFE_CLK_XOR sda_neg_clk(iSDA, ~scan_mode, SDA_clk_n);
3731
3732 // counter for 3 and 4 rising SDA when SCL is High
3733 // (else SCL resets). Whole thing held in reset if
3734 // not in HDR Mode
3735 always @ (posedge SDA_clk_n or negedge scl_rst_n)
3736 if (~scl_rst_n)
3737 stp_cnt <= 4'd0; // SCL High or not HDR
3738 else
3739 stp_cnt <= {stp_cnt[2:0], 1'b1}; // shift chain counter
3740 assign oHDR_STOP = stp_cnt[3]; // detects Exit (STOP)
170 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 185 ---\nVersion 1.2 REQUIRED ELEMENTS Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4.3.11 Electrical Specifications
4.3.11.1 DC I/O Characteristics
3741 This Section describes the DC operating parameters of the I3C Basic interface in two modes: Push-Pull Mode
3742 and Open Drain Mode. In Push-Pull Mode, the SDA pin drives at higher speeds with a totem-pole driver.
3743 Two important parameters should be noted for I3C Basic:
3744 • For typical use cases, the I3C Basic interface targets nominal operating voltages of 1.2 V, 1.8 V,
3745 and 3.3 V or less. The I3C Basic interface is not characterized for 5 V systems, but could be
3746 extended to support 5 V if sufficient driver strength, reduced diameter, and/or reduced speed is
3747 used in the system.
3748 • For peak speeds the capacitance loading allowed is reduced to 50 pF, which is much lower than
3749 Legacy I2C. On I3C Basic, higher capacitance Busses are possible, but only at reduced speeds and
3750 with reduced features (e.g., if no I2C Devices are supported).
3751 The I3C Basic Specification also defines Optional Feature F010: Alternative Electrical Specifications (see
3752 Section 6.11.2.1). This optional feature allows the I3C interface to be used with a nominal operating voltage
3753 of 1.0 V and a 100 pF capacitive loading for new usages such as Serial Presence Detect (SPD) in DDR5.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 171
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 186 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4.3.11.1.1 Typical I3C Basic Use Cases

voltage ranges.

IL IH IL IH
circuits (i.e., ESD protection) as with any GPIO or standard pad Peripheral. The board designer should be made aware of any deviations outside V min and V max.

twice the indicated current, since the rated drive strength is normally the peak current when the voltage difference is at its maximum. For V , this is SDA at V ; for V , this is

4.3.11.1.2 Interoperability with Legacy I²C Targets
3755 I3C Basic supports Legacy I2C Targets. An important feature of an I2C Target is the 50 ns Spike Filter on the
3756 SDA and SCL pads. If a Spike Filter is implemented on all I2C Devices present on the I3C Basic Bus, then
3757 the I3C Basic Bus may operate at maximum rated clock frequency. If any I2C Device does not have a Spike
3758 Filter, then the I3C Basic Bus speed is determined by the slowest Legacy I2C Device without a Spike Filter.
3759 Note:
3760 An I3C Basic Target that also supports Legacy I²C Target behaviors (i.e., that could also function on
3761 an I²C Bus) could also have a Spike Filter that is enabled by default, until and unless the I3C Basic
3762 Target learns that it is actually on an I3C Basic Bus. Refer to Section 4.3.2.1.1 for more details.
3763 Other requirements of an I2C Legacy Target are listed in Table 47.
3764 Table 47 Legacy I2C Device Requirements When Operating on I3C Basic
Feature Required Desirable Not Used Not Allowed Notes
Fm Speed X – – – –
Fm+ Speed – X – – –
HS and UFm – – X – 2
Static I2C Address X – – – –
50 ns Spike Filter – X – – 1
Clock Stretching
– – – X –
by Target
I2C Extended
– – X – 2
Address (10 bit)
I3C Basic
– – – X –
Reserved Address
Note:
1) Lack of Spike Filter will severely degrade Bus performance and eliminate certain I3C Basic Bus
features
2) “Not Used” means that the I3C Basic Controller will not make use of the I2C feature, however if the
Target supports the feature, then it will not interfere with I3C Basic Bus operation.
4.3.11.2 Timing Specification
3765 A key feature of I3C Basic is to maximize the speed of data transfer and minimize the time required for low-
3766 power Devices to remain in non-sleep states. In SDR (Single Data Rate) Mode this is accomplished by
3767 keeping Bus capacitance low and clock speed high. For large packets of data an additional speed
3768 improvement is possible using HDR Modes, where data is transferred on every clock edge. I3C Basic
3769 supports Legacy I2C Fm and Fm+ Modes. Table 48 gives reference timing requirements used in Legacy
3770 Mode.

16-Dec-2024
Note:
1) FSCL = 1 / (tDIG_L + tDIG_H). Frequency is based on the tsco, propagation time, PAD delays, and bus capacitance, all of which should be considered for an effective frequency calculation.
While the maximum sustained frequency is 12.5 MHz, a higher frequency up to 12.9 MHz could be seen due to random jitter or skew in the Controller. However, such a frequency variation
should only occur in short bursts since a frequency higher than 12.5 MHz is not expected to be sustained for longer periods of time and is likely to cause communication errors on the I3C
Basic Bus.
2) tDIG_L and tDIG_H are the clock Low and High periods as seen at the receiver end of the I3C Basic Bus using VIL and VIH (see Figure 74). The tDIG_L, tDIG_H, and tDIG_MIXED minimum values
of 32 ns are provided to allow an extreme 40/60 duty cycle at typical clock frequency of 12.5 MHz.
3) When communicating with an I3C Basic Device on a Mixed Bus, the tDIG_H_MIXED period must be constrained to make sure that I2C Devices do not interpret I3C Basic signaling as valid
I2C signaling. The tHIGH, tLOW, and tHIGH_MIXED minimum values of 24 ns are provided as a safe corner case, in order to allow enough time for processing under extreme conditions.
4) As both edges are used, the hold time needs to be satisfied for the respective edges; i.e., tCF + 3 for falling edge clocks, and tCR + 3 for rising edge clocks.
5) The clock maximum rise/fall time is capped at 60 ns. For lower frequency rise and fall, the maximum value is limited at 60 ns, and is not dependent upon the clock frequency.
6) tHD_PP is a Hold time parameter for Push-Pull Mode that has a different value for Controller mode vs. Target mode. Push-Pull Mode includes both SDR Mode and DDR Mode. In SDR
Mode the Hold time parameter is referred to as tHD_SDR, and in DDR Mode it is referred to as tHD_DDR
7) Devices with more than 20 ns of tSCO delay shall set the limitation bit in the BCR, and shall support the GETMXDS CCC to allow the Controller to read this value and adjust computations
accordingly. For purposes of system design and test conformance, this parameter should be considered together with pad delay, Bus capacitance, propagation delay, and clock triggering
points.
8) Pad delay based on 90 Ω / 4 mA driver and 50 pF load. Note that Controller may be a Target in a Multi-Controller system, and thus shall also adhere to this requirement
9) Targets with speed limitations inform the Controller via the Bus Characteristics Register that the minimum might not be acceptable. As a result, if the given SCL High period is 50 ns or
greater, then the Controller needs to accommodate for Legacy I2C Devices that might see it.
10) This parameter represents the maximum number of Delay bytes that a Target may use during HDR-BT Read transfers, at each opportunity where the Target is unable to transmit or
chooses to delay transmitting a complete Data Block. This is fundamentally different from Stalling the clock. In previous versions of this I3C Basic Specification, the HDR-BT Data Block
Delay mechanism (i.e., the “Stall (delay)” mechanism) was defined with a symbol of tBT_STALL for the maximum number of Delay bytes. This mechanism and its symbol have been renamed
for clarity. See Section 6.4.3.3.4.
Note:
It is not expected (and it would be unrealistic to expect) that all corner conditions will be present concurrently (i.e., that maximum frequency, worst duty cycle, slower edge, etc., would all
occur at the same time).
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 179
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 194 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
3776 As shown in Figure 73, the t SCO delay is the measurement of the total internal delay from the SCL input to
3777 the start of SDA output (see also [MIPI03]), excluding other factors like line capacitance and path delay that
3778 are factored out of the Device computation and put into the broader computation. The t SCO delay is applicable
3779 to both Controller Devices and Target Devices, since Controller Devices may behave as Target Devices in a
3780 Multi-Controller system based on the system configuration.

3781
Figure 73 Components of Clock-to-Data Turnaround Delay (t )
SCO
3782 Any Target Devices with a Clock-to-Data turnaround (t SCO ) delay greater than 20 ns shall set the Clock to
3783 Data Turnaround field of the maxRD Byte to 3'b111 and report this value to the Controller by private
3784 agreement.
3785 Maximum Read Turnaround Time is used to notify the Controller how long to wait before reading the data it
3786 requested. This allows the Controller to get the Target Device’s turnaround delay time for data read requests
3787 (excluding In-Band Interrupt responses). This is useful because some Targets may exhibit data read delays
3788 (as contrasted with CCC reads) due to clock-starting effects, bridging, slower inner processors, etc. The
3789 Controller can use the returned delay factor to avoid NACKs that would result from reading the Device too
3790 soon. The Target may have the data ready before the time reported, and the Controller can also estimate the
3791 time and retry reading before the maximum Read Turnaround time. The Controller should be aware that any
3792 NACKs for read requests from the Target before the maximum time is a normal condition, and no recovery
3793 methods are required; by contrast, NACKs after the maximum Data Turnaround time shall be dealt with
3794 accordingly.

3799 The start of a typical I3C Basic communication in SDR Mode is shown in Figure 76. The initial
3800 communication looks very similar to I2C, with additional commands that follow as described in Section 4.
3801 The key difference is that higher clock speed is supported, up to 12.5 MHz. The higher clock speed allows
3802 Legacy I2C Devices with 50 ns Spike Filters to ignore the communications. The Controller can then run in
3803 SDR Mode for I3C Basic Devices using full 12.5 MHz timing, though it will have to slow down in order to
3804 communicate with Legacy I2C Devices. Figure 76 shows the beginning of a transaction, where the Target
3805 acknowledges its Address.
3806 Figure 77 shows the possible continuation of an I3C Basic SDR communication, in the case where the Target
3807 does not acknowledge its Address. The Controller may either STOP the communication, or else continue with
3808 a Repeated START.\n--- Page 196 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic REQUIRED ELEMENTS Version 1.2

3828 Figure 90 illustrates the I3C Basic timing parameters related to the Spike Filter present on Legacy I2C
3829 Devices. This Spike Filter suppresses pulses shorter than 50 ns.
3830 I3C Basic Mixed Bus clocking rates are chosen to prevent Legacy I2C Devices from seeing I3C Basic Bus
3831 communications, by keeping at least the SCL High periods under 50 ns. To a Legacy I2C Device, SCL will
3832 simply appear to remain Low, i.e., no clock pulses will be received. (Low periods are also generally less than
3833 50 ns, though longer in some cases.)
3834 Figure 90 details how when the slower Open Drain timing (t DIG_H ) is used both I3C Basic Devices (bottom
3835 trace) and Legacy I2C Devices (middle trace) can see its longer periods, and therefore both can see the Bus
3836 traffic. But when I3C Basic’s faster Push-Pull timing (t DIG_H_MIXED ) is used, the shorter periods will only
3837 reach the I3C Basic Devices and not the Legacy I2C Devices, so only I3C Basic Devices can participate on
3838 the Bus. I3C Basic Push-Pull timing is specified in Table 50.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 193

5.1 DCR Byte (Device Configuration Register)
3853 Table 54 DCR Byte Values Status
Last updated in I3C Basic Specification Version I3C Basic v1.2
Is included in I3C Basic Yes
3854 Each I3C Basic Device that is connected to the I3C Basic Bus shall have an associated read-only Device
3855 Characteristics Register (DCR). This read-only register describes the I3C Basic compliant Device type (e.g.,
3856 accelerometer, gyroscope, etc.) for use in Dynamic Address assignment and Common Command Codes. The bits
3857 within the DCR shall conform to the Descriptions presented in Table 8.
3858 MIPI Alliance maintains a public registry of approved and pending DCR value assignments [MIPI02].
3859 Table 55 I3C Basic Device Characteristics Register (DCR)
Bit Name Description
DCR[7] Device ID[7] 255 available codes for describing the type of sensor, or Device.
DCR[6] Device ID[6]
Examples: Accelerometer, gyroscope, composite Devices.
DCR[5] Device ID[5]
DCR[4] Device ID[4] Default value is 8'b0: Generic Device
DCR[3] Device ID[3]
DCR[2] Device ID[2]
DCR[1] Device ID[1]
DCR[0] Device ID[0]
198 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 213 ---\nVersion 1.2 ASSIGNED VALUES Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
5.2 SETBUSCON Context Byte
3860 Table 56 SETBUSCON Context Byte Values Status
Last updated in I3C Basic Specification Version I3C Basic v1.2
Is included in I3C Basic Yes
3861 Table 57 SETBUSCON Context Values
Context
Byte Context Group Context Description
Value
0 None Reserved, do not use
1 – 63 MIPI I3C Basic Bits[7:6]: 2'b00
Specification v1.Y Bit[5]: I3C Basic Specification Editorial Revision (within Minor Version)
Minor Version 1'b0: Version 1.Y.0
1'b1: Version 1.Y.1 or greater
Bit[4]: I3C Specification Family
1'b0: MIPI I3C Specification
1'b1: MIPI I3C Basic Specification
Note: The I3C Basic Specification should be regard as a subset of the
I3C Specification.
Bits[3:0]: I3C Basic Specification Minor Version (v1.Y)
4'b0000: Illegal value, do not use
(It would encode Version 1.0, but the SETBUSCON CC was not
available in I3C v1.0.Z)
4'b0001 – 4’b1111: Version 1.1 – Version 1.15
Examples: Bit[5] Bit[4] Bits[3:0]
I3C v1.1.0: 1'b0 || 1'b0 || 4'b0001 or 8'b00000001
I3C v1.1.1: 1'b1 || 1'b0 || 4'b0001 or 8'b00100001
I3C v1.2.0: 1'b0 || 1'b0 || 4'b0010 or 8'b00000010
I3C Basic v1.1.1: 1'b1 || 1'b1 || 4'b0001 or 8'b00110001
I3C Basic v1.2.0: 1'b0 || 1'b1 || 4'b0010 or 8'b00010010
64 – 127 Other MIPI Working Reserved for higher-level protocols defined by other MIPI Alliance specifications that use
Groups MIPI I3C Basic for communications
128 – Other Standards Reserved for higher-level protocols defined by other standards developing organizations.
191 Organizations The values are assigned by MIPI Alliance I3C WG and published via MIPI’s public registry
at https://www.mipi.org/MIPI_I3C_bus_context_byte_values_public.html [MIPI07]. For details
of each registered value shown below, see the registry.
128: JEDEC
129: MCTP
130: ETSI
131 – 191: Reserved for future assignment
192 – Vendor Custom Available for private, per-vendor use (not tracked by MIPI Alliance)

Is included in I3C Basic Yes

Defining Bytes for this CCC
CCC
Command DB DB Name DB Description
Value
ENTTM 0x00 Exit Test Mode This value removes all I3C Basic Devices from Test Mode.
This value indicates that I3C Basic Devices shall return a random 32-bit
0x01 Vendor Test Mode value in the Provisioned ID during the Dynamic Address Assignment
procedure.
RSTACT 0x00 – No Reset on Target Reset Pattern
0x01 – Reset the I3C Basic Peripheral Only
0x02 – Reset the Whole Target
0x03 – Debug Network Adaptor Reset
0x04 – Virtual Target Detect
0x81 – Return Time to Reset Peripheral
0x82 – Return Time to Reset Whole Target
0x83 – Return Time for Debug Network Adaptor Reset
0x84 – Return Virtual Target Indication

for a given I3C Mode.

The directly addressed Target responds with one byte (see Additional

0x9C Get Group ML Report separate Group ML configurations (i.e., separate ML Frame formats for

GETSTATUS 0x00 TGTSTAT Returns Target Status (standard format).

0x91 PRECR
(i.e., Secondary Controller) per sub-section below.

0xD7 NASTAT
Debug Over I3C.

ENDXFER SET and GET parameters for the Data Transfer Early Termination
0x7F – procedure and Ternary Flow Control for the HDR-TSP and HDR-TSL
protocols (which are not included in I3C Basic).
Commands either all I3C Devices on the Bus, or else only some directly
addressed Devices on the Bus, to begin operating using the Data
0x55 – Transfer Early Termination procedure and Ternary Flow Control (which

0xF7 –
procedure, for the HDR-DDR protocol.

directly addressed Devices on the Bus, to begin operating using the
0xAA –
Data Transfer Early Termination procedure, as previously set up and
confirmed using ENDXFER sub-command 0xF7.
(Not included in I3C Basic) SET and GET whether a Monitoring Device’s
0xFC –
Early Termination capability is enabled vs. disabled.
GETMXDS Describes standard (Target) Write/Read speed parameters, and optional
0x00 –
Maximum Read Turnaround time.
Describes delay parameters for a Controller-capable (i.e., Secondary
0x91 – Controller) Device, and its expected Activity State during the Controller
handoff.

5.4 GETCAPS Capability Bits
3864 Table 60 GETCAPS Capability Bit Values Status
Last updated in I3C Basic Specification Version I3C Basic v1.2
Is included in I3C Basic Yes
3865 This Direct CCC allows the Controller to query an I3C Basic Target to determine what optional I3C Basic features
3866 that Target supports. It replaces the GETHDRCAP CCC, which was defined in version 1.0 of the I3C Basic
3867 Specification.
3868 It has two formats:
3869 • For GETCAPS Format 1, the I3C Basic Target may return the first 2, 3, or all 4 of the GETCAP1 through
3870 GETCAP4 Bytes shown in Figure 91. Fields within each GETCAPx Byte are detailed in Table 61 through
3871 Table 64.
3872 • The optional GETCAPS Format 2 adds a Defining Byte and returns a variable number of data bytes. The
3873 number and format of data bytes returned depends upon the Defining Byte value. GETCAPS Format 2 is
3874 detailed below.

Figure 91 GETCAPS Format 1
3876 Note:
3877 An I3C Basic Controller that supports I3C Basic version 1.1 or greater might be used with an I3C Basic Target
3878 Device that supports version 1.0 of the I3C Basic Specification. In this case, such an I3C Basic Target might
3879 only support the previous GETHDRCAP CCC format, and as a result might only return one byte (i.e., only the
3880 GETCAP1 Byte) as a response to the GETCAPS CCC. Therefore, the I3C Basic Controller must recognize
3881 this as a valid configuration, even though the I3C Basic Target would return just a single byte and would have
3882 limited capabilities. However, all I3C Basic Targets that support I3C Basic version 1.1 or greater shall return
3883 at least the first 2 bytes as a response to the GETCAPS CCC.\n--- Page 218 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic ASSIGNED VALUES Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024

Bit 7 Bit 6 Bit 5 Bit 4 Bit 3 Bit 2 Bit 1 Bit 0
HDR Mode 2: HDR Mode 1:
HDR Mode 7 HDR Mode 6 HDR Mode 5 HDR Mode 4 HDR Mode 3: HDR-TSL HDR-TSP HDR Mode 0:
Reserved Reserved Reserved Reserved HDR-BT Not included Not included HDR-DDR
in I3C Basic in I3C Basic
3885 Table 62 GETCAP2 Byte Format
Bits Field Description
7 HDR-DDR Abort CRC Is this I3C Target capable of emitting the CRC Word when a transaction in HDR-DDR Mode is aborted?
0: No
1: Yes
6 HDR-DDR Write Abort Is this I3C Target capable of issuing the Write Abort in HDR-DDR Mode?
0: No
1: Yes
5:4 Group Address Capabilities This 2-bit integer indicates the Group Address function capabilities of this I3C Basic Device.
Values:
0: Does not support Group Address function
1: Can be assigned one Group Address
2: Can be assigned two Group Addresses
3: Can be assigned three or more Group Addresses
3:0 I3C Basic 1.x Specification This 4-bit integer indicates the minor version number of the MIPI I3C Basic Specification with which this I3C
Version Basic v1.x Device complies. That is, the ‘x’ in “I3C Basic v1.x”.
Examples:
If Bits[3:0] are set to 4'b0001 (decimal 1), then the Device complies with I3C Basic v1.1.1, etc.
If Bits[3:0] are set to 4'b0010 (decimal 2), then the Device complies with I3C Basic v1.2
Note:
An I3C Device that complies with the full I3C Specification shall use the same encoding to indicate the
version number of the full I3C Specification to which it complies.
Note:
Bits[3:0] set to 4’b0000 is an illegal value.

3886 Table 63 GETCAP3 Byte Format
Bit Field Description
7 Timing Control Feature Does this I3C Target support at least one Timing Control mode? (See Note below table.)
Support 0: No 1: Yes
6 IBI MDB Support for Pending Does this I3C Basic Target expect that it can send In-Band Interrupt requests with a specific range of
Read Notification Mandatory Data Byte values to indicate a Pending Read Notification, which the Controller shall then follow
with a Private Read request to fetch the data? (See Section 4.3.6.2.2.)
0: No 1: Yes
5 HDR-BT CRC-32 Support Does this I3C Target support CRC-32 data integrity verification in HDR Bulk Transport Mode?
(See Section 6.4.3.3.)
0: No 1: Yes
4 Defining Byte Support in Does this I3C Basic Target support an optional Defining Byte for the GETSTATUS CCC?
GETSTATUS 0: No 1: Yes
3 Defining Byte Support in Does this I3C Basic Target support an optional Defining Byte for this CCC (GETCAPS)?
GETCAPS 0: No 1: Yes
2 Device to Device Transfer These capabilities are not included in the I3C Basic Specification. To gain access to them, please contact
(D2DXFER) IBI Capable MIPI Alliance.
1 Device to Device Transfer
(D2DXFER) Support
0 Multi-Lane (ML) Data Transfer Does this I3C Target support Multi-Lane (ML) Data Transfer? (See Section 6.7.3.6 and Section 6.7.)
Support 0: No 1: Yes
3887 Note:
3888 In I3C Basic v1.2 and above: Bit 7 indicates whether the Timing Control feature is supported. This allows the Controller to determine whether to expect
3889 an ACK response to the Direct SETXTIME and Direct GETXTIME CCCs.
3890 In I3C Basic v1.1.1 and below: Bit 7 was Reserved. For interoperability with legacy I3C Basic Devices, this field should be cross-checked with
3891 GETCAP2[3:0] (I3C Basic 1.x Specification Version). For example, if GETCAP2[3:0] indicates that v1.1.1 is supported, then the status information returned
3892 in Bit GETCAP3[7] shall be disregarded.
3893 In order to obtain the full view of what Timing Control modes are supported, the Controller should follow up with the GETXTIME command:
3894 For Targets supporting I3C v1.2 and above, this can be limited to Devices with GETCAP3[7] set to 1'b1.
3895 For Targets supporting I3C v1.1.1 and below, all Devices must be queried with speculative GETXTIME CCC (i.e., the ACK vs. NACK response is used
3896 as a method to inform the Controller about whether the Timing Control feature is supported).

5.4.1 GETCAPS Format 2
3898 The optional GETCAPS Format 2 adds a Defining Byte and returns a variable number of data bytes. The number and format of data bytes returned depends upon
3899 the Defining Byte value. Before using GETCAPS Format 2 for a given Target, a Controller shall determine whether that Target supports it.
3900 GETCAPS Format 2 conforms to the CCC Direct General Frame Format (Figure 20):
3901 A Target that supports the GETCAPS Format 2 CCC shall return a value of 1'b1 in bit 4 of Byte 3 (GETCAP3), when the GETCAPS CCC is sent without a Defining
3902 Byte. A Controller that sees that bit set to 1'b1 will know that the Target is capable of supporting at least one Defining Byte with the GETCAPS Format 2 CCC.
