5.4.2 Read Fixed Test Pattern (GETCAPS Format 2, Defining Byte TESTPAT 0x5A)
3905 This Direct CCC allows the Active Controller to request a Device to return a fixed 32-bit test pattern. This pattern may be used by a Controller to test signal integrity
3906 of the I3C Basic Bus or perform speed testing on the I3C Basic Bus.
3907 All Debug-capable Devices should support this test pattern and return it when addressed by the GETCAPS Format 2 CCC with Defining Byte TESTPAT in Target
3908 mode. Any Target Devices that share the same Bus as a Debug-capable Device should also support this test pattern.
3909 A Device that supports this Defining Byte shall return a fixed 32-bit test pattern, consisting of the value 0xA55AA55A. The Active Controller may terminate the
3910 read on each byte boundary. A Device that does not support this test pattern shall NACK its Target Address.
3911 Additional Defining Bytes that return other test patterns may be defined at the discretion of the implementer, in order to perform more advanced signal integrity or
3912 speed testing procedures.

5.4.3 Get Controller Feature Capabilities (GETCAPS Format 2, Defining Byte CRCAPS 0x91)
3915 This Direct CCC allows the Active Controller to query a Controller-capable I3C Device (i.e., one that is currently acting as a Secondary Controller), to read what
3916 optional Controller features and capabilities that Device supports. The Active Controller can use this information to decide whether to ACK or NACK a Controller
3917 Role Request (CRR) from this Controller-capable Device, in the event that errors or undesirable situations arise from any mismatch of configuration or expectations.
3918 All Controller-capable Devices should support the GETCAPS Format 2 CCC (with Defining Byte) in Target mode. A Device shall support the GETCAPS Format
3919 2 CCC (with Defining Byte) if it does not follow the default capability settings or has limitations on its capabilities while operating as Active Controller.
3920 Per Figure 94, a Controller-capable Device shall return either the CRCAP1 data byte only or both CRCAP1 and CRCAP2, in that order. The Active Controller
3921 may terminate the read on each byte boundary. If only the one CRCAP1 data byte is returned, then the Active Controller may assume that the default values for the
3922 CRCAP2 data byte applyCRCAP2.
3923 A Device that is not Controller-capable shall NACK its Target Address.
3924 Fields within the CRCAP1 and CRCAP2 data bytes indicate various Controller features and capabilities, as detailed in Table 67 and Table 68, respectively.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 209

3926 Table 67 CRCAP1 Byte Format
Bits Field Description
7:3 Reserved Reserved for future definition by MIPI Alliance I3C WG
2 Multi-Lane Support 1'b1: This Device may use the MLANE CCC to change the ML configuration of other I3C Targets (Section 6.7.3.6).
1'b0: This Device shall not use the MLANE CCC to change the ML configuration of other I3C Targets.
1 Group Management Support 1'b1: This Device may support Group Address handoff or management capabilities and shall use the DEFGRPA CCC
to announce the current group membership data after making any changes.
1'b0: This Device does not support Group Address capabilities. It shall not use the SETGRPA or RSTGRPA CCCs. It
will ignore any DEFGRPA CCC Broadcasts while it is not Active Controller, and it shall not send DEFGRPA
CCC Broadcasts before a Controller Role handoff to another Controller-capable Device.
0 Hot-Join Support 1'b1: This Device may support Hot-Join and will ACK an I3C Target Hot-Join Request while it is Active Controller. It
shall support DAA and also send the DEFTGTS CCC to announce the presence of newly joined I3C Targets to
other Controller-capable Devices.
1'b0: This Device does not support Hot-Join, and will NACK any I3C Target Hot-Join Requests. It may also send
DISEC CCC with the DISHJ bit set to disable Hot-Join Requests.
3927 Table 68 CRCAP2 Byte Format
Bits Field Description
7:4 Reserved Reserved for future definition by MIPI Alliance I3C WG
3 Delayed Controller 1'b1: This Device may need additional time to process Broadcast CCC data sent from the Active Controller, before it can
Handoff successfully complete the Controller Role Handoff procedure and accept the Controller Role:
1'b0: This Device does not need additional time to process Broadcast CCC data sent from the Active Controller.
2 Deep Sleep Capable 1'b1: This Device may enter a deep sleep state during which it may miss some Broadcast DEFTGTS and DEFGRPA
CCCs sent by the Active Controller. see Section 4.3.7.3.15).
1'b0: This Device shall not enter a deep sleep state.
1 Controller Pass-Back 1'b1: This Device shall automatically pass the Controller Role back to the former Active Controller from which it received
its Controller Role. It shall do so via the GETACCCR CCC, once it has finished performing any tasks that require
it to request and gain the Controller Role.
1'b0: This Device shall not automatically pass the Controller Role back to the former Active Controller.
0 In-Band Interrupt Support 1'b1: This Device may choose to accept an IBI request from any I3C Basic Target, when operating as Active Controller. It
may selectively or globally enable interrupts upon receiving the Controller Role, and shall disable interrupts
before passing the Controller Role on to another Controller-capable Device.
1'b0: This Device shall not accept any IBI requests, and will respond with NACK.
210 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 225 ---\nVersion 1.2 ASSIGNED VALUES Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
5.4.4 Get Debug Feature Capabilities (GETCAPS Format 2, Defining Byte 0xD7 DBGCAPS)
3928 This Direct CCC allows the Active Controller to query a Debug-capable I3C Basic Device, to read the version of the MIPI Debug Over I3C specification supported
3929 as well as the optional Debug features and capabilities that Device supports. An Active Controller can use this information to determine which I3C Basic Devices
3930 on the Bus support the Debug features and capabilities defined in the MIPI Debug Over I3C Specification [MIPI04].
3931 All Debug-capable Devices should support the GETCAPS Format 2 CCC (with Defining Byte) in Target mode.
3932 Per Figure 95, a Debug-capable Device shall return at least one data byte with additional data bytes depending on the Debug features and capabilities of the Device.
3933 The data bytes shall be returned in a defined order, depending on the MIPI Debug Over I3C version, per Table 69. The meaning and formatting of the data bytes is
3934 fully described in Section 6 of the MIPI Debug Over I3C Specification [MIPI04].
3935 The Active Controller may terminate the read on each byte boundary. The Active Controller shall not make any assumptions about the presence of any Debug
3936 features or capabilities, beyond the contents of any data bytes received from the Device.
3937 A Device that is not Debug-capable shall NACK its Target Address.

5.4.5 Get Virtual Target Capabilities (GETCAPS Format 2, Defining Byte 0x93 VTCAPS)
3940 This Direct CCC allows the Active Controller to query a Virtual Target capable I3C Basic Device, to read what optional features and capabilities that Device
3941 supports. The Active Controller can use this information to fully configure the Bus, and to enable advanced applications using Virtual Targets with multiple Dynamic
3942 Addresses in a single Device.
3943 All Devices that report Virtual Target capabilities should support this CCC. For additional information on Virtual Target capabilities, refer to the MIPI Alliance I3C
3944 Application Note: Virtual Devices and Virtual Targets [MIPI10].
3945 Per Figure 96, a Virtual Target capable Device shall return either the VTCAP1 data byte only, or both VTCAP1 and VTCAP2, in that order. The Active Controller
3946 may terminate the read on each byte boundary. If only the one VTCAP1 data byte is returned, then the Active Controller may assume that the default values for the
3947 VTCAP2 data byte apply.
3948 Fields within the VTCAP1 and VTCAP2 data bytes indicate various Virtual Target features and capabilities. Table 70 covers the features and capabilities dealing
3949 with the Device’s configuration. Table 71 advertises how the Device handles any downstream Virtual Targets or other Devices, if those are supported.
3950 A Device that is not Virtual Target capable shall NACK its Target Address.

3952 Table 70 VTCAP1 Byte Format
Bits Field Description
7:6 Reserved Reserved for future definition by MIPI Alliance I3C WG
5 Shared Peripheral Detect 1'b0: Device does not use shared Peripheral logic.
1'b1: Device uses shared Peripheral logic and supports the Virtual Target Detect operation as a Defining Byte for the
RSTACT CCC (see Section 4.3.7.3.23). Required for Virtual Target type 3'd5; Recommended for other Virtual
Target types. See also Section 5.2.2 in MIPI Alliance I3C Application Note: Virtual Devices and Virtual Targets
[MIPI10].
4 Side Effects 1'b0: No impact on other Virtual Targets supported by this Device, if sending CCCs to reconfigure this Target’s
Dynamic Address.
1'b1: Configuration CCCs sent to this Virtual Target’s Dynamic Address may impact other Virtual Targets that are
connected to or exposed by this Device.
3 Reserved Reserved for future definition by MIPI Alliance I3C WG
2:0 Virtual Target Type Values:
3'd0: This Device does not support or declare any Virtual Target capabilities.
3'd1: This Device is a Bridge Device, and uses the SETBRGTGT CCC (Section 4.3.7.3.17) to configure its
virtualized (bridged) Targets.
3'd2: This Device is a Bridge Device, but does not require the use of the SETBRGTGT CCC; the Device will set up
its own bridged Targets.
3'd3: This Device is a Routing Device, and uses the SETROUTE CCC (Section 4.3.7.3.20) to configure its Routes to
one or more I3C Basic Buses.
3'd4: This Device is a mixed-function Debug Virtual Target, which uses a single Target Address to access both Debug
functionality and application functionality.
3'd5: This Device supports multiple Virtual Target Addresses with shared Peripheral logic.
3'd6: This Device is a Hub that supports pass-through transactions to other Target Devices.
3'd7: Reserved
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 213
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 228 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic ASSIGNED VALUES Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
3953 Table 71 VTCAP2 Byte Format
Bits Field Description
7:5 Reserved Reserved for future definition by MIPI Alliance I3C WG
4:3 Bus Context and Conditions 2'd0: Device manages all downstream Device configuration automatically.
2'd1: Device will re-broadcast any context and event enable/disable commands to all downstream Devices when
Valid for Virtual Target types received from the Active Controller. Device can request Active Controller to send a fresh configuration.
3'd1, 3'd2, and 3'd3 only. 2'd2: Device will cache previously Broadcast context and event enable/disable configuration and use that to
configure all new downstream Devices that join its downstream Bus segments.
2'd3: Reserved
2 Address Remapping 1'b0: Device will pass addresses of downstream Devices as they are assigned.
1'b1: Device will transparently re-map addresses of downstream Devices to mask them from this Bus.
Valid for Virtual Target types
3'd1, 3'd2, and 3'd3 only.
1:0 Interrupt Requests Values:
2'd0: Device will not coalesce or route interrupt requests from any downstream Devices.
Valid for Virtual Target types 2'd1: Device must coalesce interrupt requests from downstream Devices, using this Target Address.
3'd1, 3'd2, and 3'd3 only. 2'd2: Device must route interrupt requests from downstream Devices using their Virtual Target Addresses.
2'd3: Device may do either (i.e., coalesce and/or route) when forwarding interrupts from downstream Devices.

16-Dec-2024
5.5 In-Band Interrupt MDB (Mandatory Data Byte)
3954 Table 72 In-Band Interrupt MDB Values Status
Last updated in I3C Basic Specification Version I3C Basic v1.2
Is included in I3C Basic Yes
3955 The Mandatory Data Byte of the IBI payload is the data that follows the Dynamic Address when a Device sends an
3956 IBI request. The availability of a Mandatory Data Byte, together with the payload information that follows it, is
3957 indicated by bit BCR[2] in the Bus Characteristics Register (see Section 4.3.1.2.1).
3958 It is called a Mandatory Data Byte because the Target Device takes over the line when the Controller ACKs the IBI
3959 request and the Target continues to send data; the Controller cannot decline the reception of the first byte, and must
3960 wait for the T-Bit to terminate any subsequent data.
3961 The Mandatory Data Byte provides the Controller additional information about the event that has happened, and is
3962 divided in two fields as shown in Table 13:
3963 • Interrupt Group Identifier: The three most significant bits: MDB[7:5]
3964 Values for the Interrupt Group Identifier are defined by MIPI Alliance I3C WG for different use
3965 cases.
3966 • Specific Interrupt Identifier: The five least significant bits: MDB[4:0]
3967 Example: D2DT is a MIPI Alliance I3C WG function. It uses the value 3'b001 for the Interrupt Group Identifier field,
3968 and the value 5'b10111 (5'h17) for the Specific Interrupt Identifier field.
3969 MIPI Alliance maintains a web-based registry of defined MDB values [MIPI05].
3970 Table 73 Mandatory Data Byte Field Format
Interrupt Group Identifier Field Specific Interrupt Identifier Field
MDB[7] MDB[6] MDB[5] MDB[4] MDB[3] MDB[2] MDB[1] MDB[0]

6 Optional Features
3972 Section 6 specifies all I3C Device Optional Features (OFs) defined by MIPI Alliance. Every defined OF is
3973 specified independently in a separate sub-section of Section 6, each with an independent version identifier
3974 and an independent change summary.
3975 An I3C Device may optionally implement zero or more of the Optional Features (OFs) specified in Section 6.
3976 An I3C Device could implement none of the OFs specified in Section 6, yet still be a complete, functioning
3977 I3C Device.
3978 Note:
3979 Some OFs are not technically compatible with one or more other OFs. This is noted in the table at
3980 the start of each OF section.
3981 Optional Normative: The OF specifications in Section 6 are Optional Normative. This means that although
3982 the decision of whether or not to implement any given OF is left to the I3C Device implementer, if a given
3983 OF is implemented then it shall conform to the specification given in Section 6.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 219
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 234 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURES Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024

6.1 HDR Modes
3984 This Section describes the flows and framings that are common to optional HDR Modes.
6.1.1 Technical Overview (Informative)
3985 These HDR Modes are designed to transfer more data at the same Bus frequency:
3986 • HDR Double Data Rate (HDR-DDR) Mode: see Section 6.2
3987 • HDR Bulk Transport (HDR-BT) Mode: see Section 6.4
3988 Note:
3989 For information, the full I3C Specification also includes two additional HDR Modes using Ternary
3990 encoding (see Section 6.3) :
3991 • NOT INCLUDED IN I3C BASIC: HDR Ternary Symbol Pure-bus (HDR-TSP) Mode
3992 • NOT INCLUDED IN I3C BASIC: HDR Ternary Symbol Legacy-inclusive-bus (HDR-TSL) Mode
3993 Note:
3994 It is important to note that the I3C Bus is always initialized and configured in SDR Mode, never in any
3995 of the HDR Modes. The essential basic I3C specifications are found in Section 4.
3996 Details common to all HDR Modes are detailed in this Section and in Section 4.3.10.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 221
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 236 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURES: HDR MODES Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.1.2 HDR Restart Pattern
3997 As an alternative to the HDR Exit Pattern, the HDR Restart Pattern is also available. The HDR Restart Pattern
3998 allows multiple Messages to be sent while in HDR Mode, without forcing intervening exits to SDR Mode.
3999 All I3C Targets shall detect and respond to the HDR Restart Pattern while operating in any HDR Mode that
4000 the Target supports. The HDR Restart Pattern Detector is specified in Section 6.1.2. Note that unlike the HDR
4001 Exit Pattern, the HDR Restart Pattern is only detected by I3C Targets that support the current HDR Mode.
4002 The HDR Restart Pattern is based on a subset of the HDR Exit Pattern. It is defined thus:
4003 • SDA starts High, SCL starts Low (same as HDR Exit Pattern)
4004 • SDA toggles 4 times (fall, rise, fall, rise)
4005 • The next edge is SCL rising. SDA may change with SCL rising, but SCL shall rise.
4006 • Each transition on SDA and/or SCL is separated by a time interval of at least t DIG_H (see
4007 Section 4.3.11.2)
4008 Figure 97 illustrates an HDR Restart Pattern (with edges to set SCL and SDA up into correct state) along
4009 with the necessary SCL ending edge.

Figure 97 HDR Restart with Next Edge
4011 Note:
4012 After the necessary SCL ending edge, the I3C Bus remains in that HDR Mode, and the Controller
4013 typically follows by driving SCL Low to complete the SCL pulse, which signals the start of the next
4014 HDR Mode transfer. This begins the first structured protocol element (i.e., a Command or Header)
4015 for the HDR Mode framing, per Section 6.1.4.2.\n--- Page 237 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURES: HDR MODES Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
6.1.2.1 HDR Restart and Exit Pattern Detector
4016 Any I3C Target that supports at least one HDR Mode shall include HDR Restart Pattern detection. While this
4017 function is easily incorporated into the required HDR Exit Pattern Detector, or may be part of HDR Mode
4018 support, the particular design is not mandated by this Specification (i.e., is up to the manufacturer). The HDR
4019 Restart Pattern Detector may be implemented either in digital logic or in software (Bit Banged).
4020 The remainder of this Section presents an example digital logic implementation in both schematic view and
4021 in RTL code, building upon the HDR Exit Pattern Detector presented in Section 4.3.10.2.1.
4022 The basic logic model is that SCL is held Low (0) and so SCL High (1) will reset the main detector. It also
4023 uses falling edges of SDA primarily, hence treats SDA as a clock. The HDR Restart Pattern is then detected
4024 only when two falling edges have been seen, and verifies the rising edge and then SCL change that is required
4025 for an HDR Restart.
4026 The combined HDR Restart and Exit Pattern Detector schematic is shown in Figure 98.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 223
Public Release Edition

4027
Figure 98 Combined HDR Restart and Exit Pattern Detector (Schematic)
4028 This Detector design builds on the HDR Exit Pattern Detector. After exactly two SDA falling edges are seen,
4029 followed by a rising edge, the HDR Restart Pattern Detector checks for the rising edge of SCL. It does not
4030 matter whether SDA also falls at the same time; the rising SCL is the key. This works because even if SDA
4031 were falling (and therefore triggering the next flop in the HDR Exit Pattern Detector), the upper-left flop will
4032 still hold 1 because it has not yet seen a rising edge on SDA.
4033 Note:
4034 Unlike the HDR Exit Pattern Detector, the HDR Restart Pattern Detector is only enabled when the
4035 Target is currently in a supported HDR Mode, and not when the Target has entered a Target Error
4036 state (i.e., one that can be recovered by the HDR Exit Pattern).
4037 An example RTL implementation of the Figure 98 schematic is shown in Listing 2.\n--- Page 238 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURES: HDR MODES Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4038 Listing 2 Example RTL Code for Combined HDR Pattern Detector: Exit and Reset
4039 wire scl_rst_n;
4040 wire restart_rst_n;
4041 wire SDA_clk_n; // ~SDA as clock
4042 reg[3:0] stp_cnt; // HDR STOP counter (shift chain)
4043 reg poss_restart; // possible restart
4044 reg is_restart;
4045
4046 assign scl_rst_n = ~iSCL & iIsInHDR; // SCL=1 resets
4047 assign restart_rst_n = ~iRestartDone & iIsInHDR;
4048
4049 // next uses glitch free XOR for SDA clock. Only inverts
4050 // SDA as clock if not in scan. Could add a clock mux
4051 // so uses one common clock in scan.
4052 SAFE_CLK_XOR sda_neg_clk(iSDA, ~scan_mode, SDA_clk_n);
4053
4054 // counter for 3 and 4 rising SDA when SCL is High
4055 // (else SCL resets). Whole thing held in reset if
4056 // not in HDR Mode
4057 always @ (posedge SDA_clk_n or negedge scl_rst_n)
4058 if (~scl_rst_n)
4059 stp_cnt <= 4'd0; // SCL High or not HDR
4060 else
4061 stp_cnt <= {stp_cnt[2:0], 1'b1}; // shift chain counter
4062
4063 assign oHDR_STOP = stp_cnt[3];// detects Exit (STOP)
4064
4065 // Possible Restart means exactly 2 falling edges
4066 // of SDA and then rising edge of SDA. Actual
4067 // Restart is from SCL then rising
4068 always @ (posedge iSDA or negedge restart_rst_n)
4069 if (~restart_rst_n)
4070 poss_restart <= 1'b0; // Restart ACK or not HDR
4071 else if (stp_cnt[1] & ~stp_cnt[2]) // after 2nd fall only
4072 poss_restart <= 1'b1; // SDA rise after 2 falls
4073 else
4074 poss_restart <= 1'b0; // else not possible restart
4075
4076 always @ (posedge SCL or negedge restart_rst_n)
4077 if (~restart_rst_n)
4078 is_restart <= 1'b0; // Restart ACK or not HDR
4079 else if (poss_restart)
4080 is_restart <= 1'b1; // SCL rises after SDA does
4081 else
4082 is_restart <= 1'b0; // else not restart
224 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 239 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURES: HDR MODES Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
6.1.3 CCC Framing in HDR Modes
4083 Special framings are used for transmitting Common Command Codes in HDR Modes. CCCs that are
4084 supported in any HDR Modes may be sent by an Active Controller, so long as it has entered the HDR Mode
4085 using the Enter HDR Mode CCC (see CCCs Enter HDR Mode 0 through 7, Section 4.3.7.3.9). Both
4086 Broadcast and Direct CCCs are supported, with framing details specific to each HDR Mode. As noted in
4087 Table 75, not all CCCs may be used in HDR Modes. Additionally, support for any CCCs in any particular
4088 HDR Mode for a Target is optional. Target Devices may implement support for some or all of the Command
4089 Codes that per Table 75 may be used in HDR Modes, as well as other Command Codes reserved for use by
4090 a particular MIPI Alliance Working Group or set aside for Vendor Extensions. Generally, Target Devices may
4091 implement support in HDR Modes for a subset of all permitted Command Codes that they might support in
4092 SDR Mode.
4093 The Controller shall know which Targets are active and support a particular HDR Mode, in order to determine
4094 whether a given Target will understand any Broadcast or Direct CCCs sent within an active HDR Mode. The
4095 Controller shall also determine which Targets support I3C v1.1 or later, and if so, which of them support a
4096 CCC within that HDR Mode.
4097 • For Targets that support I3C v1.1.1 or earlier: The Controller should have a priori knowledge,
4098 otherwise it may determine this by attempting to receive an acknowledgement of a Direct CCC in a
4099 manner specific to the HDR Mode (see Section 6.1.3.3).
4100 • For Targets that support I3C v1.2 or later: The Controller should use the GETCAP4 byte in the
4101 Target’s response to the GETCAPS Format 1 CCC to determine whether that Target supports CCCs
4102 in a given HDR Mode.
4103 For any Targets that do not support a given CCC in that HDR Mode, the Controller may need to re-send that
4104 CCC in another supported HDR Mode, or in SDR Mode.
4105 Table 75 summarizes which CCCs are permitted in HDR Modes, noting any per-HDR-Mode limitations and
4106 other advisory information.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 225

4109 Table 76 CCCs Not Permitted in HDR Modes
CCC Type Command
Reason
Broadcast Direct Name
X X RSTDAA Target Dynamic Address changes are a fundamental configuration change, which
should be done in SDR Mode only.
X – ENTDAA Dynamic Address Assignment can only be performed in SDR Mode.
X – ENTTM Test Mode can only be performed in SDR Mode. Test Mode is generally only used
for manufacturing or Device test.
X – ENTHDR0 HDR Modes can only be entered from SDR Mode. There is no provision for switching
through directly to a different HDR Mode, it is necessary to exit to SDR Mode first.

X – SETAASA Dynamic Address Assignment can be done in SDR Mode only. In addition,

which always takes place in SDR Mode.
– X SETDASA Dynamic Address Assignment can be done in SDR Mode only. In addition,
SETDASA (if supported) is typically used only once per Target, during Bus
Initialization which always takes place in SDR Mode.
– X SETNEWDA Target Dynamic Address changes are a fundamental configuration change, which
should be done in SDR Mode only.
– X GETPID Usually only associated with the ENTDAA CCC, or when a Secondary Controller that
was not present during Bus Initialization becomes Active Controller (pursuant to a
GETACCCR CCC) and requests information about a Target Device.
– X GETACCCR The Controller Handoff procedure is only supported in SDR Mode.

6.1.3.1 Elements, Framing and Modality
4110 Within each HDR Mode, the framing for CCC flows differs from other HDR read/write transactions,
4111 providing a modality similar to the way CCCs are framed in SDR Mode.
4112 This Specification defines a number of common Flow Elements (such as Word and Blocks) that together
4113 provide a generic framing model that can be used to describe the CCC flows for each individual HDR Mode.
4114 The following Flow Elements are defined, where ‘x’ represents an HDR Mode:
4115 • HDR-x CCC Header Block
4116 This Block has two variants:
4117 • Type Indicator
4118 • Type Selector
4119 • HDR-x Header Block
4120 This Block signals the end of a CCC Frame for the particular HDR Mode. It is neither an Indicator
4121 nor a Selector.
4122 • HDR-x CCC Data Block
4123 This Block has three variants:
4124 • When sent by the Controller as part of a Broadcast CCC flow
4125 • When sent by the Controller as part of a Direct Write or Direct SET CCC flow (i.e., in a Write
4126 Segment)
4127 • When sent by an addressed Target as part of a Direct Read or Direct GET CCC flow (i.e., in a
4128 Read Segment)
4129 • HDR-x CCC Termination Marker
4130 This marker is sent only as needed, i.e., is optional per the particular HDR Mode and per the
4131 position in a type of CCC flow.
4132 All of the above Flow Elements are common CCC Flow Elements, except the Write Segment and Read
4133 Segment variants of HDR-x CCC Data Block, and specific variants of the termination marker (optional). All
4134 Target Devices that support CCCs in an HDR Mode must be able to successfully receive and understand
4135 these common CCC Flow Elements, in order to track their place within the CCC flows, to know when to
4136 enter the modality (and to know when it has been ended), and to understand when they are being specifically
4137 addressed in a Direct CCC flow.
4138 The beginning of a CCC in an HDR Mode is indicated by use of an HDR-x CCC Header Block of type
4139 Indicator. This is an HDR-x Header Block containing a special byte value (i.e., the Broadcast Address, 7'h7E)
4140 instead of a Target Address. This is different from the beginning of other HDR transactions.
4141 The format of the Indicator HDR-x CCC Header Block is specified separately for each HDR Mode, but
4142 generally includes the Indicator, the Command Code, and the optional Defining Byte. If the HDR-x CCC
4143 Header Block includes a bitfield for the Defining Byte that is always present, then for CCCs that support an
4144 optional Defining Byte, a Defining Byte value of 8'h0 shall be equivalent to omitting the Defining Byte. This
4145 shall have the same effect as omitting the optional Defining Byte in SDR Mode for the same CCC.
4146 For Broadcast CCCs, the Controller shall send the HDR-x CCC Header Block of type ‘Indicator’, containing
4147 the Command Code and optional Defining Byte, followed by one or more HDR-x CCC Data Blocks,
4148 containing the data bytes to be Broadcast to all supported Target Devices.
4149 For Direct CCCs, the Controller shall send the HDR-x CCC Header Block of type ‘Indicator’, containing
4150 the Command Code and optional Defining Byte. The Controller shall then send the HDR Restart Pattern, and
4151 then start a new Segment by sending another HDR-x CCC Header Block, but of Selector type: it includes a
4152 Target Address and indicates whether the Direct CCC Segment is a Write Segment vs. a Read Segment.
4153 • For a Direct Write or Direct SET CCC with data, the Controller generally sends one or more HDR-
4154 x CCC Data Blocks if the selected CCC’s defined format so indicates. However, if the CCC’s
4155 defined format includes no data to be sent to the Target (i.e., if a zero-byte ‘Write’ to the Target for
228 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 243 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURES: HDR MODES Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4156 a given Command Code and optional Defining Byte is sufficient to instruct the Target to take some
4157 action), then the Controller would send the minimum number of HDR-x CCC Data Blocks for the
4158 HDR Mode’s framing. In some cases, this might be zero Data Blocks, if the HDR Mode permits.
4159 • If the Direct Write or Direct SET CCC requires a Sub-Command Byte, then the Controller
4160 shall send this Sub-Command Byte as the first byte in the Write Segment’s data message,
4161 which is the first HDR-x CCC Data Block.
4162 • For a Direct Read or Direct GET CCC, the Controller shall allow the addressed Target to control
4163 the Bus, and the Target may send one or more HDR-x CCC Data Blocks if the selected CCC’s
4164 defined format so indicates. After the end of the Segment, the Controller shall resume control.
4165 For either Broadcast or Direct CCCs, the Controller and the Targets shall observe all HDR Mode specific
4166 framing requirements:
4167 • For zero-byte CCCs or very short CCCs, if a minimum number of HDR-x CCC Data Blocks must
4168 be sent for the HDR Mode’s framing, then the sender shall provide dummy byte values of 0x00 in
4169 these Data Blocks, and the receiver shall correctly ignore these values, per the specific CCC
4170 definition.
4171 • If the length of a CCC’s data message is not evenly aligned to the size of the HDR-x CCC Data
4172 Block, then the sender shall pad the end of the data message with dummy byte values of 0x00, and
4173 the receiver shall correctly ignore any dummy byte values, per the specific CCC definition.
4174 To end either a Broadcast CCC or a Direct CCC, the Controller shall perform any HDR-x CCC End Procedure
4175 that is valid for that HDR Mode.
4176 As part of the data message that is either sent by the Controller (i.e., as part of a Broadcast CCC or a Write
4177 Segment for a Direct CCC), or sent by a Target (i.e., as part of a Read Segment for a Direct CCC), the sender
4178 shall denote the end of the data message by including an appropriately-constructed HDR-x CCC Termination
4179 Marker in a format specific for the particular HDR Mode and transaction type. The Controller shall also
4180 include a Termination Marker, at the end of the Selector type HDR-x CCC Header Block that begins the
4181 framing for a Direct CCC, before starting any Read or Write Segments. In both cases, this Termination Marker
4182 is required to allow the receiver to properly understand the end of the header block or data message and take
4183 the appropriate action to fit within the signaling for that HDR Mode.
4184 Because a Termination Marker can also serve as the transition before the next common CCC Flow Element,
4185 it may include signaling acknowledging the preceding Flow Element, or signaling that includes Bus
4186 Turnaround or other forms of Handoff between the Controller and an addressed Target.
4187 Depending on the specific HDR Mode and transaction type, this Termination Marker will take one of three
4188 possible forms:
4189 1. CRC Block
4190 This Block includes valid CRC (checksum) data for the preceding HDR-x CCC Data Block(s) or
4191 HDR-x CCC Header Block. The receiver shall use the CRC data to verify that the data received
4192 from the sender is valid.
4193 • For HDR-DDR Mode: The CRC Block is the HDR-DDR CRC Word (see Section 6.2.3.3.1.3).
4194 • For HDR-BT Mode: The CRC Block is the HDR-BT CRC Block (see Section 6.4.3.3.3).
4195 2. Turnaround or Handoff Pattern
4196 This form of Termination Marker is a specific pattern indicating Bus Turnaround or Handoff back
4197 to the Controller, if so indicated by the direction of transfer.
4198 3. No Termination Marker
4199 Under particular conditions, no Termination Marker per se will be sent.
4200 After this optional Termination Marker, the Controller shall reassert its control of the Bus (if necessary) and
4201 then send (or complete) the HDR Restart Pattern, or else continue on to perform any valid HDR-x CCC End
4202 Procedure that is appropriate for that framing.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 229
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 244 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURES: HDR MODES Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4203 Because the Termination Marker might take different forms, or might not exist on the I3C Bus at all
4204 depending on the particular HDR Mode or type of transaction, it is indicated generically in the following
4205 flow diagrams, using the label ‘TM’.
4206 At several points in the CCC flows it might be necessary for the Target to acknowledge receipt of a given
4207 Flow Element. In the following flow diagrams, such an acknowledgement may also serve as a Termination
4208 Marker; the label ‘ACK’ indicates such cases.
4209 Note:
4210 The exact location of an acknowledgement may vary, depending on the particular HDR Mode and
4211 the flow control method used to indicate acknowledgement.
4212 The framing for Direct CCCs also allows for multiple Target Addresses to be addressed, in a manner similar
4213 to the SDR Direct CCC framing model which addresses multiple Target Addresses as combinations of Read
4214 Segments (for Direct Read or Direct GET CCCs) and/or Write Segments (for Direct Write or Direct SET
4215 CCCs). The Controller may use this framing to address one or more Target Addresses by using multiple
4216 Segments, separated by the HDR Restart Pattern. When used in this manner the HDR Restart Pattern acts not
4217 as the end of the CCC (as it does with other, HDR traffic), but as the end of one Segment addressed to the
4218 indicated Target Address.
4219 Note:
4220 In this framing the HDR Restart Pattern serves as an equivalent of SDR Mode’s Repeated START in
4221 Direct CCC framing (see Section 4.3.7.2.2).
4222 Target Devices shall store the Command Code and its optional Defining Byte (as the Controller sent in the
4223 Indicator type HDR-x CCC Header Block) and act on them as individually addressed in this framing. The
4224 Command Code and optional Defining Byte values shall be stored for later use, if a Target matches its Target
4225 Address in a subsequent Selector type HDR-x CCC Header Block, until the end of the current modality is
4226 indicated via any HDR-x CCC End Procedure valid for the particular HDR Mode.
4227 The generic Broadcast CCC flow for the framings described above is shown in Figure 99; the generic Direct
4228 CCC flow for the framings described above is shown in Figure 100.

Figure 100 Begin Direct CCC Per HDR Mode\n--- Page 246 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURES: HDR MODES Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4231 The ending of the CCC modality shall be indicated by any HDR-x CCC End Procedure valid for the particular
4232 HDR Mode. The HDR Restart Pattern alone shall not indicate the end of the CCC flow, as it would end a
4233 normal HDR read/write command flow. All Target Devices that support CCCs in the particular HDR Mode
4234 shall detect the beginning of the CCC and interpret the HDR Restart Pattern appropriately for the CCC
4235 framing until they receive an HDR-x CCC End Procedure valid for that HDR Mode. Each HDR Mode may
4236 define several HDR-x CCC End Procedures, depending on the HDR Mode’s use cases and Frame formats.
4237 Upon receiving a valid HDR-x CCC End Procedure, the Target shall either end the CCC modality and expect
4238 normal HDR traffic, or else immediately re-enter the CCC modality (depending on the type of procedure) by
4239 capturing a new Command Code and optional Defining Byte. Typically, a valid HDR-x CCC End Procedure
4240 starts with a common Flow Element, such as an Indicator type HDR-x CCC Header Block (or equivalent) to
4241 start a new CCC, or an HDR-x Header Block to end the CCC Frame. Such a Block shall be addressed to the
4242 Broadcast Address (7'h7E) and shall also indicate whether the modality is ended vs. re-entered for a new
4243 CCC. In this manner the Controller can use normal HDR traffic, Broadcast CCCs, and Direct CCCs in a
4244 continuous flow within a given HDR Mode, arranged in any sequential order, without needing to exit the
4245 HDR Mode to separate these commands or traffic flows.
4246 Note:
4247 A valid HDR-x CCC End Procedure for a particular HDR Mode that indicates the end of the CCC
4248 modality might use the dummy command code 0x1F (see Table 16) instead of a valid CCC, if the
4249 framing for that HDR Mode requires the Controller to use an additional Flow Element that would
4250 otherwise contain the CCC in order to comply with valid transfers in that HDR Mode. This dummy
4251 command code 0x1F is defined for this specific purpose. When using the dummy command code
4252 0x1F, a dummy Defining Byte value 0x00 should also be used, if the additional Flow Element also
4253 has a field that would otherwise contain a valid Defining Byte.
4254 The following HDR-x CCC End Procedures are recommended, but this is not an exhaustive list of End
4255 Procedures for any given HDR Mode:
4256 • HDR Restart Pattern followed by Indicator type HDR-x CCC Header Block (or equivalent),
4257 indicating that the CCC modality is still active, but providing a new CCC and optional Defining
4258 Byte. Flow then proceeds as a new Broadcast or Direct CCC (see Figure 100 and Figure 101).
4259 • HDR Restart Pattern followed by HDR-x Header Block (or equivalent) indicating that the CCC
4260 modality is ended or no longer in ‘continuation’, followed by an optional Termination Marker to
4261 indicate the end of a transfer, followed by another HDR Restart Pattern (see Figure 101). Flow
4262 then proceeds as standard HDR read/write transactions.
4263 • Any End Procedure that indicates that the CCC modality is ended must be distinct from the
4264 previous End Procedure, in order that all Target Devices may clearly recognize it and interpret
4265 subsequent HDR transactions as standard HDR read/write transactions. Typically, this requires
4266 its first Flow Element (i.e., the HDR-x Header Block) to be clearly recognized as neither an
4267 Indicator nor a Selector.
4268 • An optional variant of the above, which ends with the HDR Exit Pattern instead of the HDR
4269 Restart Pattern. HDR Mode is then exited, and the Bus returns to SDR Mode.
4270 • HDR Exit Pattern
4271 The recommended HDR-x CCC End Procedures are shown in Figure 101.

6.1.3.2 Broadcast Address ACK
4279 When addressing all Targets using Broadcast CCCs, or when starting the Direct CCC flow, the Controller
4280 shall send an Indicator type HDR-x CCC Header Block to signal that it is trying to write to all Targets. This
4281 Indicator block shall include the Broadcast Address (7'h7E) and will generally take the form of a Write
4282 command for that HDR Mode.
4283 Each Target Device that supports CCC flows in that HDR Mode and receives an Indicator block shall use a
4284 valid HDR Flow Control method for that HDR Mode to acknowledge the Indicator block. This
4285 acknowledgement indicates that it has received the Indicator block, has acknowledged the beginning of the
4286 Broadcast CCC or Direct CCC (i.e., has entered the modality), and is ready to participate. The Target shall
4287 acknowledge even if it decides not to act on (or even if it does not support) the particular CCC or its optional
4288 Defining Byte.
4289 Note:
4290 Acknowledging the Indicator block is equivalent to SDR Mode CCC Framing, where all Targets must
4291 acknowledge the Broadcast Address at the beginning of a CCC.
4292 For Direct CCC flows, acknowledgement of the Indicator block serves the important function of
4293 ensuring that the Target Device agrees to capture the Command Code and optional Defining Byte,
4294 and is ready to be addressed by any subsequent Selector blocks in this Direct CCC flow. By
4295 acknowledging the Indicator block, the Target signals that will capture these values into its internal
4296 state and use them to decide whether to respond to that particular Command Code and optional
4297 Defining Byte, if it matches its Target Address in a subsequent Selector block that begins a Read or
4298 Write Segment. In this case, the Target is considered to be ‘primed’ with the captured values, and
4299 capable of making a rapid ACK/NACK decision at the correct point in the flow.
4300 If one such Target on the Bus is unable to respond and does not acknowledge the Indicator block,
4301 then this is equivalent to a NACK of the Broadcast Address in the Indicator block, for that HDR Mode.
4302 However, if other Targets on the Bus are present and are able to acknowledge the Indicator block,
4303 then one Target’s failure to acknowledge the Indicator block must not block the CCC flow from
4304 continuing. This typically requires the HDR Mode to define a common flow control method that can
4305 be used by multiple Targets (e.g., passive NACK, active ACK) for a Write command. If this flow control
4306 method is not enabled by default, then the Controller must enable it for the Bus, as appropriate for
4307 the HDR Mode, before sending any CCCs in that HDR Mode.
4308 The Controller shall use this acknowledgement to ensure that the CCC flow is being received by at least one
4309 Target in that HDR Mode. If the Controller does not receive acknowledgement from at least one Target, then
4310 it should initiate an error recovery procedure, which might include exiting HDR Mode and attempting to re-
4311 send the CCC in SDR Mode.
4312 Note:
4313 If a Bus has multiple Target Devices that support CCC flows in that HDR Mode, then they shall all
4314 acknowledge the Indicator block together.
4315 In Figure 99 and Figure 100, the location for acknowledgement is shown after the Indicator type HDR-x
4316 CCC Header Block. The specific location and method for the Target to indicate its acknowledgment of the
4317 Indicator block is specific to the particular HDR Mode, but generally includes some active state change that
4318 is driven by the Target, and that can be measured by the Controller.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 235
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 250 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURES: HDR MODES Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.1.3.3 Direct CCC ACK/NACK and Retry
4319 When addressing specific Targets using Direct CCCs in HDR Modes, the Controller shall indicate whether
4320 it is trying to read from the Target (i.e., a Direct Read or Direct GET) or write to the Target (i.e., a Direct
4321 Write or Direct SET). The Target Device may use a valid HDR Flow Control method to indicate that it cannot
4322 respond to a read request, or decline a write request at any point, in a manner equivalent to SDR Mode CCC
4323 Framing where it would NACK its own Target Address for a Direct CCC (see Section 4.3.7.2.2).
4324 In Figure 100, the acknowledgement is shown after the Selector type HDR-x CCC Header Block for a Read
4325 Segment and a Write Segment. The specific location and method for the Target to indicate its part in the
4326 acknowledgment of the Selector block and agreement to respond to the Direct CCC with optional Defining
4327 Byte is specific to the particular HDR Mode, but generally includes some active state change that is driven
4328 by the Target, and that can be measured by the Controller.
4329 Note that Figure 100 illustrates an example HDR Direct SET/GET CCC. However, this flow can also be
4330 applied to a Direct SET or a Direct GET, as the Read Segments and Write Segments are optional and can
4331 appear in any order as defined for the particular CCC. Some CCCs might not have any Write or Read
4332 Segments, and in some cases the HDR-x CCC Data Block might be omitted if the CCC doesn’t define any
4333 data to write to or read from a Target. Multiple Segments may address the same Target, or different Targets.
4334 If a Target is not able to respond to a Direct GET CCC immediately on the first attempt by the Controller,
4335 then it may use a method specific to the chosen HDR Mode to decline to respond, similar to a NACK of its
4336 own Target Address in SDR Mode. The Controller shall follow the same Direct GET CCC Retry model used
4337 in SDR Mode (see Section 4.3.7.2.3), and the Target should generally respond on the second attempt.
4338 A Target indicating a delayed response on the first attempt is not the same as a Target choosing to NACK its
4339 own Target Address to indicate that it does not support a given CCC and Defining Byte. As with SDR Mode,
4340 in this case the Controller would make a second attempt, and the Target would also decline to respond on the
4341 second attempt.
4342 However, if a Target declines to respond to another type of Direct CCC (i.e., not a Direct GET CCC) on the
4343 first attempt, then it might not support the given CCC and Defining Byte, in which case the Retry model
4344 would not necessarily apply.
4345 The Controller may also address Groups using Direct Write or Direct SET CCCs, if supported by the
4346 particular Direct CCC; other special conditions shall apply, per Section 6.1.3.5.
236 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 251 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURES: HDR MODES Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
6.1.3.4 Implications for Device and Bus Configuration
4347 While it is not always recommended to use CCCs in HDR Modes that might change Bus configuration or
4348 Device configuration, this might be permitted if the Active Controller follows certain safety
4349 recommendations, and if the Target Devices obey a set of conventions per their optional support for these
4350 CCCs in HDR Modes.
4351 • For Direct SET CCCs that change a Device’s configuration, a Target receiving that CCC shall
4352 verify such configuration changes after successfully receiving all data sent by the Controller (as
4353 indicated by a Termination Marker or other command coding elements specific to that HDR Mode)
4354 and then apply the configuration change immediately, at the end of the Frame for that Target.
4355 • For Broadcast CCCs that change Bus Configuration or Device Configuration for all valid Devices,
4356 all Targets receiving that CCC shall verify such configuration changes after successfully receiving
4357 all data sent by the Controller (as indicated by a Termination Marker or other command coding
4358 elements specific to that HDR Mode) and then apply the configuration change immediately.
4359 It should also be clear that any Broadcast CCCs that announce updated Bus configuration data (e.g.,
4360 DEFTGTS and DEFGRPA) while in HDR Mode will not be received by Target Devices that do not support
4361 that particular HDR Mode. In most circumstances, a Controller-capable Device should generally expect to
4362 use these CCCs in SDR Mode, unless it knows with certainty that all other Devices that need to receive this
4363 Bus configuration data (i.e., all Secondary Controllers) also support the same HDR Mode and support
4364 receiving these Broadcast CCCs in that HDR Mode. It is the Active Controller’s responsibility to ensure that
4365 all Secondary Controllers support the same HDR Mode before using these Broadcast CCCs in HDR Mode.
4366 Since custom CCCs can be defined by various MIPI Alliance WGs or Vendors for special purposes, some of
4367 these CCCs may also change additional aspects of Bus configuration or Device configuration, in order to
4368 enable as-yet-undefined use cases or features not yet covered by this Specification.
4369 Note:
4370 Groups that create their own CCCs (including MIPI Alliance WGs, outside standards groups, and
4371 Vendors) may choose to define custom CCCs in any supported HDR Mode, and so should be aware
4372 of the recommendations above when choosing whether to do so, and how to apply any configuration
4373 changes based on data received from the Active Controller in HDR Mode.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 237
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 252 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURES: HDR MODES Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.1.3.5 Implications for Group Addressing
4374 For I3C Target Devices that support Group Addresses, the Controller may choose to configure a Group
4375 Address and address multiple Targets in a Direct Write or Direct SET CCC form of any supported CCC
4376 within the HDR CCC flows, in a manner similar to Group Address support in Direct CCC flows within SDR
4377 Mode. In this manner, Group Addresses may be used in place of Dynamic (Target) Addresses in the common
4378 CCC Flow Elements (i.e., the Selector type HDR-x CCC Header Blocks for Direct CCC Write Segments),
4379 as long as the following conditions are met:
4380 • The Selector Block must indicate a Direct CCC Write Segment, since per Section 4.3.4.4 a Group
4381 Address shall only be used for Write transfers in HDR Modes.
4382 • All Targets that have been assigned to that Group Address using the SETGRPA CCC (see
4383 Section 4.3.7.3.24) must support CCC flows within that HDR Mode.
4384 • All Targets in that Group shall ACK the Group Address when addressed by the Selector block for
4385 the Write Segment.
4386 • If any Target in that Group is not able to respond to the Write Segment directed to the Group
4387 Address, then its failure to ACK the Group Address must not block the Write Segment from
4388 continuing, if the Controller detects that at least one other Target in that Group responds with an
4389 ACK.
4390 • This typically requires the HDR Mode to define a common flow control method that must be
4391 enabled by the Controller before sending any CCCs to a Group Address (see Section 6.1.3.2
4392 and Section 6.1.3.3).
4393 For Bus configurations where Group Addressing is used in conjunction with Multi-Lane, the Controller shall
4394 ensure that all Targets that have been assigned to the same Group Address are configured to use mutually
4395 interoperable Data Transfer Codings, and have been configured to use the same ML Frame format (i.e., the
4396 same Data Transfer Coding and number of additional Lanes) for transfers addressed to that Group Address.
4397 The Controller must verify this before attempting to send a Direct Write or Direct SET CCC to a Group
4398 Address in a Bus configured for Multi-Lane with HDR CCC flows.
4399 • If not all Targets in a Group are configured to use the same ML Frame format in a particular HDR
4400 Mode, then the Controller must configure such Targets to use a common ML Frame format that
4401 they all support. In some cases, this might mean using a 1-Lane compatible ML Frame format to
4402 send a Direct Write or Direct SET CCC to a Group Address.
4403 If a Target Device supports multiple current ML configurations for its assigned Group Addresses (per
4404 Section 6.7.3.1.1), then:
4405 • Within the CCC flows in HDR Modes, such a Target shall use the Address in the Selector Block to
4406 determine which ML configuration to use, as it prepares to send or receive data in that Direct CCC
4407 Segment. If the Address matches a currently assigned Group Address, then it shall use the correct
4408 ML configuration for that Group Address instead of the ML configuration for its Dynamic Address.
4409 • After assigning such a Target Device to any Group Address, that Target shall have an independent
4410 ML configuration associated with that Group Address, and the Controller might need to
4411 re-configure the Target’s ML configuration for CCCs directed to that Group Address (as a specific
4412 example of ML configuration requirements listed in Section 6.7.3.1.1).
4413 • Such configuration changes shall not affect the ML configuration for the Target’s Dynamic
4414 Address(es), and the Controller may continue sending Direct CCCs to a Target’s Dynamic
4415 Address(es) using the previously configured ML Frame format for that Dynamic Address.
238 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 253 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURES: HDR MODES Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4416 If a Target Device does not support multiple current ML configurations for its assigned Group Addresses (per
4417 Section 6.7.3.1.1), then:
4418 • Within the CCC flows in HDR Modes, such a Target shall use the current ML configuration for its
4419 Dynamic Address (i.e., its single ML configuration for that HDR Mode) as it prepares to send or
4420 receive data in that Direct CCC Segment. If the Address in the Selector Block matches any
4421 currently assigned address (i.e., Dynamic Address, Broadcast Address or any Group Address), then
4422 it shall use its single ML configuration.
4423 • After assigning that Target Device to any Group Address, the Controller might need to re-configure
4424 the ML Frame format for that Target, or for the other Targets in any other Group Addresses to
4425 which that Target Device is assigned, in order to ensure that all Target Devices that have been
4426 assigned to any particular Group Address are still configured to use the same ML Frame format
4427 after the change, since such a Target Device does not support multiple current ML configurations.
4428 Note:
4429 The Target shall use the ML configuration (i.e., the currently configured ML Frame format) for its
4430 Dynamic Address in order to properly receive all common CCC Flow Elements as defined in
4431 Section 6.1.3.1). This includes any HDR-x transfers that are addressed to the Broadcast Address,
4432 which generally include the Indicator type HDR-x CCC Header Block as well as various HDR-x CCC
4433 End Procedures that are defined for that HDR Mode. Such common CCC Flow Elements must be
4434 sent by the Controller in a form that all Target Devices supporting that HDR Mode are configured to
4435 understand and properly receive, in a mutually interoperable manner; and each such Target Device
4436 shall respond according to such configuration, according to the current ML Frame format for that
4437 HDR Mode.
4438 The requirements in this Section are a specific application of other normative requirements in this
4439 Specification (e.g., Section 4.3.4.4, Section 6.7.3.6, and Section 6.7.3.1.1). Such other sections
4440 also include other requirements, restrictions, and recommendations for Target Devices, as well as
4441 the Active Controller of the Bus. As the requirements in this Section are intended for a specific use
4442 case of HDR transfers as applied to CCC flows in HDR Modes, readers are warned not to interpret
4443 this Section as contradictory to any such requirements or restrictions in such other sections that
4444 govern all Multi-Lane configuration and all HDR transfers (including HDR transfers sent to Group
4445 Addresses).
6.1.3.6 Bus Efficiency
4446 It should be expected that remaining within a given HDR Mode is more efficient for specific, traffic-intensive
4447 use cases, and that using CCCs while in HDR Mode will help maintain this efficiency by avoiding the need
4448 for the Controller to exit HDR Mode in order to send a CCC (as with SDR Mode). It is also likely that CCC
4449 data can be sent more quickly, by taking advantage of the increased data rates of the HDR Modes and Multi-
4450 Lane functionality (if supported and configured).
4451 However, depending on the Bus Configuration and its application and use cases, the Controller might
4452 occasionally need to exit HDR Mode, in order to provide a window for Target Devices to request In-Band
4453 Interrupts, the Controller Role, or Hot-Joins as may be required per the Bus Configuration and its application
4454 and use cases.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 239
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 254 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURES: HDR MODES Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.1.4 Transition to HDR Mode Transfer
4455 The Controller is responsible for starting the HDR Mode transfer, which consists of sending the first
4456 structured protocol element, per the HDR Mode framing. The Controller shall do this after entering the HDR
4457 Mode for the first transfer in that HDR Mode (see Section 6.1.4.1), or after the HDR Restart Pattern, for
4458 subsequent transfers in that same HDR Mode (see Section 6.1.4.2).
4459 In both cases, the specific timing of when the Controller drives SDA in relation to SCL depends on the HDR
4460 Mode signaling. In this Section and its sub-sections, a possible example signaling method is provided:
4461 • For HDR Modes that use SCL as an edge-triggering clock (i.e., like SDR Mode), the first bit starts
4462 on the next SCL rising edge, and the Controller shall drive SDA when SCL is Low (i.e., before the
4463 rising edge). This ensures that the Target samples SDA on the SCL rising edge.
4464 Note:
4465 The signaling method described in this Section is intended to be used as a template for all of the
4466 HDR Modes defined in the I3C basic Specification. Since other signaling methods are possible, this
4467 Section does not provide an exhaustive list of possible signaling methods that might be defined for
4468 future HDR Modes.
240 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 255 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURES: HDR MODES Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
6.1.4.1 Entering the HDR Mode After ENTHDRx CCC
4469 To enter an HDR Mode, the Controller sends the Broadcast CCC for a particular HDR Mode, and then drives
4470 SDA after the completion of the T-Bit (i.e., on or after the falling edge of the SCL pulse for C9) to begin the
4471 first structured protocol element in that HDR Mode, according to the HDR Mode framing.
4472 From that point, the Controller follows the common timing rules for starting a transfer in that HDR Mode
4473 per Section 6.1.4. Note that the I3C Bus transitions from SDR Mode to that HDR Mode after the falling edge
4474 of the SCL pulse for C9.
4475 Note:
4476 The Target is also required to enable its HDR Exit Pattern Detector after the Broadcast ENTHDRx
4477 CCC; see Section 4.3.10.2.1.
4478 Figure 102 illustrates the signaling for entering a generic HDR Mode (shown as “HDR-x”) after the
4479 ENTHDRx CCC. In this example, this HDR Mode uses SCL as a dedicated, edge-triggering clock, so the
4480 Controller sets up SDA before the next SCL rising edge, to start sending the first structured protocol element
4481 (i.e., a Command or Header) for the first HDR Mode transfer.

6.1.4.2 Next HDR Mode Transfer After HDR Restart Pattern
4486 To remain in an HDR Mode after the completion of an HDR Mode transfer that ends with the HDR Restart
4487 Pattern (see Figure 97), the Controller completes the SCL pulse by driving SCL Low, and then drives SDA
4488 to begin the first structured protocol element in that HDR Mode, according to the HDR Mode framing.
4489 From that point, the Controller follows the common timing rules for starting a transfer in that HDR Mode
4490 per Section 6.1.4. Note that the I3C Bus remains in the same HDR Mode through the HDR Restart Pattern.
4491 Figure 103 illustrates the signaling for an HDR Restart Pattern followed by starting the next HDR Mode
4492 transfer, in a generic HDR Mode (shown as “HDR-x”) that uses SCL as a dedicated, edge-triggering clock.
4493 This diagram might be a continuation of the signaling shown in Figure 102: if so, then the I3C Bus remains
4494 in the same HDR Mode through the HDR Restart Pattern and the next HDR Mode transfer. After the
4495 completion of the SCL pulse that validates the HDR Restart Pattern, the Controller sets up SDA before the
4496 next SCL rising edge, to start sending the first structured protocol element for the next HDR Mode transfer
4497 (similar to Figure 102).

6.2 F001: HDR-DDR Mode
4499 This Section defines I3C Optional Feature F001: HDR-DDR Mode.
4500 Table 78 Optional Feature 001 Status
Optional Feature ID F001
Optional Feature Name HDR-DDR Mode
Optional Feature Version 1
Optional Feature specification was last updated in I3C Spec Version I3C v1.2
Optional Feature is not compatible with other Optional Features –
Optional Feature is included in I3C Basic Yes
4501 Changes in this Version
4502 For detailed changes in this version, see the “diff” file between I3C v1.1.1 and I3C v1.2 in the MIPI
4503 Specification Repository [MIPI09].
6.2.1 Scope and Purpose (Informative)
4504 The scope of Section 6.2 is limited to fully specifying I3C Optional Feature F001: HDR-DDR Mode. An I3C
4505 Device that implements F001 is required to implement all shall statements in Section 6.2.3, and can
4506 optionally implement any combination of may statements in Section 6.2.3. In addition, MIPI Alliance
4507 recommends that manufacturers follow all should statements in Section 6.2.3.
6.2.2 Technical Overview (Informative)
4508 Like SDR Mode, HDR-DDR Mode uses SCL as a clock; however, unlike SDR Mode, Data and Commands
4509 change SDA on both SCL edges (when High and when Low), effectively doubling the data rate. By contrast,
4510 in SDR Mode SDA is changed only when SCL is Low. Since SDR Mode defines it as a START or a STOP
4511 for SDA to change while SCL remains High, HDR-DDR Mode is classified as an HDR Mode in order to
4512 prevent confusion.
4513 HDR-DDR is an I3C HDR mode, which is entered in the standard way (see the CCC ENTHDR0,
4514 Section 4.3.7.3.9) and exited via the HDR Exit Pattern. As with the other HDR modes, Messages while in
4515 the HDR-DDR mode are separated using the HDR Restart pattern.
4516 Once in HDR-DDR Mode, the Controller issues a Command Word to start every transfer (unless NACKed).
4517 In HDR-DDR Mode, Commands shall be issued only by a Controller. Data Words may be issued by a
4518 Controller or by a Target, depending on the particular Command (i.e., Write or Read). At least one Data Word
4519 shall be issued, unless the Target does not accept (ACK) the Command per Section 6.2.3.4.
4520 Figure 104 illustrates a typical HDR-DDR Mode Frame with two HDR Commands and their associated data:
4521 • I3C START or Repeated START
4522 • I3C CCC to enter HDR-DDR Mode
4523 • After entering HDR-DDR Mode, there is one HDR-DDR Command Word, followed by one or
4524 more HDR-DDR Data Words, and then an HDR-DDR CRC Word
4525 • Then an HDR Restart Pattern
4526 • Then another HDR-DDR Command Word, HDR-DDR Data Word, and HDR-DDR CRC Word
4527 • Finally, HDR Mode is ended via the HDR Exit Pattern followed by I3C STOP
4528 Note:
4529 An HDR-DDR CRC Word might also be emitted after an abort, per Section 6.2.3.4.

4531 Following the HDR-DDR CRC Word, the Controller provides either the HDR Restart Pattern or the HDR
4532 Exit Pattern.\n--- Page 259 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURE F001 Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
6.2.3 Technical Specification
6.2.3.1 HDR-DDR Signaling
4533 The HDR-DDR Protocol uses the same signaling as SDR, but operates with SDA changing after each SCL
4534 edge. In HDR-DDR Mode, just as in SDR Mode, only the I3C Bus Controller drives the SCL line. For
4535 Commands, the SDA line is driven by the Controller. For Data, the SDA line is driven either by the Target or
4536 by the Controller, depending on the Command direction. Bus Turnaround behavior is specified in
4537 Section 6.2.3.4.
4538 HDR-DDR Mode is entered using the ENTHDR0 CCC (see Section 4.3.7.3.9). After the ENTHDR0 CCC
4539 and its ninth bit (the T-Bit), the SCL falling edge begins HDR-DDR Mode. The first HDR-DDR bit starts on
4540 the next SCL rising edge; that is, the Controller drives SDA when SCL is Low, and the Target samples SDA
4541 on the SCL rising edge. This allows the entry to HDR-DDR Mode, as shown in Figure 109.
6.2.3.2 HDR-DDR Structured Protocol Elements
4542 HDR-DDR moves data by Words. A Word generally contains 16 payload bits, as two bytes in a byte stream,
4543 and two parity bits. Four HDR-DDR Word Types are defined: Command Word, Data Word, CRC Word, and
4544 Reserved Word.
4545 The HDR-DDR Protocol precedes each 18-bit Word with a 2-bit Preamble, for a total of 20 bits per Word.
4546 The Preamble:
4547 • Indicates the type of data that follows: either Command, Data, or CRC;
4548 • Allows the Controller to terminate a Read, and to determine whether the Target is willing to
4549 respond to a Read;
4550 • Allows the Target to NACK a Write before the first Data Word, if it has been configured to do so
4551 (see Section 6.2.3.4.4); and
4552 • Allows the Target to request a Write termination between Data Words, if it has been configured to
4553 do so (see Section 6.2.3.4.4).
4554 The related flow control procedures are described in Section 6.2.3.4.
4555 The main structuring of the HDR-DDR mode is broken into 3 top-level units:
Command Word N * Data Words CRC Word
(Address and Command Code) Each word contains 2 bytes (for Single-Lane) (CRC-5)
or additional bytes (for Multi-Lane)
4556 Figure 105 shows the defined HDR-DDR structured protocol elements, where the Preamble bits determine
4557 the type of HDR-DDR Word.

4559 Preamble bit interpretation depends on the context, as shown in Table 79. The context controls the roles of
4560 Controller and Target, such that for Data, the Target or Controller drives the second Preamble bit after the
4561 other I3C Device has Parked High. The Preamble value of 2'b00 is reserved for special use, as indicated when
4562 such use is defined.
4563 Table 79 HDR-DDR Preamble Values

4564 HDR-DDR Mode defines four Word Types (see Figure 105):
4565 • Command Word:
4566 • The Preamble before a Command Word shall have the value 2'b01.
4567 • The length of a Command Word shall be 18 bits (16 value bits, 2 parity bits).
4568 HDR-DDR Mode Command Codes allow up to 128 Write or Read Commands,
4569 respectively (see Section 6.2.3.3).
4570 • Data Word:
4571 • The Preamble before a Data Word shall contain the value 2'b10 or 2'b11 (see Table 79).
4572 • The length of a normal Data Word shall be 18 bits (16 value bits as two bytes, 2 parity bits).
4573 • CRC Word:
4574 • The Preamble before a CRC Word shall have the value 2'b01.
4575 • The length of a CRC Word shall be 10 clocked bits (4 bit 4'hC token, 5 bits of CRC-5
4576 checksum, 1 bit of 1'b1 setup to prepare the Bus for the following HDR Exit Pattern or HDR
4577 Restart Pattern).
4578 • The value of the upper nibble (first 4 bits after the Preamble) of a CRC Word shall be 4'hC.
4579 • There is no parity checking for a CRC Word.
4580 • Following the falling edge of the last SCL pulse, the SDA shall be kept High (i.e., Open Drain
4581 class Pull-Up from Controller, while Target is in High-Z), for a time at least equal to t DIG_H .
4582 • Following transmission of a CRC Word, an I3C Device shall set up SCL and SDA to allow for
4583 an HDR Restart Pattern or HDR Exit Pattern.
4584 The CRC-5 algorithm is specified in Section 6.2.3.6.
4585 • Reserved Word (For special use only):
4586 • The Preamble before a Reserved Word (i.e., not a CRC Word) shall have the value 2'b01.
4587 • The length of a Reserved Word shall be 18 bits (4-bit token 4'hD / 4'hE / 4'hF, 12 reserved bits,
4588 and 2 parity bits).

4590 HDR-DDR Mode also allows for special use cases to be defined in the future, via Preamble value

4592
Figure 106 HDR-DDR Preamble Bits State Diagram
4593 • A normal HDR-DDR Read Request shall be composed of a Command Word from the Controller,
4594 followed by one or more Data Words from the Target.
4595 • If the Controller requests the Read transfer to be terminated, then the Target shall conditionally
4596 send the CRC Word after termination, based on the configuration set by the ENDXFER CCC
4597 (see Section 6.2.3.4.5).
4598 • If the Read transfer ends normally, then the Target shall send the CRC Word after the last Data
4599 Word.
248 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 263 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURE F001 Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4600 • A NACKed HDR-DDR Read Request shall be composed of a Command Word, followed by the
4601 Target not ACKing (driving SDA to Low) in the second Preamble bit, before what would be the
4602 first Data Word (see Section 6.2.3.4.2).
4603 • An HDR-DDR Write Request shall be composed of a Command Word, followed by one or more
4604 Data Words, all from the Controller.
4605 • If the Target requests the Write transfer to be terminated, then the Controller shall conditionally
4606 send the CRC Word after termination, based on the configuration set by the ENDXFER CCC
4607 (see Section 6.2.3.4.6).
4608 • If the transfer ends normally, then the Controller shall send the CRC Word after the last Data
4609 Word.
4610 • A NACKed HDR-DDR Write Request shall be composed of a Command Word, followed by the
4611 Target not ACKing (driving SDA to Low) in the second Preamble bit, before what would be the
4612 first Data Word (see Section 6.2.3.4.1).
4613 Each Command Word and each Data Word is 20 bits in length (i.e., 20 clock edges) including a two-bit
4614 Preamble. At 10 MHz the raw bit rate is 20 Mbps; at 12.5 MHz the raw bit rate is 25 Mbps. Because each
4615 Word includes two Preamble bits and two Parity bits, the real data rate (considering only the two bytes in the
4616 16 payload bits per Word) is the raw rate times 16/20, or 16 Mbps at 10 MHz (20 Mbps at 12.5 MHz), which
4617 is a measure of the 16-bit data rate.
4618 The common format used by HDR-DDR Command Words, Data Words, and Reserved Words is illustrated
4619 in Table 80. The Command details are explained in Section 6.2.3.3. The format used by HDR-DDR CRC
4620 Words is a subset of the Command Word and the Data Word (see Section 6.2.3.6).
4621 Table 80 HDR-DDR Word Format: Command, Data, Reserved
Parity
Preamble Payload
(not the CRC)
2 bits 16 bits 1 bit 1 bit

Command Code or
2'b01: Command or CRC Odd index Payload 1 and Even index

2'b10, 2'b11: Data or Abort bits Payload bits
4622 The two parity bits are formed from the payload bits, using an XOR function:
4623 • PA1 = Parity of the odd index Payload bits:
4624 D[15] ^ D[13] ^ D[11] ^ D[9] ^ D[7] ^ D[5] ^ D[3] ^ D[1]
4625 • PA0 = Parity of the even index Payload bits and 1:
4626 D[14] ^ D[12] ^ D[10] ^ D[8] ^ D[6] ^ D[4] ^ D[2] ^ D[0] ^ 1

4629 On a WRITE transaction, the Controller controls the whole transfer; as a result, the transition from the CRC
4630 Word to either the HDR Restart Pattern or the HDR Exit Pattern is done with only the Controller driving the
4631 SDA, while the Target releases SDA (i.e., allows High-Z) (see Figure 107).

Figure 107 HDR-DDR CRC and HDR Restart Pattern or HDR Exit Pattern in Write Transaction\n--- Page 266 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F001 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4633 On a Read transaction, the Target conditionally drives the CRC Word (as noted above) and the Controller
4634 takes over generation of the HDR Restart Pattern or HDR Exit Pattern. See Figure 108 for the signal diagram
4635 for this SDA handoff.
4636 This procedure has the following steps:
4637 1. The Target actively transfers the data, while the Controller is in High-Z.
4638 2. The Target actively drives the CRC Word, including pulling the setup bit (bit[6]) High.
4639 3. After a time of t SCO elapses after the falling edge of CRC_CLK6, the Target releases SDA on
4640 High-Z (Figure 108, point 3)
4641 4. The Controller keeps its SDA output on High-Z until the start of rising edge of CRC_CLK6,
4642 when it enables the Open Drain class Pull-Up (Figure 108, point 1). The SDA line remains
4643 driven by the Target.
4644 5. The Controller starts driving SDA High when it starts the falling edge of CRC_CLK6 (point 2
4645 in the diagram). Since the Target was driving the setup bit (bit[6]) High, the two Devices are
4646 now driving in parallel.
4647 6. After a time of at least t DIG_H elapses after the falling edge of CRC_CLK6, the Controller may
4648 start the first falling edge of the SDA, commencing either the HDR Restart Pattern or the HDR
4649 Exit Patterns (Figure 108, point 4).
4650 7. The result of this procedure is a safe handoff of the SDA line from the Target to the Controller.

6.2.3.3 HDR-DDR Command Coding
4652 In HDR-DDR Mode a Command Word follows the initial ENTHDR0 CCC (and any HDR Restart Pattern).
4653 This Command Word uses the normal 18-bit model. Table 82 illustrates the Command Word format for HDR-
4654 DDR Mode.
4655 Table 82 HDR-DDR Command Word Format
Size
Bits Field Notes
(bits)
15 Read/Write 1 1: Read (Target to Controller)
0: Write (Controller to Target)
14:8 Command Code 7 128 possible Write commands
128 possible Read commands
7:1 Target Address 7 Same Dynamic Address as used in I3C SDR
Protocol
0 Parity Adjustment 1 Ensures that PA0 contains 1'b1, which allows
for easier Bus Turnaround1
Note:
1) Because PA0 is the XOR of the even index Payload bits and 1, it is equal to:
XOR_outer( 1, this bit, XOR_inner( 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14 ) )
As a result, this bit should be set to the result of XOR_inner().
4656 Regarding bits 15:8 together, the possible Command Code spaces for Read Commands and Write Commands
4657 in HDR-DDR Mode are illustrated in Table 83.
Table 83 Read and Write Command Spaces for HDR-DDR Mode
Command
Command Functions
Codes
0x00 – 0x7F Available for any use for writes
0x80 – 0xFF Available for any use for reads
4658 HDR-DDR Command Words indicate the direction of data movement: either Write (Controller to Target), or
4659 Read (Target to Controller). After the Command Word, one or more Data Words are sent by the Controller
4660 (for a Write) or by the Target (for a Read, unless NACKed) until done, followed by the CRC Word (unless
4661 NACKed or aborted, per ENDXFER configuration). Finally, the Controller issues either an HDR Restart
4662 Pattern or an HDR Exit Pattern. The Controller may also terminate a Read prematurely, per Section 6.2.3.4.3,
4663 however this is not a normal end for a Read, and should be used sparingly. The Target may request the
4664 termination of a Write if configured to do so, per Section 6.2.3.4.6.

4667 Command Code after HDR Restart Pattern.
