6.2.3.3.1.1 HDR-DDR CCC Header Block
4688 The HDR-DDR CCC Header Block has several different formats that are used in the framing of CCC flows
4689 in HDR-DDR Mode. Two of these formats are commonly used: an ‘Indicator’ type, used at the start of every
4690 CCC, and a ‘Selector’ type, used at the start of a Write Segment or Read Segment for Direct CCCs. A generic
4691 Header Block (which is neither an ‘Indicator’ type nor a ‘Selector’ type) may also be used for other purposes,
4692 such as CCC End Procedures.
4693 The HDR-DDR CCC Header Block always begins with an HDR-DDR Command Word, which shall take its
4694 structure (see Table 81 and Table 82). All Targets that support CCC flows in HDR-DDR Mode must
4695 acknowledge the HDR-DDR Header Block’s Command Word if addressed, unless defined otherwise for a
4696 particular usage in CCC flows. If an acknowledgement is required, then Bit[0] shall contain a value chosen
4697 to ensure that the PA0 field (Parity bit 0, see Table 81) contains the value 1'b1, per Section 6.2.3.4.1.
4698 Start of CCC: An HDR-DDR CCC Header Block that begins the framing for a Broadcast CCC or a Direct
4699 CCC shall consist of one HDR-DDR CCC Indicator Word, followed by one HDR-DDR CCC Command
4700 Word:
4701 • The HDR-DDR CCC Indicator Word shall take the structure of the HDR-DDR Command Word
4702 Format (see Table 81 and Table 82):
4703 • Bit[15] shall contain 1'b0, indicating a WRITE command (Controller to Target).
4704 • Bits[14:8] are reserved, and shall be ignored by the Target Devices.
4705 • Bits[7:1] shall contain the Broadcast Address (7'h7E).
4706 • Acknowledgement is always required, so Bit[0] shall contain a value chosen to ensure that all
4707 Targets supporting CCC flows in HDR-DDR Mode will acknowledge this Indicator Word.
4708 • The HDR-DDR CCC Command Word shall take the structure of the HDR-DDR Data Word Format
4709 (see Table 81).
4710 • Bits[15:8] shall contain the Command Code, which may be a Broadcast CCC or a Direct CCC.
4711 • Bits[7:0] shall contain the optional Defining Byte for the Command Code. If the CCC does not
4712 support an optional Defining Byte, then this field shall contain the value 8'h00.
4713 Start of Segment: An HDR-DDR CCC Header Block that begins a Read Segment or Write Segment in
4714 Direct CCC framing shall consist of one HDR-DDR CCC Selector Word.
4715 • The HDR-DDR CCC Selector Word shall take the structure of the HDR-DDR Command Word
4716 Format:
4717 • Bit[15] distinguishes a Read Segment vs. a Write Segment:
4718 • For the start of a Write Segment (i.e., a Direct Write or Direct SET CCC) this field shall
4719 contain 1'b0 to indicate a Write (Controller to Target).
4720 • For the start of a Read Segment (i.e., a Direct Read or Direct GET CCC) this field shall
4721 contain 1'b1 to indicate a Read (Target to Controller).
4722 • Bits[14:8] are reserved, and shall be ignored by the Target Devices.
4723 • Bits[7:1] shall contain the Dynamic Address of the Target to be addressed for Write or Read in
4724 this Segment. For Write Segments, Bits[7:1] might also contain a valid Group Address (per
4725 Section 6.1.3.5).
4726 • Acknowledgement is always required, so Bit[0] shall contain a value chosen to ensure that the
4727 addressed Target (or all Targets in the addressed Group) will acknowledge this Selector Word.
4728 This facilitates ACK/NACK for a Target (or Group) addressed by a Direct Write or Direct SET
4729 CCC (per Section 6.2.3.4.1), and facilitates both ACK/NACK and Bus Turnaround for a Target
4730 addressed by a Direct Read or Direct GET CCC (per Section 6.2.3.4.2).
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 259
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 274 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F001 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.2.3.3.1.2 HDR-DDR CCC Data Block
4731 An HDR-DDR CCC Data Block may follow an HDR-DDR CCC Header Block, in the appropriate location
4732 for either a Broadcast CCC flow (see Figure 111) or a Direct CCC flow (see Figure 112).
4733 • Broadcast: The HDR-DDR CCC Data Block in a Broadcast CCC flow shall take the structure of
4734 the HDR-DDR Data Word Format (see Table 81) and shall contain 16 bits (2 bytes) of data in its
4735 payload.
4736 • Per Section 6.1.3, all HDR-DDR CCC Data Blocks shall be transmitted using SDA[0] (i.e.,
4737 1-Lane mode) in order to ensure that all Target Devices that support HDR-DDR are always
4738 able to receive and understand the Broadcast CCC data, regardless of their currently
4739 configured ML Frame format, or the number of additional Lanes they might support.
4740 • Direct: The HDR-DDR CCC Data Block in a Direct CCC flow shall have the same structure as
4741 that of a Broadcast CCC flow (i.e., an HDR-DDR Data Word using SDA[0] in 1-Lane mode).
4742 • If the total data length is an odd number of bytes, then the sender shall pad the Data Block with
4743 an extra byte with value 8’d0, and the receiver shall discard the extra padding byte.
4744 Note:
4745 Multi-Lane support for HDR-DDR Mode is not included in I3C Basic. The above configuration
4746 advisory is provided in order to maintain full compatibility with I3C Target Devices that might support
4747 Multi-Lane for HDR-DDR Mode, as defined in version 1.1 or higher of the full I3C Specification.
260 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 275 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURE F001 Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
6.2.3.3.1.3 HDR-DDR CRC Block
4748 An HDR-DDR CRC Block serves as the Termination Marker, as described in the HDR-x generic CCC flows.
4749 To conform to the HDR-DDR Mode Framing model (see Figure 104), this Termination Marker uses the same
4750 structure as the standard HDR-DDR CRC Word.
4751 • For the end of Broadcast data sent in a Broadcast CCC flow, the HDR-DDR CRC Block shall be a
4752 single HDR-DDR CRC Word transmitted by the Controller in 1-Lane mode (see Table 81 and
4753 Figure 107), sent as a common CCC flow element to ensure that all Targets will receive it.
4754 • For the end of the HDR-DDR CCC Command Word sent in a Direct CCC flow, the HDR-DDR
4755 CRC Block shall be a single HDR-DDR CRC Word transmitted by the Controller in 1-Lane mode
4756 as described above.
4757 • For the end of a Read Segment or Write Segment in a Direct CCC flow, the HDR-DDR CRC
4758 Block shall be a single HDR-DDR CRC Word (i.e., the same as both flows above).
4759 The HDR-DDR CRC Block shall be included with all common CCC Flow Elements, as it must be received
4760 and understood by all HDR-DDR Targets, including those that only support 1-Lane mode.
4761 Note:
4762 Both Broadcast and Direct CCC flows are special types of standard HDR-DDR transfers, so the
4763 HDR-DDR CRC Block is used, per standard HDR-DDR transfer requirements. As noted above (i.e.,
4764 for the HDR-DDR CCC Data Block definition) Multi-Lane support for HDR-DDR Mode is not included
4765 in I3C Basic, so the HDR-DDR CRC Block must always use the same format (i.e., Data Transfer
4766 Coding) as the HDR-DDR Data Block that precedes it.
6.2.3.3.1.4 HDR-DDR CCC Indicator Word and Header ACK
4767 Per Section 6.1.3.2, a Target that supports CCCs in HDR-DDR Mode shall indicate acknowledgement of the
4768 receipt of the HDR-DDR CCC Indicator Word, as well as the HDR-DDR CCC Header Block’s Command
4769 Word when used for other purposes, using the standard method of acknowledging an HDR-DDR write, per
4770 Section 6.2.3.4.1 and Figure 114. Such a Target shall detect matching Command Words in CCC flows
4771 (including Indicator Words) that are addressed to the Broadcast Address (i.e., 7'h7E) and have a correctly
4772 calculated PA0 field (i.e., Parity bit 0, see Table 82) that contains the value 1'b1.
4773 • This applies to the Indicator Word when used with both Broadcast CCC flows and Direct CCC
4774 flows, since the same HDR-DDR CCC Indicator Word format is used to signal the beginning of
4775 both flows.
4776 • This also applies to the Command Word when used appropriately for other purposes in CCC flows,
4777 such as CCC End Procedures.
4778 If the Controller does not receive acknowledgement from at least one Target, then it shall perform any valid
4779 HDR-DDR CCC End Procedure. Additionally, it may initiate an error recovery procedure, which might
4780 include exiting HDR-DDR Mode and attempting to re-send the CCC in SDR Mode.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 261
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 276 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F001 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.2.3.3.1.5 HDR-DDR Direct CCC Write Segment
4781 An HDR-DDR CCC Write Segment for a Direct Write or Direct SET CCC shall start with an HDR-DDR
4782 CCC Selector Word to indicate the Address of the Target (or Group) to receive the data, followed by one or
4783 more HDR-DDR CCC Data Blocks of the appropriate structure, as required by the CCC definition.
4784 After the Controller sends the HDR-DDR CCC Selector Word, the Target shall then either indicate acceptance
4785 of the Direct CCC by acknowledging the WRITE Command, or else reject the Direct CCC by not responding
4786 to the Controller. The Target shall use the cached Command Code and optional Defining Byte from the
4787 previously received HDR-DDR CCC Command Word, and shall decide whether to accept or reject the Direct
4788 CCC immediately after receiving the HDR-DDR CCC Selector Word and matching its own Dynamic Address
4789 (or Group Address, if assigned and applicable).
4790 Note:
4791 If the CCC definition does not require data to be written to the Target or Group, then the Controller
4792 shall send a single HDR-DDR CCC Data Block that contains padding bytes (i.e., values of 8'd0). The
4793 Target shall discard the extra padding bytes, based on the cached Command Code and optional
4794 Defining Byte that were sent previously, as part of the CCC Framing in HDR-DDR Mode.
4795 The Target shall use the standard HDR-DDR Flow Control methods to either accept or reject an HDR-DDR
4796 WRITE Command, as defined in Section 6.2.3.4.1 and as illustrated in Figure 114 (accept) or Figure 115
4797 (reject).
4798 If the Target rejects the Direct CCC, then the Controller shall either send the HDR Restart Pattern and attempt
4799 another Read or Write Segment for this CCC, or else perform any valid HDR-DDR CCC End Procedure. For
4800 Direct Write or Direct SET CCCs to a Group Address, the Controller only sees this rejection if no Targets
4801 accept the Direct CCC.
4802 If the Target accepts the Direct CCC, then the Controller shall send one or more HDR-DDR CCC Data Blocks
4803 of the appropriate structure, if required by the CCC definition. Then the Controller shall send an HDR-DDR
4804 CRC Word to terminate the data and end the Write Segment.
4805 At the end of a Write Segment, the Controller shall either send the HDR Restart Pattern and attempt another
4806 Read or Write Segment for this CCC, or else perform any valid HDR-DDR CCC End Procedure.
6.2.3.3.1.6 HDR-DDR Direct CCC Read Segment
4807 An HDR-DDR CCC Read Segment for a Direct Read or Direct GET CCC shall start with an HDR-DDR
4808 CCC Selector Word to indicate the Address of the Target to provide data for the CCC, followed by a Bus
4809 Turnaround. In response, the indicated Target will either accept the Direct CCC by transmitting one or more
4810 HDR-DDR CCC Data Blocks of the appropriate structure, or else reject the Direct CCC by not responding
4811 to the Bus Turnaround condition.
4812 The Target shall use the standard HDR-DDR Flow Control methods to either accept or reject an HDR-DDR
4813 READ Command, as defined in Section 6.2.3.4.2 and as illustrated in Figure 116 (accept) or Figure 117
4814 (reject).
4815 If the Target accepts the Direct CCC, then the Controller shall yield control of the Bus to the Target so that it
4816 can control the Bus to send the Data Blocks. After sending the last Data Block, the Target shall send an HDR-
4817 DDR CRC Word, indicating the end of the read transfer. Upon receiving the HDR-DDR CRC Word the Target
4818 shall yield control of the Bus, and the Controller shall resume control.
4819 At the end of a Read Segment, the Controller shall either send the HDR Restart Pattern and attempt another
4820 Read or Write Segment for this CCC, or else perform any valid HDR-DDR CCC End Procedure.
262 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 277 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURE F001 Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
6.2.3.3.1.7 HDR-DDR CCC End Procedures
4821 As illustrated in Figure 118, the HDR-DDR CCC ends using one of three possible CCC End Procedures.
4822 • Option 1: END HDR-DDR CCC Followed by New HDR-DDR CCC
4823 This End Procedure indicates the end of a Broadcast or Direct CCC in HDR-DDR Mode, and the
4824 beginning of a new Broadcast or Direct CCC in HDR-DDR Mode.
4825 This End Procedure begins with an HDR Restart Pattern, followed by an HDR-DDR CCC Header
4826 Block containing a new CCC and optional Defining Byte. This starts either a Broadcast CCC flow
4827 (Figure 111) or a Direct CCC flow (see Figure 112) while remaining in the CCC modality.
4828 The Controller starts a new CCC by sending one HDR-DDR Command Word, acting as the
4829 HDR-DDR CCC Indicator Word and containing the Broadcast Address; followed by one
4830 HDR-DDR Data Word, acting as the HDR-DDR CCC Command Word and containing the
4831 Command Code and optional Defining Byte for the new CCC.
4832 Additional HDR-DDR Words shall follow, per the CCC flow type.
4833 The flow steps are:
4834 1. Controller sends HDR Restart Pattern.
4835 2. Controller sends HDR-DDR CCC Indicator Word, using the same format as described above.
4836 3. Targets acknowledge receipt of Indicator Word, as defined above.
4837 4. Controller sends HDR-DDR CCC Command Word, including the Command Code and
4838 optional Defining Byte.
4839 5. Flow continues as Broadcast CCC or Direct CCC, as shown above.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 263
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 278 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F001 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
4840 • Option 2: END HDR-DDR CCC Followed by Another HDR-DDR Generic Transaction (+
4841 Optional HDR Exit)
4842 This End Procedure indicates the end of CCCs in HDR-DDR, and either a return to standard
4843 HDR-DDR transactions or a subsequent exit from HDR-DDR Mode.
4844 This End Procedure begins with an HDR Restart Pattern, followed by an HDR-DDR Command
4845 Word which ends the CCC flow, but is neither an Indicator nor a Selector. This Command Word is
4846 a common flow element that is sent to all Targets and must be acknowledged (i.e., like the
4847 Indicator Word).
4848 This is followed by an HDR-DDR Data Word, a common flow element that is required for HDR-
4849 DDR WRITE transfers (per Section 6.2.3.2). This Data Word contains the dummy command code
4850 value (i.e., 0x1F, see Table 16) to indicate the end of the CCC modality in HDR-DDR Mode, per
4851 Section 6.1.3.1.
4852 Note:
4853 This HDR-DDR Data Word is not an HDR-DDR CCC Command Word.
4854 This is followed by an HDR-DDR CRC Word as a common flow element. This CRC Word’s
4855 checksum is computed from the contents of the preceding HDR-DDR Data Word.
4856 After this, the HDR Restart Pattern signals the end of CCC modality, and that the next HDR-DDR
4857 Command Word must be treated as a standard HDR-DDR transaction (not as a CCC flow).
4858 Alternatively, the HDR Exit Pattern signals the end of HDR-DDR Mode and a subsequent return
4859 to SDR Mode.
4860 In either case, the HDR-DDR Command Word that indicates a write to the Broadcast Address
4861 followed by an HDR-DDR Data Word that contains the dummy command code value (i.e., 0x1F)
4862 is distinct from any other valid CCC flow in HDR-DDR Mode. Target Devices shall interpret this
4863 flow as the ending of the CCC modality.
4864 The flow steps are:
4865 1. Controller sends HDR Restart Pattern.
4866 2. Controller sends HDR-DDR Command Word (i.e., not Indicator or Selector), using the
4867 following values:
4868 • Bit[15] shall contain the value 1'b0, indicating a WRITE command (Controller to Target).
4869 • Bits[14:8] are reserved, and shall be ignored by the Target Devices.
4870 • Bits[7:1] shall contain the Broadcast Address (7'h7E).
4871 • Bit[0] shall follow the general rule for all Command Words in HDR-DDR CCC Header
4872 Blocks, to facilitate ACK/NACK by all Targets.
4873 3. Targets acknowledge receipt of the HDR-DDR Command Word, as above.
4874 4. Controller sends HDR-DDR Data Word, using the following values:
4875 • Bits[15:8] shall contain the dummy command code (0x1F) to end the CCC modality.
4876 • Bits[7:0] are reserved, and shall contain zeros.
4877 5. Controller sends HDR-DDR CRC Word.
4878 Targets detect this flow (i.e., Command Word, one Data Word with dummy Command
4879 Code, and CRC Word) and end CCC modality.
4880 6. Controller either sends HDR Restart Pattern, or HDR Exit Pattern.
4881 • Option 3: END HDR-DDR CCC and Return to SDR Mode
4882 This is the simplest End Procedure. It indicates the end of CCCs in HDR-DDR Mode and an
4883 immediate exit from HDR-DDR Mode, when the Controller sends the HDR Exit Pattern.

6.2.3.4 HDR-DDR Flow Control Elements
4885 The I3C HDR-DDR protocol provides several procedures that allow either the Controller or the Target to
4886 control the progress of data transfers. This Section describes these flow control elements.
4887 HDR-DDR flow control is configured by the ENDXFER CCC which controls the specific parameters of the
4888 Data Transfer Early Termination procedure. Section 6.2.3.4.4 covers the flow control configuration details.
4889 Termination of a data transfer at the request of the Receiver is detailed in Section 6.2.3.4.5 and
4890 Section 6.2.3.4.6. The procedures allowing the Receiver to terminate a data transfer shall be used only after
4891 the system is appropriately configured. Once configured, the Controller and Target(s) shall use the
4892 appropriate procedures in these sections for both Read Commands and Write Commands, since the Receiver
4893 varies depending on the direction of the Command.
4894 Note:
4895 The HDR-DDR flow control configuration defined in Section 6.2.3.4.4 and flow control procedures
4896 defined in Section 6.2.3.4.5 through Section 6.2.3.4.6 are new for version 1.1 of the I3C
4897 Specification (i.e., they were not defined in version 1.0 of the I3C Specification). Since some older
4898 Targets that support HDR-DDR Mode might not support such procedures, it is the Controller’s
4899 responsibility to determine which Targets support these flow control procedures, and also to enable
4900 HDR-DDR flow control appropriately (if supported). However, all of the flow control procedures
4901 defined in this section and its sub-sections are mandatory for Targets that support HDR-DDR Mode
4902 and also comply with version 1.1 or later of the I3C Specification, as well as version 1.1.1 or later of
4903 the I3C Basic Specification.
4904 If HDR-DDR flow control is not enabled (i.e., the default state), then:
4905 • The Target shall accept (i.e., shall absorb) all Data Words for a Write Command sent by the
4906 Controller.
4907 • This applies even if the Target does not support the Command Byte and will simply drop the
4908 data from the Controller.
4909 • If ACK/NACK is not enabled, then the Controller will have full control of all Preamble bits.
4910 • The Target may NACK (i.e., may ignore) a Read Command from the Controller.
4911 • The Controller may use the procedures defined in Section 6.2.3.4.5 to terminate a Read Command.
4912 However if this occurs, then the Target is always obligated to send the CRC Word after the last
4913 Data Word.
4914 • The Target shall not terminate a Write Command.
4915 • Additional details in Section 6.2.3.4.5 through Section 6.2.3.4.6 do not apply.
4916 If HDR-DDR flow control is supported, and if it is subsequently enabled by the Controller using the
4917 ENDXFER CCC (per Section 6.2.3.4.4), then:
4918 • The Target may NACK a Write Command if it has been configured to do so (i.e., if Bit[4] = 1'b0 in
4919 the data byte for Sub-Command 0xF7).
4920 • In this case, the Target may use the procedure in Section 6.2.3.4.1.2 Ignoring the Write
4921 Command which will be interpreted as a NACK.
4922 • For Read Commands:
4923 • The Controller and Target may use the procedures in Section 6.2.3.4.5 to either end or continue
4924 the transfer of Data Words from the Target for the Read Command. If the Controller terminates
4925 the Read transfer, then the Target shall conditionally send the CRC Word after the last Data
4926 Word, per the configuration.
4927 • For Write Commands: (i.e., if Bit[5] = 1'b0 in the data byte for Sub-Command 0xF7)
4928 • The Controller and Target may use the procedures in Section 6.2.3.4.6 to either end or continue
4929 the transfer of Data Words from the Controller for the Write Command. If the Target
4930 terminates the Write transfer, then the Controller shall conditionally send the CRC Word after
4931 the last Data Word, per the configuration.
266 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 281 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURE F001 Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
4932 • If the Target supports Write Abort, then it shall indicate this by returning Bit[6] = 1'b1 in the
4933 GETCAP2 byte, when responding to the GETCAPS CCC (see Section 5.4).
6.2.3.4.1 Command to Write Data to Target
4934 In response to a Write Command in HDR-DDR Mode, a Target shall either accept one or more Data Words,
4935 or else ignore the Write Command.
4936 Note:
4937 Per Table 80, the Target’s decision to accept or ignore the Write Command shall determine the
4938 Preamble Value of the first HDR-DDR Data Word. See Figure 110 and Figure 111.
6.2.3.4.1.1 Accepting the Write Command
4939 To accept the DDR WRITE from the Controller, the Target actively drives SDA Low before the C1 falling
4940 edge (i.e., while SCL is High) for the first HDR-DDR Data Word.
4941 Figure 114 illustrates Target acceptance (ACK) signaling for the Controller’s DDR WRITE command, using
4942 numbered blue dots that correspond to the numbered steps below.
4943 The significant steps are:
4944 1. The Controller:
4945 A. Has selected bit 0 of the Command Word (the Parity Adjustment bit) to ensure that parity
4946 bit PA0 (the last bit in the Word) is High (1'b1)
4947 B. Disables the active drive of SDA
4948 C. Enables the Open Drain class Pull-Up structure on SDA, keeping SDA High
4949 2. The first Preamble bit, PRE1, is found to be High (1'b1)
4950 3. Once t SCO elapses after the C1 rising edge, the Target actively drives SDA Low
4951 4. The Controller then:
4952 A. Finds that the second Preamble bit, PRE0, is Low (1'b0), and that therefore the Target has
4953 accepted the DDR WRITE command, and
4954 B. Disables the Open Drain class Pull-Up, and
4955 C. Drives actively SDA Low, in parallel with the Target
4956 5. Once t SCO elapses after the C1 falling edge, the Target releases the SDA on High-Z
4957 6. After a suitable delay, the Controller then starts actively driving SDA High or Low, as required
4958 by the value of the first data bit (D0.7)
4959 The Controller continues transmitting the data, eventually ending the transfer with the CRC Word. The
4960 procedure for the Controller ending the data stream is specified in Section 6.2.3.4.2.

6.2.3.4.1.2 Ignoring the Write Command
4962 To ignore the DDR WRITE from the Controller, the Target allows SDA to remain High through the C1 falling
4963 edge (i.e., while SCL goes from High to Low) for the first HDR-DDR Data Word. This is only allowed if the
4964 Controller previously used the ENDXFER CCC to enable ACK/NACK capability for the Write Command
4965 (see Section 6.2.3.4.4).
4966 Note:
4967 Reasons for the lack of a response could include that the Target might not be present on the Bus, or
4968 that the Target might not want to accept the DDR WRITE command (e.g., the Command Byte is not
4969 supported Command Byte or because the Target is not ready).
4970 Figure 115 illustrates signaling when the Target does not respond to the Controller’s DDR WRITE command,
4971 using numbered blue dots that correspond to the numbered steps below.
4972 The significant steps are:
4973 1. The Controller:
4974 A. Has selected bit 0 of the Command Word (the Parity Adjustment bit) to ensure that parity
4975 bit PA0 (last bit of the Word) has the value High (1'b1)
4976 B. Enables the Open Drain class Pull-Up structure on SDA, keeping SDA High
4977 2. The Controller finds the first Preamble bit, PRE1, to be High (1'b1)
4978 3. Because the Target does not control SDA, the second Preamble bit, PRE0, is also found to be
4979 High (1'b1)
4980 As a result, the Controller can consider the Target to be non-responsive to the DDR WRITE
4981 Command
4982 4. The Controller concludes by starting either an HDR Restart, or an HDR Exit Pattern,
4983 respectively.

6.2.3.4.2 Command to Read Data from Target
4985 In response to a Read Command in HDR-DDR Mode, a Target shall either return one or more Data Words,
4986 or else ignore the Read Command. If the Command asks the Target to return one or more Data Words, then
4987 the I3C Bus Turnaround occurs on the Preamble value 2'b10 immediately preceding the Data.
4988 Note:
4989 Per Table 79, the Target’s decision to accept or ignore the Read Command shall determine the
4990 Preamble Value of the first HDR-DDR Data Word. See Figure 116 and Figure 117 below.
6.2.3.4.2.1 Accepting the Read Command
4991 To accept the DDR READ from the Controller, the Target actively drives SDA Low before the C1 falling
4992 edge (i.e., while SCL is High) for the first HDR-DDR Data Word. Figure 116 illustrates Target acceptance
4993 (ACK) signaling for the Controller’s DDR READ command, using numbered blue dots that correspond to
4994 the numbered steps below.
4995 The significant steps are:
4996 1. The Controller:
4997 A. Has selected bit 0 of the Command Word (the Parity Adjustment bit) to ensure that parity
4998 bit PA0 (the last bit in the Word) is High (1'b1)
4999 B. Disable the active drive of SDA
5000 C. Enables the Open Drain class Pull-Up structure on SDA, keeping SDA High
5001 2. The first Preamble bit, PRE1, is found to be High (1'b1)
5002 3. Once t SCO elapses after the C1 rising edge, the Target actively drives SDA Low
5003 4. The Controller then:
5004 A. Finds that the second Preamble bit, PRE0, is Low (1'b0), and that therefore the Target has
5005 accepted the DDR READ command, and
5006 B. Disables the Open Drain class Pull-Up and releases SDA on High-Z
5007 5. Once t SCO elapses after the C1 falling edge, the Target actively drives SDA either High or Low,
5008 as required by the value of the first data bit (D0.7)
5009 The Target continues transmitting the data, eventually ending the transfer with the CRC Word. The procedure
5010 for the Target ending the data stream is specified in Section 6.2.3.4.3.

6.2.3.4.2.2 Ignoring the Read Command
5012 To ignore the DDR READ from the Controller, the Target allows SDA to remain High through the C1 falling
5013 edge (i.e., while SCL goes from High to Low) when the Controller tries to drive the first HDR-DDR Data
5014 Word.
5015 Note:
5016 Reasons for the lack of a response could include that the Target might not be present on the Bus, or
5017 that the Target might not want to accept the DDR READ command.
5018 Figure 117 illustrates signaling when the Target does not respond to the Controller’s DDR READ command,
5019 using numbered blue dots that correspond to the numbered steps below.
5020 The significant steps are:
5021 1. The Controller:
5022 A. Has selected bit 0 of the Command Word (the Parity Adjustment bit) to ensure that parity
5023 bit PA0 (last bit of the Word) has the value High (1'b1)
5024 B. Enables the Open Drain class Pull-Up structure on SDA, keeping SDA High
5025 2. The Controller finds the first Preamble bit, PRE1, to be High (1'b1)
5026 3. Because the Target does not control SDA, the second Preamble bit, PRE0, is also found to be
5027 High (1'b1)
5028 A. As a result, the Controller can consider the Target to be non-responsive to the DDR READ
5029 4. The Controller concludes by starting either an HDR Restart, or an HDR Exit Pattern,
5030 respectively.

6.2.3.4.3 End of a Complete Data Frame Transfer
5032 The number of Data Words associated with a Command is in many cases a contract between the Controller
5033 and Target; that is, it depends on the particular Command Code. However, the HDR-DDR Protocol supports
5034 the transmission of variable length Data by the Controller (for a Write) by the Target (for a Read).
5035 Additionally, a Controller may terminate a Read (see Section 6.2.3.4.4) or a Target can request a termination
5036 of a WRITE (see Section 6.2.3.4.5).
5037 For a Write (Controller to Target), the Target shall know that Data transmission is done when a CRC Word
5038 followed by the HDR Restart Pattern or HDR Exit Pattern is detected.
5039 For a Read (Target to Controller), the Target shall issue a CRC Word upon conclusion of the data transmission,
5040 and then shall Park the SDA line High on the first bit after the CRC 5-bit value, followed by tri-stating the
5041 SDA line. The Controller shall see the CRC Word, and then expect to see the 1 (Parked). The Controller shall
5042 then make sure that the SCL line is Low, and then transmit an HDR Restart Pattern or HDR Exit Pattern,
5043 after the Target has High-Z’ed the SDA line.
6.2.3.4.4 HDR-DDR Early Transfer Termination Set-Up
5044 The Controller usually ends a Write Message with a CRC Word, and then continues with either an HDR
5045 Restart Pattern or an HDR Exit Pattern. For HDR-DDR Mode, the normal model is that the Target ends a
5046 Read with a CRC Word, and then hands control of the I3C Bus back to the Controller. Both these cases are
5047 described in Section 6.2.3.4.3.
5048 However, HDR-DDR Mode also allows the Controller to terminate a Read early if necessary, for example if
5049 there is an unexpected need to regain the Bus. HDR-DDR Mode also allows the Target to request early
5050 termination of a Write data transfer if necessary, for example if the Target’s internal storage overflows.
5051 Early termination signaling is accomplished using the two Preamble bits that the Controller or Target transmit
5052 before every Data Word. As Table 81 illustrates, the first Preamble bit (PRE1) is always High (1'b1) and is
5053 read on the rising edge of SCL. The second Preamble bit (PRE0) is read on the falling edge of the same SCL
5054 pulse. If PRE0 is Low (1'b0), then this indicates termination of the data transfer; if PRE0 is High (1'b1), then
5055 the data transfer continues.
5056 There are two possibilities for the sender to end the transmission: either providing the corresponding CRC
5057 Word, or ending the data string without it. Since the CRC is computed on the complete Message, there are
5058 implementations where CRC could not be calculated in time. In order to accommodate both types of design,
5059 the HDR-DDR early Message termination offers the option of either providing the CRC Word, or not
5060 providing it.
5061 To avoid any possible collision between active drivers in the Target and Controller, the choice of whether to
5062 provide the CRC is controlled by a set-up procedure which is acknowledged by the Devices involved in the
5063 particular HDR-DDR data transfer.
5064 The ENDXFER CCC (Section 4.3.7.3.22) controls the set-up and invocation of the Data Transfer Early
5065 Termination Procedure for the HDR-DDR protocol. The related ENDXFER CCC’s Defining Byte acts as a
5066 sub-command that determines the follow-up actions.

276 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 291 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURE F001 Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
5068 ENDXFER’s Broadcast or Direct sub-command 0xF7 shall be used during Data Transfer Protocol
5069 configuration. The sub-command byte 0xF7 is followed by one additional data byte (see Table 84) containing
5070 the parameters for the HDR-DDR Data Transfer Early Termination procedure.
5071 Table 84 ENDXFER CCC Additional Data Byte for Sub-Command 0xF7 (HDR-DDR Protocol)
Bits Description Values
[7:6] CRC Word Indicator 2'b11: No CRC Word follows Early
Termination request
2'b01: CRC Word follows Early
Termination request (Checksum
uses CRC-5 algorithm, per
Section 6.2.3.6)
Other: Reserved for future definition
by MIPI Alliance
[5] Enable WRITE Early Termination Request 1'b0: Enable
1'b1: Disable
[4] Enable ACK/NACK Capability for WRITE Command 1'b0: Enable
1'b1: Disable
[3:0] Reserved for future use. –
5072 The Controller starts the set-up procedure by sending the ENDXFER CCC with sub-command 0xF7 and the
5073 additional data byte, indicating the desired set of parameters. If the Controller uses the Direct version of
5074 ENDXFER, then the RnW bit shall be 1'b0 (WRITE) for the directly addressed Devices.
5075 Then the Controller sends the Direct ENDXFER CCC with sub-command 0xF7. It addresses each distinct
5076 recipient of the previous ENDXFER with the RnW bit set to 1'b1 (READ). Note that a READ command
5077 cannot be sent to a Group Address. Each addressed Device shall respond with its active additional data byte;
5078 if the value is identical to what was sent, then the Controller receives confirmation that the set-up is correct.
5079 If the value is different, then the Controller can repeat the ENDXFER CCC with the 0xF7 sub-command,
5080 using either the Broadcast version or the Direct version, with RnW set to 1'b0 (WRITE) for the directly
5081 addressed Devices.
5082 If the Controller determines that the set-up was successful, then it shall then send the ENDXFER CCC with
5083 sub-command 0xAA. If the Direct version of ENDXFER is used, then RnW is set to 1'b0 (WRITE) for the
5084 directly addressed Devices and the data is set to 0xAA (i.e., the sub-command value is reused for the data
5085 byte value, to facilitate error detection).
5086 The Controller then checks for correct receipt of the command by using the Direct ENDXFER CCC with
5087 sub-command 0xAA, and with RnW set to 1'b1 (READ), for each directly addressed Device (not for a Group
5088 Address). Each addressed Device shall respond with a data byte containing the additional data byte pertaining
5089 to ENDXFER sub-command 0xF7 that is currently active in its system. If this value is correct, then the
5090 Controller shall begin communications using the agreed-upon Data Transfer Early Termination procedure. If
5091 the value is not correct, then the Controller shall either abort the Data Transfer Early Termination procedure,
5092 or else repeat the set-up process.
5093 The Controller may start a new set-up at any time during the configuration process, or during the running
5094 stage, by sending an ENDXFER CCC with sub-command 0xF7 and a new additional data byte. The new set-
5095 up overwrites the old set-up.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 277
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 292 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F001 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.2.3.4.5 Controller Ends or Continues the Read Transfer
5096 The Controller has the option to either end, or continue, a Read transfer from the Target. The early ending
5097 can be done either with or without the CRC Word, as described in this Section.
5098 Note:
5099 Per Table 80, the Controller’s decision to continue the Read Transfer shall determine the Preamble
5100 Value of the next HDR-DDR Data Word, whereas the Controller’s decision to end the Read Transfer
5101 is effectively a Preamble Value of 2'b10 followed by an HDR Restart Pattern or HDR Exit Pattern.
5102 See Figure 115, Figure 116, and Figure 117.
5103 The procedures for ending the Read Transfer without the CRC Word and with the CRC Word are identical
5104 for the first 7 steps, as shown below. The Target shall determine whether to emit the CRC Word based on
5105 prior configuration using the ENDXFER CCC, per Section 6.2.3.4.4.
6.2.3.4.5.1 Ending the Read Transfer without CRC Word
5106 To end the DDR Read transfer, the Controller actively drives SDA Low before the C1 falling edge (i.e., while
5107 SCL is High), to signal the Target to stop returning data.
5108 Figure 119 illustrates the ending of the Read transfer without the CRC Word, using numbered blue dots that
5109 correspond to the numbered steps below.
5110 The significant steps are:
5111 1. After the last parity bit (PAR0), the Target drives SDA High for the next Preamble bit (PRE1)
5112 which has the value 1'b1
5113 2. The Controller then:
5114 A. On SCL, starts the Rising edge of clock pulse C1, and
5115 B. Enables the Open Drain class Pull-Up structure on SDA
5116 3. Once t SCO elapses after C1’s rising edge, the Target then:
5117 A. Stops actively driving SDA, and
5118 B. Releases SDA on High-Z
5119 4. After a time period longer than the Target’s t SCO , the Controller starts driving SDA Low.
5120 One of the simplest ways to determine the necessary delay is to use a half cycle of the SCL clock.
5121 It could be even the full cycle, which would preserve the phase on the driver’s logic block. The
5122 resultant SCL pulse is longer than the 50 ns that is required for coexistence on the Bus with
5123 Legacy I2C Devices. The signal waveform is a typical Repeated START; this is acceptable, since
5124 in the case of a Mixed Bus it is required to be followed by the HDR Exit Pattern and a STOP.
5125 5. After another delay similar to the one described in step 4, the Controller starts driving SCL
5126 Low, starting C1’s falling edge
5127 6. The Target finds SDA being driven Low by the Controller, setting the second Preamble bit
5128 (PRE0) to 1'b0. Since this means the end of the Read transaction, the Target keeps SDA on
5129 High-Z.
5130 7. After another delay similar to the one described in step 4, the Controller starts driving SDA
5131 High in order to prepare for either the HDR Restart Pattern or the HDR Exit Pattern.
5132 At this point, SCL is Low and SDA becomes High (actively driven by the Controller), while the
5133 Target has both lines on High-Z
5134 At this point the Target-to-Controller Read data transfer has concluded.

16-Dec-2024
6.2.3.4.5.2 Ending the Read Transfer with CRC Word
5136 To end the DDR Read transfer, the Controller actively drives SDA Low before the C1 falling edge (i.e., while
5137 SCL is High), to signal the Target to stop returning data and return the CRC Word.
5138 Figure 120 shows the signal diagram for the case where the READ transfer ends with the Target providing
5139 the CRC Word, with the 4'hC token. The figure uses numbered blue and red dots that correspond to the
5140 numbered steps below.
5141 The significant steps are:
5142 1. After the last parity bit (PAR0), the Target drives SDA High for the next Preamble bit (PRE1),
5143 which must be 1'b1
5144 2. The Controller:
5145 A. Starts the SCL Rising edge of C1 clock pulse, and
5146 B. Enables the Open Drain class pull-up structure on SDA
5147 3. Once t SCO elapses after the rising edge of C1, the Target then:
5148 A. Stops the active drive of SDA, and
5149 B. Releases SDA on High-Z
5150 4. After a period of time longer than the Target’s t SCO , the Controller then starts driving SDA Low.
5151 One of the simplest ways to determine the necessary delay is to use a half cycle of the SCL
5152 clock. It could be even the full cycle, which would preserve the phase on the driver’s logic
5153 block. The resultant SCL pulse is longer than the 50 ns required for coexistence with Legacy
5154 I2C Devices. The signal waveform is a typical Repeated START; this is acceptable, since in the
5155 case of a Mixed Bus it is required to be followed by the HDR Exit Pattern and a STOP.
5156 5. After another delay similar to that described in step 4, the Controller Starts driving SCL Low,
5157 starting the falling edge of C1
5158 6. The Target then:
5159 A. Finds SDA being driven Low by the Controller, setting the second Preamble bit (PRE0) to
5160 1'b0
5161 B. Since this condition means the end of the Read transaction, the Target preserves the SDA
5162 on High-Z
5163 7. From this point on, the red bordered dots apply. After another delay similar to that descried in
5164 step 4, the Controller starts driving SDA High. This can be done even immediately after the
5165 falling edge of the C1, since it is under the Controller’s control.
5166 8. The Controller has provided the rising edge of the first SCL of the CRC Word, CLK_CRC1.
5167 Since SDA was High, the Target assesses the first bit of the CRC token as 1'b1.
5168 9. At the falling edge of CLK_CRC1, the Target reads the second bit of the CRC token as 1'b1,
5169 since the Controller was still driving SDA High.
5170 10. The Controller then drives SDA Low
5171 11. At the rising edge of CLK_CRC2, the Target read the third bit of the CRC token as 1'b0, since
5172 the Controller was driving SDA Low.
5173 12. After its t SCO the Target starts driving SDA Low, in parallel with the Controller (there is no
5174 conflict, both lines are driven Low by the Devices).
5175 13. The Controller then:
5176 A. Starts driving the falling edge of the CLK_CRC2 Low, and
5177 B. Releases SDA on High-Z
5178 14. The Target (and the Controller) reads the fourth bit of the CRC token as 1'b0, since the Target
5179 was driving SDA Low
5180 15. After a period of time longer than the Target’s t SCO , the Target starts driving SDA per the
5181 calculated CRC. The Target has had enough time to switch its output to the calculated CRC
5182 (almost two SCL clocks).
280 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 295 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURE F001 Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
5183 At this point, the Read data transfer from the Target to the Controller has entered the CRC-based Ending
5184 Procedure.
5185 Note:
5186 Up until dot 7, the steps are identical to Figure 119. The numbered blue dots (steps 1–7) are the
5187 same as ending the Read Transfer without the CRC Word, and the numbered red dots (steps 8–15)
5188 show the additional steps for ending the Read Transfer with the CRC Word.

6.2.3.4.5.3 Continuing the Read Transfer
5190 To continue the DDR Read data transfer from the Target, the Controller allows SDA to remain High through
5191 the C1 falling edge (i.e., while SCL goes from High to Low) at the start of the next HDR-DDR Data Word.
5192 Figure 121 illustrates continuation of the Read data transfer, using numbered blue dots that correspond to
5193 the numbered steps below.
5194 The significant steps are:
5195 1. After the last parity bit (PAR0), the Target drives SDA High for the next Preamble bit (PRE1)
5196 which must have the value 1'b1
5197 2. The Controller then:
5198 A. On SCL, starts the Rising edge of clock pulse C1, and
5199 B. Enables the Open Drain class Pull-Up structure on SDA
5200 3. Once the Target’s t SCO elapses after C1’s rising edge, the Target then:
5201 A. Stops actively driving SDA, and
5202 B. Releases SDA on High-Z
5203 4. At clock pulse C2’s falling edge, the Target finds that SDA is High
5204 5. After the Target’s t SCO elapses, the Target then starts actively driving SDA for the payload’s
5205 first data bit (D0.7)
5206 6. After a suitable delay, the Controller then:
5207 A. Disables the Open Drain class Pull-Up structure on SDA, and
5208 B. Sets SDA on High-Z
5209 This delay shall be greater than or equal to Target’s t SCO . The start of C2’s rising edge would be a
5210 safe moment. Depending upon the Controller’s design, a shorter delay could be used.
5211 7. After the Target’s t SCO , elapses, the Target starts driving SDA for the payload’s second data bit
5212 (D0.6)
5213 After step 7, the Target-to-Controller data transfer continues.

6.2.3.4.6 Target Ends or Continues the Write Transfer
5215 The Target has the option to either end, or continue, a Write transfer from the Controller. The early ending
5216 can be done either with or without the CRC Word, as detailed in this Section.
5217 Note:
5218 Per Table 79, the Target’s decision to continue or end the Write Transfer shall determine the
5219 Preamble Value of the next HDR-DDR Data Word. See Figure 122, Figure 123, and Figure 124.
5220 The procedures for ending the Read Transfer without the CRC Word and with the CRC Word are
5221 identical for the first 6 steps, as shown below. The Controller shall determine whether to emit the
5222 CRC Word based on prior agreement with the Target(s) as configured with the ENDXFER CCC, per
5223 Section 6.2.3.4.4.
6.2.3.4.6.1 Ending the Write Transfer without CRC Word
5224 To end the DDR Write transfer, the Target actively drives SDA Low before the C1 falling edge (i.e., while
5225 SCL is High), to request the Controller to end the transfer.
5226 Figure 122 illustrates the ending of the Write transfer without the CRC Word, using numbered blue dots that
5227 correspond to the numbered steps below.
5228 The significant steps are:
5229 1. After the last parity bit (PAR0), the Controller drives SDA High for the next Preamble bit
5230 (PRE1) which must have the value 1'b1
5231 2. The Controller then:
5232 A. On SCL, starts the rising edge of clock pulse C1, and
5233 B. Disables the active drive of SDA, and
5234 C. Enables the Open Drain class Pull-Up structure on SDA
5235 3. After the Target’s t SCO elapses, the Target actively drives SDA Low
5236 4. The Controller then:
5237 A. On SCL, starts the falling edge of clock pulse C1
5238 The Controller knows that SDA was pulled Low by the Target; therefore, PRE0 is 1'b0
5239 B. The Controller then starts actively driving SDA Low
5240 At this point both the Controller and the Target are actively driving SDA Low, in parallel
5241 5. Once the Target’s t SCO elapses, the Target releases SDA on High-Z
5242 6. After a suitable delay, the Controller then starts actively driving SDA High
5243 One way to determine the necessary delay is to use one half of an SCL clock cycle.
5244 7. At this point, following another delay similar to the one in step 6:
5245 A. SDA is High, actively driven by the Controller
5246 B. SCL is Low, actively driven by the Controller
5247 C. The Target has both SCL and SDA released on High-Z
5248 D. The Controller can start either an HDR Restart Pattern, or an HDR Exit Pattern
5249 At this point, the Controller-to-Target data transfer has concluded.

Public Release Edition\n--- Page 300 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F001 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.2.3.4.6.2 Ending the Write Transfer with CRC Word
5251 To end the DDR Write transfer, the Target actively drives SDA Low before the C1 falling edge (i.e., while
5252 SCL is High), to request the Controller to end the transfer.
5253 Figure 123 shows the signal diagram for the case where the WRITE transfer ends with the Controller
5254 providing the CRC, with 4'hC token. The figure uses numbered blue and red dots that correspond to the
5255 numbered steps below.
5256 The significant steps are:
5257 1. After the last parity bit (PAR0), the Controller drives SDA High for the next Preamble bit
5258 (PRE1) which must be 1'b1
5259 2. The Controller then:
5260 A. Starts the rising edge of C1, and
5261 B. Disables the active drive of SDA, and
5262 C. Enables the Open Drain class pull-up structure on SDA
5263 3. After the Target’s t SCO elapses, the Target actively drives SDA Low
5264 4. The Controller tan:
5265 A. Starts the falling edge of C1, and
5266 B. Knows that, since the Target pulled SDA Low, the PRE0 bit has value 1'b0; and
5267 C. Starts actively driving SDA Low, in parallel with the Target
5268 5. After the Target’s t SCO elapses, the Target releases SDA on High-Z
5269 6. From this point on, the red bordered dots apply. After a suitable delay, the Controller starts
5270 actively driving SDA High.
5271 7. The Controller has provided the rising edge of the first SCL of the CRC Word (CLK_CRC1).
5272 Since SDA was High, the Target reads the first bit of the CRC token as 1'b1.
5273 8. At the falling edge of CLK_CRC1, the Target reads the second bit of the CRC token as 1'b1,
5274 since the Controller was still driving SDA High.
5275 9. The Controller then drives SDA Low
5276 10. At the rising edge of CLK_CRC2, the Target reads the third bit of the CRC token as 1'b0, since
5277 the Controller drove SDA Low.
5278 11. At the falling edge of CLK_CRC2, the Target (and the Controller) reads the fourth bit of the
5279 CRC token as 1'b0, since the Target drove SDA Low.
5280 12. The Controller then starts driving the SDA per the calculated CRC.
5281 At this point, the WRITE data transfer from Controller to Target has entered the CRC-based Ending
5282 Procedure.
5283 Note:
5284 Up until dot 6, the transactions are identical to Figure 122. The numbered blue dots (steps 1–6) are
5285 the same as ending the Write Transfer without the CRC Word, and the numbered red dots (steps 7–
5286 12) show the additional steps for ending the Write Transfer with the CRC Word.

16-Dec-2024
6.2.3.4.6.3 Continuing the Write Transfer
5288 To continue the DDR Write data transfer, the Target allows SDA to remain High through the C1 falling edge
5289 (i.e., while SCL goes from High to Low) at the start of the next HDR-DDR Data Word.
5290 Figure 124 illustrates continuation of the Write data transfer, using numbered blue dots that correspond to
5291 the numbered steps below.
5292 The significant steps are:
5293 1. After the last parity bit (PAR0), the Controller drives SDA High for the next Preamble bit
5294 (PRE1) which must have the value 1'b1
5295 2. The Controller then:
5296 A. On SCL, starts the rising edge of clock pulse C1, and
5297 B. Disables the active drive of SDA, and
5298 C. Enables the Open Drain class Pull-Up structure on SDA
5299 3. The Controller then:
5300 A. On SCL, starts the falling edge of clock pulse C1
5301 The Controller knows that the Target left SDA High; therefore, PRE0 is 1'b1
5302 B. The Controller then starts actively driving SDA High
5303 4. After a suitable delay, the Controller then starts actively driving SDA either High or Low, as
5304 required for the payload’s first data bit (D0.7).
5305 One simple way to determine the delay is to use one half of an SCL clock cycle.
5306 5. On SCL, the Controller then starts the rising edge of clock pulse C2.
5307 At this point, the Target has both SCL and SDA on High-Z
5308 6. The Target then registers the value of the payload’s first data bit (D0.7)
5309 7. The Target then registers the value of the payload’s second data bit (D0.6)
5310 After step 7, the Controller-to-Target data transfer continues.

6.2.3.4.7 Controller Clock Stalling in HDR-DDR Mode
5312 In HDR DDR Mode, the I3C Controller may stall the I3C Bus during the SCL Low period at specific
5313 transitory conditions.
5314 Controller Clock stalling may be necessary and could be applied:
5315 • Because the absolute or relative timing of a Message to a specific Target, or to all Targets, needs to
5316 be carefully controlled. Clock Stalling provides the Controller with fine-grained data timing control.
5317 • Because the I3C Controller needs to internally synchronize data. This may be due to parts of the
5318 Controller’s system waking up in response to data, to changing state, or to otherwise needing time
5319 during a transaction in cases of Overflow/Underrun.
5320 As detailed in the following sub-sections, during HDR DDR communication the most common points at
5321 which the I3C Controller may stall (i.e., may hold SCL Low) are:
5322 • First data bit following a DDR Write Command Acknowledge
5323 • First token bit following a DDR write early termination by a Target
5324 • Write data transfer, PRE1 of data word or CRC word
5325 • Read data transfer, PRE1 of data word or CRC word
5326 • HDR Restart Pattern or HDR Exit Pattern first SDA falling edge generation
6.2.3.4.7.1 First Data Bit Following a DDR Write Command Acknowledge
5327 The DDR Write Acknowledge requires the Target Devices to send an ACK/NACK for a DDR command. The
5328 ACK is done during the SCL High period, which could require the Target Devices to analyze and prepare a
5329 response to the command. This ACK will last through the SCL High period, and the Target Devices will
5330 release the SDA line during SCL Low period. This could be dependent on the t sco and setup time, which could
5331 require a longer time for a correct ACK/NACK analysis and data transfer. During this time, the Controller
5332 may stall the Clock to analyze the ACK/NACK response of the Target Devices. See Figure 125.

6.2.3.4.7.2 First Token Bit Following a DDR Write Early Termination by a Target
5334 During an early termination, the Target drives the SDA Low during the PRE0 to terminate a Controller write
5335 operation. This will require the Controller to abort sending data and send a CRC or proceed with an HDR
5336 Restart. This requires the Controller to take time to process the write termination request. The Controller may
5337 stall the Clock and proceed with the appropriate course of action based on the configuration that was set
5338 during the ENDXFER setup. See Figure 126.

6.2.3.5 HDR-DDR Error Detection
5354 Four error types are defined for HDR-DDR Mode:
5355 1. Framing – the Preamble 2-bits before Command and Data shall be valid values per Table 79.
5356 This supports a positional error detection mechanism:
5357 • A Command Word shall always follow the Enter HDR CCC and HDR Restart Pattern, and
5358 shall never appear in any other position. It is an error condition for a Command Word to
5359 appear in any other position, or to be missing where expected.
5360 • A Data Word shall always follow either a Command Word or another Data Word, and shall
5361 never appear in any other position. It is an error condition for a Data Word to appear in any
5362 other position, or to be missing where expected.
5363 • A single CRC Word shall always follow the last Data Word for a Command, such that it ends
5364 the Message. As a result, a CRC Word shall always be followed by either the HDR Restart
5365 Pattern or the HDR Exit Pattern. It is error condition for a CRC Word to appear in any other
5366 position, or to be missing where expected.
5367 • The first nibble of a valid CRC shall contain the allowed value 4'hC. Any other value in the
5368 first nibble should be considered a framing error.
5369 2. Parity encoding (for transmitters) parity checking (for receivers) shall be performed on all
5370 Command Words and Data Words. Mismatched parity is an error condition.
5371 3. CRC-5 encoding (for transmitters) and CRC-5 checking (for receivers) shall be performed on
5372 all payload bits for Command Words and Data Words. Mismatched CRC is an Error.
5373 4. NACK by the Target on a Read command is not a normal behavior (ACK is normal). The
5374 Controller may choose to treat a NACK on a Read command as a possible framing error, and
5375 take the actions detailed below.
5376 Any error detected by the Target should result solely in waiting for HDR Exit Pattern or HDR Restart Pattern
5377 to be safe.
5378 If the Controller detects an error in a Read, then it shall issue SCL clocks until 19 SCL clocks (38 bits) have
5379 been seen with SDA High. Because a continuous High run of this length cannot occur in a valid data
5380 transmission, this ensures that the Target has released the line. The Controller shall emit either an HDR Exit
5381 Pattern or an HDR Restart Pattern.
5382 Note:
5383 The Controller can choose to treat the NACK of a Read command as a possible line error, and
5384 therefore use the same approach in order to ensure that the Target is not driving the Bus.
5385 If an error occurs in the RnW Bit during a Private Read Transfer, then the Write Data from the Controller
5386 might conflict with the Read Data from the Target. Both the Controller and the Target should always monitor
5387 the Data that they each transmit. If the Controller or Target performs such monitoring, and if the monitored
5388 Data differs from the Data that the Controller or Target intended to send (except for Data transferred during
5389 a Dynamic Address Arbitration procedure), then this shall be considered an error. After detecting this error,
5390 both the Controller and the Target should stop the transmission; if they do so, then the Target shall wait for
5391 an HDR Exit Pattern or HDR Restart Pattern. After the Controller sends the HDR Exit Pattern or HDR Restart
5392 Pattern, then it may retry the transmission.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 295
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 310 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F001 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.2.3.6 HDR-DDR CRC-5 Algorithm
5393 The CRC-5 checksum is computed on a complete Message, including the Command Word and all Data
5394 Words.
5395 • For a Command Word, the CRC-5 checksum is computed based on the value of the 16-bit payload,
5396 including:
5397 • The Read vs. Write bit
5398 • The Command Value
5399 • The Target Address, and
5400 • The lowest-order bit (Parity adjustment).
5401 • For Data Words, the CRC-5 checksum is computed based on all Data 16-bit values transmitted for
5402 that Command.
5403 The CRC-5 value is initialized to 0x1F. The CRC-5 polynomial is the same as used in [USB01]:
5404 CRC5 = X5 + X2 + X0
5405 The reason why CRC-5 is adequate is that most errors will be caught by the Parity bits, or by incorrect
5406 Preamble bits. The Parity bits will detect runs of one, two, three, or more errors in a row. Any Clock errors
5407 not detected by the Parity bits would generally be detected as framing errors. That is, the Preamble would
5408 not match the allowed patterns, including 2'b01 leading into the CRC. As a result, any error that could be
5409 missed by the Parity bits will result in a shift of the CRC polynomial, and therefore will be detected as a CRC
5410 error. There would have to be two separated errors in the same Word, with either both errors occurring in
5411 even-index bits or with both errors occurring in odd-index bits, to produce correct Parity bits. However these
5412 two errors would not correct the CRC, and as a result would still be detected. Even with two such error pairs
5413 in two different Words, the probability of producing the same CRC value is very low, even though the CRC-5
5414 checksum has only 32 possible values. Since any higher error density would render the I3C Bus generally
5415 unusable, it can be concluded that the protection that CRC-5 provides is sufficient for the use cases addressed
5416 by this Specification.
5417 CRC-5 is specified in [USB01]. Two versions of the logic for computing the CRC-5 checksum are shown
5418 below:
5419 • First, for one bit at a time,
5420 • Second, for the more practical situation of two bits at a time (i.e., on SCL falling edge).
5421
5422 // CRC5 builds from each bit as it comes in, using the registered SDA_r.
5423 // The next_CRC5 is registered on each cycle as CRC5[4:0].
5424 // But note that it would have to be on each SCL edge, so two
5425 // alternating buffers would be needed.
5426 assign next_crc0 = SDA_r ^ CRC5[4];
5427 assign next_CRC5[4:0] = {CRC5[3:2], next_crc0^CRC5[1],
5428 CRC5[0], next_crc0};
5429
5430 // CRC5 builds from 2 bits at a time (registered d_rise_r and live d_fall),
5431 // on falling edge of SCL (by convention - could be on rising instead).
5432 // The d_fall could be d_fall_r, but then order must match input order
5433 // of the data bits.
5434 // The CRC therefore processes the two bits by combining the 2 bit shifts.
5435 // The next_CRC5 is registered on each cycle as CRC5[4:0]
5436 assign next_CRC5 = {CRC5[2], d_rise_r^CRC5[4]^CRC5[1], d_fall^CRC5[3]^CRC5[0],
5437 d_rise_r^CRC5[4], d_fall^CRC5[3]};
296 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 311 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURE F002 Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024

6.4 F003: HDR-BT Mode
5442 This Section defines I3C Optional Feature F003: HDR-BT Mode.
5443 Table 86 Optional Feature 003 Status
Optional Feature ID F003
Optional Feature Name HDR-BT Mode
Optional Feature Version 1
Optional Feature specification was last updated in I3C Spec I3C Basic v1.2
Version
Optional Feature is not compatible with other Optional Features F008 D2D Tunneling
Optional Feature is included in I3C Basic Yes
5444 Changes in this Version
5445 For detailed changes in this version, see the “diff” file between I3C v1.1.1 and I3C v1.2 in the MIPI
5446 Specification Repository [MIPI09].
6.4.1 Scope and Purpose (Informative)
5447 The scope of Section 6.4 is limited to fully specifying I3C Optional Feature F003: HDR-BT Mode. An I3C
5448 Device that implements F003 is required to implement all shall statements in Section 6.4.3, and can
5449 optionally implement any combination of may statements in Section 6.4.3. In addition, MIPI Alliance
5450 recommends that manufacturers follow all should statements in Section 6.4.3.
6.4.2 Technical Overview (Informative)
5451 HDR-BT is provided to give the highest possible throughput using a Clock-and-Data transmission model.
5452 Based on the number of SDA Lanes (4 for Quad Lane configuration, 2 for Dual Lane, or 1 for Single Lane),
5453 HDR-BT provides 8x, 4x, and 2x the raw SDR rate, respectively, at the same clock frequency. Real data rates
5454 are about 8.7x, 4.36x, and 2.18x SDR real data rates, and 4.8x, 2.4x, and 1.2x HDR-DDR real data rates. In
5455 particular, at 12.5 MHz HDR-BT can move real data at 97 Mbps (12 Mbytes/second) over Quad Lane
5456 (SDA[3:0]), and ½ and ¼ that respectively for the Dual Lane and Single Lane configurations.
5457 HDR-BT is an I3C HDR mode like the others. As a result, it is entered in the standard way (see the CCC
5458 ENTHDR3, Section 4.3.7.3.9) and exited via the HDR Exit Pattern. As with the other HDR modes, Messages
5459 while in the HDR-BT mode are separated using the HDR Restart pattern.
5460 HDR-BT also brings other key features in addition to speed:
5461 • CRC-16 (and optionally CRC-32) for data integrity.
5462 Significantly, HDR-BT also has the receiver verify to the sender whether the transmitted CRC
5463 value for each Message Frame actually matched the CRC value calculated from the received data.
5464 This allows the transmitter to know with certainty whether it can release the buffer and proceed, or
5465 will have to re-send the data (whether immediately or scheduled for a later time).
5466 • Allows the option for the Target to drive the SCL Clock during Read, when the Target supports it.
5467 This can be useful with long transmission lines, as there is no Clock-out to Data-in roundtrip time.
5468 • Decouples Command and Control from Data, allowing division of labor between the Peripheral
5469 and system:
5470 • Fully supportive of classical I2C and I3C protocol models, including write of Index or
5471 Command byte (or bytes) preceding the Data (for write) or the Read request (for read)
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 299
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 314 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
5472 • Also appropriate for writing and reading file equivalents, where the command is the offset,
5473 using up to 32-bit offsets
5474 • Uses a wide data block model to facilitate more natural processing for higher rate data.
5475 • In particular, because of the use of wide data blocks, HDR-BT is well suited to direct mapping
5476 to internal SRAM (e.g., 32-bit, 64-bit, etc.), wide internal buses (e.g., AXI, OCP, etc.), and
5477 Encryption/decryption cipher modes with inherent block sizes (e.g., 64-bit, 128-bit).
5478 • Although HDR-BT moves data in 32-byte data blocks, the last data block can “be ragged” and
5479 transmit fewer than 32 data bytes (i.e., 2, 4, 6, … to 32 bytes).
5480 • HDR-BT is consistent no matter how many Data Lanes are used (1, 2, or 4 SDA Lanes).
5481 • Further, HDR-BT is intended to allow a fully-registered interface on both sides – no
5482 combinatorial decisions required
5483 While HDR-BT is primarily aimed at Multi-Lane Buses, it can also be used in a Single-Lane context,
5484 providing about 1.2x the real data rate of HDR-DDR. This provides the option of using a single high-rate
5485 protocol regardless of the number of data Lanes needed. The rest of this Section will focus on Dual Lane and
5486 Quad Lane configurations, but HDR-BT protocol in Single Lane configuration works exactly the same – it
5487 just takes 2x or 4x more clocks to move the same amount of data.
300 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 315 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.4.3 Technical Specification
5488 The transport form of HDR-BT is purely byte-oriented. Unlike other I3C forms, the protocol has no extra
5489 bits, just a pure byte stream, even for control and data bytes.
5490 The wire form of HDR-BT is a DDR style, meaning that the 1, 2, or 4 SDA Lanes change on each clock edge
5491 and are read on the following edge, using a Setup time model (vs. Hold):
5492 • For the Controller on Write, which Drives the clock and data, this means the SDA(s) change(s)
5493 only after the SCL has completely transitioned (as with normal I3C).
5494 • For the Target on Read, when only driving the SDA(s) but not SCL, the SDA(s) change(s) only
5495 after the SCL change has been received/detected.
5496 • For the Target on Read, when the Target is driving both clock and data, this means the SDA(s)
5497 change(s) only after the SCL has completely transitioned.
5498 Signal diagrams for starting HDR-BT Mode transfers are shown in Figure 131 and Figure 132. Figure 131
5499 shows the HDR-BT Header Block (see Section 6.4.3.3.1) after ENTHDR3, and Figure 132 shows the
5500 HDR-BT Header Block after the HDR Restart Pattern.

Figure 132 HDR-BT Header Block After HDR Restart Pattern
5503 Note:
5504 Both Figure 131 and Figure 132 are derived from the edge-triggering examples shown in
5505 Section 6.1.4. In both cases, the Controller positions SDA[0] (i.e., SDA) after the falling SCL edge,
5506 and before the SCL rising edge labeled “C1”. This bit shall be read by the addressed Target (or
5507 Group) as Bit[0] of the Address byte, which is the “RnW” bit for the transfer (per Section 6.4.3.3).
5508 The HDR-BT Header Block is always sent according to the Lane configuration of the currently
5509 configured HDR-BT Data Transfer Coding for Multi-Lane (see Section 6.7.3.5.3.1) which is a global
5510 setting for the I3C Bus, using the defined Bit Packing for Data Bytes and Transition Bytes.
5511 Figure 133 illustrates a typical HDR-BT Mode Frame with two HDR-BT transfers and associated data:
5512 • I3C START or Repeated START
5513 • I3C CCC to Enter HDR-BT Mode
5514 • After entering HDR-BT Mode, there is one HDR-BT transfer. This can take either of two forms:
5515 1. A write or read transfer with data, which starts with an HDR-BT Header Block, followed by
5516 one or more HDR-BT Data Blocks, and then an HDR-BT CRC Block; or
5517 2. A write or read transfer without data which only uses an HDR-BT Header Block (i.e., no
5518 HDR-BT Data Blocks and no HDR-BT CRC Block).
5519 • Then an HDR Restart Pattern, followed by another HDR-BT transfer with data, having a similar
5520 flow.
5521 • Finally, HDR-BT Mode is ended via the HDR Exit Pattern followed by I3C STOP.\n--- Page 317 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024

5523 Note:
5524 Although Figure 133 shows several examples of HDR-BT transfers in a particular order, in actual
5525 practice there is no need for these transfers to follow this order. See Section 6.4.3.3 for more details
5526 on the HDR-BT structured protocol elements that comprise transfers in HDR-BT Mode. Additionally,
