6.4.3.1 SDA Lane Bit Packing: Data Bytes (Commands, Data, CRC)
5529 On the SDA[x] Lanes, HDR-BT always packs bytes of Data in the same way, based on number of Lanes as
5530 shown in Table 87 through Table 89.
5531 Note that Transition control packing is different, per Section 6.4.3.2.
5532 Note:
5533 Unlike SDR Mode, the bits are transmitted starting with LSb and progressing to MSb.
5534 Table 87 Data Byte Bit Packing: Single Lane
SDA Lane Clock 0 Clock 1 Clock 2 Clock 3
SDA[0] Bit0 Bit1 Bit2 Bit3 Bit4 Bit5 Bit6 Bit7
Table 88 Data Byte Bit Packing: Dual Lane
SDA Lane Clock 0 Clock 1
SDA[0] Bit0 Bit2 Bit4 Bit6
SDA[1] Bit1 Bit3 Bit5 Bit7
Table 89 Data Byte Bit Packing: Quad Lane
SDA Lane Clock 0
SDA[0] Bit0 Bit4
SDA[1] Bit1 Bit5
SDA[2] Bit2 Bit6
SDA[3] Bit3 Bit7
304 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 319 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.4.3.2 SDA Lane Bit Packing: Transition Bytes
5535 HDR-BT always packs bytes of Transition content, whether pure Transition or Transition and Control, in a
5536 consistent way. In particular, the SDA[0] Lane always follows the I3C “Park1,High-Z” convention for the
5537 first two half-clocks of the byte. As a result, SDA[0] is always 1 (Parked) for the rising SCL edge, and then
5538 treated as High-Z for the falling SCL edge, to enable flow control.
5539 To keep things simple, the convention is to treat the “Park1” and “High-Z” as Bit[0] and Bit[1] of the byte,
5540 with the low-level logic handling the repositioning based on number of Lanes as shown in Table 90 through
5541 Table 92. That is, the byte received will have 2'b11 in Bits[1:0] when the High-Z was not driven Low, with
5542 the remaining bits representing the content.
5543 When compared with the bit packing for data bytes, the transition bytes change as follows:
5544 • For Single Lane: No changes, since all Bits are sent in order.
5545 • For Dual Lane: Bit[2] is sent where Bit[1] would typically be sent for a data byte (i.e., the rising
5546 edge of SDA[1] for Clock 0).
5547 • For Quad Lane: Bit[4] is sent where Bit[1] would typically be sent for a data byte (i.e., the rising
5548 edge of SDA[1] for Clock 0).
Table 90 Transition Byte Bit Packing: Single Lane
SDA Lane Clock 0 Clock 1 Clock 2 Clock 3
SDA[0] Park1 High-Z Bit2 Bit3 Bit4 Bit5 Bit6 Bit7
Note:
Compared to a data byte, Bits[7:2] are unchanged. See Table 87 for comparison.
Table 91 Transition Byte Bit Packing: Dual Lane with Bit2 Swizzle
SDA Lane Clock 0 Clock 1
SDA[0] Park1 High-Z Bit4 Bit6
SDA[1] Bit2 (See Note 1) Bit3 Bit5 Bit7
Note:
1. Bit2 takes the typical place for Bit1 (i.e., for a data byte), since the High-Z bit on SDA[0] is
always Bit1 for a transition byte.
2. Compared to a data byte, Bits[7:3] are unchanged. See Table 88 for comparison.
Table 92 Transition Byte Bit Packing: Quad Lane with Bit4 Swizzle
SDA Lane Clock 0
SDA[0] Park1 High-Z
SDA[1] Bit4 (See Note 1) Bit5
SDA[2] Bit2 Bit6
SDA[3] Bit3 Bit7
Note:
1. Bit4 takes the typical place for Bit1 (i.e., for a data byte), since the High-Z bit on SDA[0] is
always Bit1 for a transition byte.
2. Compared to a data byte, Bits[3:2] and Bits[7:5] are unchanged. See Table 88 for
comparison.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 305
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 320 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.4.3.3 HDR-BT Mode Structured Protocol Elements
5549 The main structuring of the HDR-BT mode is broken into 3 top-level units:
Header Block N * Data Blocks CRC-16 or CRC-32
7 bytes Each block is 32 bytes data + 1 byte control 6 bytes
Target may emit SCL if Read
5550 Where:
5551 • The Header indicates the Target (or Group) and the Direction for the transfer, as well as 4 bytes
5552 optionally associated with command/index/offset and/or expected length.
5553 This follows the normal I2C and I3C protocol of the first byte(s) on Write being the
5554 index/command, and Write preceding Read containing the index/command.
5555 Note:
5556 The actual interpretation is a contract between Controller and Target.
5557 • Data is 0 or more 32-byte Data Blocks. The processed length of the last block may be ragged.
5558 Data Blocks use 33 bytes for each 32 bytes of real data, with one byte used for transition and
5559 control.
5560 • CRC shall be either a CRC-16 or a CRC-32 verification at the end of the Message, occupying 6
5561 bytes. The selection between CRC-16 and CRC-32 shall be made by the Controller, and shall be
5562 indicated in the Header.
5563 • If this is a write without data (e.g., a Broadcast CCC without a data payload), then the Controller
5564 shall not send any Data Blocks and shall not send the CRC Block.
306 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 321 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.4.3.3.1 Header Block
5565 The Header Block shall be 7 bytes in length, formed as 6 bytes + 1 Transition byte:
Byte 0 Byte 1 Byte 2 Byte 3 Byte 4 Byte 5 Byte 6
Address Cmd0 Cmd1 Cmd2 Cmd3 Control Transition
5566 Where:
5567 • Address shall be either:
5568 • The 7-bit I3C Dynamic Address of the Target to be addressed for this transfer in Bits[7:1], with
5569 Bit[0] = 1'b0 for a Write, or = 1'b1 for a Read; or
5570 • A valid 7-bit Group Address (if assigned and supported) in Bits[7:1], with Bit[0] set to 1'b0 (as
5571 this case is always a Write); or
5572 • The I3C Broadcast Address (i.e., 7'h7E) in Bits[7:1], which indicates framing for CCC
5573 transmission in HDR-BT Mode (per Section 6.4.3.4).
5574 • Cmd0 to Cmd3 may be used as the indicator to the Target (or Group) as to the meaning/context of
5575 the data:
5576 • If Address is a Dynamic Address or Group Address (i.e., is not 7'h7E), then this transfer is a
5577 generic Write or Read transfer.
5578 For a generic Write or Read transfer, fields Cmd0 through Cmd3 would typically be used
5579 as with standard I3C SDR protocols, for command/index/offset and length (actual or
5580 maximum), but the exact meaning is a contract between the Controller and the Target (or
5581 all Targets in an assigned Group).
5582 • If Address is 7'h7E (as defined above), then per Section 6.4.3.4 this is a CCC.
5583 • If this is the first HDR-BT Header Block with Address 7'h7E, then Cmd0 shall be the CCC
5584 Code, Cmd1 shall be the optional Defining Byte, and the Controller starts the CCC
5585 modality (see Section 6.4.3.4.1).
5586 • Subsequent HDR-BT Header Blocks shall comprise CCC flows, until the Controller exits
5587 the CCC modality by performing a valid HDR-BT CCC End Procedure per
5588 Section 6.4.3.4.7.
5589 Note:
5590 Per Table 16, only some CCCs may be used in HDR-BT Mode.
5591 • Control is a byte and shall be formatted thus:
5592 • Bit[0] = 1'b0 for a Write, or = 1'b1 for a Read. It shall match Address Bit[0].
5593 • Bit[1] = 1'b0 if CRC-16 is used, or = 1'b1 if CRC-32 is used.
5594 • The Controller shall not select CRC-32 if the addressed Target (or Group) does not support
5595 CRC-32.
5596 • The Target shall not accept (i.e., shall NACK) a Message indicating CRC-32 if it does not
5597 support CRC-32. (If Address indicates a Group Address, then all Targets assigned to that
5598 Group Address must support CRC-32.)
5599 • Bit[2]:
5600 • For a Read, Bit[2] = 1'b0 if the Controller transmits SCL, or = 1'b1 if the Target transmits
5601 SCL.
5602 The Controller shall not select “Target transmits SCL” unless the Target supports
5603 it. The Target shall not accept (i.e., shall NACK) a Message with Bit[2] set to 1'b1
5604 if it does not actually support driving SCL.
5605 • For a Write, Bit[2] is Reserved and shall be set to 1'b0.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 307
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 322 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
5606 • Bit[3] = 1'b0 for a normal Message, or = 1'b1 for a continuation of a Direct CCC (GET or
5607 SET).
5608 • Bit[4] shall be set to 1'b1 for all HDR-BT Transfers, per the new HDR-BT framing.
5609 Note:
5610 In version 1.1.1 of the I3C Specification, Bit[4] was reserved (i.e., was always set to 1'b0)
5611 and this indicated the older framing for HDR-BT transfers. Starting with version 1.2 of the
5612 I3C Specification, Bit[4] set to 1'b1 now indicates the new HDR-BT framing.
5613 The new HDR-BT framing can be summarized thus:
5614 • Previously, Broadcast CCCs used bytes Cmd1 through Cmd3 in the Indicator Block
5615 to hold the first data bytes of the CCC. Bit[4] set to 1'b1 now indicates that Broadcast
5616 CCCs will no longer use those bytes as data bytes (per Section 6.4.3.4.1). All data
5617 bytes for Broadcast CCCs will now be sent in HDR-BT Data Blocks (i.e., after the
5618 Indicator Block).
5619 • Previously, the HDR-BT CRC Block was defined as required for all HDR-BT transfers.
5620 Bit[4] set to 1'b1 now indicates that the CRC Block is no longer required for, and will
5621 not be sent with, zero-byte transfers, which includes certain flow elements for CCCs
5622 sent in HDR-BT Mode (i.e., Header Blocks with no subsequent Data Blocks).
5623 • Previously, the Controller was required to send one HDR-BT CCC Header Block to
5624 end the CCC modality for both Broadcast and Direct CCCs sent in HDR-BT Mode.
5625 Bit[4] set to 1'b1 now indicates that the Header Block is no longer required for, and
5626 will not be sent, when ending the CCC modality for Broadcast CCCs, since the CCC
5627 modality will end automatically with the HDR Restart Pattern.
5628 I3C Targets that interoperate with older I3C Controllers (i.e., those that only support v1.1.1
5629 of the I3C Specification) might also need to support the older framing for HDR-BT
5630 transfers, where Bit[4] would always be set to 1'b0.
5631 • Bit[5] is Reserved and shall be set to 1'b0.
5632 • Bits[7:6] = Parity 0 and Parity 1 on the whole Header (up through this Control byte), using the
5633 even and odd positioned bits respectively. This follows a model similar to HDR-DDR (see
5634 Section 6.2.3.2), using an XOR function:
5635 Bit[6] = Parity of the even index bits, from Header Bytes 0 through 5, and 1'b1:
5636 Address[0] ^ Address[2] ^ Address[4] ^ Address[6] ^
5637 Cmd0[0] ^ Cmd0[2] ^ Cmd0[4] ^ Cmd0[6] ^
5638 Cmd1[0] ^ Cmd1[2] ^ Cmd1[4] ^ Cmd1[6] ^
5639 Cmd2[0] ^ Cmd2[2] ^ Cmd2[4] ^ Cmd2[6] ^
5640 Cmd3[0] ^ Cmd3[2] ^ Cmd3[4] ^ Cmd3[6] ^
5641 Control[0] ^ Control[2] ^ Control[4] ^ 1'b1
5642 Bit[7] = Parity of the odd index bits, from Header Bytes 0 through 5:
5643 Address[1] ^ Address[3] ^ Address[5] ^ Address[7] ^
5644 Cmd0[1] ^ Cmd0[3] ^ Cmd0[5] ^ Cmd0[7] ^
5645 Cmd1[1] ^ Cmd1[3] ^ Cmd1[5] ^ Cmd1[7] ^
5646 Cmd2[1] ^ Cmd2[3] ^ Cmd2[5] ^ Cmd2[7] ^
5647 Cmd3[1] ^ Cmd3[3] ^ Cmd3[5] ^ Cmd3[7] ^
5648 Control[1] ^ Control[3] ^ Control[5]
5649 Note:
5650 The Controller shall compute the Parity for all other bits in the Header Block, including any
5651 reserved fields; but not for any fields in the following Transition byte, which would not yet
5652 have been sent on the SDA Lane(s) by the time the Control byte was sent. If the received
308 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 323 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
5653 Parity bits do not match the computed Parity of the preceding field, then the Target should
5654 not acknowledge (i.e., should NACK) the HDR-BT Header Block.
5655 • Transition occupies one byte, and shall use the SDA[0] Line for the first 2 half-clocks, and shall
5656 follow the I3C “Park1,High-Z” approach for Bus transition and acceptance (see Section 6.4.3.2).
5657 • Bits[1:0]:
5658 • Bit[0] as SDA[0]’s first half-clock shall be Parked High by the Controller
5659 • Bit[1] as SDA[0]’s second half-clock shall be High-Z by Controller; the Target (or all
5660 Targets in the Group) shall either drive it Low in order to accept the Write or Read, or else
5661 leave it undriven to not accept (see Section 6.4.3.7).
5662 Note:
5663 If there is a Read request, and if the Target sources the clock, then the Controller
5664 shall also release the clock during the second half-clock period if the Target is
5665 accepting (i.e., if SDA[0] is driven to zero), so that the Target drives the next edge.
5666 The SCL handoff follows the convention that if the Target is accepting, then it shall
5667 drive/hold the SCL line High (i.e., the same as the Controller) during this half-clock, so
5668 that they overlap.
5669 If the Target does not drive the clock within tBT_HO (i.e., the HDR-BT handoff period)
5670 after accepting, then the Controller shall regain control.
5671 • Bit[2]:
5672 For a Read:
5673 • Bit[2] = 1'b0 indicates that the Target is permitted to use the Data Block Delay
5674 mechanism if it needs extra time to prepare data to return for the Read transfer.
5675 See Section 6.4.3.3.4 for more details.
5676 The Controller shall not use Bit[2] = 1'b0 if it already allowed, or previously configured,
5677 another Target to terminate a long transfer (per Section 6.4.3.3.4).
5678 • Bit[2] = 1'b1 indicates that the Target must not use Data Block Delay for this read
5679 transfer.
5680 For a Write:
5681 • Bit[2] is Reserved and shall be set to 1'b0
5682 • Bits[4:3] are Reserved and shall be set to 2'b00
5683 • Bits[7:5] are Reserved and cannot be used for future purposes. The Controller shall drive all
5684 bits to High on the last half-clock of the Header Block, according to the current Multi-Lane
5685 Coding:
5686 For Write transfers:
5687 • For Single Lane: Bit[7] on SDA[0] shall be driven High by the Controller. The
5688 Controller may optionally drive Bits[6:5] High.
5689 • For Dual Lane: Bits[7:6] on SDA[1:0] shall be driven High by the Controller.
5690 The Controller may optionally drive Bit[5] High.
5691 • For Quad Lane: Bits[7:5] on SDA[3:0] shall be driven High by the Controller.
5692 For Read transfers:
5693 • For Single Lane: Bit[7] on SDA[0] shall be driven High by the Controller. This
5694 allows the Target (i.e., the transmitter) to safely start the Transition_Control byte
5695 of the first Data Block. The Controller may optionally drive Bits[6:5] High.
5696 • For Dual-Lane: Bit[6] on SDA[0] shall be driven High by the Controller, and
5697 Bit[7] on SDA[1] shall be Parked High (i.e., driven High and then High-Z) by the
5698 Controller. This allows the Target (i.e., the transmitter) to safely start the
5699 Transition_Control byte of the first Data Block. The Controller may optionally
5700 drive Bit[5] High.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 309
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 324 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
5701 • For Quad-Lane: Bits[7:5] on SDA[3:1] shall be Parked High (i.e., shall be driven
5702 High and then High-Z) by the Controller. This allows the Target (i.e., the
5703 transmitter) to safely start the Transition_Control byte of the first Data Block.
5704 Note:
5705 Previous versions of the I3C Specification defined Bits[7:5] differently, and treated
5706 them as reserved (3'b000) always. While these bits are still considered to be reserved
5707 and not usable for future purposes, implementers must now ensure that all SDA lines
5708 are put into a suitable state for the last half-clock of the Header Block, especially for
5709 HDR-BT Read Transfers. This makes it possible for the addressed Target to safely
5710 drive the necessary values at the start of the Transition_Control byte in the Data
5711 Block, i.e., on the next rising clock edge after the end of this Transition byte. In many
5712 cases it is simpler for the Controller to simply drive Bits[7:5] to High and then Park
5713 (i.e., High-Z) the SDA[x:0] lanes after the last half-clock.
310 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 325 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.4.3.3.2 Data Blocks
5714 Each Data block shall be formed as 33 bytes:
Transition_Control Data 0 to 31
5715 Where:
5716 • Transition_Control occupies one byte, and shall use the SDA[0] line for the first two half-clocks,
5717 and follows the I3C “Park1,High-Z” approach for termination by the receiver (see Section 6.4.3.2).
5718 • Bits[1:0]:
5719 • Bit[0] as SDA[0]’s first half-clock shall be Parked High by the transmitter of the data (i.e.,
5720 by the Controller for a Write, or by the Target for a Read).
5721 • Bit[1] as SDA[0]’s second half-clock shall be High-Z’ed by the transmitter of the data (i.e.,
5722 by the Controller for a Write, or by the Target for a Read).
5723 • The receiver may drive SDA[0] Low to terminate the transmission, otherwise the
5724 receiver leaves it undriven to allow the data to continue normally. If so, then the
5725 transmitter shall drive the next bit on SDA[0] in this Transition_Control byte to
5726 1'b0. (This does not apply to Quad Lane, due to the Transition Byte framing).
5727 Note:
5728 If terminated, then the transmitter will emit a CRC Block as the next byte (see
5729 Section 6.4.3.7). Termination takes priority over the Data Block Delay mechanism.
5730 • Bit[2]:
5731 • Bit[2] shall be 1'b0 if this is not the Last Data Block, or (for Single Lane only) if the
5732 receiver has terminated the HDR-BT transfer (i.e., if SDA[0] was driven Low by the
5733 receiver for Bit[1]).
5734 • Bit[2] shall be 1'b1 if this is the intended Last Data Block to be sent by the transmitter (i.e.,
5735 immediately before the CRC Block).
5736 • Bit[3] = Parity adjustment, to ensure odd parity of this Transition_Control byte bits [2] to [7].
5737 This is not parity of the data. The data validity is handled by the CRC at the end (i.e., in the
5738 CRC Block; see Section 6.4.3.3.3).
5739 • The transmitter shall write a value equal to XOR(1'b1, Bit[2], Bits[7:4]).
5740 • The receiver shall ensure that the overall parity is odd, i.e., 1'b0 == XOR(1'b1, Bits[7:2]).
5741 Note:
5742 This parity adjustment mechanism is similar to the one used in the HDR-DDR
5743 Command Word (see Section 6.2.3.3 for more details). If the Transition_Control
5744 parity does not match, or if the “Park1” is not 1'b1, then there is a framing error. The
5745 High-Z may be 1'b0 if the other side has terminated or if a separate Target was
5746 permitted to terminate and has done so. See Section 6.4.3.3.4 for details.
5747 • If this is the Last Data Block before the CRC Block, then:
5748 • Bits[7:4] = Byte-pairs in Data Block minus 1 (i.e., 0xF if all 32, or 0x0 for 2 bytes)
5749 • If this is not the Last Data Block, then:
5750 • Bits[7:5] are Reserved and shall be set to 3'd0.
5751 • Bit[4]:
5752 For a Read:
5753 • Bit[4] = 1'b0 indicates that the Target (as transmitter) is sending a valid Data
5754 Block.
5755 • Bit[4] = 1'b1 indicates that the Target (as transmitter) is using the Data Block
5756 Delay mechanism, to delay sending the next Data Block in this transfer. The
5757 Target may only use the Data Block Delay mechanism if the Controller
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 311
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 326 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
5758 indicated that the Target was permitted to do so (i.e., if it sent the Transition
5759 byte with Bit[2] = 1'b0 in the Header Block for this Read transfer; see
5760 Section 6.4.3.3.4 for more details).
5761 For a Write: Bit[4] shall be set to 1'b0.
5762 • Data shall be transmitted from Byte 0 to Byte 31.
5763 Note:
5764 For the Last Data Block, the receiver shall ignore padding Bytes past the ragged length. These extra
5765 bytes should be set to 8'd0, but may contain any value.
5766 Note:
5767 It is recommended for the Last Data Block length to match the receiver’s natural data width, such as
5768 32 bits or 64 bits to match internal memory or buses. Further, if the data is encrypted, then the
5769 encryption algorithm’s blocking size should be considered (e.g., 128-bit for AES).
312 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 327 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.4.3.3.3 CRC Block
5770 The CRC Block is formed as 6 bytes:
Byte 0 Byte 1 Byte 2 Byte 3 Byte 4 Byte 5
CRC2 CRC3 Transition_
Control CRC0 CRC1
if CRC-32 if CRC-32 Verify
5771 Where:
5772 • Control is a byte that indicates that this CRC Block is not another Data Block, since Bit[0] is 0
5773 (Low) instead of 1 (i.e., Parked High).
5774 The CRC Block should be expected, since either the previous Data Block indicated it was the Last
5775 Data Block, or else the transfer was terminated (i.e., High-Z driven Low by the receiver).
5776 The Control byte also verifies the CRC type previously selected in the Header Block, and whether
5777 the previous Data Block was a Last Data Block, or was Terminated.
5778 Therefore, the Control byte shall contain:
5779 • Bits[2:0] = 3'b000 always.
5780 • Bit[3] = Parity adjustment, to ensure odd parity of this Control byte’s Bits[7:2]:
5781 • The transmitter shall write a value equal to XOR(1'b1, Bit[2], Bits[7:4]).
5782 • •The receiver shall ensure that the overall parity is odd, i.e., 1'b0 == XOR(1'b1, Bits[7:2]).
5783 Note:
5784 This parity adjustment mechanism is similar to the one used in the HDR-DDR Command Word
5785 (see Section 6.2.3.3 for more details). If the parity does not match, or if Bit[0] is not = 1'b0, or if
5786 Bit[1] is not = 1'b0, then there is a framing error.
5787 • Bit[4] = 1'b0 always
5788 • Bit[5] = Same value as the Header Block’s Control byte, Bit[1]: 1'b0 for CRC-16, or 1'b1 for
5789 CRC-32.
5790 Note:
5791 If this value does not match the value from the Header bit, then there is an error.
5792 • Bit[6] = 1'b0 if the transaction ended normally, or = 1'b1 if the previous Data Block was
5793 terminated (i.e., if its High-Z was driven to 1'b0 by the receiver).
5794 Note:
5795 If the value of Bit[6] does not agree with what the receiver believes actually happened, then
5796 there is an error. That is, if the receiver did not see the last Data Block terminated (either by
5797 itself, or by a separate Device), and the transmitter says it was terminated, then there is an
5798 error. Likewise, if the receiver did not see the last Data Block marked as Last, but the
5799 transmitter says it ended normally, then there is an error.
5800 • Bit[7] = Reserved and shall be set to 1'b0.
5801 • Bytes CRC0 to CRC3 shall contain the value of the specified CRC algorithm, as computed by the
5802 transmitter: either CRC-16, or CRC-32 (if supported).
5803 The transmitter shall calculate the checksum based on the valid contents of all previous Data
5804 Blocks that were sent before this CRC Block, using the following requirements:
5805 • For each Data Block except the Last Data Block, this includes the entire 33-byte contents of
5806 the Data Block (i.e., the value in the Transition_Control byte, as well as all 32 Data bytes).
5807 • For the Last Data Block, this includes the Transition_Control byte, as well as the number of
5808 valid Data bytes in the Last Data Block (i.e., as indicated by the number of byte-pairs, sent in
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 313
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 328 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
5809 Bits[7:4] of the Transition_Control byte). However, any padding bytes in the Last Data Block
5810 shall be ignored.
5811 • In either case, all bits in the Transition_Control byte shall always be included, even if the
5812 receiver terminates the transfer during the Transition_Control byte (i.e., by driving SDA[0] to
5813 Low).
5814 • For terminated transfers, the actual values driven on the SDA[0] Lane shall be used, and the
5815 transmitter shall include only the Transition_Control byte. Note that the transmitter will
5816 start emitting a CRC Block as the next byte after the Transition_Control byte, if the
5817 receiver terminates the transfer (see Section 5.2.4.3.2).
5818 In this case, for the purposes of checksum calculation, the transmitter shall not
5819 include any subsequent data bytes that it intended to send, but would have been
5820 skipped due to a terminated transfer.
5821 The following structured protocol elements shall not be included in the checksum calculation:
5822 • The Header Block shall be ignored.
5823 • If this is a Read transfer, and if the Target (as transmitter) was permitted to use the Data Byte
5824 Delay mechanism (see Section 5.2.4.3.4), then the transmitter shall ignore any Delay bytes that
5825 it might send in place of valid Data Blocks. Only valid Data Blocks shall be used in the
5826 calculation of the checksum.
5827 • The Control byte in the CRC Block shall be ignored.
5828 The receiver shall also calculate the checksum based on the same requirements, as it receives each
5829 Data Block during the transfer.
5830 If Bit[1] in the Header Block’s Control byte is 1'b0, then the transmitter shall use CRC-16:
5831 For CRC-16, the generator polynomial is:
5832 X16 + X15 + X2 + 1
5833 Note:
5834 For shift register implementations, this polynomial is expressed as 0x8005 in big-endian
5835 notation, or 0xA001 in little-endian notation.
5836 • The initial value is all 1s (i.e., 0xFFFF).
5837 • The natural bit order of data in HDR-BT Mode is little endian, so Bytes 1 and 2 (fields
5838 CRC0 and CRC1) shall contain the calculated checksum:
5839 • Byte 1 (CRC0) shall contain bits[7:0] of the checksum
5840 • Byte 2 (CRC1) shall contain bits[15:8] of the checksum
5841 • Bytes 3 and 4 (fields CRC2 and CRC3) shall contain all 0s.
5842 If Bit[1] in the Header Block’s Control byte is 1'b1, then the transmitter shall use CRC-32:
5843 For CRC-32, the generator polynomial is:
5844 X32 + X26 + X23 + X22 + X16 + X12 + X11 + X10 + X8 + X7 + X5 + X4 + X2 + X + 1
5845 Note:
5846 For shift register implementations, this polynomial is expressed as 0x04C11DB7 in
5847 big-endian notation, or 0xEDB88320 in little-endian notation.
5848 • The initial value is all 1s (i.e., 0xFFFFFFFF).
5849 • The natural bit order of data in HDR-BT Mode is little-endian, so Bytes 1 through 4 (fields
5850 CRC0 through CRC3) shall contain the calculated checksum, as follows:
5851 • Byte 1 (CRC0) shall contain bits[7:0] of the checksum
5852 • Byte 2 (CRC1) shall contain bits[15:8] of the checksum
5853 • Byte 3 (CRC2) shall contain bits[23:16] of the checksum
5854 • Byte 4 (CRC3) shall contain bits[31:24] of the checksum.
314 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 329 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
5855 Example CRC Checksum Calculations
5856 Table 93 presents example CRC checksum calculations as a reference. All values are presented
5857 using big-endian notation, and the CRC-16 and CRC-32 values are based on the initial value of all
5858 1s. For each example it is assumed that the CRC checksum is calculated from a data message
5859 having a single byte.
Table 93 Example CRC Checksum Calculations
Data Byte CRC-16 CRC-32
0x00 0x40BF 0x2DFD1072
0x91 0xEC7E 0xAAF5B3A0
0xF7 0xC6FE 0x0E2477CD
5860 Note:
5861 In the examples above, the computed checksum after the single data byte could also be
5862 viewed as the state after processing the first byte of a longer message. The example for data
5863 byte 0xF7 is relevant, as this matches the expected value of the Transition_Control byte for a
5864 Last Data Block that contains 32 valid data bytes (see Section 6.4.3.3.2).
5865 • The Transition_Verify byte serves two purposes:
5866 • For a Read, it is used to hand the SDA[x] lines off, back to the Controller; similarly, if the
5867 Target is sourcing SCL, then Transition_Verify allows handing the SCL sourcing role back as
5868 well.
5869 • For a Read or Write, it allows the receiver (which could be either the Controller or the Target)
5870 to verify that the value provided in the CRC field (i.e., sent in fields CRC0 through CRC3)
5871 matched the output of the selected CRC algorithm, and therefore that the Message was
5872 correctly received. The receiver shall use the same method for computing the CRC checksum
5873 as the transmitter, by using the valid contents of all received Data Blocks (as defined above).
5874 Transition_Verify shall use the SDA[0] line for the first two half-clocks, and shall follow
5875 the I3C “Park1,High-Z” approach for completion by the receiver (see Section 6.4.3.2).
5876 • Bits[1:0]:
5877 • Bit[0] as SDA[0]’s first half-clock shall be Parked High by the transmitter of the data (i.e.,
5878 by the Controller for a Write, or by the Target for a Read).
5879 • Bit[1] as SDA[0]’s second half-clock shall be High-Z’ed by the transmitter of the data (i.e.,
5880 by the Controller for a Write, or by the Target for a Read).
5881 To confirm that the CRC field value matched the output of the selected CRC
5882 algorithm, the receiver shall drive SDA[0] Low. Alternatively, if the CRC did
5883 not match, then the receiver shall leave the SDA[0] line undriven (and therefore
5884 High) for Bit[1].
5885 Note:
5886 If the Target was driving the SCL during a Read, then it shall hand off the SCL on the
5887 second half-clock. The Controller shall drive/hold the SCL line High during this
5888 second half-clock, matching the Target, and then drive the next SCL clock edge.
5889 For error handling, if the Target stops driving the Clock for more than tBT_FREQ, then
5890 the Controller will take over the clock. This would happen in the event that the
5891 Controller has lost framing due to an error.
5892 • Bits[4:2] = 3'b000 always.
5893 Note:
5894 In previous versions of this I3C Specification, Bits[4:2] were marked as reserved.
5895 However, due to the different bit packing schemes for HDR-BT transition bytes (which
5896 depend on the Multi-Lane configuration of the addressed Target or Group for a
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 315
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 330 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
5897 transfer), these bits cannot be usable for any meaningful purpose by the receiver, and
5898 thus may never be used by the receiver to indicate any post-transfer status or
5899 subsequent actions.
5900 • Bits[7:5]:
5901 • Reserved for future use by MIPI I3C WG. These bits shall be set to 3'd0.
5902 For HDR-BT Read transfers, the Target shall release SDA[0] after the second half-clock (i.e.,
5903 Bit[1]). Subsequent actions shall depend on the bit packing scheme, per Section 6.4.3.2, as
5904 follows:
5905 • If the Target was configured to use a bit packing scheme with multiple clock cycles (i.e.,
5906 Single Lane or Dual Lane): If the Controller (as receiver) does not verify the transfer and
5907 leaves SDA[0] undriven for the second half-clock, then it shall pull SDA[0] Low after Bit[1]
5908 (i.e., the following half-clock), unless it loses framing or there is an error.
5909 • If the Target was configured to use a bit packing scheme with a single clock cycle (i.e.,
5910 Quad Lane): If the Controller (as receiver) does not verify the transfer and leaves SDA[0]
5911 undriven for the second half-clock, then SDA[0] shall remain High through the end of the
5912 Transition_Verify byte. After this, the CRC Block ends and the Controller typically drives
5913 either the HDR Restart Pattern or the HDR Exit Pattern.
5914 For HDR-BT Read transfers that use additional Data Lanes, the Target shall drive these Data
5915 Lanes (e.g., SDA[1:3]) to Low for the first half-clock, and then High-Z these Lanes for the second
5916 half-clock (i.e., along with the High-Z of SDA[0]). If the Target was providing SCL clock, then
5917 this coincides with the handoff of SCL and SDA[0] to the Controller (as receiver). Unless the
5918 Controller encounters a framing issue and must use an error recovery procedure (i.e., without
5919 verifying the Read transfer), the Controller shall take ownership of these additional Data Lanes for
5920 the second half-clock, and shall drive them Low at the appropriate time to either verify or not
5921 verify the transfer.
5922 For HDR-BT Write transfers, the Controller (as transmitter) shall drive SDA[0] to Low as
5923 required by the configured bit packing scheme, after providing the receiver (i.e., the addressed
5924 Target or Group) an opportunity to verify the transfer for the second half-clock. After this
5925 opportunity, the Controller shall control and drive SDA[0] as required by the bit packing scheme
5926 to ensure that Bits[4:2] are always 3'd0:
5927 • If the receiver was configured to use a bit packing scheme with multiple clock cycles
5928 (i.e., Single Lane or Dual Lane): If the receiver does not verify the transfer and leaves SDA[0]
5929 undriven (i.e., High) for the second half-clock, then the Controller shall pull SDA[0] Low after
5930 Bit[1] (i.e., the following half-clock); and subsequently shall either hold SDA[0] Low through
5931 the end of the Transition_Verify byte, or optionally (i.e., by prior agreement and configuration)
5932 High-Z or drive to High SDA[0] as needed (i.e., for any such reserved Bits[7:5] that might be
5933 used) per the bit packing scheme. Following the last clock cycle, the Controller shall pull
5934 SDA[0] High before it drives either the HDR Restart Pattern or the HDR Exit Pattern.
5935 • If the receiver was configured to use a bit packing scheme with a single clock cycle (i.e.,
5936 Quad Lane): If the receiver does not verify the transfer and leaves SDA[0] undriven (i.e.,
5937 High) for the second half-clock, then SDA[0] shall remain High through the end of the
5938 Transition_Verify byte. After this, the CRC Block ends and the Controller shall drive either the
5939 HDR Restart Pattern or the HDR Exit Pattern.
5940 For HDR-BT Write transfers that use additional Data Lanes, the Controller shall drive these
5941 Data Lanes (e.g., SDA[1:3]) to Low (i.e., 0 values) for the first half-clock. Subsequently, the
5942 Controller shall either hold these Data Lanes Low for the remaining clock cycles of the
5943 Transition_Verify byte; or optionally (i.e., by prior agreement and configuration) High-Z or drive
5944 to High any relevant Data Lanes as needed (i.e., for any such reserved Bits[7:5] that might be
5945 used) for the receiver’s bit packing scheme.
316 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 331 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
5946 Note:
5947 The HDR-BT CRC Block diagrams in Section 6.4.3.6 show the reserved Bits[7:5] as 3'd0.
6.4.3.3.4 Data Block Delay Mechanism
5948 The Data Block Delay mechanism is intended to allow Targets to get additional time when returning Read
5949 data. It is mainly intended for situations where the Controller provides the clock (i.e., controls SCL for a
5950 Read transfer). However, it may still be useful when the Target controls SCL for a Read transfer, because it
5951 allows the Controller to terminate the Read transfer instead of requiring the Controller to continue waiting
5952 for the Target to provide data (i.e., where the Controller would become stuck for up to the maximum number
5953 of delay attempts).
5954 Operational Flow
5955 At the start of each HDR-BT Read transfer, the Controller shall determine whether the Target is permitted to
5956 use the Data Block Delay mechanism, and indicate this in Bit[2] of the Transition byte of the Header Block:
5957 if Bit[2] has a value of 1, then the Target is not permitted to use the Data Block Delay mechanism. However,
5958 if Bit[2] has a value of 0, then the Target (as transmitter) is permitted to use the Data Block Delay mechanism
5959 (see Section 6.4.3.3.1).
5960 Note:
5961 The remainder of this Section assumes that the Controller indicated that the Target was permitted to
5962 use the Data Block Delay mechanism during an HDR-BT Read transfer.
5963 During the Read transfer, as the Target (as transmitter) prepares to send each Data Block except the Last Data
5964 Block (per Section 6.4.3.3.2), it may choose either of two options:
5965 Either:
5966 • Send the next Data Block with a full 32 bytes of data,
5967 First, the Target sends the Transition_Control byte, with Bit[4] having a value of 0; then the
5968 Target sends the remaining 32 bytes of Data (i.e., Byte 0 to Byte 31). This is a complete Data
5969 Block.
5970 Or:
5971 • Delay sending the Data Block, and send a Delay byte in its place.
5972 The Target sends a single Transition_Control byte (per Section 5.2.4.3.2), using the same
5973 “Park1,High-Z” mechanism on SDA[0], and the Controller (as receiver) leaves SDA[0] at High-Z
5974 (i.e., to continue the transfer). In Bit[4] the Target sends a value of 1, indicating that it is delaying
5975 the next Data Block and not providing a full Data Block at this time.
5976 The Target (as transmitter) may send up to the maximum number of Delay bytes (i.e., up to t BT_DBD at each
5977 opportunity) before it must either send the next real Data Block (i.e., with a full 32 bytes of data), or send a
5978 Last Data Block to end the HDR-BT Read transfer.
5979 Usage Notes
5980 The Controller (as receiver) shall examine the value of Bit[4] to determine whether the first byte is the
5981 Transition_Control byte (i.e., is the start of a valid Data Block), or is a Delay byte. Upon making this
5982 determination, the Controller may decide that it does not wish to continue waiting for delayed Data Blocks
5983 and instead wishes to end the transfer, and it may do so using the “Park1,High-Z” mechanism on SDA[0], at
5984 any of the Delay bytes (i.e., as it would with a typical Data Block).
5985 Note:
5986 HDR-BT packs bytes of Transition content using different bit packing schemes, based on the Multi-
5987 Lane configuration of the Target that is addressed in the Header Block (per Section 6.4.3.2). Since
5988 these bit packing schemes have different locations for Bit[4] with respect to the timing and the
5989 “Park1,High-Z” mechanism, the knowledge that a received byte is a Delay byte (and not the first valid
5990 Transition_Control byte in a real Data Block) might be transmitted after the Controller’s opportunity
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 317
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 332 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
5991 to drive SDA[0] Low to terminate the transmission. In QUAD Lane configuration, the Target sends
5992 Bit[4] early enough to give the Controller the opportunity to terminate the transmission. However, in
5993 DUAL Lane or SINGLE Lane configurations, the Target sends Bit[4] later, so Controller must react
5994 afterwards. As a result, the next opportunity for the Controller to use the “Park1,High-Z” mechanism
5995 (for DUAL Lane and SINGLE Lane configurations) is at the start of the next Delay byte, or the next
5996 real Data Block.
5997 The Target may use the Data Block Delay mechanism repeatedly throughout the HDR-BT Read transfer,
5998 before the first Data Block or any subsequent Data Block, as long as Bit[2] of Transition_Control is 1'b0
5999 (i.e., is not a Last Data Block) and the Controller has not terminated this transfer (i.e., pulls SDA[0] to Low
6000 as part of “Park1,High-Z” for this Data Block).
6001 The Data Block Delay mechanism should not be used when the Controller has activated any other Targets
6002 wishing to have the right to terminate the transaction, as they might not be able to track this mechanism
6003 properly. That is, when the Controller is Reading from Target A, but has also activated Target B in order to
6004 be able to terminate the transaction, then it should indicate that Data Block Delay is not permitted.
6005 If the Controller controls the SCL for a Read transfer, and if the Target is not permitted to use the Data Block
6006 Delay mechanism but still cannot provide Data Blocks to safely maintain the HDR-BT Read transfer (i.e.,
6007 due to internal data readiness), then the Controller might need to slow the SCL (i.e., reduce the data rate) to
6008 better match the Target’s capability. This either should be arranged by private contract between the Controller
6009 and Target, or should be discovered from the Target before the Controller starts the HDR-BT Read transfer.
6010 Note:
6011 The I3C Specification does not define a mechanism for discovering a Target’s capability to provide
6012 Read data using HDR-BT, or whether it can sustain HDR-BT Read transfers at a given minimum
6013 transfer rate. It is assumed that this mechanism could be a private contract between the Controller
6014 and the Target, or could be discovered and controlled by other configuration methods.
6015 Alternatively, if the Target is unable to provide the next Data Block that contains a full 32 bytes of data, then
6016 it may emit a Last Data Block and end the transaction early.
6017 Note:
6018 The I3C Specification does not define a mechanism for the Target (as transmitter) to explicitly inform
6019 the Controller (as receiver) that a Last Data Block was unexpected, in cases where the Target was
6020 unable to provide a Data Block that contained a full 32 bytes of data. This may happen if the Target
6021 was unable to provide a full 32 bytes of data at a sustained rate due to extended delay conditions
6022 (i.e., a prolonged stall from other application logic). The Target may encounter this situation, even
6023 when using the Data Block Delay mechanism, if it had already sent the maximum number of Delay
6024 bytes before the next Data Block. The Target may also encounter this situation if it was not permitted
6025 to use the Data Block Delay mechanism. In either case, the Controller must handle such a situation
6026 where the full data message for a Read transfer is shorter than expected, based on the specific I3C
6027 content protocol or a private contract between Controller and Target.
318 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 333 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.4.3.3.5 Performance
6028 The Bus efficiency can be seen in Table 94 using 12.5 MHz; all numbers can be scaled to Freq:
Table 94 Bus Efficiency
Single / Dual / Quad
Raw Rate Real Data Rate
Measurement Real Data Rate
(Single / Dual / Quad) Calculation1
(Rounded)
24 Mbps / 48 Mbps /
Data blocks 25 Mbps / 50 Mbps / 100 Mbps 32 / 33 * freq
96.97 Mbps
23.96 Mbps / 47.9 Mbps /
1 KByte Message “ / ” 1024 / (1024+12) * 32 / 33 * freq

Note:
1. The equation for Messages (vs. just data blocks) factors in the header and CRC as overhead.
However, to keep it simple, the expression is incorrect by 12*33/32 or 0.375*freq, so about 1 KHz
worst case.
6029 Worst case and average delay when terminating a Message early at 12.5 MHz:
6030 • Single Lane: Worst Case: 10.5 µs, Average: 5.28 µs. Plus 1.92 µs for CRC
6031 • Dual Lane: Worst Case: 5.28 µs, Average: 2.64 µs. Plus 960 ns for CRC
6032 • Quad Lane: Worst Case: 2.64 µs, Average 1.32 µs. Plus 480 ns for CRC
6033 Note:
6034 • Parity is only lightly used in this protocol. It is mainly used to catch framing errors. The positive
6035 Verification at the end (after the CRC) ensures that the transmission was correct in terms of
6036 bits and framing.
6037 • The Verify model allows the I3C Controller’s Host system to operate a TCP/IP style windowing
6038 mechanism to avoid the processor having to check too often. This means that the Host can
6039 queue up blocks of, e.g., 1 K to 10 K bytes, and then pipeline back the verify
6040 successes/failures, allowing the Host to resubmit any that failed, freeing up ones that
6041 succeeded.

6.4.3.4 CCC Transmission in HDR-BT Mode
6042 A special HDR-BT syntax is used for transmitting Common Command Codes in the HDR-BT protocol. The
6043 following formats are defined, as specific instantiations of the generic HDR CCC flows (see Section 6.1.3).

Figure 135 Begin Direct CCC in HDR-BT\n--- Page 336 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2

6.4.3.4.1 HDR-BT CCC Header Block
6047 Two versions of the HDR-BT Header Block are defined: HDR-BT CCC Indicator Block and HDR-BT CCC
6048 Selector Block.
6.4.3.4.1.1 HDR-BT CCC Indicator Block
6049 An HDR-BT CCC Header Block that begins the framing for a Broadcast CCC or a Direct CCC shall be
6050 referred to as the HDR-BT CCC Indicator Block.
6051 The HDR-BT CCC Indicator Block shall take the structure of the HDR-BT Header Block Format (see
6052 Section 6.4.3.3.1). It is a common CCC Flow Element, and shall always be transmitted according to the bit
6053 packing format for the currently configured HDR-BT Coding (see Section 6.7.3.5.3.1).
6054 • Field Address (byte 0) shall contain the Broadcast Address (7'h7E).
6055 • Field Cmd0 (byte 1) shall contain the Command Code.
6056 • Per Section 4.3.7.2, the upper bit of the Command Code is always 1'b0 for Broadcast CCCs or
6057 1'b1 for Direct CCCs
6058 • Field Cmd1 field (byte 2) shall contain the optional Defining Byte for the Command Code.
6059 If the CCC does not support an optional Defining Byte, then the Cmd1 field shall contain the
6060 value 8'h00.
6061 • For Broadcast CCCs Only: Field Cmd2 (byte 3) shall contain the optional Sub-Command Byte
6062 for the Command Code.
6063 If the Broadcast CCC does not support an optional Sub-Command Byte, or if this an Indicator
6064 Block for a Direct CCC, then field Cmd2 shall contain the value 8'h00.
6065 • For All CCCs: Field Cmd3 (byte 4) is reserved and shall contain the value 8'h00.
6066 Note:
6067 In previous versions of the I3C Specification, the Controller would send the first Broadcast
6068 CCC data bytes either in fields Cmd1/Cmd2/Cmd3 if no Defining Byte, or in fields
6069 Cmd2/Cmd3 if the Defining Byte was in Cmd1. This has changed in version 1.2 of the I3C
6070 Specification. For Broadcast CCCs, all data bytes shall now be sent in one or more HDR-BT
6071 CCC Data Blocks after this Indicator Block. The use of this new framing is indicated by a value
6072 of 1'b1 in Bit[4] of field Control (see below). However, Controllers might need to use the old
6073 framing (i.e., as defined in I3C v1.1.1 and earlier) when sending Broadcast CCCs to older I3C
6074 Targets that do not support the new framing.
6075 • Field Control (byte 5) shall be set to indicate the start of a CCC:
6076 • Bit[0] shall contain 1'b0 to indicate a Write.
6077 • Bit[3] shall contain 1'b0 to indicate the beginning of a CCC framing.
6078 • Bit[4] shall contain 1'b1 to indicate use of the new HDR-BT framing, as described above.
6079 • Other bitfields shall be set accordingly.
6080 As noted earlier, in the new HDR-BT framing (indicated by Bit[4] being set to 1'b1) all of the following
6081 requirements apply to CCCs:
6082 • For Broadcast CCCs: The Controller shall send all data bytes in one or more HDR-BT Data Blocks.
6083 • HDR-BT CRC Blocks are no longer required for zero-byte transfers. This includes certain flow
6084 elements for CCCs, i.e., Header Blocks with no subsequent Data Blocks.
6085 • The Controller shall not send the HDR-BT Header Block to end the CCC modality for Broadcast
6086 CCCs.
6087 Targets will conditionally acknowledge receipt of the Indicator Block (i.e., during the Transition byte), per
6088 Section 6.4.3.4.4.
322 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 337 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6089 After the Indicator Block:
6090 • For Broadcast CCCs Only: If (and only if) this Broadcast CCC has data bytes, then the Controller
6091 shall conditionally send one or more Data Blocks followed by the CRC Block. Otherwise there are
6092 no data bytes and the Controller shall not send any Data Blocks and shall not send the CRC Block.
6093 The CCC modality shall automatically end with the HDR Restart Pattern.
6094 • For Direct CCCs Only: The Indicator Block for Direct CCCs will not have any data bytes, so the
6095 Controller shall not send any Data Blocks and shall not send the CRC Block. The CCC modality
6096 shall continue through subsequent Read Segments and/or Write Segments, until the Controller uses
6097 a suitable End of CCC Procedure (per Section 6.4.3.4.7).
6.4.3.4.1.2 HDR-BT CCC Selector Block
6098 An HDR-BT CCC Header Block that begins a Read Segment or a Write Segment in Direct CCC framing
6099 shall be referred to as the HDR-BT CCC Selector Block.
6100 The HDR-BT CCC Selector Block shall take the structure of the HDR-BT Header Block Format (see
6101 Section 6.4.3.3.1). It is a common CCC Flow Element, and shall always be transmitted according to the bit
6102 packing format for the currently configured HDR-BT Coding (see Section 6.7.3.5.3.1).
6103 • Field Address (byte 0) shall contain the Address of the Target (or Group) to be addressed in this
6104 Segment of the Direct CCC.
6105 • Field Cmd0 (byte 1) shall contain the optional Sub-Command Byte for the Command Code.
6106 • If the Direct CCC does not support an optional Sub-Command Byte, then field Cmd0 shall
6107 contain the value 8'h00.
6108 • Fields Cmd1/Cmd2/Cmd3 (bytes 2–4) shall be reserved, and shall each be set to 8'h00.
6109 • Field Control (byte 5) shall be set to indicate the continuation of a Direct CCC:
6110 • Bit[0] shall contain 1'b0 to indicate a Write Segment (i.e., Direct Write or Direct SET), or 1'b1
6111 to indicate a Read Segment (i.e., Direct Read or Direct GET). Note that Group Addresses may
6112 only be used with Write Segments.
6113 • Bit[3] shall contain 1'b1 to indicate the continuation of a Direct CCC framing.
6114 • Bit[4] shall contain 1'b1 to indicate use of the new HDR-BT framing as described above.
6115 • Other bitfields shall be set accordingly.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 323
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 338 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.4.3.4.2 HDR-BT CCC Data Block
6116 An HDR-BT CCC Data Block may follow an HDR-BT CCC Header Block, in the appropriate place for either
6117 a Broadcast CCC flow or a Direct CCC flow (see Figure 134 or Figure 135).
6.4.3.4.2.1 Broadcast CCC Flow
6118 The HDR-BT CCC Data Block in a Broadcast CCC flow shall take the structure of the HDR-BT Data Block
6119 Format (see Section 6.4.3.3.2), and shall contain up to 32 bytes of data in its payload.
6120 As described in Section 6.1.3, HDR-BT CCC Data Blocks for Broadcast CCCs are common CCC Flow
6121 Elements and shall always be transmitted according to the bit packing format for the currently configured
6122 HDR-BT Coding (see Section 6.7.3.5.3.1).
6123 Note:
6124 Since Broadcast CCCs in HDR-BT Mode are sent to all Targets, a Target should not pull SDA[0] to
6125 Low during the “Park1,High-Z” of the Transition_Control byte of an HDR-BT CCC Data Block for a
6126 Broadcast CCC. Doing so would inappropriately terminate the Broadcast CCC data transfer to all
6127 Targets.
6.4.3.4.2.2 Direct CCC Flow
6128 The HDR-BT CCC Data Block in a Direct CCC flow (as part of a Write Segment or Read Segment) is a per-
6129 Target CCC Flow Element, and shall be transmitted according to the bit packing format of the currently
6130 configured Multi-Lane Frame format and Data Transfer Coding (see Section 6.7.3.5.3.1) for the addressed
6131 Target (or Group).
6132 This may use a different bit packing format, and may use more additional data Lanes than the preceding
6133 HDR-BT CCC Selector Block. If so, then the Controller and indicated Target (or Targets in a Group, if
6134 applicable) shall correctly switch from one bit-packing format to another, as part of the CCC flow.
6135 • For Direct CCC Write Segments With Data: The Controller shall send the HDR-BT CCC Data
6136 Block(s) as well as the following HDR-BT CCC CRC Block. Following these, the Controller shall
6137 either send another Segment, or perform a suitable End of CCC Procedure (per Section 6.4.3.4.7).
6138 • For Direct CCC Write Segments Without Data: The Controller shall not send any HDR-BT CCC
6139 Data Blocks and shall not send an HDR-BT CCC CRC Block. Instead, it shall either send another
6140 Segment, or perform a suitable End of CCC Procedure (per Section 6.4.3.4.7) after the Indicator
6141 Block.
6142 • For Direct CCC Read Segments: If the addressed Target responds to the Selector Block with ACK,
6143 then this Target shall send the HDR-BT CCC Data Block(s) and the following HDR-BT CCC CRC
6144 Block. Following these, the Controller shall either send another Segment, or perform a suitable End
6145 of CCC Procedure (per Section 6.4.3.4.7).
6146 Note:
6147 For a Direct CCC, no HDR-BT CCC Data Blocks or CRC Block will be sent with the Indicator Block.
324 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 339 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.4.3.4.3 HDR-BT CCC CRC Block
6148 An HDR-BT CCC CRC Block serves as the Termination Marker, as described in the HDR-x generic CCC
6149 flows. To conform to the HDR-BT Mode Framing model (see Section 6.4.3.3), this Termination Marker uses
6150 the same structure as the standard HDR-BT CRC Block.
6151 Note:
6152 For Write CCCs Only: If the Controller does not send any HDR-BT CCC Data Blocks (i.e., for a
6153 zero-byte write), then the Controller shall not send the HDR-BT CRC Block. Instead, the Controller
6154 shall either (a) Perform the appropriate End of CCC procedure based on the type of CCC (Direct
6155 Write vs. Broadcast); or (b) For Direct CCCs only, send the HDR Restart Pattern followed by the next
6156 HDR-BT CCC Selector Block (i.e., to address the next Target with the same Direct CCC). In this
6157 case, the Transition byte in the preceding Indicator Block or Selector Block acts as the Termination
6158 Marker.
6.4.3.4.3.1 Broadcast CCC Flow
6159 For the end of Broadcast data in a Broadcast CCC flow, the Controller shall transmit the HDR-BT CRC Block
6160 (see Section 6.4.3.3.3) at the end of the Broadcast data message (i.e., after the last HDR-BT CCC Data
6161 Block).
6162 As described in Section 6.1.3, HDR-BT CCC CRC Blocks for Broadcast CCCs are common CCC Flow
6163 Elements and shall always be transmitted according to the bit packing format for the currently configured
6164 HDR-BT Coding (see Section 6.7.3.5.3.1). This bit packing format shall be the same as that used in the
6165 preceding HDR-BT CCC Data Block.
6.4.3.4.3.2 Direct CCC Flow
6166 Indicator Block: The Controller shall not send the HDR-BT CCC CRC Data Block with the Indicator Block.
6167 Segment: For the end of a Read or Write Segment sent in a Direct CCC flow, the HDR-BT CRC Block is a
6168 per-Target CCC Flow Element, and shall be transmitted according to the bit packing format of the currently
6169 configured Multi-Lane Frame format and Data Transfer Coding (see Section 6.7.3.5.3.1) for the addressed
6170 Target or Group. This bit packing format shall be the same as that used in the preceding HDR-BT CCC Data
6171 Block.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 325
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 340 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.4.3.4.4 HDR-BT CCC Indicator Block ACK
6172 Per Section 6.1.3.2, a Target that supports CCCs in HDR-BT Mode shall indicate acknowledgement of the
6173 receipt of the HDR-BT CCC Indicator Block, using the standard method of acknowledging an HDR-BT write
6174 (i.e., by using Bits[1:0] of the Transition byte in the HDR-BT Header Block per Section 6.4.3.3.1).
6175 The following conditions apply:
6176 • If the Parity bits in the Control byte do not match the computed Parity of the preceding fields in the
6177 HDR-BT Header Block, then the Target shall NACK (i.e., shall not acknowledge) the Indicator
6178 Block, since this is likely a transmission error.
6179 • For Older Targets: If Bit[4] in the Control byte is set to 1'b1 but the Target does not understand the
6180 new HDR-BT framing, then the Target should NACK the Indicator Block.
6181 • For all other situations, the Target shall ACK the Indicator Block.
6182 All such Targets will acknowledge a properly formed Indicator Block for both Broadcast CCC flows and
6183 Direct CCC flows, since the same HDR-BT CCC Indicator Block format is used to signal the beginning of
6184 both flows.
6185 If the Indicator Block’s Command Code (byte 1) indicates a Direct CCC, then all Targets receiving that
6186 Indicator Block shall capture the new Command Code and optional Defining Byte (byte 2) and use these if
6187 they are addressed in a future Write or Read Segment in the Direct CCC framing.
6188 Note:
6189 For Direct CCC flows, acknowledgement serves the important function of ensuring that a Target
6190 Device understands the CCC framing, has captured the Command Code and optional Defining Byte
6191 from the Indicator Block, and is ready to both (a) Match the Address on the next HDR-BT Header
6192 Block that is a Selector Block, and (b) Make a decision to respond to the Direct CCC if addressed,
6193 per Section 6.1.3.2.
6194 If the Controller does not receive acknowledgement from at least one Target, then it shall perform any valid
6195 HDR-BT CCC End Procedure. Additionally, the Controller may initiate an error recovery procedure, which
6196 might include exiting HDR-BT Mode and attempting to re-send the CCC in SDR Mode.
326 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 341 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.4.3.4.5 HDR-BT CCC Write Segment
6197 An HDR-BT CCC Write Segment for a Direct Write or Direct SET CCC shall start with an HDR-BT CCC
6198 Selector Block to indicate the Address of the Target (or Group) to receive the data, followed by one or more
6199 HDR-BT CCC Data Blocks of the appropriate structure and format, if required by the CCC definition.
6200 However, if the CCC definition does not require data to be written to the Target, then no additional Data
6201 Blocks and no CRC Block shall be sent.
6202 After the Controller sends the HDR-BT CCC Selector Block, the Target shall either indicate acceptance of
6203 the Direct CCC by acknowledging the write, or else reject the Direct CCC by not responding to the Controller.
6204 The Target shall use the cached Command Code and optional Defining Byte from the previously received
6205 HDR-BT CCC Indicator Block, and shall decide whether to accept or reject the Direct CCC immediately
6206 after receiving the HDR-BT CCC Selector Block and matching its own Dynamic Address (or Group Address,
6207 if assigned and applicable).
6208 The Target shall use the standard methods to either accept (i.e., ACK) or reject (i.e., NACK) an HDR-BT
6209 write, as defined in Section 6.4.3.3.1 regarding the handling of Bits[1:0] of the HDR-BT Header Block’s
6210 Transition byte field.
6211 If the Target rejects the Direct CCC, then the Controller shall either send the HDR Restart Pattern and attempt
6212 another Read or Write Segment for this CCC, or else perform any valid HDR-BT CCC End Procedure. For
6213 Direct CCCs to a Group Address, the Controller only sees this rejection if no Targets accept the Direct CCC.
6214 If the Target accepts the Direct CCC, then the Controller shall either:
6215 • For Direct Write CCCs With Data: Send one or more HDR-BT CCC Data Blocks of the appropriate
6216 structure, if required by the CCC definition, and then send an HDR-BT CCC CRC Block to
6217 terminate the data and end the Segment.
6218 • For Zero-Byte Direct Write CCCs Only: Send no HDR-BT CCC Data Blocks and no HDR-BT
6219 CCC CRC Block, since the Segment ends after the Selector Block.
6220 At the end of a Write Segment, the Controller shall either send the HDR Restart Pattern and attempt another
6221 Read or Write segment for this CCC, or else end the CCC modality by performing any valid HDR-BT CCC
6222 End Procedure, per Section 6.4.3.4.7.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 327
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 342 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.4.3.4.6 HDR-BT CCC Read Segment
6223 An HDR-BT CCC Read Segment for a Direct Read or Direct GET CCC shall start with an HDR-BT CCC
6224 Selector Block to indicate the Address of the Target to provide data for the CCC, followed by a Bus
6225 Turnaround. In response, the indicated Target will either accept the Direct CCC by transmitting one or more
6226 HDR-BT CCC Data Blocks of the appropriate structure, or else reject the Direct CCC by not responding to
6227 the Bus Turnaround condition.
6228 The Target shall use the standard methods to either accept (i.e., ACK) or reject (i.e., NACK) an HDR-BT
6229 read, as defined in Section 6.4.3.3.1 regarding the handling of Bits[1:0] of the HDR-BT Header Block’s
6230 Transition byte field.
6231 If the Target accepts the Direct CCC, then the Controller shall yield control of the Bus to the Target, so that
6232 it can control the Bus to send the Data Blocks. After sending the Last Data Block, the Target shall send an
6233 HDR-BT CRC CCC Block, indicating the end of the read transfer. Upon sending the HDR-BT CCC CRC
6234 Block, the Target shall yield control of the Bus, and the Controller shall resume control.
6235 At the end of a Read Segment, the Controller shall either send the HDR Restart Pattern and attempt another
6236 Read or Write Segment for this CCC, or else end the CCC modality by performing any valid HDR-BT CCC
6237 End Procedure per Section 6.4.3.4.7.
328 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 343 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.4.3.4.7 HDR-BT CCC End Procedures
6238 As illustrated in Figure 136, the HDR-BT CCC ends using one of several possible CCC End Procedures.
6.4.3.4.7.1 Option 1: For Direct CCCs Only: End HDR-BT CCC Followed by New HDR-BT
CCC
6239 This End Procedure indicates the end of a Direct CCC in HDR-BT Mode, and the beginning of a new
6240 Broadcast or Direct CCC in HDR-BT Mode.
6241 This End Procedure begins with an HDR Restart Pattern, followed by an HDR-BT CCC Indicator Block
6242 which provides a new CCC with optional Defining Byte. Since the Indicator Block’s Control field (byte 5)
6243 signals the beginning of a CCC framing (i.e., Bit[3] is set to 1'b0) the Target shall interpret this as the end of
6244 a Direct CCC framing (if present).
6245 Following the Indicator Block, additional HDR-BT Blocks shall follow, per the CCC flow type.
6246 The flow steps are:
6247 1. Controller sends HDR Restart Pattern.
6248 2. Controller sends HDR-BT CCC Indicator Block, using the same format described above.
6249 The bit packing format of this Block shall be appropriate for the current Coding’s common CCC
6250 flow elements (see Section 6.7.3.5.3.1).
6251 3. Targets acknowledge receipt of Indicator Block, as defined above.
6252 4. Flow continues as Broadcast CCC or Direct CCC, as shown above.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 329
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 344 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.4.3.4.7.2 Option 2: For Direct CCCs Only: End HDR-BT CCC Followed by HDR-BT
Generic Transaction (+ Optional HDR Exit)
6253 This End Procedure indicates the end of a Direct CCC in HDR-BT Mode, and either a return to standard
6254 HDR-BT transactions, a subsequent Broadcast CCC in HDR-BT Mode, or a subsequent exit from HDR-BT
6255 Mode.
6256 This End Procedure begins with an HDR Restart Pattern, followed by a special HDR-BT Header Block which
6257 ends the CCC flow, but is neither an Indicator nor a Selector. Since this Header Block is neither an Indicator
6258 nor a Selector, no CCC or optional Defining Byte is included. And since no data bytes are sent, this End
6259 Procedure has no HDR-BT Data Blocks and no HDR-BT CRC Block either.
6260 After this, the HDR Restart Pattern signals the end of CCC modality, and that the next HDR-BT Header
6261 Block would be treated as a standard HDR-BT transaction (not as a CCC flow) unless it is addressed to 7'h7E
6262 (i.e., if the next message is a Broadcast CCC). Alternatively, the HDR Exit Pattern signals the end of HDR-
6263 BT Mode and a subsequent return to SDR Mode.
6264 In either case, this HDR-BT Header Block is distinguished from an HDR-BT CCC Indicator Block because
6265 it indicates a ‘Read’ command from the Broadcast Address (7'h7E), which would otherwise be an invalid
6266 combination for HDR-BT transactions. Target Devices shall interpret this condition as the Termination
6267 Marker ending the CCC modality for the Direct CCC, since this condition is distinct from the start of a new
6268 Broadcast CCC or Direct CCC (per CCC End Procedure Option 1, above).
6269 The flow steps are:
6270 1. Controller sends HDR Restart Pattern.
6271 2. Controller sends HDR-BT Header Block, using the following values:
6272 • The Address field (byte 0) shall contain the Broadcast Address (7'h7E / R).
6273 • The Control field (byte 5) shall be set to indicate the end of the CCC modality:
6274 • Bit[0] contains 1'b1 to indicate a Read.
6275 • Bit[3] contains 1'b0 to indicate that Direct CCC framing is not continued from a prior
6276 Selector Block (if applicable).
6277 • Bit[4] contains 1'b1 to indicate use of the new HDR-BT framing as described above.
6278 • Other bitfields shall be set accordingly.
6279 • The bit packing format of this Block shall be appropriate for the current Coding’s common
6280 CCC flow elements (see Section 6.7.3.5.3.1).
6281 3. Targets do not acknowledge receipt of the HDR-BT Header Block.
6282 4. Targets detect this distinct pattern (i.e., Read from 7'h7E with no Data Block) and end CCC
6283 modality.
6284 5. Controller sends either HDR Restart Pattern, or HDR Exit Pattern.
6285 • If HDR Exit Pattern, then Controller exits HDR-BT Mode and returns the Bus to SDR Mode.
6.4.3.4.7.3 Option 3: For Broadcast CCCs Only: Send HDR Restart Pattern
6286 After a Broadcast CCC, the CCC modality ends automatically, and the Controller simply sends an HDR
6287 Restart Pattern and then continues with subsequent HDR-BT transactions.
6288 The single flow step is:
6289 1. Controller sends HDR Restart Pattern.
6.4.3.4.7.4 Option 4: End HDR-BT CCC and Return to SDR Mode
6290 This is the simplest End Procedure option. It indicates the end of CCCs in HDR-BT Mode and an immediate
6291 exit from HDR-BT Mode, when the Controller sends the HDR Exit Pattern.

Figure 136 HDR-BT CCC End Procedure Options
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 331
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 346 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.4.3.5 Options
6293 Targets that support HDR-BT shall support CRC-16.
6294 Targets that support HDR-BT may optionally also support CRC-32. When the GETCAPS Format 1 CCC is
6295 sent (see Section 4.3.7.3.19), a Target that supports CRC-32 in HDR-BT Mode shall return a value of 1'b1 in
6296 bit 5 of the GETCAP3 byte (the third returned data byte). A Controller that sees the value 1'b1 in that bit will
6297 know that the Target is capable of supporting CRC-32 in HDR-BT Mode.
6298 Targets that support HDR-BT shall support SCL from the Controller on Read. They may also optionally
6299 support emitting their own SCL on Read.
6300 Note:
6301 The I3C Specification does not define a mechanism for discovering a Target’s capability to control
6302 SCL for Read transfers. The Controller may select whether the Target should control SCL or not
6303 control SCL, using Bit[2] of the Control byte in the Header Block. Per Section 6.4.3.3.1, a Target
6304 that does not actually support driving SCL for Read transfers shall not accept (i.e., must provide
6305 NACK for) the Read transfer request, if it receives such a Header Block with Bit[2] set to 1 in the
6306 Control byte. In this case, the Controller should retry the same Read transfer with Bit[2] set to 0, to
6307 indicate that the Controller will control SCL for the Read transfer. If the Read transfer request’s
6308 Header Block was otherwise valid, then the Target should accept (i.e., should provide ACK for) this
6309 Read transfer.
6310 Targets may choose to support the Data Block Delay mechanism for Read transfers. This mechanism allows
6311 the Target to delay sending a Data Block (when permitted by the Controller, as indicated in the Header Block)
6312 if it does not have enough data bytes to fill the Data Block (see Section 6.4.3.3.4).
6313 Note:
6314 The I3C Specification does not define a mechanism for discovering whether the Target supports the
6315 Data Block Delay mechanism. If the Target does not support the Data Block Delay mechanism, then
6316 it shall ignore the value of Bit[2] in the Control byte of the Header Block, when the Header Block
6317 indicates that this is a Read transfer. In such a case, the Target must either return full Data Blocks,
6318 except for the Last Data Block; or mitigate the situation, depending on whether it controls SCL for
6319 this Read transfer.
6320 A) If the Target controls SCL for this Read transfer, then it must either reduce the data rate or end
6321 the transfer early, if it is unable to sustain the transfer of data bytes.
6322 B) If the Target does not control SCL for this Read transfer, or if it does not have that capability,
6323 then it must end the transfer early, if it is unable to sustain the transfer of data bytes.
6324 If the Target does support the Data Block Delay mechanism, and if it was permitted to use this
6325 mechanism for a Read transfer, then the Target may use either of these methods above that might
6326 be valid or permitted, and for which it has the capability; alternately, it may also use Delay bytes, per
6327 Section 6.4.3.3.4.

second half-clock of C1. If receiver drives SDA[0] to Low may drive Transition_Control Bit[4] High (shown in blue)

the Transition_Control byte. Transmitter then emits a CRC of t BT_DBD Delay bytes. (Does not apply for Last Data Block

6.4.3.7 Transfer Flow Control
6337 HDR-BT Mode provides several procedures that allow either the Controller or the Target to control the
6338 progress of data transfers. This Section describes these flow control elements.
6339 Note:
6340 In contrast to HDR-DDR Mode and HDR-Ternary Modes, in HDR-BT Mode the flow control elements
6341 are always available, i.e., the Controller does not need to use any special configuration procedures
6342 to configure Targets to either utilize, or properly react to, the flow control elements during transfers.
6343 This is in contrast to HDR-DDR Mode and the HDR-Ternary Modes, where flow control must be
6344 explicitly configured via the ENDXFER CCC.
6.4.3.7.1 Controller Clock Stalling
6345 To avoid Underrun or Overflow situations, the Controller may stall the I3C Bus. Stalling occurs during the
6346 SCL Low period, under the conditions described below.
6347 During the Header Block:
6348 • The Controller may stall after the last falling edge of the Control byte in the HDR-BT Header
6349 Block, and hold SCL Low before the Transition byte. Subsequently, driving SCL to High for the
6350 first rising edge of the Transition byte will end the stall.
6351 During a Write or Read Transfer:
6352 • The Controller may stall after the last falling clock edge of the Transition_Control byte in the
6353 HDR-BT Data Block, and hold SCL Low before the first data byte. Subsequently, driving SCL to
6354 High for the first rising edge of the first data byte will end the stall.
6355 • However, if the receiver pulls SDA[0] to Low for Bit[1] to terminate the transfer, then the
6356 Controller should not stall the I3C Bus.
6357 Note:
6358 Clock stalling in HDR-BT Mode is not allowed if the Target transmits SCL for a Read transfer. Instead
6359 of stalling, the Target should use the Data Block Delay mechanism (see Section 6.4.3.3.4).
6360 Clock stalling is not defined during the HDR-BT CRC Block, primarily because it should not be
6361 necessary to stall after the Last Data Block. (The transmitter should not need extra time to compute
6362 the CRC checksum for the transmitted data bytes, and the receiver should not need extra time to
6363 compute the CRC checksum for the received data bytes and compare it with the CRC checksum that
6364 it will receive in the CRC Block.)
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 341
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 356 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F003 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.4.3.7.2 Transfer Flow Control During Write Transfers
6365 The Target can use the Transition byte, Transition_Control byte, and Transition_Verify byte to accept or
6366 reject a Write transfer, or to provide flow control at defined points during the Write transfer.
6367 Figure 146 shows several examples of Write transfer flow control:
6368 • A Target that does not accept the Write transfer;
6369 • A Target that accepts the Write transfer and receives all Data Blocks;
6370 • A Target that terminates the Write transfer before the Controller has sent all Data Blocks; and
6371 • A Target that indicates a CRC mismatch (or other error) after it has received the Data Blocks.

6.4.3.7.3 Target Flow Control During Read Transfers
6373 The Target can also use the Transition byte, Transition_Control byte, and Transition_Verify byte to accept
6374 or reject a Read transfer, or to provide flow control at defined points during the Read transfer. If the Data
6375 Block Delay mechanism is permitted (per Section 6.4.3.3.4), then the Target may also use this mechanism to
6376 delay sending Data Blocks while it prepares data to send to the Controller.
6377 Figure 147 shows several examples of Read transfer flow control:
6378 • A Target that does not accept the Read transfer;
6379 • A Target that accepts the Read transfer and sends all Data Blocks (i.e., without any delay or
6380 termination);
6381 • A Target that handles the Controller terminating the Read transfer, and subsequently provides the
6382 CRC Block after the last Data Block that it sent; and
6383 • A Target that uses the Data Block Delay mechanism before sending the first Data Block (i.e.,
6384 assuming that was is allowed to do so by the Controller, as indicated by Bit[2] = 1'b0 in the
6385 Transition byte of this transfer's Header Block).
