6.5 F004: Secondary Controller
6387 This Section defines I3C Optional Feature F004: Secondary Controller.
6388 Table 95 Optional Feature 004 Status
Optional Feature ID F004
Optional Feature Name Secondary Controller
Optional Feature Version 1
Optional Feature specification was last updated in I3C Spec Version I3C v1.2
Optional Feature is not compatible with other Optional Features –
Optional Feature is included in I3C Basic Yes
6389 Changes in this Version
6390 For detailed changes in this version, see the “diff” file between I3C v1.1.1 and I3C v1.2 in the MIPI
6391 Specification Repository [MIPI09].
6.5.1 Scope and Purpose (Informative)
6392 The scope of Section 6.1 is limited to fully specifying I3C Optional Feature F004: Secondary Controller. An
6393 I3C Device that implements F004 is required to implement all shall statements in Section 6.5.3, and can
6394 optionally implement any combination of “may” statements in Section 6.5.3. In addition, MIPI Alliance
6395 recommends that manufacturers follow all “should” statements in Section 6.5.3.
6.5.2 Technical Overview (Informative)
6396 It is possible for an I3C Bus to have multiple Devices on the Bus that are capable of being an Active
6397 Controller. These Devices, which initially act as a Target but can also accept the Active Controller Role from
6398 an Active Controller, are referred to as Secondary Controllers. The following sections specify how to
6399 implement a Secondary Controller, and define how the handoff of the Active Controller Role to a Secondary
6400 Controller is completed.
6401 Secondary Controllers are not required to complete Bus Configuration, and they can be implemented for
6402 special feature handling, power savings, or other various purposes. The minimum requirements for a
6403 Secondary Controller are specified in Section 6.5.3.3.1 and Section 6.5.3.3.2.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 345
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 360 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE 004 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.5.3 Technical Specification
6404 An I3C Bus may have multiple Controller-capable Devices, with varying capabilities and purposes. All
6405 Controller-capable Devices shall support the required Bus Management procedures while acting as Active
6406 Controller. Although only one I3C Controller-capable Device can act as Active Controller of the I3C Bus at
6407 any given time, the role of Active Controller may be passed between Controller-capable Devices, using the
6408 Controller to Controller Handoff Procedure (see Section 6.5.3.2). The state of a Controller-capable Device
6409 currently holding the role of Active Controller on the I3C Bus is referred to as the Controller Role.
6410 At least one Controller-capable Device shall support the required Bus Configuration procedures for a Bus. A
6411 Secondary Controller may perform some of these Bus Configuration procedures, as documented in
6412 Section 6.5.3.3 depending on the specific application for the I3C Bus and the intended use cases of the
6413 Secondary Controller. A Secondary Controller that cannot, or might not, perform all required Bus
6414 Configuration procedures should defer or pass the Controller Role to another suitable Controller-capable
6415 Device if it encounters a condition that it cannot or might not handle while acting as Active Controller.
6416 Additionally, the Controller-capable Device that has the job of initializing the I3C Bus holds the special role
6417 of Primary Controller. A Primary Controller Device is, by definition, the first Controller-capable Device to
6418 be the Active Controller on the Bus. The Primary Controller acts as the authority for the initial configuration
6419 of the Bus and all Devices, including any Legacy I2C Devices. Therefore, the Primary Controller must be
6420 capable of all required Bus Configuration procedures. However, once the Bus has been initialized and
6421 configured, the Primary Controller can also pass the Controller Role to any other Controller-capable Device
6422 currently acting as Secondary Controller.
6423 Using the Handoff procedure, either multiple Controller-capable Devices can cooperatively pass the
6424 Controller Role back and forth, or else one Controller-capable Device can direct the flow of passing the
6425 Controller Role among other Controller-capable Devices according to the capabilities of any I3C Secondary
6426 Controllers on the Bus and the I3C Bus’s particular application.
6427 A Secondary Controller may request the Controller Role from the Active Controller using the Controller Role
6428 Request flow detailed in Section 4.3.6.3. Alternately, the Active Controller may choose a particular
6429 Secondary Controller to receive the Controller Role, even if that Secondary Controller did not request the
6430 Controller Role. In either case, the names Active Controller and Secondary Controller largely reflect the
6431 Controller-capable Device’s current role at any given point in time.
6432 After acknowledging a Controller Role Request or choosing a Secondary Controller to receive the Controller
6433 Role, the Active Controller should:
6434 1. Prepare the Bus and the indicated Secondary Controller for Handoff (see Section 6.5.3.1);
6435 2. Pass the Controller Role to the indicated Secondary Controller using the Controller to
6436 Controller Handoff Procedure (see Section 6.5.3.2); and
6437 3. Monitor the Bus to ensure that the indicated Secondary Controller asserts its Controller Role
6438 (see Section 4.3.8.2.4 on Error Type CE3).
6439 The process for passing the Controller Role is the same in either direction: it is always initiated by the Active
6440 Controller (i.e., the Controller-capable Device holding the Controller Role at that point in time). In most
6441 cases the immediately previous Active Controller can request to receive the Controller Role back again, or
6442 the indicated Secondary Controller can automatically pass the Controller Role back once it has finished its
6443 tasks. However, in some Bus implementations it might be advisable to designate one Controller-capable
6444 Device to direct and manage the flow of passing the Controller Role to/from any Secondary Controllers.
6445 While acting as Active Controller, a Secondary Controller may choose not to handle any Bus Configuration
6446 procedures. In some circumstances, a Secondary Controller might be intended for a limited purpose or scope
6447 on a particular I3C Bus, and as a result might limit its activities while serving as Active Controller to some
6448 subset of what it is capable of performing; for example, by not supporting Bus Configuration procedures.
346 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 361 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURE F004 Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
6.5.3.1 Preparing for Handoff
6449 It may be necessary for the Active Controller to issue one or more commands on the I3C Bus to prepare other
6450 I3C Target Devices for the Handoff and ensure an optimal transition of the Controller Role, irrespective of
6451 whether the handoff is due to a Secondary Controller requesting the Controller Role via a Controller Role
6452 Request, or is due to the Active Controller having simply chosen to pass the Controller Role to a particular
6453 Secondary Controller.
6454 There are many possible variations of the steps that the Active Controller should take to prepare for a Handoff.
6455 Figure 148 presents a generalized flow, and this Section provides a recommended procedure.

Figure 148 Pre-Handoff Steps Flow
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 347
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 362 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE 004 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6457 Recommended procedure:
6458 1. If the Active Controller intends to pass the Controller Role to a selected Secondary Controller
6459 that did not send a Controller Role Request, then the Active Controller should verify that the
6460 selected Secondary Controller is active and ready to respond to additional commands (see
6461 Section 4.3.7.3.15, the GETSTATUS CCC).
6462 Note:
6463 If the Secondary Controller responds to the GETSTATUS CCC and returns a value of 2'b11 in
6464 bits 7:6 (i.e., LSB) of the GETSTATUS Format 1 CCC (see Table 27), then this indicates that it
6465 is not able to participate in any subsequent steps to prepare for Handoff. The Active Controller
6466 should stop the procedure at this point, and may initiate an error recovery procedure.
6467 2. If the Active Controller needs to disable Hot-Joins, Target Interrupt Requests, or other Bus
6468 events that could interfere with the Handoff, then it should send the appropriate Broadcast or
6469 Direct CCCs to disable those events before the Handoff (see Section 4.3.7.3.1, the
6470 ENEC/DISEC CCCs).
6471 Note:
6472 Once the Handoff is complete, the new Active Controller should re-enable any events that are
6473 disabled in this step.
6474 3. If the Active Controller is Multi-Lane capable, and does not have knowledge that the selected
6475 Secondary Controller supports and is configured to use the same Multi-Lane Modes and
6476 codings that any Multi-Lane capable Target Devices have been configured to use, then the
6477 Active Controller should re-configure the I3C Bus such that all Target Devices are configured
6478 to use an ML Frame format that is known to be supported by the selected Secondary Controller
6479 (see Section 6.7.3.6, the MLANE CCC).
6480 Note:
6481 In some situations, ensuring maximum compatibility and preventing communication errors after
6482 Handoff might require resetting these Multi-Lane capable Target Devices to fundamental 2-
6483 Wire I3C Mode (i.e., 1-Lane).
6484 4. If the Active Controller knows that the selected Secondary Controller must be put into a
6485 different Activity State before Handoff, then the Active Controller shall send the appropriate
6486 Broadcast or Direct CCCs to put the Bus (or selected Devices) into a different Activity State
6487 (see Section 4.3.7.3.18 regarding the GETMXDS CCC with optional Defining Byte, and
6488 Section 4.3.7.3.2, the ENTASx CCCs).
6489 5. If the Active Controller determines that the indicated Secondary Controller has been in a “deep
6490 sleep” state and may need to be re-synchronized with the most current list of I3C Targets and
6491 Group Addresses, then the Active Controller should send the appropriate Broadcast CCCs (see
6492 Section 4.3.7.3.7, the DEFTGTS CCC, and Section 4.3.7.3.26, the DEFGRPA CCC).
6493 Afterwards, the Active Controller should poll the Secondary Controller to ensure that it has
6494 successfully processed this data and indicates that it is ready to accept the Controller Role (see
6495 Section 4.3.7.3.15 regarding the GETSTATUS CCC with optional Defining Byte).
6496 While steps 2–4 can generally be performed in any order, step 5 (or any other steps requiring
6497 re-synchronization of data) should be done after steps 1–4, and immediately before checking for the
6498 Secondary Controller’s status in processing the Broadcast data (if this is supported and indicated).
6499 Figure 148 presents a generalized flow of the steps the Active Controller should take to prepare for a Handoff.
6500 Some of these steps require the Active Controller to determine some of the optional Secondary Controller
6501 features and capabilities advertised by the Secondary Controller. In most cases, these can be determined by
6502 reading the data returned by the Secondary Controller in response to various CCCs with a Defining Byte (see
6503 Section 4.3.7.3.18 regarding the GETMXDS CCC with optional Defining Byte, and Section 4.3.7.3.19
6504 regarding the GETCAPS CCC with optional Defining Byte):
348 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 363 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURE F004 Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
6505 • If the Active Controller has not already gathered this information and needs to determine whether
6506 the Secondary Controller requires special handling based on these features and capabilities, then it
6507 shall do so before taking any related steps that would require this information.
6508 • However, if the Active Controller has already gathered this information in advance, then it may
6509 rely on cached data, and does not need to gather it again.
6510 • Once all the data has been gathered, the Active Controller must make its decisions relating to the
6511 pre-Handoff flow steps, based on the data returned from the Secondary Controller.
6512 When the Active Controller has verified that the Bus is configured correctly, and the selected Secondary
6513 Controller indicates that it is ready to accept the Controller Role, the Active Controller shall initiate the
6514 Controller to Controller Handoff Procedure per Section 6.5.3.2 (next).
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 349
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 364 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE 004 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.5.3.2 Controller to Controller Handoff Procedure
6515 After the Active Controller has prepared for Handoff, the Active Controller shall then issue a GETACCCR
6516 CCC (Section 4.3.7.3.16) followed by a STOP if the Handoff was successful, whereupon it shall release
6517 control of the SCL line, and therefore also releasing control of the I3C Bus (i.e., passing the Controller Role)
6518 to the selected Secondary Controller.
6519 Following the STOP and Bus Available Condition, the selected Secondary Controller assumes the role of the
6520 Active Controller and takes control of the I3C Bus. The former Active Controller should monitor the I3C Bus
6521 to ensure that the new Active Controller asserts its Controller Role, according to the flow described in
6522 Section 4.3.8.2.4 regarding Error Type CE3.
6523 Figure 89 presents a simplified and generalized picture of the Controller to Controller Handoff procedure.
6524 While there are many possible variations to this procedure, the most significant states are:
6525 1. At the end of data transfer from a successful GETACCCR CCC, the current Active Controller
6526 (indicated by ‘CCR’ in the Figure) controls SCL and SDA, and the new Controller capable
6527 Device that will become the new Active Controller (indicated by ‘NCR’ in the Figure, initially
6528 acting as a Secondary Controller) has SCL and SDA in High-Z state.
6529 2. The CCR provides the rising edge of SCL, while keeping SDA Low
6530 3. After t SU_STO elapses, the CCR drives SDA High, using either an active drive or the Open Drain
6531 class Pull-Up. However, once SDA is High the Open Drain class Pull-Up shall be used.
6532 4. After the NCR determines that SDA is High, and after both its Clock to Data Turnaround Time
6533 (t SCO ) and the time of flight elapse, it then takes two actions:
6534 A. The NCR actively drives SCL High, overlapping with the active drive of the CCR; and
6535 B. The NCR enables its Open Drain class Pull-Up, in parallel with the Open Drain class Pull-
6536 Up of the CCR
6537 Neither of these actions conflicts with the CCR.
6538 Note:
6539 Although tSCO generally applies to any Target Device, in this step it applies to the NCR because
6540 prior to the Bus transfer the NCR performs in the Target role (i.e., as a Secondary Controller
6541 unit until it receives the Controller Role).
6542 In the Figure, tSCO is shown starting as if the CCR has used the Open Drain drive of SDA High.
6543 This illustrates the worst condition for this stage of the Handoff, showing the latest possible
6544 moment when the NCR starts overlapping the line controls with the CCR.
6545 5. After a time delay of t CRHPOverlap , the Active Controller (i.e., the CCR):
6546 A. Releases SCL to High-Z; and
6547 B. Disables its Open Drain class Pull-Up on SDA and sets SDA to High-Z
6548 Note:
6549 In the Figure, tCRHPOverlap is shown in the worst case: on the Open Drain drive of SDA, and
6550 where the CCR starts counting it from the lower side of the SDA rising edge. The CCR must
6551 control the lines until the NCR could safely take over, and that is certain after tDIG_OD_L Min. As
6552 a result, tCRHPOverlap is greater than or equal to tDIG_OD_L Min.
6553 6. After t NEWCRLock (i.e., the time interval during which the NCR shall not drive SDA Low), the
6554 NCR may actively drive SDA Low, producing a START.
6555 The Targets may also drive SDA Low at this point, since SDA is held by an Open Drain class Pull-
6556 Up. The t NEWCRLock period timing parameter is similar to I3C’s t AVAL parameter and I2C’s t BUF
6557 parameter, and as such, requires different values for Pure Bus vs. Mixed Bus.
6558 Note:
6559 In the Figure, tNEWCRLock is shown as starting at the SDA rising edge in Open Drain driving
6560 mode, similar to I3C’s tAVAL parameter and I2C’s tBUF parameter.
350 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 365 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURE F004 Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
6561 7. After t CAS expires, the NCR may drive SCL Low, producing the first SCL falling edge.
6562 Following this SCL falling edge, the new Active Controller shall actively drive SCL, while also
6563 driving SDA in Open Drain mode with the Arbitrable Address.
6564 After the Handoff procedure, the new Active Controller (i.e., NCR in the steps above) should issue a START
6565 to assert its Controller Role within 100 μs, or the time interval indicated by its Activity State (as reported by
6566 the GETMXDS CCC with optional Defining Byte, per Section 4.3.7.3.18), whichever is greater. If the new
6567 Active Controller has not issued a START, then it shall respond to SDA being pulled Low by pulling SCL
6568 Low; this is a START, and the new Active Controller may follow it with any valid Bus activity that is
6569 sufficient to assert the Controller Role.
6570 However, if the new Active Controller does not pull SCL Low within 100 μs of SDA being pulled Low, then
6571 it might lose the Controller Role to the former Active Controller (i.e., CCR in the steps above). To avoid a
6572 hung Bus, the former Active Controller should detect whether the new Active Controller has taken over the
6573 Bus per the Error Type CE3 flow described in Section 4.3.8.2.4.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 351
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 366 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE 004 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.5.3.3 Secondary Controller Functions
6574 A Secondary Controller Device initially acts as a Target when it joins the Bus (either at Bus Initialization, or
6575 via Hot-Join) and, like any other Target, shall respond to the standard (i.e., Required) CCCs.
6576 A Secondary Controller shall indicate that it is a Controller-capable Device, by returning a value of 2'b01 in
6577 its BCR Device Role bits (see Table 7) when queried by the Active Controller. The Active Controller shall
6578 use this value to determine whether any Secondary Controllers are on the Bus, so it knows whether it is
6579 obligated to send out other CCCs (such as DEFTGTS or DEFGRPA) that contain Broadcast data that the
6580 Secondary Controller needs to use when it becomes Active Controller.
6581 Some Secondary Controllers may require special handling before becoming Active Controller, as listed in
6582 the steps to prepare for Handoff (see Section 6.5.3.1). Therefore, such Devices must advertise certain
6583 capabilities or status to the Active Controller, by supporting one or more optional Defining Bytes for the
6584 following CCCs:
6585 • GETSTATUS Format 2 CCC with the PRECR Defining Byte (see Section 4.3.7.3.15)
6586 • GETMXDS Format 3 CCC with the CRHDLY Defining Byte (see Section 4.3.7.3.18)
6587 • GETCAPS Format 2 CCC with the CRCAPS Defining Byte (see Section 4.3.7.3.19)
6588 A Secondary Controller may automatically enter lower-power modes or a “deep sleep” state based on
6589 inactivity, or via commands from other connected logic. Such a Secondary Controller should be capable of
6590 returning to an active state when it detects and responds to certain CCCs sent by the Active Controller, since
6591 the Active Controller may check to ensure that the Secondary Controller is online and ready to receive the
6592 Controller Role (see Section 4.3.8.2.5) before starting the process of preparing for Handoff (see
6593 Section 6.5.3.1). This is especially important for situations where the Active Controller is preparing to pass
6594 the Controller Role, but the Secondary Controller did not send a Controller Role Request.
6595 For such situations, the Secondary Controller must return from its lower-power mode or “deep sleep” state,
6596 respond to CCCs sent by the Active Controller to prepare for Handoff, and remain ready to receive the
6597 Controller Role, unless it is constrained from doing so. If the Secondary Controller is constrained or unable
6598 to participate, then it may indicate this status by responding to the GETSTATUS Format 1 CCC (see
6599 Section 4.3.7.3.15) and returning a value of 2'b11 in bits 7:6 (see Table 27). However, if the Secondary
6600 Controller is able to participate and it supports the GETSTATUS Format 2 CCC with the PRECR Defining
6601 Byte, then it shall treat this CCC and Defining Byte as a definitive indication that the Active Controller
6602 intends to pass the Controller Role in the near future.
6603 A Secondary Controller may initiate a Controller Role Request (see Section 4.3.6.3) to the Active Controller.
6604 A Secondary Controller may also accept the Controller Role from any Active Controller (including the
6605 Primary Controller), at which point it becomes the new Active Controller.
6606 Once granted control of the Bus by the previous Active Controller via the Controller to Controller Handoff
6607 procedure, the Secondary Controller maintains control until another Controller-capable Device is granted
6608 Bus control. After the Secondary Controller transitions to the being the Active Controller, it could encounter
6609 Bus management activities besides the data transfers that it initiates itself, including: In-Band Interrupts, Hot-
6610 Join Requests, and Controller Role Requests from other Controller-capable Devices such as another
6611 Secondary Controller or the previous Active Controller.
6612 A Secondary Controller shall support the scenarios described in the following subsections 6.5.3.3.1 through
6613 6.5.3.3.9, according to its capabilities.
6614 Per Section 6.5.3.3.3, the Secondary Controller may choose to perform some or all Bus Configuration
6615 procedures while acting as Active Controller. And per Section 6.5.3.3.4, the Secondary Controller may also
6616 choose not to perform any Bus Configuration procedures while acting as Active Controller. In either case, if
6617 the Secondary Controller receives a request (e.g., a Hot-Join Request) that it elects not to handle, then it may
6618 choose to defer the request by sending a DISEC CCC (Section 4.3.7.3.1), and may also immediately pass the
6619 Controller Role along to a more capable Controller that can handling that request. Any Secondary Controller
6620 acting as Active Controller may still freely use Bus Management procedures, in addition to the ENEC/DISEC
6621 CCCs (Section 4.3.7.3.1), to enable or disable requests on the Bus.
352 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 367 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURE F004 Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
6.5.3.3.1 Hardware and Software Requirements
6622 A Secondary Controller capable of acting as Active Controller shall have the following minimum hardware
6623 and software capabilities:
6624 1. Memory for:
6625 A. All Bus Devices’ capabilities and functions at system level
6626 B. Retaining the Dynamic Addresses of all I3C Bus Devices
6627 C. Retaining the data transfer protocol that the I3C Bus Devices are capable of
6628 D. Retaining the active Group Addresses, as well as the lists of all I3C Bus Devices assigned
6629 to these Group Addresses, if applicable and supported
6630 E. Retaining the currently-configured Multi-Lane Modes and codings of all I3C Bus Devices
6631 that support Multi-Lane, if applicable and supported
6632 2. Link requirements for Targets that are intended to transmit In-Band Interrupt requests (IBI),
6633 e.g., the clock speed and the maximum length of data transfer
6634 3. Data transfer protocol capability needed for communicating to all I3C Bus Devices
6.5.3.3.2 Minimum Bus Management Procedures
6635 A Secondary Controller capable of acting as Active Controller shall perform the following minimum Bus
6636 Management procedures:
6637 1. During Bus initialization, if a Secondary Controller is connected to the I3C Bus, then it shall
6638 monitor the Bus for the Primary Controller to send a DEFTGTS CCC. A Secondary Controller
6639 shall also monitor the Bus for any Active Controller to send a subsequent DEFTGTS CCC
6640 containing any updates as a result of Dynamic Address changes, or of subsequent Hot-Join
6641 Requests. Using the most recent Broadcast, the Secondary Controller shall acquire the
6642 Addresses and Characteristics of all Devices on the I3C Bus from the data in that DEFTGTS
6643 CCC (see Section 4.3.7.3.7).
6644 A Secondary Controller may also monitor the Bus during Dynamic Address assignment (see
6645 Section 4.3.4.2).
6646 2. Manage the In-Band Interrupt procedure. The Secondary Controller needs to know the
6647 meaning of the interrupt from the Target. If the Secondary Controller accepts the IBI, then the
6648 Secondary Controller shall service the IBI appropriately (see Section 6.5.3.3.5).
6649 3. Procedure for requesting transfer of Bus control to another Controller-capable Device,
6650 including the Primary Controller or any previous Active Controller. The Secondary Controller
6651 uses the GETACCCR CCC (Get Accept Controller Role, Section 4.3.7.3.16). The Secondary
6652 Controller should also handle Controller Role Requests from another Controller-capable
6653 Device.
6654 In addition, while acting as Active Controller a Secondary Controller may also utilize other CCCs to change
6655 the state of the Bus, or the state of any Devices, such as entering an Activity State, or disabling Target Events
6656 such as Interrupts or Controller Role Requests. If a Secondary Controller does so, then before passing the
6657 Controller Role to another Controller-capable Device the Secondary Controller should restore the state of the
6658 Bus, or the state of any Devices, to a prior state, or to a state known to be compatible with the prior state.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 353
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 368 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE 004 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.5.3.3.3 Bus Configuration Procedures
6659 A Secondary Controller capable of changing the Bus Configuration while acting as Active Controller may
6660 perform any of the following Bus Configuration procedures:
6661 1. Perform the Dynamic Address Assignment procedure for Hot-Join Devices:
6662 A. In order to be able to assign the correct priority level to the Hot-Join Device, the
6663 Secondary Controller needs to know all Bus Devices’ functionality at the system level.
6664 B. The Secondary Controller may also perform the Dynamic Address Assignment procedure
6665 while acting as Active Controller, without a Hot-Join event. It may also reset a Device’s
6666 Dynamic Address, or directly assign a Dynamic Address to a Device for any other reason.
6667 C. If the Secondary Controller changes the Bus Configuration for the above reasons while
6668 acting as Active Controller, then it shall issue the DEFTGTS CCC (Define List of Targets,
6669 Section 4.3.7.3.7) to ensure that other Controller-capable Devices have the same
6670 knowledge.
6671 2. Manage Group Address assignments for other I3C Devices that support Group Addressing, if
6672 applicable and supported.
6673 To support Group Addressing, a Secondary Controller uses the following CCCs:
6674 • SETGRPA (Set Group Address), Section 4.3.7.3.24
6675 • RSTGRPA (Reset Group Address), Section 4.3.7.3.25
6676 • DEFGRPA (Define List of Group Addresses), Section 4.3.7.3.26
6677 The Secondary Controller shall also monitor the Bus for an Active Controller to send DEFGRPA
6678 CCCs containing Group Address data, comprising lists of the I3C Target Devices assigned to each
6679 known Group Address, if any Group Addresses are known and reported in a DEFTGTS CCC.
6680 If the Secondary Controller changes any Group Address assignments while acting as Active
6681 Controller, then it shall send the appropriate Group Address data using the DEFTGTS
6682 (Section 4.3.7.3.7) and DEFGRPA (Section 4.3.7.3.26) CCCs to ensure that other Controller-
6683 capable Devices have the same knowledge.
6684 3. Configure other Multi-Lane capable I3C Devices to use any supported Multi-Lane Modes and
6685 codings while acting as Active Controller, if applicable and supported. The Secondary
6686 Controller uses the MLANE CCC for this (Multi-Lane Data Transfer Control, Section 6.7.3.6).
6687 A Secondary Controller that can perform any of these Bus Configuration procedures shall advertise its
6688 support for these features and capabilities by providing the appropriate bit values in the CRCAP1 byte that
6689 is returned when an Active Controller sends the Direct GETCAPS Format 2 CCC with the optional CRCAPS
6690 Defining Byte (Secondary Controller Capabilities, see Section 4.3.7.3.19).
6691 In some cases, a Secondary Controller might not need to assign or change any Dynamic Addresses for any
6692 Devices, nor assign nor change Group Addresses for any Devices, nor change the Multi-Lane configurations
6693 of any Devices, while acting as Active Controller. The decision on whether to use these Bus Configuration
6694 procedures could depend upon the Secondary Controller’s available features and capabilities, upon
6695 instructions or settings received from another Controller (including the Primary Controller), or upon the
6696 specific use cases required for that Secondary Controller within the Bus.
354 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 369 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURE F004 Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
6.5.3.3.4 Reduced Functionality Secondary Controllers
6697 Secondary Controllers are not required to implement or perform any Bus Configuration procedures. The
6698 Secondary Controller may defer or transfer the Bus control to another Controller-capable Device when
6699 needed; see Section 6.5.3.3.1 and Section 6.5.3.3.2.
6700 A Secondary Controller with reduced functionality shall have the following minimum capabilities:
6701 1. Memory sufficient for retaining Device Addresses and Characteristics of any Targets it
6702 supports
6703 2. Memory sufficient for retaining the Device Address and Characteristics of the Controller to
6704 which it will return Bus control
6.5.3.3.5 In-Band Interrupt Handling
6705 A Secondary Controller may either accept an In-Band-Interrupt request from a Target, or refuse it.
6706 Since the Target emits its Address in the Arbitrated Address header, the Secondary Controller may base the
6707 decision to accept or reject the IBI based on the Address (per Section 4.3.6) and/or the Secondary Controller’s
6708 intended use case. If a Secondary Controller elects to accept an IBI from a particular Target, then it shall
6709 service that IBI request normally per Section 5.1.6.2.
6710 Some Secondary Controllers may elect to simply refuse IBIs from all Addresses, whereas other Secondary
6711 Controllers may base the decision on the Address in each IBI request. If a Secondary Controller elects to
6712 refuse an IBI from a particular Target for any reason, then it should issue the DISEC CCC to the interrupting
6713 Target, with the DISINT bit set (Disable Target Events Command, Section 4.3.7.3.1), and then defer IBI
6714 handling to another capable Controller.
6715 A Secondary Controller that refuses IBIs from all Target Addresses should also issue a Broadcast DISEC
6716 CCC with the DISINT bit set, to disable interrupts from all Targets. To avoid Target issues that might arise
6717 from an IBI not being accepted and serviced in a timely manner, a Secondary Controller that refuses an IBI
6718 should also plan to pass the Controller Role to another capable Controller within a reasonably short time after
6719 refusing that IBI.
6.5.3.3.6 Hot-Join Management
6720 A Secondary Controller may either support Hot-Join Requests, or not support them.
6721 If a Secondary Controller supports Hot-Join Requests, then it may either manage the Hot-Join Request while
6722 acting as Active Controller per Section 4.3.5, or else defer management to a capable Controller by issuing a
6723 Broadcast DISEC CCC to disable Hot-Join Requests by setting the DISHJ bit (Disable Target Events
6724 Command, Section 4.3.7.3.1).
6725 If a Secondary Controller does not support Hot-Join Requests, then it shall deny any Hot-Join Requests and
6726 defer management to another capable Controller.
6.5.3.3.7 Group Address Management
6727 A Secondary Controller may either support Group Address commands, or not support them.
6728 If a Secondary Controller supports Group Address commands, then it can participate in the transfer of Group
6729 Address data and manage the Group Addresses of I3C Target Devices as described in Section 4.3.4.4. If the
6730 Secondary Controller determines that it needs to perform this function while acting as Active Controller, then
6731 it may use the Group Address commands to set or reset Group Addresses for any I3C Targets that support
6732 Group Addressing. The Secondary Controller may also participate in the transfer of Group Address data to
6733 another Controller-capable Device using the DEFGRPA CCC (Define List of Group Addresses,
6734 Section 4.3.7.3.26).
6735 If a Secondary Controller does not support Group Addressing, then it shall not send these CCCs and cannot
6736 participate in the transfer of Group Address data, either before or after Handoff.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 355
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 370 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE 004 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6737 If the Controller Role passes through a Secondary Controller that does not support Group Address
6738 management, and that does not use the DEFGRPA CCC to pass on any Group Address data received from
6739 the previous Active Controller to a future Controller, then it shall be the responsibility of either the previous
6740 Active Controller, or of a future Controller, to resolve any issues relating to this Secondary Controller’s lack
6741 of support for Group Addressing.
6.5.3.3.8 Multi-Lane Management
6742 A Secondary Controller may either support Multi-Lane Data Transfer, or not support it.
6743 If while acting as Active Controller a Secondary Controller that supports Multi-Lane Data Transfer
6744 determines that it is necessary to configure other Multi-Lane capable I3C Devices as described in Section 6.7,
6745 then it may do so. In this situation, the Secondary Controller shall rely upon the Active Controller to have
6746 configured the I3C Bus, and all Multi-Lane capable I3C Devices, to use a compatible Multi-Lane Frame
6747 format before the Secondary Controller accepts the Controller Role.
6748 However if a Secondary Controller does not support Multi-Lane Data Transfer, then the Active Controller
6749 shall configure the I3C Bus and all Multi-Lane capable I3C Devices to use fundamental 2-Wire I3C mode
6750 (i.e., 1-Lane) before passing the Controller Role to that Secondary Controller.
6.5.3.3.9 Interoperability Among Controllers Based on Different I3C Versions
6751 If multiple Controller-capable Devices on a given I3C Bus support different versions of the MIPI I3C
6752 Specification, then certain features or capabilities added in later I3C versions will not be supported (nor even
6753 known to exist) by Devices based on earlier I3C versions, raising potential interoperability issues.
6754 In particular, some features and capabilities added in I3C version 1.1 may require special handling during
6755 Controller Role Handoff and the recommended Handoff preparation flow. These potential issues could impact
6756 the new Active Controller after Handoff, or any subsequent communications between a Controller and a
6757 Target.
6758 To avoid leaving the Bus in a non-functioning state, the system designer must give the following situations
6759 careful consideration:
6760 • Use cases that depend upon features or capabilities introduced in I3C v1.1, such as Multi-
6761 Lane Data Transfer, Group Addressing, or flow control in HDR Modes.
6762 A Controller based on an earlier version of I3C might not support these features or capabilities,
6763 and could potentially be negatively impacted if such features or capabilities were to be enabled.
6764 • Use cases that depend upon Target-initiated requests specified in a more recent I3C
6765 Specification version, such as Device to Device Tunneling.
6766 A Controller based on an earlier version of I3C might not be able to understand and/or handle such
6767 requests.
6768 • Use cases that pass the Controller Role between Controllers based on different I3C
6769 Specification versions.
6770 If a given Bus implementation calls for Controller Role Handoffs between multiple Controllers,
6771 and not all of those Controllers are based on the same version of the I3C Specification, then the
6772 system designer is responsible for ensuring smooth Controller Role Handoff between these
6773 Devices, without interoperability issues or loss of communications. In particular, some Controllers
6774 might require or expect special handling to prepare for a successful Handoff (see Section 6.5.3.1).
356 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 371 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F005 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.6 F005: Timing Control
6775 This Section defines I3C Optional Feature F005: Timing Control.
6776 Table 96 Optional Feature 005 Status
Optional Feature ID F002
Optional Feature Name Timing Control
Optional Feature Version 1
Optional Feature specification was last updated in I3C Spec Version I3C v1.2
Optional Feature is not compatible with other Optional Features –
Optional Feature is included in I3C Basic Yes (partial)
6777 Changes in this Version
6778 For detailed changes in this version, see the “diff” file between I3C v1.1.1 and I3C v1.2 in the MIPI
6779 Specification Repository [MIPI09].
6.6.1 Scope and Purpose (Informative)
6780 The scope of Section 6.6 is limited to fully specifying I3C Optional Feature F005: Timing Control. An I3C
6781 Device that implements F005 is required to implement all shall statements in Section 6.6.3, and can
6782 optionally implement any combination of may statements in Section 6.6.3. In addition, MIPI Alliance
6783 recommends that manufacturers follow all should statements in Section 6.6.3.
6.6.2 Technical Overview (Informative)
6784 I3C includes optional Timing Control and Timestamping of events generated by I3C Devices resident on the
6785 I3C Bus. This Timing Control framework allows uncertainties affecting the transmission or reception of
6786 timing information (for example: Bus activity, busy Controller or Target, operation latency, system jitter, etc.)
6787 to be nullified.
6788 The I3C Timing Control framework provides:
6789 • Flexible implementation with significant capability enhancements
6790 • Means for synchronizing the timing references/clocks, and subsequently the events, of Target
6791 Devices on the I3C Bus to the timing reference/clock of the Controller
6792 This can result in reduced energy consumption at the system level.
6793 • Transmission of timing information, with minimal complexity for the Target Devices
6794 • Controllable timing accuracy, suitable for the intended use cases
6795 I3C defines two forms of main systems and events for Timing Control: Synchronous mode and Asynchronous
6796 mode. These modes and their versions can be used independently and concurrently on a given I3C Bus, per
6797 the application-specific requirements, with only one restriction: a Target can enable only one Asynchronous
6798 mode variant at a time. If a Target is configured to one of the Asynchronous modes, and the Controller sends
6799 an unsupported Asynchrnous mode, then the Target shall remain in the previously configured mode. If Targets
6800 support the new mode, then they shall disable the previous mode and switch to the new mode.
6801 • Synchronous Time Control (see Section 6.6.3.1): NOT INCLUDED IN I3C BASIC.
6802 • Asynchronous Time Control (see Section 6.6.3.2): Targets timestamp the moment at which they
6803 acquire sampled data, permitting the Controller to time-correlate samples received from multiple,
6804 different sensors in an easier and more accurate manner. Timestamping ensures that the Controller
6805 always has the time at which sensor data acquisition occurred (subject to the timing granularity
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 357
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 372 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F005 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6806 supported), even if there are latencies in moving the data from the Target to the Controller. Four
6807 different Asynchronous Time Control versions (‘Modes’) provide different methods for measuring
6808 time, and/or calibrating timing, with increasing accuracy.
6809 Note:
6810 I3C Basic only includes Asynchronous Time Control (Mode 0). The full I3C Specification includes
6811 additional Asynchronous Time Control Modes that provide different methods for measuring time
6812 and/or calibrating timing, with increasing accuracy.
6.6.2.1 General Principles
6813 The exchange of timing information is based on agreements between the Controller and Targets.
6814 The elements of this agreement are:
6815 1. Two I3C Common Command Codes (CCCs):
6816 • Set Exchange Timing Information (SETXTIME), see Section 6.6.3.3, and
6817 • Get Exchange Timing Information (GETXTIME), see Section 6.6.3.4.
6818 These CCCs:
6819 • Identify Bus events and markers (i.e., specific SDA edge and/or SCL edge),
6820 • Transfer timing or control information (e.g., ‘abort’ command),
6821 • Specify which timing control procedure is in effect, and
6822 • Query the Target Device for Exchange Timing support details, such as which Timing Control
6823 Mode(s) are supported, the current Timing Control state, frequency, inaccuracy, etc.
6824 2. One defined and Mode-specific marker is sent by the Controller, and used to synchronize the
6825 Targets
6826 3. One data collection event is sent by the Target. It includes the time information previously
6827 requested by the Controller.
358 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 373 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F005 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.6.3 Technical Specification
6.6.3.1 Synchronous Systems and Events
6828 This section is not included in the I3C Basic Specification. To gain access to this capability, please contact
6829 MIPI Alliance.
6830 In systems where multiple sensors (or other Target Devices) provide periodically sampled data, it is
6831 advantageous to instruct the Targets to be able to collect the data at essentially synchronized times, so that
6832 the Controller can read several Targets’ data in a single system awake period.
6833 In a typical system, different Targets will sample their data at different, uncorrelated times. This is true even
6834 if all Targets are set to the same sampling frequency, because Target-to-Target oscillator accuracy differences
6835 will cause drift over time. In I3C’s Synchronous Time Control mechanism, the Controller periodically emits
6836 a synchronization pulse, called a SYNC Tick. This method permits all Target data sampling to occur very
6837 close together in time, so that Targets can prepare and activate their individual sampling mechanisms, even
6838 if there are variances across their clocks.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 359
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 374 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F005 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.6.3.2 Asynchronous Systems and Events
6839 In the full I3C Specification this section defines four variations (called Modes, summarized in Table 97) of
6840 a method for increasing the precision of the acquired time-of-occurrence for an event occurring in an I3C
6841 Target connected to an I3C Controller. The overall method is called Asynchronous Timing Control. In this
6842 version of the I3C Basic Specification, only Async Mode 0 is supported.
6843 The actual event itself might not be periodic, and the Controller and Target Devices need not use the same
6844 time base clock (source, frequency, or accuracy). The procedure can help address the large
6845 inaccuracies/offsets to the event’s acquired time-of-occurrence that IBI launch/acknowledge latencies can
6846 introduce.
6847 In I3C Asynchronous Timing Control, a Target Device timestamps events occurring within that Target, and
6848 then notifies the Controller about it by generating an In-Band Interrupt (IBI). The Target’s IBI might not
6849 reach the Controller immediately, either because the I3C Bus was busy, or because the Target lost IBI
6850 Arbitration, or because the Controller did not immediately accept the IBI. The Asynchronous mechanism
6851 allows the I3C Controller to convert the Target’s timestamp value to its own internal time scale, in terms of
6852 both timing resolution and timing accuracy.
6853 Terminology
6854 • T_C1: Target counter from Event occurrence to a Controller-initiated end-of-count (EOT_C1)
6855 point depending on Mode (e.g., IBI Acknowledged point in Mode 0 & Mode 1).
6856 • T_C2: Target counter from EOT_C1 to a second Controller-initiated end-of-count, EOT_C2 (e.g.,
6857 T-Bit of IBI Mandatory byte in Modes 0 & 1).
6858 Figure 149 illustrates the I3C Asynchronous Timing Control model. In this model, the I3C Controller Device
6859 maintains its own notion of time (“I3C Controller Ref Counter” in the Figure) using whatever timing source
6860 and units it needs; for example, a real-time clock, or a simple 32-bit rollover timer/counter. This notion of
6861 time allows the Controller to determine the relative timing among multiple events generated by different
6862 Targets. These relative time differences can then be used to correlate the Target-generated events, for example
6863 to support sensor fusion, to plot sensor events vs. absolute time, or for other purposes.
360 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 375 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F005 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024

6864
Figure 149 Graphical Representation of Async Mode 0 Timestamp Interpolation
6865 I3C Target Devices report the occurrence of sensor events to the I3C Controller using IBI, but because the
6866 IBI can be delayed before reaching the Controller, the Controller’s notion of time alone is not sufficient to
6867 accurately gauge event timing. For this reason, the Target maintains its own counter (“Target Device Counter
6868 (SC1)” in Figure 149), driven by its own clock. At the time when the Target is able to generate the IBI to
6869 report the occurrence of a sensor event to the I3C Controller, the number of Target clocks elapsed since the
6870 event occurred (‘T_C1’ in the Figure) and the calibration period (‘T_C2’) shall be sent as IBI payload of the
6871 same IBI.
6872 The Target Device counts can be converted into Controller time via a Controller-provided method whereby
6873 the Target can measure the time between two points (‘EOT_C1’ and ‘EOT_C2’ in the Figure) as T_C1, in
6874 terms of number of its clock cycles whose approximate clock frequency is known to the Controller through
6875 the GETXTIME CCC, see Section 6.6.3.4). The Target then counts the time (T_C2) from EOT_C1 to
6876 EOT_C2, and then communicates both T_C1 and T_C2 to the Controller as IBI payload. This allows the
6877 Controller to correlate T_C1 and T_C2 with its own internal counter values C_REF and C_C2, respectively.
6878 The Controller knows the center clock frequency of the Target Device (again via the GETXTIME CCC), and
6879 can compare the Target-provided count with its expected value, given the known length of time reported by
6880 T_C2. The delta between the expected count and the actual count can be used as guidance to improve the
6881 accuracy of the T_C2 value, derived from the GETXTIME CCC, and thereby the accuracy of the event’s
6882 calculated time-of-occurrence.
6883 Note that the higher the frequency of the Target clock vs. the effective SCL clock frequency during the SC1-
6884 to-T_C2 measurement, the more accurate the error delta will be. This means that the Controller could gain
6885 more accuracy in the scale factor by extending the T_C1-to-T_C2 period.
6886 The Target shall report the T_C1 value as a 2-byte (16-bit) value, transmitting the Least Significant Byte first,
6887 and then the Most Significant Byte. The Target shall report the T_C2 value as a 1-byte value. If either count
6888 overflows (which can happen with a busy Bus with a large transaction), then the Target shall report values of
6889 all 1'b1 bits: 8'hFF for T_C2, and 16'hFFFF for T_C1.\n--- Page 376 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F005 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6890 A Target may choose to not count T_C2 value if its counter’s clock source is insufficiently accurate, however
6891 in this case it shall report a value of 8’h00 for T_C2 in the IBI payload. A Controller receiving a T_C2 value
6892 of 8’h00 shall rely solely on the frequency value reported by GETXTIME for scaling. Targets having very
6893 stable clocks, especially when fast enough, could help the Controller achieve higher time-stamping accuracy
6894 by supporting and reporting the T_C2 count.
6895 In all four Asynchronous Modes, the key points are:
6896 1. All I3C Devices supporting any of the Asynchronous Modes shall drive a Mandatory Byte after
6897 Acknowledgement of their IBI. As a result, the BCR[2] bit for these I3C Devices shall be set
6898 (i.e., shall have the value 1'b1).
6899 In the Mandatory Byte:
6900 • Bits[7:5] shall be set to 3'b100, indicating that a timestamp follows.
6901 • Bits[4:0] shall be set by the Target and read by the Controller. These bits are typically used to
6902 convey some aspect of the event, and are interpreted according to a private contract between
6903 the two Devices.
6904 2. Two mutually identifiable Events on the I3C Bus between I3C Controller and I3C Target
6905 Devices:
6906 • End of T_C1 (EOT_C1): The T_C1 latch point and the start of the Calibration Window to
6907 count T_C2.
6908 The actual event used depends on which of the four Asynchronous Modes is being used.
6909 It could be either the IBI ACK point of the current IBI transaction, or an SCL edge, or
6910 some other event on the I3C Bus.
6911 • End of T_C2 (EOT_C2): The end of the Calibration Window.
6912 This event depends on which of the four Asynchronous Modes is being used. It could be
6913 either the T-Bit following the IBI’s Mandatory Byte for the current IBI transaction, or an
6914 SCL edge, or some other event on the I3C Bus.
6915 3. Additional common Events (aBE) are defined for other Asynchronous Modes that are not
6916 supported in I3C Basic.
6917 4. After the Mandatory Byte, the Target shall send its timestamp values:
6918 • T_C1: The 16-bit timing counter from the original sensor-generated event
6919 • T_C2: An 8-bit reference/calibration count
6920 • aBE_TICKs: An optional 1-byte value indicating the number of aBEs that the Target has
6921 detected, from the sensor event to the IBI-ACK. These are not defined for Asynchronous Mode
6922 0.
6923 The values of both T_C1 and T_C2 are in terms of the Target’s time scale. The Controller will
6924 convert these values into its own time scale, taking into consideration the Target’s counter clock
6925 frequency as reported by the GETXTIME CCC.
6926 In cases where the aBE event is used, the Controller then can calculate when the event occurred by
6927 taking the timestamp of the aBE event indicated by the Target (by count) and subtracting the time
6928 in T_C1 (translated to Controller time resolution).
6929 In order to support a wide range of applications, several degrees of accuracy are required for such
6930 timing related information. As a result, I3C provides four different Asynchronous Modes which
6931 are summarized in Table 97 and detailed in the following subsections. Each of these methods
6932 addresses a different level of accuracy, and presents a distinct complexity tradeoff option for the
6933 Controller and/or Target. The SETXTIME CCC (see Section 6.6.3.3), is used to enter all four
6934 Asynchronous Modes, using the Sub-Command Byte field to select the desired Mode.
362 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 377 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F005 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6935 Note:
6936 The full I3C Specification defines additional Timing Control modes that present additional choices,
6937 including different levels of accuracy and distinct complexity tradeoff options for the Controller and/or
6938 Target. I3C Controllers that support the full I3C Specification may optionally use the same SETXTIME
6939 CCC to enter these other Timing Control modes, and I3C Targets that support the full I3C
6940 Specification might optionally implement support for such Timing Control modes. However, such
6941 Timing Control modes shall not be supported by or enabled by any I3C Devices that support the I3C
6942 Basic Specification.
6943 These other Timing Control modes are not included in I3C Basic. To gain access to these other
6944 Timing Control modes, please contact MIPI Alliance.

6.6.3.2.1 Async Mode 0: Asynchronous Basic Mode
6945 All I3C Controller Devices capable of Asynchronous Timing Control procedures shall support Async Mode
6946 0, Basic Asynchronous Timestamping. This Mode expects that a reasonably accurate and stable clock source
6947 is available in the Target to drive the timer counter. Note that in this Mode, the two SCL edges both occur
6948 after the Target and the Controller agree that the transaction was valid.
6949 To select Async Mode 0, the Controller sends the SETXTIME CCC (see Section 6.6.3.3) with the Sub-
6950 Command Enter Async Mode 0 (0xDF).
6951 Terminology
6952 • EOT_C1: First SCL positive-going edge after the IBI ACK for the Target Device
6953 • EOT_C2: First SCL positive-going edge after the T-Bit of the IBI’s Mandatory Byte
6954 • aBE: Not used
6955 Figure 149 graphically depicts Async Mode 0 timestamp data transfer from the Target to the Controller,
6956 allowing the Controller to calculate the time at which sensor data sampling has taken place. It is presumed
6957 that the Target is able to transfer the data within a time interval that the Target’s timing counter can
6958 meaningfully measure (i.e., that the clock driving the timing counter preserves a sufficiently stable frequency
6959 to increment it in a substantially linear manner with respect to time).

6960
Figure 150 Example of Asynchronous Mode 0 Timestamp Data Transfer
6961 Figure 150 illustrates the basic sequence of operations during a simple asynchronous time information
6962 transfer:
6963 1. The top black baseline shows the events visible outside of the Target and the Controller
6964 2. The middle (red) baseline shows the activity inside the Target’s timing counters
6965 3. The bottom black baseline presents the virtual and real activity inside the Controller’s timing
6966 counter\n--- Page 380 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F005 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6967 In more detail, Figure 150 illustrates that:
6968 4. At sensor sampling/event time, the Target shall start its internal timing counter, and initiate the
6969 IBI request.
6970 5. If the IBI request is accepted, then:
6971 A. Immediately:
6972 • The Target shall record its internal timing counter, T_C1
6973 • The Controller shall record its internal timing counter, C_REF
6974 B. At the T-Bit of the IBI payload’s mandatory byte:
6975 • The Target shall record its internal timing counter, T_C2
6976 • The Controller shall record its internal timing counter, C_C2
6977 C. The Target shall transfer the recorded T_C1 & T_C2 values in the prescribed order.
6978 6. If the IBI request is not accepted, then:
6979 • The Target shall continue to increment its own counter for T_C1
6980 • The Target shall wait for the next opportunity for the IBI request to be issued & accepted
6981 • When the IBI request is eventually accepted, the Target shall proceed as described in step 2
6982 above.
6983 7. Evaluating the timing counter data:
6984 If the numbers are non-zero, then the Controller calculates the real time interval based on its
6985 internal timing counter, using the formula:
6986 C_TS = C_REF – C_C2 * T_C1/T_C2
6987 • If T_C1 is zero, then no time information is assigned to the event
6988 • If T_C2 is zero, then the scaling shall be done based on the Target Device counter’s clock
6989 frequency as can be read with GETXTIME CCC.
6990 Note that the above method of recovering the target event’s timestamp is only suitable for periods during
6991 which the Target clock is appropriately stable. The calibration time available is the duration elapsed between
6992 EOT_C1 and EOT_C2, which in this mode is 9-bit durations on the I3C Bus for the Mandatory Byte. To
6993 enable better calibration for Targets using low-frequency clocks, the Controller may optionally use clock
6994 Stalling during the Mandatory Byte transfer, thus lengthening the calibration window; however, clock
6995 Stalling has the disadvantage that IBIs will consume more I3C Bus time.
6996 If better calibration is required and clock Stalling is not an option, then other, more refined procedures or
6997 other Async Modes can be used.

6.6.3.3 Set Exchange Timing Information (SETXTIME)
7004 The SETXTIME CCC provides the framework for Controller(s) and Target(s) to exchange event timing
7005 information for purposes such as synchronizing controls, collecting or reconstructing timestamps, and
7006 specifying the timing data procedure. The SETXTIME CCC is available both as a Broadcast Command
7007 (Figure 151) and as a Direct Command (Figure 152).
7008 The SETXTIME CCC always includes the one-byte Sub-Command field, which acts as an identifier that
7009 defines the specific function of the CCC. For some Sub-Command values, additional Data Bytes follow.
7010 Interpretation of these additional Data Bytes depends upon the particular Sub-Command value, as detailed
7011 in Table 99.
7012 Table 98 Set Exchange Timing Information Command Codes (SETXTIME)

6.6.3.4 Get Exchange Timing Support Information (GETXTIME)
7021 This Direct CCC (Figure 153) provides the framework for the Controller to query the Exchange Timing
7022 capabilities supported by the I3C Targets. The GETXTIME CCC causes the addressed Target to return four
7023 data bytes containing key information on supported Timing Control modes, current state, and internal
7024 oscillator/clock frequency and inaccuracy.
7025 The Command Code for the GETXTIME Direct CCC is 0x99. Support for this CCC is required if an I3C
7026 Target supports any Timing Control modes.

7028 The queried I3C Target returns four data bytes, with the following interpretations:
7029 • Supported Modes Byte: Bit mask indicating which Timing Control Mode(s) the Target supports
7030 (see Table 100). If a bit is set (has value 1'b1), then that Target supports the corresponding Timing
7031 Control Mode.

7032 • State Byte: Bit mask indicating which Timing Control Mode (if any) is currently enabled for the
7033 Target, and whether any counter overflows have occurred since the most recent previous check (see
7034 Table 101). If a Timing Control Mode bit is set (has value 1'b1), then that Target has currently
7035 enabled the corresponding Timing Control Mode. If the Overflow bit is set (has value 1'b1), then
7036 that Target experienced a counter overflow since the most recent previous check.

7037 • Frequency Byte: This byte represents the frequency of the Target’s internal oscillator, in
7038 increments of 0.5 MHz (i.e., 500 KHz), up to 127.5 MHz. The value 0 is an exception, and
7039 indicates an internal oscillator frequency of approximately 32 KHz.
7040 Example: A value of 8'd20 represents an oscillator frequency of 10.0 MHz.
7041 • Inaccuracy Byte: This byte represents the maximum variation of the Target’s internal oscillator in
7042 1/10th percent (0.1%) increments, up to 25.5%.
7043 Example: A value of 8'd25 represents a maximum frequency variation of 2.5%.

6.7 F006: Multi-Lane
7044 This Section defines I3C Optional Feature F006: Multi-Lane.
7045 Table 102 Optional Feature 006 Status
Optional Feature ID F006
Optional Feature Name Multi-Lane
Optional Feature Version 1
Optional Feature specification was last updated in I3C Spec I3C v1.2
Version
Optional Feature is not compatible with other Optional Features –
Optional Feature is included in I3C Basic Yes (partial)
7046 Changes in this Version
7047 • For detailed changes in this version, see the “diff” file between I3C v1.1.1 and I3C v1.2 in the MIPI
7048 Specification Repository [MIPI09].
6.7.1 Scope and Purpose (Informative)
7049 The scope of Section 6.7 is limited to fully specifying I3C Optional Feature F006: Multi-Lane. An I3C
7050 Device that implements F006 is required to implement all shall statements in Section 6.7.3, and can
7051 optionally implement any combination of may statements in Section 6.7.3. In addition, MIPI Alliance
7052 recommends that manufacturers follow all should statements in Section 6.7.3.
6.7.2 Technical Overview (Informative)
7053 Although I3C’s HDR Modes achieve high data rates using two physical wires and advanced data transfer
7054 protocols, some applications would benefit from further increases in data throughput. Multi-Lane Data
7055 Transfer (ML) is one approach for achieving such increases, by employing any available additional physical
7056 wires to transfer the data payload faster. In ML the normal I3C SDA line is referred to as SDA[0], and the
7057 additional wires are called SDA[1], SDA[2], and SDA[3].
7058 ML’s advantages include reducing energy consumption and increased flexibility in adding features (e.g.,
7059 procedures to improve data integrity).
7060 Example: An application with a Camera module using Quad Lanes, an IMU using Dual Lanes,
7061 and a simple sensor (such as a temperature sensor) using only I3C SCL and SDA[0] wires, would
7062 benefit from ML capability.
7063 ML data transfer is available for data transfers between ML-capable I3C Devices that have ML functionality
7064 enabled.
7065 Depending upon the Bus design, a given ML-capable Device could reside on the same 2-wire I3C Bus as
7066 other non-ML-capable Devices (and/or other ML-capable Devices not currently in an ML-enabled state).
7067 Because ML performs all I3C Bus management functions (i.e., START, EXIT, Enter HDR, CCC, Flow
7068 Control, etc.) in the ordinary manner using only the SCL and SDA[0] lines, they remain valid and unchanged
7069 for all I3C Devices. As a result, ML transfers on an I3C Bus do not adversely affect any non-ML Devices
7070 resident on that Bus.
7071 ML data transfer is available for supported I3C Modes (e.g., HDR-BT), using a per-Mode ML Frame Format
7072 that specifies how SCL, SDA (which for ML purposes is referred to as SDA[0]), and the additional data
7073 wire(s) are used for ML data transfers in that Mode (see Section 6.7.3.3). During each ML Frame, ML-
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 373
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 388 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
7074 capable (and ML-enabled) Devices transfer data using SCL, SDA[0], and the additional data wires supported
7075 by that ML Frame format, while any non-ML Devices use only SCL and SDA[0] as usual.
7076 For each supported I3C Mode, each ML Frame format shall define the sequence and timing of the structured
7077 protocol elements (i.e., Words or Blocks) that are used for control and data in ML transfers:
7078 • For some ML Frame formats, the sequence and timing are similar or identical to I3C’s normal (i.e.,
7079 non-ML) Frame format, adding only the traffic on the additional wires, namely:
7080 • SDA[1] for DUAL Lane transfers, and
7081 • SDA[1], SDA[2], and SDA[3] for QUAD Lane transfers.
7082 • For other ML Frame formats, the sequence and timing might differ from the equivalent (i.e., non-
7083 ML) Frame format.
7084 During ML data transfers, activity on the additional data wire(s) (i.e., SDA[1], SDA[2], and SDA[3]) would
7085 not be visible to any non-ML Devices that might be resident on that Bus. Additionally, any ML-capable
7086 Devices that support the same I3C Mode but might not be connected to the same additional data wires, or
7087 that have not been enabled and configured to utilize these data wires, might not see or properly understand
7088 any ML transfers that use more data wires. For such cases, this might lead to failure of interoperability
7089 between Devices supporting the same I3C Mode. The specific nature of interoperability failures might depend
7090 on the currently configured Data Transfer Coding of such Devices, as well as the ML Frame format used for
7091 a particular ML transfer. To prevent such situations, Controllers shall ensure that all Devices supporting that
7092 same I3C Mode are configured to use a compatible (i.e., interoperable) Data Transfer Coding that enables
7093 interoperability on the I3C Bus. The recommended Data Transfer Coding(s) to use for such situations
7094 requiring interoperability shall be defined for each I3C Mode that supports Multi-Lane.
7095 Note:
7096 Multi-Lane (ML) Frame Formats for other I3C Modes (e.g. SDR, HDR-DDR and HDR-TSP) are not
7097 included in I3C Basic. Placeholders for these I3C Modes appear under Section 6.7.3.3.
7098 Section 6.7.3.3 specifies the Data Transfer Codings for the supported ML Frame formats, for all I3C Modes.
374 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 389 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.7.3 Technical Specification
7099 Set-up of an ML data transfer on an ML-capable I3C Bus has two main parts:
1. Configure and enable the I3C Devices capable of ML transfers using the MLANE CCC
(Section 6.7.3.1)
2. The ML Frame format (Section 6.7.3.3)
7100 ML-capable Devices shall default to SINGLE Lane configuration (i.e., basic 2-wire I3C), unless the system
7101 designer selects a different default configuration.
6.7.3.1 ML Device Configuration
7102 Controllers shall use the MLANE CCC (see Section 6.7.3.6) to set up and control ML functionality for ML-
7103 capable Devices. The MLANE CCC sets or gets, for a given I3C Mode (Table 108), the number of Additional
7104 Data Lanes (Table 103) and the Data Transfer Coding number (see Section 6.7.3.3) to use while
7105 communicating to one or more ML-capable Target Devices using that I3C Mode.
7106 Controllers shall configure and enable ML-capable Devices via the MLANE CCC prior to starting ML data
7107 transfers, for each I3C Mode for which ML data transfers are to be used. An ML-capable Device may be
7108 configured to use any supported number of Additional Data Lanes for any supported I3C Mode.
7109 The Controller shall configure each ML-capable Device with knowledge of its capabilities, and with
7110 awareness of the other Devices that support the same I3C Mode(s) and would also be used with ML data
7111 transfers, ensuring that such configuration is compatible with the configuration and ML capabilities of such
7112 other Devices.
7113 It is the Controller’s responsibility to successfully manage the configuration of all Target Devices on the Bus
7114 when ML data transfers are used. This depends on capability, connectivity, coordination, and configuration.
7115 To build a successful Multi-Lane configuration for all Devices on the I3C Bus, the Controller shall first
7116 determine which Target Devices are ML-capable, and then test each such Target Device’s connectivity to the
7117 Additional Data Lanes (i.e., by using the Direct GET version of the MLANE CCC). For each I3C Mode in
7118 which Multi-Lane Transfers will be used, the Controller shall then determine which ML Frame formats are
7119 supported by a ML-capable Target Device, and coordinate this with similar data collected from all other ML-
7120 capable Target Devices that support that I3C Mode. Finally, once it determines the correct settings that use
7121 interoperable Data Transfer Codings, the Controller shall appropriately configure the I3C Bus and all Target
7122 Devices, using the MLANE CCC to set the ML Frame Format that shall be used in each I3C Mode.
7123 An ML-capable Target Device shall have only one ML configuration setting (i.e., number of Additional Data
7124 Lanes and Data Transfer Coding number) per I3C Mode at a time, which is associated with its Dynamic
7125 Address, unless such a Device has multiple Dynamic Addresses per Section 6.7.3.1.2. The Controller shall
7126 choose the appropriate Defining Byte as the Sub-Command to configure an ML-capable Target Device for
7127 ML transfers in that I3C Mode.
7128 Note:
7129 The appropriate ML configuration setting (i.e., ML Frame format) for a particular I3C Mode might
7130 depend on the other Devices on the I3C Bus that also support that same I3C Mode (per Section 6.7).
7131 Controllers shall ensure interoperability by choosing an ML configuration setting that is known to be
7132 interoperable with other such Devices (i.e., a compatible Data Transfer Coding) that also support that
7133 I3C Mode. A recommended procedure for Multi-Lane configuration should include using the MLANE
7134 CCC with the Sub-Command for Get ML Capabilities (i.e., Defining Byte 0xFF) to discover the
7135 supported capabilities (i.e., the possible ML configurations) of all ML-capable Devices, before using
7136 the MLANE CCC with other Sub-Commands to configure any ML-capable Devices for ML transfers
7137 for specific I3C Modes.
7138 Controllers should use the GETCAPS Format 1 CCC (see Section 4.3.7.3.19 and Table 63) to
7139 determine whether a Target Device is ML-capable, before using the MLANE CCC to configure and
7140 enable it for ML transfers. Additionally, for ML transfers in any HDR Mode, Controllers should also
7141 ensure that the HDR Mode is supported by such a Target Device, by using the GETCAPS Format 1
7142 CCC (see Table 61). Controllers must also ensure that each Target Device that advertises ML
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 375
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 390 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
7143 transfer capability is indeed connected to the appropriate number of additional data lanes (per Figure
7144 97), before configuring such a Target Device to use a ML Frame format requiring such data lanes.
7145 Special additional configuration requirements apply for ML-capable Devices that support Group
7146 Addressing (see Section 6.7.3.1.1), ML-capable Devices that present Virtual Targets with multiple
7147 Dynamic Addresses (see Section 6.7.3.1.2), and ML-capable Devices that do both of the above.
7148 Once received by the Target, the ML configuration for the Target’s Dynamic Address shall remain in effect
7149 until a later MLANE CCC is correctly received, or until a RESET sequence that includes ML configuration
7150 is performed by the Controller.
7151 The default Lane configuration for ML-capable Devices shall be SINGLE Lane (3'b000). As a result, the
7152 default value of the MLANE Data Byte is 0x00.
