6.7.3.1.1 ML Devices with Group Addresses
7154 An ML-capable Target Device may also support Group Address capabilities (see Section 4.3.4.4) as indicated
7155 by bits[5:4] of the GETCAPS CCC’s GETCAP2 byte (see Section 4.3.7.3.19 and Table 62), and a Controller
7156 may assign such a Target to a Group Address and then subsequently send transactions to that Group Address
7157 or to the Target’s Dynamic Address using Multi-Lane formatting. The Controller needs to know the ML
7158 capabilities of each Target Device in a Group, and configure them appropriately, such that all transactions
7159 sent to that Group Address are properly understood and received by all Target Devices that have been assigned
7160 to that Group Address.
7161 Note:
7162 Per Section 4.3.4.4, a Target Device that supports Group Address capabilities shall receive transfers
7163 addressed to its Dynamic Address as well as any assigned Group Addresses; all such addresses are
7164 in effect simultaneously. If such a Target also supports any HDR Modes, then the assigned Group
7165 Addresses are in effect for HDR Write transfers as well as SDR Write transfers, and the Controller
7166 may send transfers to the Group Address using any I3C Mode that this Target (and other Targets
7167 assigned to the same Group Address) might support.
7168 Before the Controller assigns a Group Address to an ML-capable Target Device on an I3C Bus that supports
7169 Multi-Lane, it also needs to know the ML configuration of the other Target Devices that are assigned to that
7170 Group Address, and take steps to ensure that all such Target Devices are configured to use the same number
7171 of Additional Data Lanes and the same Data Transfer Coding, when it sends transfers to that Group Address.
7172 The Controller also needs to know whether the Target only supports a single ML configuration (i.e., variant
7173 2'b00) that applies to its Dynamic Address as well as any currently-assigned Group Addresses, or whether
7174 the Target supports separate ML configurations (i.e., a different number of Additional Data Lanes or an
7175 alternate Data Transfer Coding) for each assigned Group Address (i.e., variant 2'b01).
7176 The decision to support multiple ML configurations within a Target Device (i.e., the variant that is reported
7177 by the MLANE Direct GET CCC with the Sub-Command for Group ML Capabilities) is left up to the
7178 implementer; however, support for separate ML configurations is strongly recommended for ML-capable
7179 Target Devices that will be used in mixed-lane Group configurations (i.e., for I3C Buses where Target
7180 Devices assigned to a Group Address do not all support the same number of Additional Data Lanes).
7181 Note:
7182 Per Section 6.7.3.1.2, a composite Device that has shared Peripheral logic and supports a separate
7183 Dynamic Address for each Virtual Target that is presented on the Bus shall support a separate ML
7184 configuration for each Dynamic Address. If such a composite Device also supports Group Address
7185 capabilities, then it shall also support separate ML configurations for each currently assigned Group
7186 Address (i.e., variant 2'b01) per this section, such that each Dynamic Address may be assigned to
7187 Group Addresses in a similar manner (i.e., act according to the requirements for variant 2'b01 as
7188 detailed below).
7189 If an I3C Bus contains ML-capable Targets of both variants (i.e., variant 2'b00 and variant 2'b01), then the
7190 Controller shall not assign the same Group Address to Target Devices of both variants, due to the increased
7191 complexity of configuring Multi-Lane transfers for such a Group Address, as well as the possibility of
7192 misconfiguration among such Target Devices.
7193 Variant 2'b01
7194 If an ML-capable Target that supports Group Address capabilities also supports separate ML configurations
7195 (i.e., variant 2'b01), then it shall maintain a configuration for each currently assigned Group Address, once
7196 assigned by the Controller using the SETGRPA CCC (see Section 4.3.7.3.24). A Target’s initial ML
7197 configuration for a newly-assigned Group Address shall be set to the common ML configuration (i.e., ML
7198 Frame format) for the I3C Mode and the configuration of the Device, per Section 6.7.3.1:
7199 • For HDR-BT Mode, the initial common ML configuration shall be the same as the default ML
7200 configuration (as above). However, the common ML configuration may subsequently be changed,
7201 if the Controller configures the Device to use an ML Frame format that is based on any alternate
7202 Data Transfer Coding (per Section 6.7.3.5.3).
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 377
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 392 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
7203 • For Group Address assignments that were present before configuring such a Device to use an
7204 alternate Data Transfer Coding, the Device shall change the ML configuration to use the new
7205 common ML Frame format for this alternate Data Transfer Coding. In some cases, this might
7206 result in an ML configuration for an assigned Group Address changing to reflect the common
7207 ML Frame format, which may include a different number of additional data lanes than what
7208 was originally configured for that Group Address.
7209 • For Group Address assignments that were configured with the SETGRPA CCC after such a
7210 Device was configured to use an alternate Data Transfer Coding, the Device shall use the new
7211 common ML configuration for this alternate Data Transfer Coding.
7212 • In both cases, the data returned in response to the MLANE Direct GET CCC with the Sub-
7213 Command for Get Group Report (i.e., Defining Byte 0x9C) shall always reflect the correct
7214 status of each Group Address assignment.
7215 • Additional requirements may apply for ML-capable Devices that also present multiple Virtual
7216 Targets (per Section 6.7.3.1.2).
7217 The Controller may subsequently configure all such Targets that support separate ML configurations and are
7218 assigned to this Group Address to use a different ML Frame format for WRITE transactions directed to the
7219 Group, by sending the MLANE Direct SET CCC to the Group Address. The Controller shall only use the
7220 Group Address with the MLANE CCC if and only if all such Targets (i.e., members assigned to that Group
7221 Address) support separate ML configurations, and only if all such Targets support the indicated ML Frame
7222 format. It is the responsibility of the Controller to make this determination before assigning ML-capable
7223 Targets to a Group Address, and before sending the MLANE Direct SET CCC to a Group Address with a
7224 particular Data Byte value with the intent to configure the Group (i.e., to set the ML Frame format for all
7225 Targets that are members assigned to that Group Address).
7226 The Controller should determine whether an ML-capable Target does support separate ML configurations by
7227 using the MLANE Direct GET CCC (see Section 6.7.3.6) with the Sub-Command for Group ML Capabilities
7228 (i.e., Defining Byte 0x9B). A Target that supports separate ML configurations shall indicate this support (see
7229 Table 108) as well as the number of Group Addresses to which it is currently assigned.
7230 To verify that an ML configuration change sent to the Group Address was successfully applied, the Controller
7231 should use the MLANE Direct GET CCC with the Sub-Command for Get Group ML Report (i.e., Defining
7232 Byte 0x9C) for each Target assigned to that Group (i.e., by sending the MLANE Direct GET CCC with
7233 Defining Byte 0x9C to each Target’s Dynamic Address) to read its current ML configurations for all currently
7234 assigned Group Addresses.
7235 The Controller may also reset all current ML configurations for a Target (including any configured Group
7236 Address configurations) by sending the MLANE CCC with the Sub-Command for Reset ML (i.e., Defining
7237 Byte 0x7F). This shall reset all such Targets assigned to the Group Address to a known common ML Frame
7238 format.
7239 If a Target supports multiple current ML configurations for all its assigned Group Addresses, then each ML
7240 configuration may be set to any of the supported ML Frame formats, as returned by the MLANE Direct GET
7241 CCC with the Sub-Command for Get ML Capabilities (i.e., Defining Byte 0xFF), as long as they are all
7242 configured to be mutually interoperable within the same I3C Mode.
7243 Note:
7244 Special restrictions apply for certain ML Frame formats that are not interoperable with the current ML
7245 Frame format that is configured for the Dynamic Address to which the Group Address is assigned.
7246 Such restrictions apply for I3C Modes (such as HDR-BT) that define alternate Data Transfer Codings
7247 that are not interoperable with the default Data Transfer Coding. In no case shall a Device allow any
7248 assigned Group Address to be configured to use an ML Frame format that is based on a Data Transfer
7249 Coding that is not interoperable with the Data Transfer Coding of the Dynamic Address’s currently
7250 configured ML Frame format.
7251 The Controller may use the MLANE CCC to configure the current ML Frame format for transfers addressed
7252 to the Group Address (i.e., by sending the MLANE Direct SET CCC to the Group Address) to set this
378 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 393 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
7253 configuration, which will not change the current ML configuration for the Target’s Dynamic Address of any
7254 such Target within that Group. Such a Target will keep track of its multiple current configurations for its
7255 assigned Addresses, and will use the Address received for each transaction to match and select the correct
7256 ML configuration based on the Address.
7257 Note:
7258 If such an ML-capable Target supports separate ML configurations for each assigned address (i.e.,
7259 multiple ML configurations for its assigned Group Addresses), then it must do so for each I3C Mode
7260 in which it supports Multi-Lane transfers. An ML-capable Target shall not implement partial support
7261 for separate ML configurations for only certain I3C Modes (i.e., variant 2'b01) and use a single ML
7262 configuration (i.e., variant 2'b00) for other I3C Modes. The variant is a global option that applies to
7263 the Target Device.
7264 Per Section 6.7.3.6, such an ML-capable Target shall also support the MLANE CCC as a Broadcast
7265 SET CCC in addition to a Direct SET CCC addressed to an assigned Group Address, for supported
7266 I3C Modes. In such a case, the MLANE Broadcast SET CCC shall change the ML configuration for
7267 all currently assigned Group Addresses as well as the assigned Dynamic Address, and may also set
7268 (or reset) the common ML configuration for newly-assigned Group Addresses, if the I3C Mode allows
7269 that to be changed (i.e., for alternate Data Transfer Codings, as noted above).
7270 Variant 2'b00
7271 However, if an ML-capable Target that supports Group Address capabilities does not support separate ML
7272 configurations (i.e., variant 2'b00), then the ML configuration for the Target’s Dynamic Address shall also be
7273 applied to all Group Addresses to which the Target is currently assigned. For such Targets, the Controller
7274 might need to re-configure other Targets in all Group Addresses to which this Target is assigned, such that
7275 all Targets use the same ML Frame format. Alternately, the Controller might need to frequently reconfigure
7276 the ML Frame format for this Target, when switching from directed transactions using the Target’s Dynamic
7277 Address to those using an assigned Group Address (or vice versa). It is the Controller’s responsibility to
7278 determine whether this needs to happen (and how often), which will depend on the ML capabilities of any
7279 other Targets that are currently assigned to the same Group Address (or any other assigned Group Addresses,
7280 if applicable).
7281 In some cases, ML configurations using Additional Data Lanes simply might not be possible for ML-capable
7282 Targets that only support a single ML configuration (i.e., variant 2'b00), depending on the ML capabilities
7283 and current ML configuration of other Targets that might be assigned to the same Group Address.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 379
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 394 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.7.3.1.2 ML Devices with Multiple Dynamic Addresses
7284 If an ML-capable Target Device is a Virtual Target with its BCR bit[4] set to 1 (see Table 7), and also shares
7285 its Peripheral logic with other Virtual Targets within the same composite Device (see Section 4.3.2.1.2 and
7286 Section 4.3.7.3.23, i.e. the RSTACT CCC with Defining Byte 0x04 in Table 38), then the Device shall
7287 support separate ML configurations for each Dynamic Address that such a Device presents to the I3C Bus.
7288 Note:
7289 Before assigning separate ML configurations to each Virtual Target, the Controller should use the
7290 GETCAPS Format 2 CCC with Defining Byte VTCAPS (see Section 4.3.7.3.19) to determine which
7291 Devices present Virtual Targets using shared Peripheral logic; and also use the RSTACT CCC with
7292 Defining Bytes 0x04 and 0x84 (see Section 4.3.7.3.23) to correctly identify which Dynamic
7293 Addresses are associated together within the same composite Device having shared Peripheral
7294 logic. With this knowledge, the Controller can prepare for any interactions that might result from
7295 sending the MLANE Direct SET CCC to one Dynamic Address, if such interactions or side effects
7296 are defined for a particular I3C Mode.
7297 For such composite Devices, using the MLANE Direct SET CCC to read or change the current ML
7298 configuration for one Virtual Target’s Dynamic Address shall only act on that Virtual Target, and shall not
7299 affect any other Virtual Targets within the same Device, unless the Controller sends a MLANE Direct SET
7300 CCC for a particular I3C Mode to configure one Dynamic Address to use an Data Transfer Coding that is not
7301 interoperable with the default Data Transfer Coding or current Data Transfer Coding of the ML-capable
7302 Device. Such exceptions are specifically defined for such alternate Data Transfer Codings in sub-sections of
7303 Section 6.7.3.3, for the I3C Modes with interoperability advisories. Note that such alternate Data Transfer
7304 Codings also carry special requirements that apply to the entire ML-capable Device with respect to its support
7305 for ML transfers in that I3C Mode.
7306 In all other cases, the Controller may send an MLANE Direct SET CCC to configure one Dynamic Address
7307 with a valid ML configuration, and the Device shall apply that valid ML configuration to only the Virtual
7308 Target using that assigned Dynamic Address (i.e., with no effect on other Virtual Targets using other assigned
7309 Dynamic Addresses).
7310 Note:
7311 An ML-capable Target Device that presents multiple Virtual Targets might also support Group
7312 Addressing (see Section 6.7.3.1.1) in addition to multiple Dynamic Addresses. If so, then the
7313 conditions in that Section shall also apply, and each assigned Dynamic Address may be assigned to
7314 any available Group Address.
7315 Per Section 6.7.3.6, such an ML-capable Target shall also support the MLANE CCC as a Broadcast
7316 SET CCC in addition to a Direct SET CCC addressed to an assigned Group Address, for supported
7317 I3C Modes. In such a case, this shall attempt to change the ML configuration for all currently assigned
7318 Group Addresses as well as the assigned Dynamic Address, and may also set (or reset) the common
7319 ML configuration for newly-assigned Group Addresses. For HDR Modes, this may also change the
7320 common Data Transfer Coding that is used for ML transfers that are addressed to the Broadcast
7321 Address (e.g., for CCC flows in HDR Modes).
7322 All Virtual Targets within the same Device should return the same output when using the MLANE Direct
7323 GET CCC with Defining Byte 0xFF (see Table 108) to read the supported ML Frame formats. In cases where
7324 a Device has been set to use an alternate Data Transfer Coding (i.e., one that is not interoperable with the
7325 default Data Transfer Coding, as above) then the Device may filter the output of the MLANE Direct GET
7326 CCC, and only return the possible ML Frame formats that may be chosen within the currently configured
7327 Data Transfer Coding.
7328 If the Controller uses the MLANE Direct SET CCC with Defining Byte 0x7F, then the Sub-Command for
7329 Reset ML shall only apply to the addressed Virtual Target (i.e., the Dynamic Address to which it was sent),
7330 and shall not affect any other Virtual Targets within the same Device. In all cases, the Sub-Command for
7331 Reset ML as a Direct SET CCC shall not cause the Device to cause (or attempt to apply) an inconsistent set
7332 of ML configurations that might have a mix of Data Transfer Codings for different Virtual Targets that are
7333 not interoperable; for such special cases, the defined behavior of a Sub-Command for Reset ML as a Direct
380 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 395 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
7334 SET CCC shall be specifically defined in the appropriate sub-sections of Section 6.7.3.3 that might define
7335 alternate Data Transfer Codings for a particular I3C Mode (as above). If not so defined, then the Sub-
7336 Command for Reset ML as a Direct SET CCC shall have the effect of returning one Virtual Target’s Dynamic
7337 Address to the default Data Transfer Coding with the default Lane Configuration.
7338 If the Controller uses the MLANE Broadcast CCC with the Sub-Command for Reset ML (i.e., Defining Byte
7339 0x7F), then this action shall apply to all Virtual Targets (i.e., all assigned Dynamic Addresses within the
7340 Device) and return them all to the default Data Transfer Coding with the default Lane Configuration.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 381
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 396 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.7.3.2 The ML Frame
7341 Before starting any ML data transfers, the Controller shall configure the resident ML-capable Devices using
7342 the appropriate version of the MLANE CCC (see Section 6.7.3.6). Controllers should use MLANE’s Direct
7343 GET Sub-Command for the relevant I3C Modes, to check that the current ML configuration of each ML-
7344 capable Device is correct for all I3C Modes in which Multi-Lane transfers will be used. A Device’s ML
7345 configuration parameters (i.e., Data Transfer Coding number and number of Additional Data Lanes) per I3C
7346 Mode remain in effect until a new MLANE SET CCC for that I3C Mode is correctly received, or until the
7347 Controller uses the MLANE CCC with the Sub-Command for Reset ML (i.e., Defining Byte 0x7F) to reset
7348 a single Device using its Dynamic Address (i.e., via Direct CCC) or to reset all Devices on the Bus (i.e., via
7349 Broadcast CCC).
7350 Since all I3C Bus management is done using only the SCL and SDA[0] lines, an I3C ML Frame starts as
7351 specified by the ordinary framing rules for the selected I3C Mode. That is, the START, Address Arbitration,
7352 and entry into an HDR Mode (if so chosen) are all done in the same manner as for I3C SINGLE Lane
7353 configuration. During the ML data transfer, and within the Frame, ML-capable (and ML-enabled) Devices
7354 shall automatically communicate using the configured number of additional data Lanes and according to the
7355 configured Data Transfer Coding. EXIT from the I3C Frame follows the ordinary I3C rules specified for the
7356 selected I3C Mode.
7357 For Multi-Lane transfers using any HDR Mode, the following common structured protocol elements are
7358 defined for ML transfers while in that HDR Mode:
7359 • HDR-x Header Element, which typically indicates the Dynamic Address (or Group Address, if
7360 supported) to which the transfer is directed
7361 • In HDR-BT Mode, this is the HDR-BT Header Block (see Section 6.4.3.3.1).
7362 • HDR-x Data Element, which typically contains data bytes using Additional Data Lane(s)
7363 • In HDR-BT Mode, this is the HDR-BT Data Block (see Section 6.4.3.3.2).
7364 • HDR-x Termination Element, which indicates the end of the transfer
7365 • In HDR-BT Mode, this is the HDR-BT CRC Block (see Section 6.4.3.3.3).
7366 Figure 154 illustrates a typical ML Frame in a generic HDR Mode, shown as “HDR-x”. Although the
7367 example shows a period with no Address Arbitration, the same basic principles also apply for Direct
7368 addressing and Address Arbitration.
1. START of Frame, I3C Reserved Byte with RnW = 1'b0, Enter HDRx
7369 This section takes place traditionally, using SDR Mode over SCL and SDA[0]
2. HDR-x Header Element
7370 This structured protocol element is sent by the Controller, and must be received and understood by
7371 all Devices that support this HDR Mode. As such, the Controller shall ensure that any data sent on
7372 the additional data wire(s) is either “optional” in nature, for Devices that are either not ML-
7373 capable or not configured to listen to such wires; or that all such Devices are ML-capable, and
7374 configured for a Data Transfer Coding that allows them to receive and understand the data sent on
7375 such wires.
7376 3. HDR-x Data Element(s) comprising a data Transfer
7377 This structured protocol element is sent using the ML-enabled Lanes and the chosen Data Transfer
7378 Coding for this HDR Mode. It might be sent once or in a repeated sequence, per the HDR Mode.
7379 • For a Write transfer, the Controller sends these structured protocol elements, and the addressed
7380 Device (or all Devices that are members of the addressed Group) indicated by the HDR-x
7381 Header Element receives them, having been configured to understand the Data Transfer
7382 Coding and the same number of additional data wires.
7383 • For a Read transfer, the addressed Device sends these structured protocol elements, and the
7384 Controller receives them according to the known Data Transfer Coding for the Device and this
7385 specific transfer.
382 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 397 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
7386 4. HDR-x Termination Element
7387 This structured protocol element is optionally sent per the rules of the HDR Mode, typically by the
7388 same Device that also sends the HDR-x Data Element(s) as part of the data transfer.
7389 Additional specific activity per HDR Mode (such as Bus Turnaround) might follow; this is not
7390 shown in this example.
7391 5. HDR Restart Pattern
7392 Restarting the ML frame uses the usual HDR Restart Pattern on SCL and SDA[0], and then all
7393 Devices supporting that HDR Mode start monitoring to receive the next the HDR-x Header
7394 Element (jump to step 2 above).
7395 6. End of ML Frame
7396 Ending the ML frame uses the usual HDR Exit Pattern on SCL and SDA[0], and then all Devices
7397 start monitoring the Bus Lanes as the Bus returns to SDR Mode.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 383
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 398 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024

Public Release Edition\n--- Page 399 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
7399 The Figure 154 example shows three sample ML transfers: one for QUAD-lane, one for DUAL-lane, and
7400 one for SINGLE-lane. This ML framing model represents how any HDR Mode might support ML transfers
7401 for any Lane Configuration (see Table 103) using the same basic ML framing model. This model enables
7402 generic HDR read/write transfers using some or all additional data wires, as well as CCC flows (i.e.,
7403 Broadcast CCCs and Direct CCCs) per Section 6.1.3.1.
7404 For each HDR Mode that supports Multi-Lane, this Specification shall define Data Transfer Codings for that
7405 HDR Mode, such that the specific HDR-x Header Elements sent by the Controller shall be understood and
7406 received by all Devices. However, on an I3C Bus with multiple Devices supporting that HDR Mode, the
7407 Controller shall have the responsibility of ensuring that all such Devices are capable of receiving (and
7408 configured to understand) the HDR-x Header Elements. In some cases, multiple such Devices might use
7409 different Lane Configurations. This might require the use of a compatible Data Transfer Coding, specific to
7410 each HDR Mode.
7411 • For the Figure 154 example, the use of multiple transfers with different Lane Configurations
7412 would require a Coding that is compatible with all Devices on the Bus. Any data sent on the
7413 additional data wire(s) for the “HDR-x Data Element” and optional “HDR-x Termination Element”
7414 must only be “optional” in nature, for such Devices that are either not ML-capable or not
7415 configured to listen to all such wires. For this reason, the HDR-x Header Elements only show SCL
7416 and SDA[0] with required data, whereas SDA[1] through SDA[3] are shown as “Might be
7417 optional”.
7418 • For other cases where all such Devices are configured to use some (or all) of the additional data
7419 wires, the Controller might use an “alternate mode” Data Transfer Coding that uses additional data
7420 wires for the HDR-x Header Elements, since it knows that all such Devices can receive and
7421 understand the HDR-x Header Element in order to know when they are being addressed. If an
7422 HDR Mode defines such “alternate mode” Data Transfer Codings for these use cases, then their
7423 use and configuration shall be clearly specified, along with guidelines for interoperability.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 385
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 400 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024

7425 Mode that supports Multi-Lane. For each supported I3C Mode, the 1-Lane (Default) Mode is also detailed in
7426 the referenced section.

7429 In addition to the above, the full I3C Specification includes definitions for more ML Frame formats:
7430 SDR Based ML Frame Formats:

6.7.3.4 (Unused Heading)
7440 Note:
7441 This heading number is not used in the I3C Basic Specification. It has been retained to preserve
7442 section number synchronization with the full I3C Specification.

16-Dec-2024
6.7.3.5 HDR Mode for Multi-Lane
7443 For I3C Buses that support Multi-Lane (ML) functionality (see Section 6.7), the Controller may configure
7444 any ML-compatible Target Devices to use additional data Lanes to increase data transfer rates, in a supported
7445 HDR Mode that supports multiple ML Frame formats using these additional data Lanes.
7446 Note:
7447 For I3C Basic, HDR-BT is the only HDR Mode that supports Multi-Lane (ML) functionality.
7448 However, if some Target Devices that support this HDR Mode do not support the same ML Frame formats
7449 (i.e., if there is varying support for the number of additional data Lanes, or for alternate Data Transfer Codings
7450 with different ML Frame formatting), then compatibility issues can arise as these Target Devices would not
7451 be able to correctly receive and understand all the common CCC Flow Elements the Controller transmits.
7452 The same issues can arise if any of the Target Devices are configured to use ML Frame formats that are not
7453 mutually interoperable with the rest of the Target Devices, even if all Target Devices support such ML Frame
7454 formats.
7455 If a Controller chooses to use any ML Frame formats that use additional data Lanes and change the formatting
7456 of common CCC Flow Elements, then the Controller must ensure that all Target Devices can support (and
7457 are configured to use) mutually interoperable ML Frame formats before attempting to drive any CCC flows
7458 using such an ML Frame format.
7459 In general, all supported HDR Modes include a 1-Lane compatibility mode that is supported by all Multi-
7460 Lane capable Target Devices in their default configuration for that HDR Mode, as well as all Target Devices
7461 that only connect to one Lane. To ensure maximum compatibility, a Controller should generally use 1-Lane
7462 compatible ML Frame formats for the common CCC Flow Elements, so that all Target Devices can properly
7463 receive and understand the CCC flows. This includes all HDR-x CCC Header Blocks, and any HDR-x CCC
7464 Data Blocks that are part of Broadcast CCC flows.
7465 For such a Bus, if a Controller has previously configured some Target Devices to use an ML Frame format
7466 that uses additional data Lanes or different formatting, then the Controller must use a “compatibility mode”
7467 ML Frame format that uses the 1-Lane compatible Frame formatting to transmit all common CCC Flow
7468 Elements, and such Target Devices shall use the 1-Lane compatible Frame formatting to receive the common
7469 CCC Flow Elements. However, different formatting that uses additional data Lanes shall be used for per-
7470 Target transactions involving such Target Devices (if previously configured to do so) for the HDR-x CCC
7471 Data Blocks within the Read Segments or Write Segments of Direct CCC flows.
7472 Alternately, a Controller may configure all Target Devices to use an “alternate mode” Coding that uses
7473 additional data Lanes or different formatting, in order to increase the performance or efficiency of HDR CCC
7474 flows. Such an “alternate mode” Coding might intentionally be incompatible with the “compatibility mode”
7475 Coding for this HDR Mode, but it should not be used unless all Target Devices support it. The Controller
7476 must ensure that all Target Devices are configured to use a mutually interoperable “alternate mode” Coding,
7477 and the rules for the “alternate mode” Coding shall apply.
7478 The specifics of Multi-Lane support for each HDR Mode are listed in the Framing details (see Table 77).
7479 Each Section describing a specific HDR Mode lists the Flow Elements that may utilize Multi-Lane Frame
7480 formatting, while maintaining compatibility with all other Target Devices that might not be configured to use
7481 ML Frame formats. The ML Frame formats that are mutually interoperable are also defined in this
7482 specification.
7483 Since the Controller has the responsibility for configuring Multi-Lane for the Bus and all compatible Target
7484 Devices as well as initiating the CCC flow, it shall conform to these requirements, and shall ensure that all
7485 configuration is successfully completed. Likewise, the Controller must ensure that any core CCC Flow
7486 Elements that must be received and understood by all Target Devices supporting that HDR Mode are sent in
7487 an appropriate ML Frame format, as defined in the ML Frame formats that have been configured for all Target
7488 Devices, and that all Target Devices are configured to be mutually interoperable with respect to these core
7489 CCC Flow Elements. Likewise, all Target Devices shall also correctly receive and understand the Indicator
7490 and Selector blocks of the CCC flow in that ML Frame format, even if they have been otherwise configured
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 387
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 402 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
7491 to use an ML Frame format that uses additional Lanes or a different ML Frame format for standard (i.e., non-
7492 CCC) HDR transactions.
7493 Use of the MLANE CCC within an HDR Mode should be managed carefully, since the Direct SET forms of
7494 this CCC provide the unique ability to change a Target Device’s ML Frame format, and that directly impacts
7495 a Target’s ability to receive and understand an HDR-x CCC Data Block sent by a Controller, and also impacts
7496 a Target’s ability to respond on the data Lanes in a format that the Controller expects it to utilize for a given
7497 ML Frame format. As such, any misunderstanding or mismatch of expectations between a Controller and a
7498 Target may result in communication errors, which would require the Controller to catch any resulting errors
7499 and initiate a recovery procedure to restore communications.
7500 In some situations, this may require temporarily resetting a Target’s ML Frame format using the MLANE
7501 CCC (see Section 6.7.3.6) with Defining Byte value 0x7F (see Table 108). If the Target fails to respond to
7502 this CCC with Defining Byte value 0x7F while in HDR Mode, then the Controller should exit HDR Mode.
7503 To avoid these issues, it is strongly recommended to not use the Direct SET forms of this CCC within any
7504 HDR Mode, unless the Controller is able to ensure that all possible communication errors can be reliably
7505 detected and resolved.

7508 section number synchronization with the full I3C Specification.

6.7.3.5.3 HDR-BT Based ML Frame Formats
7512 For all HDR-BT Based ML Frame formats, the MLANE Defining Byte value shall be 0x23 (i.e., the same
7513 numerical value as the ENTHDR3 CCC).
7514 Within Defining Byte 0x23 there could be several Data Transfer Coding schemes specifying the data payload
7515 format and optional additional information being transferred. Additionally, several “alternate mode” Data
7516 Transfer Codings have been defined, which allow for enhanced performance by using additional data Lanes
7517 for transferring HDR-BT Header Blocks, as well as the common CCC flow elements in the HDR-BT CCC
7518 flows (see Section 6.7.3.5.3.1 and Table 105).
7519 The default Data Transfer Coding is HDR-BT Coding 0 (compatible mode). Other Data Transfer Codings
7520 could be added, as suitable for specific applications or performance requirements.
7521 The currently defined ML Frame formats for HDR-BT Based ML Frames are shown in Table 105.

7523 Note:
7524 The “Alternate Mode” Codings for HDR-BT Mode shall not be used unless all HDR-BT Target Devices
7525 on the Bus are ML-capable and have been configured to use interoperable Codings.
7526 Configuring an HDR-BT capable Target Device to use an “Alternate Mode” Coding shall change how
7527 the Device interprets the HDR-BT Header Block, which affects its ability to match an assigned
7528 Address for subsequent HDR-BT transfers. This includes Devices that support Group Addressing per
7529 Section 6.7.3.1.1, as well as Devices that support multiple Dynamic Addresses as Virtual Targets
7530 per Section 6.7.3.1.2.
7531 Selecting an “Alternate Mode” Coding requires the Controller to use a different formatting for all
7532 HDR-BT Header Blocks, as well as all common CCC flow elements in the HDR-BT CCC flows. If any
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 389

7533 HDR-BT Target Device is unable to understand that formatting due to an incompatible configuration,
7534 or due to being non-ML-capable, then it will not properly receive and understand many of the HDR-
7535 BT Blocks, and this might cause communication errors. See Section 6.7.3.5.3.1 for more details on
7536 which HDR-BT Multi-Lane Frame formats are interoperable for “Alternate Mode”.

6.7.3.5.3.1 HDR-BT Codings and Interoperability for CCCs
7545 HDR-BT defines several “Alternate Mode” ML Frame formats that may optionally be used for increased performance efficiency. However, not all of these are
7546 mutually interoperable. Table 106 lists the supported Codings and defines how they affect the formatting of various CCC flow elements that comprise CCC flows
7547 in HDR-BT Mode.

7549 All supported ML Frame formats shown in Table 105 shall indicate which of these Data Transfer Codings
7550 they support, which in a mixed ML-configuration Bus determines their level of interoperability with other
7551 ML Frame formats.
7552 As noted in Section 6.7.3.5.3, configuring the I3C Bus (i.e., all HDR-BT capable Targets) to use an “Alternate
7553 Mode” Data Transfer Coding requires all such Targets to be configured to use the “Alternate Mode” Coding.
7554 This configuration may be done individually for each Target, by sending the MLANE Direct SET CCC with
7555 Defining Byte 0x23 for each HDR-BT capable Target; or it may be done simultaneously for all Targets, by
7556 sending the MLANE Broadcast CCC with Defining Byte 0x23.
7557 However, the Controller shall not attempt to configure any ML-capable HDR-BT Targets to use any
7558 “Alternate Mode” Coding, unless it first ensures that all other HDR-BT Targets are capable of interoperating
7559 with this Coding and may be configured to use a mutually interoperable ML Frame format that is based on
7560 the same Coding.
7561 When a Target that supports any HDR-BT “Alternate Mode” Codings first receives either an MLANE Direct
7562 SET CCC with Defining Byte 0x23 sent to an assigned Dynamic Address, or an MLANE Broadcast CCC
7563 with Defining Byte 0x23 that is sent to the I3C Bus; and either CCC has a message with a Data Byte that
7564 selects an ML Frame format based on such an “Alternate Mode” Coding which is not interoperable with
7565 Coding 0, this shall change the HDR-BT configuration for the Device, and affect how the Device and its
7566 Peripheral logic must interpret HDR-BT Header Blocks for all subsequent HDR-BT transfers. For HDR-BT
7567 Mode, this configuration is defined as a ‘sticky’ state, and shall also determine how subsequent MLANE
7568 CCCs are either accepted or rejected.
7569 • Such a change shall become ‘sticky’ if accepted, and shall affect the Peripheral logic that receives
7570 HDR-BT transfers within the Device:
7571 • If the Device receives the MLANE Direct SET CCC with Defining Byte 0x23, then such a
7572 change shall only be accepted if the Device was previously configured to use Coding 0 (i.e.,
7573 not in the ‘sticky’ state).
7574 • If the Device receives the MLANE Broadcast CCC with Defining Byte 0x23, then such a
7575 change shall always be accepted.
7576 • While in a ‘sticky’ state, the Device shall not accept a configuration change via the MLANE Direct
7577 SET CCC with Defining Byte 0x23 to use any other ML Frame formats that are not interoperable
7578 with that “Alternate Mode” Coding.
7579 • However, the Device shall continue to accept configuration changes via the MLANE Broadcast
7580 CCC with Defining Byte 0x23 to use any other ML Frame formats that are valid and supported.
7581 For such MLANE Broadcast CCCs, ‘sticky’ state checking is bypassed.
7582 For HDR-BT Targets that support multiple assigned Addresses, HDR-BT defines several sets of conditions
7583 as valid actions (per Section 6.7.3.1) that apply to the use of the MLANE CCC with “Alternate Mode”
7584 Codings:
7585 • Entering the ‘sticky’ state (i.e., switching from Coding 0 to any “Alternate Mode” Coding) affects
7586 all assigned Addresses within the Device (i.e., the shared Peripheral logic that receives HDR-BT
7587 transfers and matches assigned Addresses within HDR-BT Header Blocks). The Device shall not
7588 accept any subsequent MLANE Direct SET CCCs that would attempt to re-configure one assigned
7589 Address to use a Data Transfer Coding that is incompatible (i.e., not interoperable) with the first
7590 “Alternate Mode” Coding that was used to put the Device into the ‘sticky’ state.
7591 • If the Device supports separate ML configurations for Group Addresses (per Section 6.7.3.1.1),
7592 then:
7593 • On entering the ‘sticky’ state, the Device shall change the ML configuration for all currently
7594 assigned Group Addresses to the common ML Frame format for that “Alternate Mode”
7595 Coding, per Table 106. The Device shall also apply this same common ML Frame format as
7596 the initial ML configuration for any subsequently assigned Group Addresses.
7597 • Such a configuration change shall also limit the available ML Frame formats that the Device
7598 might accept upon subsequent use of the MLANE Direct SET CCC with Defining Byte 0x23.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 395
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 410 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
7599 This shall prevent any assigned Group Address from being re-configured to use a Coding that
7600 is not interoperable with the Dynamic Address (or one of the Dynamic Addresses, if multiple
7601 Dynamic Addresses are supported).
7602 • Per Section 6.7.3.1.1, an MLANE Direct SET CCC sent to an assigned Group Address shall
7603 not cause the Device to enter or leave the ‘sticky’ state, nor shall the Device change the ML
7604 configuration for any assigned Dynamic Address as a result. The Device shall only accept an
7605 MLANE Direct SET CCC with an ML Frame format sent to an assigned Group Address, if that
7606 ML Frame format is compatible (i.e., interoperable) with the current ML configuration of the
7607 associated Dynamic Address.
7608 • If the Device supports multiple assigned Dynamic Addresses (i.e., a composite Device that
7609 presents multiple Virtual Targets, per Section 6.7.3.1.2), then:
7610 • The first such MLANE Direct SET CCC sent to any assigned Dynamic Address with an ML
7611 Frame format based on an “Alternate Mode” Coding shall also change the ML configuration
7612 for the Device’s other assigned Dynamic Addresses, to use the common ML Frame format for
7613 that “Alternate Mode” Coding.
7614 In this case, the Controller may (and should) send subsequent MLANE Direct SET CCCs
7615 to the Device’s other assigned Dynamic Addresses, with compatible ML Frame formats
7616 (or the same ML Frame format).
7617 • Similarly, the MLANE Broadcast CCC shall cause a simultaneous ML configuration change to
7618 all assigned Dynamic Addresses, as though the Device had received (and accepted) a series of
7619 MLANE Direct SET CCCs that were sent to each of its assigned Dynamic Addresses.
7620 • Note that the provided ML Frame format (i.e., the Data Byte after the Defining Byte) for each
7621 MLANE SET CCC (either Direct or Broadcast) must be valid and supported for the Device, if
7622 such configuration changes are to take effect, per Section 6.7.3.6.
7623 • Subsequent MLANE Direct SET CCCs sent to any assigned Dynamic Address shall only be
7624 accepted if the provided ML Frame format is compatible with that same “Alternate Mode”
7625 Coding (i.e., uses the same Coding, or one that is expressly defined to be interoperable). This
7626 shall prevent any assigned Dynamic Address from being subsequently re-configured to use a
7627 Coding that is not interoperable with other Dynamic Addresses.
7628 • If the Device supports both Group Addresses and multiple Dynamic Addresses (i.e., the union of
7629 both), then both sets of conditions listed above shall apply.
7630 Once the Controller has configured the I3C Bus and all known HDR-BT capable Target Devices to use an
7631 “Alternate Mode” Coding, it must avoid such situations that might arise, in cases where a new Target Device
7632 might join or re-join the Bus (i.e., a Hot-Join Request, or a return from a deep sleep state with loss of
7633 configuration) and the Controller has not yet determined whether the Target Device supports HDR-BT Mode
7634 (and, if so, whether it also supports and can be configured to use an interoperable Coding). For some
7635 situations, the Controller must immediately stop all HDR-BT transfers if it detects an unknown Target Device,
7636 until it can fully determine such a Target Device’s capabilities and current configuration.
7637 An HDR-BT Target that has been configured to use Coding 0 with additional data Lanes (i.e., DUAL or
7638 QUAD transfers in Coding 0) may be switched back to 1-Lane compatibility mode (in Coding 0) via the
7639 MLANE CCC, using Defining Byte 0x23 and Data Byte 0x00, via Broadcast CCC or Direct CCC. This result
7640 can also be achieved by sending the MLANE CCC with the Sub-Command for Reset ML (i.e., Defining Byte
7641 0x7F) in its Broadcast CCC or Direct SET CCC formats. However, an HDR-BT Target that has been
7642 configured to use an “Alternate Mode” Coding may only be configured back to 1-Lane compatibility mode
7643 (i.e., back to Coding 0 and leaving the ‘sticky’ state) by sending the MLANE Broadcast CCC (i.e., not a
7644 Direct SET CCC) with Defining Byte 0x23 and Data Byte 0x00, or by sending the MLANE Broadcast CCC
7645 with the Sub-Command for Reset ML.
7646 Note:
7647 When an HDR-BT Target that has been configured to use an “Alternate Mode” Coding leaves the
7648 ‘sticky’ state or accepts a new ML Frame format based on an “Alternate Mode” Coding (i.e., by
396 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 411 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
7649 receiving the MLANE Broadcast CCC with either sub-command, as defined above), the ML
7650 configurations for all assigned Addresses shall be simultaneously configured back to either 1-Lane
7651 compatibility mode (i.e., with the Sub-Command for Reset ML), or to the new ML Frame format (i.e.,
7652 with Defining Byte 0x23).
7653 Figure 158 illustrates the valid state transitions for HDR-BT Targets that support any “Alternate Mode”
7654 Codings, when used with the MLANE SET CCC with Defining Byte 0x23.

7656 Note:
7657 In Figure 158, the solid lines show the valid state transitions for entering the ‘sticky’ state from Coding
7658 0, via the MLANE Direct SET CCC or the MLANE Broadcast CCC. The dashed lines show the valid
7659 state transitions within a currently configured Coding, via the MLANE Direct SET CCC with Defining
7660 Byte 0x23. Such state transitions apply to Devices that support a single Address (i.e., only a Dynamic
7661 Address) as well as Devices that support multiple Addresses (i.e., any combination of Virtual Targets
7662 with multiple Dynamic Addresses, or currently assigned Group Addresses). The circles with double
7663 lines show the common ML configurations for each Coding, which shall be used for each newly
7664 assigned Group Address (if supported). This diagram does not show the valid state transitions for
7665 leaving the ‘sticky’ state or changing to a different ‘Alternate Mode’ Coding via the MLANE Broadcast
7666 CCC, although such valid state transitions are always possible via the MLANE Broadcast CCC with
7667 Defining Byte 0x23.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 397

16-Dec-2024
7668 In a Bus that mixes 1-Lane Target Devices (or ML-capable Target Devices in their default 1-Lane
7669 configuration) with 2-Lane ML and/or 4-Lane ML Target Devices that are all configured for Coding 0, any
7670 Target Devices that are configured for SINGLE Lane Coding 0 shall ignore any HDR-BT Data Blocks and
7671 HDR-BT CRC Blocks that are part of a transaction (either generic, or Direct CCC flow) addressed to another
7672 Target Device that is configured for DUAL Lane Coding 0 or QUAD Lane Coding 0. Because these DUAL
7673 Lane or QUAD Lane formatted Blocks might not have the same format and length that a Target Device in its
7674 default configuration can understand, such a Target Device may instead ignore these HDR-BT Blocks and
7675 wait for the HDR Restart Pattern, after which it will be able to receive and understand a subsequent HDR-
7676 BT Header Block and correctly parse its Address field.
7677 CCC transactions in HDR-BT that use Multi-Lane shall conform to the special requirements of the CCC
7678 framing and modality for HDR-BT (see Section 6.4.3.4), as a specific instantiation of the generic CCC
7679 framing and modality for all HDR Modes (see Section 6.1.3). Such HDR-BT-capable Targets shall monitor
7680 the Bus for HDR-BT transactions that are either HDR-BT generic write/read transfers, or common CCC flow
7681 elements, according to the currently configured Data Transfer Coding of the Device:
7682 • If such a Target only has a single Dynamic Address: Then the ML configuration (i.e., currently
7683 configured ML Frame format) for the Target’s Dynamic Address shall apply to match either the
7684 assigned Dynamic Address or the Broadcast Address (i.e., 7'h7E) for common CCC flow elements,
7685 according to the Data Transfer Coding for this ML configuration.
7686 • If such a Target has multiple Dynamic Addresses (i.e., a composite Device that presents Virtual
7687 Targets, per Section 6.7.3.1.2): Then the ML configuration (i.e., currently configured ML Frame
7688 format) for all such Dynamic Addresses must be interoperable, so that the Target can match the
7689 Broadcast Address (i.e., 7'h7E) with the specified ML Frame formatting of the Data Transfer
7690 Coding, for common CCC flow elements (per Table 106).
7691 Coding 0
7692 The default ML Frame format for HDR-BT Devices is Coding 0 (Compatible Mode) which requires no
7693 additional data Lanes.
7694 Coding 0 is intended for Buses with HDR-BT Targets of mixed support for Multi-Lane’s additional data
7695 Lanes. It is interoperable with all 1-Lane, 2-Lane ML, and 4-Lane ML HDR-BT Targets that support Coding
7696 0.
7697 In Coding 0:
7698 • All HDR-BT Header Blocks shall be transmitted using SDA[0] (i.e., 1-Lane mode) in order to
7699 ensure that all Targets that support HDR-BT are always able to receive and understand the format
7700 of the Header Block. This includes the specific variants of the Header Block used for the common
7701 CCC flow elements in the HDR-BT CCC flows. The bit packing shall follow the formats shown in
7702 Table 87 for Data Bytes and Table 90 for the Transition Byte.
7703 • For generic HDR-BT transactions (i.e., not part of the HDR-BT CCC flows), all subsequent
7704 HDR-BT Data Blocks and the HDR-BT CRC Block shall be transmitted in a bit packing format
7705 appropriate for the Target’s currently configured ML Frame format (see Table 106). If the Target is
7706 not ML-capable, or has not been configured to use a ML Frame format that uses additional data
7707 Lanes, then this bit packing format shall be the standard 1-Lane HDR-BT format (i.e., SDA[0]
7708 only).
7709 • For all HDR-BT CCC flows, all common CCC flow elements shall be transmitted using SDA[0]
7710 (i.e., 1-Lane mode), so that all HDR-BT Targets can participate in the CCC flows regardless of
7711 their currently configured ML Frame format or the number of additional data Lanes they support.
7712 In this manner, all ML Frame formats that support Coding 0 are mutually interoperable for the
7713 common CCC flow elements.
7714 • Following the HDR-BT CCC Selector Block in a Direct CCC flow, all subsequent HDR-BT Data
7715 Blocks and the HDR-BT CRC Block that are addressed to a Target currently configured for Coding
7716 0 shall be transmitted in a bit packing format appropriate for the Target’s currently configured ML
7717 Frame format (see Table 106).
398 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 413 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
7718 Coding 3
7719 An optional Coding for HDR-BT Devices is Coding 3 (“Alternate Mode”), which requires at least one
7720 additional data Lane.
7721 Coding 3 is intended for Buses with HDR-BT Targets that support at least one additional data Lane for Multi-
7722 Lane. It is interoperable with all other “Alternate Mode” Codings marked as Coding 3 in Table 105, and
7723 expressly not interoperable with any 1-Lane HDR-BT Targets, or any 2-Lane ML HDR-BT Targets that do
7724 not support Coding 3. When addressing HDR-BT Targets that support three additional data Lanes, it provides
7725 additional transfer speed for these per-Target transactions, while still allowing interoperability with any other
7726 HDR-BT Targets that only support one additional data Lane and have been configured for Coding 3.
7727 In Coding 3:
7728 • All HDR-BT Header Blocks shall be transmitted using SDA[0] and SDA[1], using the DUAL Lane
7729 bit packing formats shown in Table 88 for Data Bytes and Table 91 for the Transition Byte. See
7730 Figure 138 for the HDR-BT Header Block format, Figure 141 for the HDR-BT Data Block
7731 format, and Figure 145 for the HDR-BT CRC Block format.
7732 • For generic HDR-BT transactions (i.e., not part of the HDR-BT CCC flows), all subsequent
7733 HDR-BT Data Blocks and the HDR-BT CRC Block shall be transmitted in a bit packing format
7734 appropriate for the Target’s currently configured ML Frame format, using either DUAL Lane or
7735 QUAD Lane formats (see Table 106).
7736 • For all HDR-BT CCC flows, all common CCC flow elements shall be transmitted using SDA[0]
7737 and SDA[1], using the same DUAL Lane bit packing formats. All HDR-BT Targets that are
7738 currently configured for Coding 3 can participate in the CCC flows regardless of the number of
7739 additional Lanes (i.e., Lanes beyond SDA[1]) that they are configured to use. In this manner, all
7740 ML Frame formats that support Coding 3 are mutually interoperable for the common CCC flow
7741 elements.
7742 • Following the HDR-BT CCC Selector Block in a Direct CCC flow, all subsequent HDR-BT Data
7743 Blocks and the HDR-BT CRC Block addressed to a Target currently configured for Coding 3 shall
7744 be transmitted in a bit packing format appropriate for the Target’s currently configured ML Frame
7745 format, using either DUAL Lane or QUAD Lane formats (see Table 106).
7746 This “Alternate Mode” is not compatible with other ML-capable HDR-BT Targets not configured to use an
7747 “Alternate Mode” Coding marked as Coding 3, or other HDR-BT Targets that are not ML-capable. Such
7748 Targets will not correctly receive and understand any HDR-BT Header Blocks transmitted in this bit packing
7749 format, and as a result they will not participate in any HDR-BT transactions, and could even cause
7750 communication errors.
7751 Note:
7752 In Coding 3, the Controller shall not use the SINGLE Lane formats for any HDR-BT structured
7753 protocol elements, such as the HDR-BT Header Block, Data Block or CRC Block.
7754 Once an HDR-BT capable Target that supports Coding 3 has been configured to use an ML Frame
7755 format that uses Coding 3 or any other “Alternate Mode” Coding that is interoperable, the entire
7756 Device enters a ‘sticky’ state and shall not accept any configuration changes via the MLANE CCC
7757 that might use any other Coding not interoperable with Coding 3, except for certain MLANE sub-
7758 commands sent as a Broadcast CCC. If the MLANE Direct SET CCC is used, then such a Device
7759 may only enter the ‘sticky’ state from Coding 0.
7760 A Controller shall not enable any ML-capable HDR-BT Targets to use any “Alternate Mode” Coding marked
7761 as Coding 3, unless it first ensures that all other HDR-BT Targets are capable of interoperating with Coding
7762 3. A Controller shall not initiate any HDR-BT transactions using DUAL Lane or QUAD Lane bit packing
7763 formats for all HDR-BT Blocks, unless it has previously configured all HDR-BT Targets on the Bus to use
7764 an “Alternate Mode” Coding marked as Coding 3. Once a Controller has configured all HDR-BT Targets on
7765 the Bus to use Coding 3, it must drive all HDR-BT transactions using the correct bit packing format for each
7766 HDR-BT Block, according to the conditions above.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 399
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 414 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
7767 Coding 7
7768 An optional Coding for HDR-BT Devices is Coding 7 (“Alternate Mode”), which requires three additional
7769 data Lanes.
7770 Coding 7 is intended for Buses with HDR-BT Targets that support three additional data Lanes for Multi-
7771 Lane. It is not interoperable with any other Codings in Table 106, and is expressly not interoperable with any
7772 1-Lane or 2-Lane ML HDR-BT Targets, nor with any 4-Lane ML HDR-BT Targets that do not support Coding
7773 7.
7774 In Coding 7:
7775 • All HDR-BT Blocks shall be transmitted using SDA[0], SDA[1], SDA[2], and SDA[3], using the
7776 QUAD Lane bit packing formats shown in Table 89 for Data Bytes and Table 92 for the Transition
7777 Byte. See Figure 138 for the HDR-BT Header Block format, Figure 141 for the HDR-BT Data
7778 Block format, and Figure 145 for the HDR-BT CRC Block format.
7779 • For generic HDR-BT transactions and for all HDR-BT CCC flows, all HDR-BT Data Blocks and
7780 HDR-BT CRC Blocks shall also be transmitted in the same QUAD Lane bit packing format.
7781 This “Alternate Mode” is not compatible with other ML-capable HDR-BT Targets that are not configured to
7782 use an “Alternate Mode” Coding marked as Coding 7, or other HDR-BT Targets that are not ML-capable.
7783 Such Targets will not correctly receive and understand any HDR-BT Header Blocks transmitted in this bit
7784 packing format, and as a result they will not participate in any HDR-BT transactions, and could even cause
7785 communication errors.
7786 Note:
7787 In Coding 7, the Controller shall not use the SINGLE Lane or DUAL Lane formats for any HDR-BT
7788 structured protocol elements, such as the HDR-BT Header Block, Data Block or CRC Block.
7789 Once an HDR-BT capable Target that supports Coding 7 has been configured to use an ML Frame
7790 format that uses Coding 7 or any other “Alternate Mode” Coding that is interoperable, the entire
7791 Device enters a ‘sticky’ state and shall not accept any configuration changes via the MLANE CCC
7792 that might use any other Coding not interoperable with Coding 7, except for certain MLANE sub-
7793 commands sent as a Broadcast CCC. If the MLANE Direct SET CCC is used, then such a Device
7794 may only enter the ‘sticky’ state from Coding 0.
7795 A Controller shall not enable any ML-capable HDR-BT Targets to use any “Alternate Mode” Coding marked
7796 as Coding 7, unless it first ensures that all other HDR-BT Targets are capable of interoperating with Coding
7797 7. A Controller shall not initiate any HDR-BT transactions using QUAD Lane bit packing formats for all
7798 HDR-BT Blocks, unless it has previously configured all HDR-BT Targets on the Bus to use an “Alternate
7799 Mode” Coding marked as Coding 7. Once a Controller has configured all HDR-BT Targets on the Bus to use
7800 Coding 7, it must drive all HDR-BT transactions using QUAD Lane bit packing formats for all HDR-BT
7801 Blocks.
400 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 415 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.7.3.5.3.2 HDR-BT DUAL Coding
7802 The default Coding for HDR-BT DUAL is Coding 0 (compatible mode); its additional Data Byte used by the
7803 MLANE CCC is 0x01 (i.e., {5'd0, 3'd1}).
7804 In this ML Frame format, all HDR-BT Blocks that are part of generic HDR-BT transactions (except for the
7805 HDR-BT Header Blocks), or that comprise the per-Target CCC flow elements, shall be transmitted using
7806 SDA[0] and SDA[1], using the DUAL Lane bit packing formats shown in Table 88 for Data Bytes and Table
7807 91 for the Transition Byte. See Figure 141 for the HDR-BT Data Block format, and Figure 145 for the
7808 HDR-BT CRC Block format. For additional details, see also Coding 0 in Section 6.7.3.5.3.1.
7809 Any Targets that are not configured for DUAL Lane Coding 0 might not be able to receive and understand
7810 any HDR-BT Data Blocks and HDR-BT CRC Blocks that are not part of a transaction (either generic, or
7811 Direct CCC flow) addressed to their Target Address. Because these Blocks might not have the same format
7812 and length as their currently configured ML Frame format, such a Target may instead ignore these HDR-BT
7813 Blocks and wait for the HDR Restart Pattern, after which it will be able to receive and understand a
7814 subsequent HDR-BT Header Block and correctly parse its Address field.
7815 “Alternate Mode” Codings for DUAL Lane: Coding 3
7816 In some Bus configurations, it might be desirable to utilize one additional data Lane for HDR-BT Blocks, for
7817 increased performance and Bus efficiency. This requires all Targets on the Bus that support HDR-BT to be
7818 configured to support ML Frame formats that are interoperable (i.e., that expect to use the same number of
7819 additional data Lanes in the same locations in HDR-BT transactions).
7820 One “Alternate Mode” Coding for HDR-BT DUAL is Coding 3; its additional Data Byte used by the MLANE
7821 CCC is 0x19 (i.e., {5'd3, 3'd1}).
7822 In this ML Frame format, HDR-BT Blocks that are part of generic HDR-BT transactions (except for the
7823 HDR-BT Header Blocks), or that comprise the per-Target CCC flow elements, shall be transmitted using
7824 SDA[0] and SDA[1], using the DUAL Lane bit packing formats listed above. For additional details, see also
7825 Coding 3 in Section 6.7.3.5.3.1.
7826 As previously stated, the HDR-BT Header Blocks and all HDR-BT Blocks comprising the core CCC flow
7827 elements shall be transmitted in DUAL Lane bit packing format.
7828 In a Bus that mixes 2-Lane ML and 4-Lane ML Targets, all of which are configured for Coding 3, any Targets
7829 configured for DUAL Lane Coding 3 shall ignore any HDR-BT Data Blocks and HDR-BT CRC Blocks that
7830 are part of a transaction (either generic, or Direct CCC flow) addressed to another Target configured for
7831 QUAD Lane Coding 3.
7832 Any Targets not configured for DUAL Lane Coding 3 might not be able to receive and understand any HDR-
7833 BT Data Blocks and HDR-BT CRC Blocks that are not part of a transaction (either generic, or Direct CCC
7834 flow) addressed to their Target Address. Because these Blocks might not have the same format and length as
7835 their currently configured ML Frame format, such a Target may instead ignore these HDR-BT Blocks and
7836 wait for the HDR Restart Pattern, after which it will be able to receive and understand a subsequent HDR-
7837 BT Header Block and correctly parse its Address field.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 401
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 416 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.7.3.5.3.3 HDR-BT QUAD Coding
7838 The default Coding for HDR-BT QUAD is Coding 0 (Compatible Mode); its additional Data Byte used by
7839 the MLANE CCC is 0x03 (i.e., {5'd0, 3'd3}).
7840 In this ML Frame format, all HDR-BT Blocks that are part of generic HDR-BT transactions (except for the
7841 HDR-BT Header Blocks), or that comprise the per-Target CCC flow elements, shall be transmitted using
7842 SDA[0], SDA[1], SDA[2], and SDA[3], using the QUAD Lane bit packing formats shown in Table 89 for
7843 Data Bytes and Table 92 for the Transition Byte. See Figure 141 for the HDR-BT Data Block format, and
7844 Figure 145 for the HDR-BT CRC Block format. For additional details, see also Coding 0 in
7845 Section 6.7.3.5.3.1.
7846 Any Targets not configured for QUAD Coding 0 might not be able to receive and understand any HDR-BT
7847 Data Blocks and HDR-BT CRC Blocks that are not part of a transaction (either generic, or Direct CCC flow)
7848 addressed to their Target Address. Because these Blocks might not have the same format and length as their
7849 currently configured ML Frame format, such a Target may instead ignore these HDR-BT Blocks and wait for
7850 the HDR Restart Pattern, after which it will be able to receive and understand a subsequent HDR-BT Header
7851 Block and correctly parse its Address field.
7852 “Alternate Mode” Codings for QUAD Lane
7853 In some Bus configurations, it might be desirable to utilize the three additional data Lanes for HDR-BT
7854 Blocks, for increased performance and Bus efficiency. This requires all Targets on the Bus that support HDR-
7855 BT to be configured to support ML Frame formats that are interoperable (i.e., that expect to use the same
7856 number of additional data Lanes in the same locations in HDR-BT transactions).
7857 Coding 3
7858 One “Alternate Mode” Coding for HDR-BT QUAD is Coding 3; its additional Data Byte used by the
7859 MLANE CCC is 0x1B (i.e., {5'd3, 3'd3}).
7860 In this ML Frame format, HDR-BT Blocks that are part of generic HDR-BT transactions (except for the
7861 HDR-BT Header Blocks), or that comprise the per-Target CCC flow elements, shall be transmitted using
7862 SDA[0], SDA[1], SDA[2], and SDA[3], using the QUAD Lane bit packing formats listed above. See also
7863 Coding 3 in Section 6.7.3.5.3.1.
7864 As previously stated, the HDR-BT Header Blocks and all HDR-BT Blocks comprising the core CCC flow
7865 elements shall be transmitted in DUAL Lane bit packing format.
7866 In a Bus that mixes 2-Lane ML and 4-Lane ML Targets, all of which are configured for Coding 3, any Targets
7867 configured for QUAD Lane Coding 3 shall ignore any HDR-BT Data Blocks and HDR-BT CRC Blocks that
7868 are part of a transaction (either generic, or Direct CCC flow) addressed to another Target configured for
7869 DUAL Lane Coding 3.
7870 Any Targets not configured for QUAD Lane Coding 3 might not be able to receive and understand any HDR-
7871 BT Data Blocks and HDR-BT CRC Blocks that are not part of a transaction (either generic, or Direct CCC
7872 flow) addressed to their Target Address. Because these Blocks might not have the same format and length as
7873 their currently configured ML Frame format, such a Target may instead ignore these HDR-BT Blocks and
7874 wait for the HDR Restart Pattern, after which it will be able to receive and understand a subsequent HDR-
7875 BT Header Block and correctly parse its Address field.
7876 Coding 7
7877 Another “Alternate Mode” Coding for HDR-BT QUAD is Coding 7; its additional Data Byte used by the
7878 MLANE CCC is 0x3B (i.e., {5'd7, 3'd3}).
7879 In this ML Frame format, all HDR-BT Blocks shall be transmitted using SDA[0], SDA[1], SDA[2], and
7880 SDA[3], using the QUAD Lane bit packing formats listed above. See also Coding 7 in Section 6.7.3.5.3.1.
7881 As QUAD Lane Coding 7 is not interoperable with any other Codings, nor with any other HDR-BT Targets
7882 supporting fewer than 3 additional data Lanes, it shall not be used in any Bus configuration in which it could
7883 cause communication errors.
402 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 417 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.7.3.6 Multi-Lane Data Transfer Control (MLANE)
7884 The MLANE CCC is available in Broadcast (Figure 159) and Direct (Figure 160) versions. It is used to set
7885 up and control ML functionality for ML-capable Target Devices (see Section 6.7) by setting or getting, for a
7886 given I3C Mode (Table 108), the number of Additional Data Lanes (Table 103) and Data Transfer Coding
7887 number (see Section 6.7.3.3) to use while communicating to the Target using that I3C Mode.
7888 This CCC always includes the one-byte Defining Byte field, which acts as a GET, SET, or SET/GET sub-
7889 command. Many of the defined sub-commands that have Broadcast CCC or Direct SET CCC versions also
7890 require one or more additional Data Bytes, as detailed in Table 108.
7891 Note:
7892 For SDR Mode Only: The GET version of the SET/GET MLANE CCC requires the addressed Target
7893 to provide the DATA/T values on the Additional Data Lanes (i.e., on SDA[1] for DUAL Lane
7894 configurations, and on SDA[1], SDA[2], and SDA[3] for QUAD Lane configurations). The Controller
7895 can use this to test whether the Target is connected to the Additional Data Lanes. Since I3C Basic
7896 only supports Multi-Lane transfers for HDR-BT Mode, this shall apply for Defining Byte 0x23 only.
7897 Figure 161 shows the CCC GET format for QUAD Lane configurations; the version for DUAL Lane
7898 configurations does not use SDA[2] or SDA[3].
7899 For HDR Modes: See MLANE Command Name in Table 75 for details on the format of the Target’s
7900 response to the Format 2 (Direct GET) MLANE CCC while in HDR Modes.

7906 Note:
7907 An I3C Controller that supports any version of the I3C Basic Specification might be on the same I3C Bus with an I3C Target that supports the full I3C
7908 Specification, and it might receive a report using the MLANE Direct GET CCC with the Sub-Command for Get ML Capabilities (i.e., Defining Byte 0xFF) or
7909 other Sub-Commands that include ML Frame formats for I3C Modes that the I3C Controller does not support. In such a case, the I3C Controller shall ignore
7910 any ML Frame formats that pertain to such I3C Modes (i.e., those marked as “Not included in I3C Basic” in Table 108), and shall not use the corresponding
7911 Sub-Commands for such I3C Modes, as the I3C Controller would not have the capability to support Multi-Lane Data Transfers for such I3C Modes.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 407
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 422 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.7.3.6.1 Direct SET Sub-Commands Sent to a Dynamic Address
7912 The Controller may send the MLANE Direct SET CCC with the Sub-Commands to set the ML Frame format
7913 for supported I3C Modes (i.e., Defining Byte 0x23) when addressed to a Target Device’s Dynamic Address.
7914 The Target shall receive and acknowledge the CCC, to write a supported ML Frame format with the
7915 Additional Data Byte for that I3C Mode, and the Target shall store this Data Byte in its ML configuration
7916 (i.e., configured ML Frame format) for the corresponding I3C Mode. Unless defined otherwise for a
7917 particular I3C Mode, the Target shall return this same Data Byte from its stored ML configuration upon
7918 receiving the MLANE Direct GET CCC with the same sub-command, addressed to the same Dynamic
7919 Address. The Target shall not reset or change this stored ML configuration, unless it properly receives the
7920 Sub-Command for Reset ML, or receives any other valid action that might be defined for specific I3C Modes
7921 (i.e., as defined in sub-sections of Section 6.7.3.3). The stored ML configuration for each supported I3C
7922 Mode shall be stored and used for subsequent transfers involving this Dynamic Address (and the Broadcast
7923 Address, if the Target supports CCC flows in HDR Modes per Section 6.1.3) unless it is subsequently reset,
7924 or changed by a SET Sub-Command (or another valid action). See Section 6.7.3.1 for more details on ML-
7925 capable Device configuration.
7926 If the Device presents multiple Virtual Targets and uses shared Peripheral logic to manage transactions
7927 addressed to multiple Dynamic Addresses (per Section 4.3.2.1.2), then the ML configurations (i.e.,
7928 configured ML Frame formats) for each assigned Dynamic Address shall be configured and stored
7929 independently. All conditions above shall apply for each Dynamic Address, and the Device shall honor the
7930 SET/GET semantics for the MLANE Direct CCC with Sub-Commands addressed to any Dynamic Addresses.
7931 Each CCC with Sub-Command to set a stored ML configuration shall only change the ML configuration for
7932 the particular Dynamic Address (per Section 6.7.3.1 and Section 6.7.3.1.2) unless other requirements apply
7933 for specific I3C Modes (i.e., as defined in sub-sections of Section 6.7.3.3) that have valid actions that would
7934 create exceptions to the requirements in Section 6.7.3.1.2 for independent ML configurations per each
7935 Dynamic Address.
7936 Note:
7937 The Controller should determine whether such a Device has multiple Dynamic Addresses and
7938 presents multiple Virtual Targets using shared Peripheral logic, before attempting to set an ML
7939 configuration with the MLANE Direct SET CCC to any Dynamic Address exposed by such a Device.
408 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 423 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.7.3.6.2 Direct SET Sub-Commands Sent to a Group Address
7940 If the Target Device supports Group Address capabilities (per Section 4.3.4.4) and also supports separate
7941 Group ML configurations (i.e., variant 2'b01 per Section 6.7.3.1.1) then the Target shall also receive and
7942 acknowledge the MLANE Direct SET CCC with Sub-Commands for supported I3C Modes (i.e., Defining
7943 Byte 0x23) when addressed to an assigned Group Address. If the Target accepts the CCC with such a Sub-
7944 Command, then it shall only set its stored ML configuration (i.e., configured ML Frame format) for
7945 transactions involving that Group Address, but shall not change the stored ML configuration for any other
7946 assigned addresses. For HDR Modes, if the Target accepts the CCC with such a Sub-Command, then the
7947 stored ML configuration (if valid) shall be used for all subsequent transfers addressed to that Group Address
7948 (i.e., generic HDR Write commands); if the Target also supports CCC flows in that HDR Mode (per
7949 Section 6.1.3), then this ML configuration shall also be used for Direct SET CCCs that are addressed to that
7950 Group Address in that particular HDR Mode (per Section 6.1.3.5). See Section 6.7.3.1.1 for more details on
7951 Group ML configuration. The Target shall not acknowledge the CCC with such a Sub-Command when
7952 addressed to any Group Address to which it has not been assigned.
7953 If the Target supports Group Address capabilities (per Section 4.3.4.4) and does not support separate Group
7954 ML configurations (i.e., variant 2'b00, per Section 6.7.3.1.1), then the Target shall not acknowledge the
7955 MLANE Direct SET CCC with Sub-Commands for supported I3C Modes (i.e., Defining Byte 0x23) when
7956 addressed to an assigned Group Address. For such a Target, the stored ML configuration (i.e., configured ML
7957 Frame format) for transfers involving this Dynamic Address shall also be used for subsequent transfers
7958 involving any assigned Group Addresses. See Section 6.7.3.1.1 for more details.
7959 Note:
7960 If the Target does not support Group Address capabilities, then the Target shall only acknowledge
7961 the MLANE Direct SET CCC with Sub-Commands for its supported I3C Modes (i.e., Defining Byte
7962 0x23) when addressed to its assigned Dynamic Address.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 409
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 424 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic OPTIONAL FEATURE F006 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.7.3.6.3 Broadcast SET Sub-Commands
7963 The Controller may also send the MLANE CCC with the Sub-Commands to set the ML Frame format for
7964 supported I3C Modes (i.e., Defining Byte 0x23) as a Broadcast CCC, and all Target Devices on the I3C Bus
7965 shall acknowledge the CCC (per Table 15). However, only Targets that support the MLANE CCC and also
7966 support the particular I3C Mode indicated by the Defining Byte may attempt to apply the ML configuration
7967 that follows the Defining Byte in the Broadcast SET CCC message. Each such Target shall compare the ML
7968 configuration to see whether it is valid (i.e., supported) for that I3C Mode: if so, then it shall store this Data
7969 Byte in its ML configuration (i.e., configured ML Frame format) for the corresponding I3C Mode, as applied
7970 to its assigned Dynamic Address (or all assigned Dynamic Addresses, if it is a Device that presents multiple
7971 Virtual Targets, per Section 4.3.2.1.2 and Section 6.7.3.1.2) as well as all currently assigned Group Addresses
7972 (if supported, per Section 6.7.3.1.1). See Section 6.7.3.1 for more details on ML-capable Device
7973 configuration.
7974 If the provided ML configuration is not valid (i.e., not supported) for that I3C Mode, then the Target shall not
7975 apply the Data Byte and shall not make any configuration changes based on the Broadcast SET CCC message.
7976 Additionally, if the Target does not support the particular I3C Mode indicated by the Defining Byte, then it
7977 shall not act on the rest of the Broadcast SET CCC message. However, in both cases the Controller would
7978 not know that the Target did not make any changes to its ML configuration as a result of this Broadcast SET
7979 CCC, so the Controller should follow up by sending the MLANE Direct GET CCC with the same sub-
7980 command, addressed to each Dynamic Address that is known to support that particular I3C Mode.
7981 Note:
7982 If some I3C Targets on the Bus do not support certain I3C Modes, or if some Targets do not support
7983 certain ML Frame formats for a given I3C Mode, then the MLANE Broadcast CCC with a given
7984 Defining Byte and Data Byte would not always configure all such Targets to use the same ML Frame
7985 format for the same I3C Mode. It is the responsibility of the I3C Controller to determine which ML
7986 configuration changes (including those sent using the MLANE CCC as a Broadcast SET CCC) have
7987 been accepted by all Targets, and whether any such Targets might not have acted on the Broadcast
7988 SET CCC message. For situations where the Controller discovers that some Targets did not accept
7989 or apply such an ML configuration change, the Controller must resolve the situation by ensuring that
7990 all Targets are configured to use ML Frame formats that are mutually interoperable for each I3C
7991 Mode, or using an error recovery procedure which might include the Sub-Command for Reset ML
7992 (i.e., Defining Byte 0x7F).
