6.10 F009: Controller Clock Stalling
8001 This Section defines I3C Optional Feature F009: Controller Clock Stalling.
8002 Table 111 Optional Feature 009 Status
Optional Feature ID F009
Optional Feature Name Controller Clock Stalling
Optional Feature Version 1
Optional Feature specification was last updated in I3C Spec I3C v1.2
Version
Optional Feature is not compatible with other Optional Features –
Optional Feature is included in I3C Basic Yes
8003 Changes in this Version
8004 For detailed changes in this version, see the “diff” file between I3C v1.1.1 and I3C v1.2 in the MIPI
8005 Specification Repository [MIPI09].
6.10.1 Scope and Purpose (Informative)
8006 The scope of Section 6.10 is limited to fully specifying I3C Optional Feature F009: Controller Clock Stalling.
8007 An I3C Device that implements F009 is required to implement all shall statements in Section 6.10.3, and can
8008 optionally implement any combination of may statements in Section 6.10.3. In addition, MIPI Alliance
8009 recommends that manufacturers follow all should statements in Section 6.10.3.
6.10.2 Technical Overview (Informative)
8010 In SDR Mode the I3C Controller is permitted to Stall the I3C Bus during the SCL Low period, but only under
8011 the specific, transitory conditions described in this Section.
8012 Stalling may be necessary for either of two reasons:
8013 1. The absolute or relative timing of a Message to a specific Target, or to all Targets, needs to be
8014 carefully controlled. Clock Stalling provides the Controller with fine-grained data timing
8015 control.
8016 2. The I3C Controller needs to internally synchronize data. This may be due to parts of the
8017 Controller’s system waking up in response to data, to changing state, or to otherwise needing
8018 time during a transaction.
8019 Note that Stalling impacts Bus performance. For example, it will reduce the Bus capacity and increase the
8020 latency of any Devices issuing In-Band Interrupts.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 415
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 430 ---\nSpecification for I3C OPTIONAL FEATURE F009 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.10.3 Technical Specification
8021 In SDR Mode the I3C Controller may Stall the I3C Bus during the SCL Low period, but only under the
8022 specific, transitory conditions described in this Section.
8023 To Stall the Bus, the I3C Controller shall hold SCL Low while the Bus is in one of the four following
8024 conditions, each of which is detailed below:
8025 1. I3C/I2C Transfer, ACK/NACK Phase
8026 2. Write Data Transfer, Parity Bit
8027 3. I3C Read Transfer, Transition Bit
8028 4. Dynamic Address Assignment, First Bit of Assigned Address
8029 The maximum Stall time (SCL Low period) shall be 15 ms; note, this is an absolute maximum. The Controller
8030 should use the shortest Stall duration possible given current circumstances, per Table 112.
8031 Table 112 Controller Clock Stall Times
Maximum
Condition Section
Stall Time
I3C/I2C Transfer, ACK/NACK Phase 6.10.3.1 100 µs
Write Data Transfer, Parity Bit 6.10.3.2 100 µs
I3C Read Transfer, Transition Bit 6.10.3.3 100 µs
Dynamic Address Assignment, First Bit of Assigned Address 6.10.3.4 15 ms
8032 In all cases, after Stalling the SCL Low period, the continuation of the clock (i.e., the first SCL rising edge
8033 after the extended Low period) shall follow the normal rules for I3C SDR (for I3C SDR Messages), or for
8034 I2C (for I2C Messages) including rise time, change in SDA (if any) before SCL rise, next bit, etc. For example,
8035 the I3C Controller might choose to terminate the Frame after this Stalled bit with a STOP; but in this case
8036 the Controller is required to observe the requirements for STOP timing, as appropriate.
8037 It is recommended to use Controller Clock Stalling only when necessary and unavoidable. In all other
8038 circumstances the Controller should avoid Controller Clock Stalling because of its negative impacts on Bus
8039 performance. In order to help guide system designers, Data Sheets for I3C Controller Devices should include
8040 appropriately detailed SCL Stalling parameters.
416 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 431 ---\nVersion 1.2 OPTIONAL FEATURE F009 Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
6.10.3.1 I3C/I2C Transfer, ACK/NACK Phase
8041 Controller Clock Stalling during the ACK/NACK phase of the I3C/I2C transfer (see Figure 162) indicates
8042 one of the following:
8043 • The Controller can allow the I3C/I2C Target to prepare to receive data (for write transfers) or
8044 transmit data (for read transfers)
8045 • The Controller can Stall SCL during write or read transfers to Legacy I2C Targets in case of
8046 underrun and overflow situations
8047 • The Controller can Stall the clock during the ACK/NACK phase of the incoming In-Band Interrupt
8048 (Target Interrupt Request or Controller Role Request), to decide whether to ACK or NACK
8049 depending upon the incoming In-Band Interrupt
8050 • The Controller can allow the Target to respond with data for the directed GET CCC commands if
8051 the Target is not ready with the CCC data to be returned
ACK/

NACK
SDA
C7 C8 C9
SCL
Target on Open-Drain mode
Controller drives Push-Pull
Controller extends SCL LOW

Figure 162 Controller Clock Stalling in ACK Phase
6.10.3.2 Write Data Transfer, Parity Bit
8053 The Controller can Stall SCL between data bytes of an I3C Write Data transfer, by Stalling the clock during
8054 the Low period of the Parity Bit, in case of Underrun situations. See Figure 163.

16-Dec-2024
6.10.3.3 I3C Read Transfer, Transition Bit
8056 The Controller can Stall SCL between data bytes of an I3C Read Data transfer, or before terminating, by
8057 Stalling the clock during the Low period of the Transition Bit, in case of Overflow situations. See Figure
8058 164 through Figure 168.

6.10.3.4 Dynamic Address Assignment, First Bit of Assigned Address
8064 The Controller can Stall SCL during the Low period of the first bit of the Assigned Address phase of the
8065 Enter Dynamic Address Assignment CCC command (ENTDAA, see Section 4.3.7.3.4), for example to gain
8066 time to assign the Dynamic Address to the Device based on the Target’s Bus Characteristics Register BCR
8067 (see Section 4.3.1.2.1) and Device Characteristics Register DCR (see Section 4.3.1.2.2) bytes. See Figure
8068 169.

6.11 F010: Alternative Electrical Specifications
8070 This Section defines I3C Optional Feature F010: Alternative Electrical Specifications.
8071 Table 113 Optional Feature 010 Status
Optional Feature ID F010
Optional Feature Name Alternative Electrical
Specifications
Optional Feature Version 1
Optional Feature specification was last updated in I3C Spec I3C v1.2
Version
Optional Feature is not compatible with other Optional Features –
Optional Feature is included in I3C Basic Yes
8072 Changes in this Version
8073 This feature is new for I3C v1.2.
6.11.1 Scope and Purpose (Informative)
8074 The scope of Section 6.11 is limited to fully specifying I3C Optional Feature F010: Alternative Electrical
8075 Specifications.
8076 An I3C Device that implements F010 may choose any of the available sub-sections within Section 6.11.2 as
8077 a alternative definitions of the electrical specifications, rather then strictly following the standard electrical
8078 specifications defined in Required Elements Section 4.3.11.
8079 For each of the chosen sub-sections that apply, an I3C Device that uses alternative electrical specifications is
8080 required to implement all shall statements in that sub-section, and can optionally implement any combination
8081 of may statements in that sub-section. In addition, MIPI Alliance recommends that manufacturers follow all
8082 should statements in that sub-section.
8083 Note:
8084 As a result, it is possible that shall, should, and may statements for this Optional Feature will
8085 override some of the requirements given in the standard electrical specifications defined in Required
8086 Elements Section 4.3.11.
8087 The purpose of this Optional Feature is to provide flexibility for implementers addressing special use cases
8088 where the standard electrical specifications defined in Section 4.3.11 would be infeasible or unsuitable. This
8089 allows the I3C Specification to be used as a base specification, despite differences in the native electrical
8090 specifications as defined by other standards bodies that govern those use cases.
8091 Unless specified otherwise, the alternative electrical specifications in this Optional Feature allow the same
8092 I3C protocol to be used in special use cases where the standard electrical specification does not work for a
8093 particular use case, but the upper protocol layers would still be used.
8094 In some cases, vendors and other standards bodies might define extensions to, or deviations from, the standard
8095 I3C content protocol. This may include custom CCCs as well as other methods of I3C Target discovery and
8096 configuration. The I3C Specification does not preclude this, however implementers are cautioned that such
8097 extensions or deviations might made an I3C Device usable only for the intended use case, i.e., not usable for
8098 general use cases.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 421
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 436 ---\nSpecification for I3C OPTIONAL FEATURE F010 Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
6.11.2 Technical Specification
8099 Alternative electrical specifications may optionally be used for special I3C Bus use cases.
8100 Note:
8101 The MIPI I3C WG strongly recommends that I3C Device implementers use the standard electrical
8102 specifications (see Section 4.3.11) for I3C Devices that are meant for general usage across a broad set of
8103 use cases, unless there is a compelling reason why the standard electrical specifications would not be suitable
8104 for a specific use case.
6.11.2.1 Low Voltage / High Capacitive Load I3C Use Cases
8105 For special Buses with lower voltage and higher capacitive loads (i.e., greater than 50 pF), the following characteristics
8106 should be used. In some scenarios, Buses with higher capacitive loads might not be able to achieve the maximum
8107 clock speed of 12.5 MHz.
8108 Note:
8109 The characteristics defined in Table 114 were originally included only in v1.1.1 and earlier of the MIPI I3C
8110 Basic Specification [MIPI11]. These characteristics are intended for special use cases only, i.e., the SPD bus
8111 that is used with JEDEC-compliant DDR5 memory modules.

4) For higher capacitance Buses, Pull-Up for Open Drain should be switched off during I3C Push-Pull operation. This may be implemented as: A Pull-Up
internally; a current source internally; an external Pull-Up resistor driven by a pin; or any combination of the above. The effective resistance comprising the
Open Drain Pull-Up and High-Keeper Pull-Up shall be sized sufficiently to allow the Target to pull SDA Low within t for adjusted clock speeds, and to allow

Annex A Changes to Required Elements
8113 This Annex summarizes the changes made to Section 4 Required Elements in version 1.2 of this
8114 Specification.
8115 For detailed changes in this version, see the “diff” file between I3C Basic v1.1.1 and I3C Basic v1.2 in the
8116 MIPI Specification Repository [MIPI09].
8117 Changes to Required Elements in I3C Basic Version 1.2
8118 • Document reorganization to move all assigned values to Section 5 Assigned Values:
8119 • SETBUSCON CCC context bytes moved to Section 5.2 SETBUSCON Context Byte
8120 • GETCAPS capability bytes (for both Format 1 and Format 2) moved to Section 5.4 GETCAPS
8121 Capability Bits
8122 • IBI MDB values moved to Section 5.5 In-Band Interrupt MDB (Mandatory Data Byte)
8123 • Document reorganization to move every optional feature to an independent subsection of
8124 Section 6 Optional Features:
8125 • HDR Modes (common definitions) moved to Section 6.1
8126 • HDR-DDR Mode moved to Section 6.2
8127 • HDR-Ternary Modes moved to Section 6.3 (details not included for I3C Basic)
8128 • HDR-BT Mode moved to Section 6.4
8129 • Secondary Controller requirements and definitions moved to Section 6.5
8130 • Timing Control feature moved to Section 6.6:
8131 • This includes the definitions of the SETXTIME and GETXTIME CCCs
8132 • Multi-Lane Data Transfer feature moved to Section 6.7:
8133 • This includes the definition of the MLANE CCC
8134 • Monitoring Device feature moved to Section 6.8
8135 • ENDXFER CCC details moved:
8136 • Moved to Section 6.2 for HDR-DDR Mode
8137 • Not included for HDR-Ternary Modes
8138 • To Section 6.8 for Monitoring Devices
8139 • Device-to-Device Tunneling feature moved to Section 6.9
8140 • Controller Clock Stalling requirements and definitions moved to Section 6.10
8141 • All items that were previously addressed in Errata 01 for I3C Basic v1.1.1, including:
8142 • Removed restrictions on values used in SETMWL and GETMWL CCCs
8143 • Requirements for an I3C Basic Target that initially acted as an I²C Target with a Spike Filter
8144 enabled
8145 • Updated default read value for RSTACT Direct Read CCC; new value of 0x80 means that no reset
8146 action has been configured
8147 • Clarifications on timing parameter t SCO in several diagrams
8148 • Updates for Targets sending IBI payloads:
8149 • When sending IBI data payload, a Target is now required to observe the configured MRL
8150 (Maximum Read Length) values
8151 • Clarified that a Target is required to use the T-Bit (i.e., T=0) to end the IBI data payload after
8152 sending the last byte
8153 • When a Target’s Dynamic Address is reset, all assigned Group Addresses are also reset
8154 • Clarifications on the use of passive Hot-Join
8155 • Changed ENTDAA CCC from Required to Conditional
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 427
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 442 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic ANNEX A Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
8156 • Also clarified details on when a Target is required to support the ENTDAA CCC
8157 • Clarifications on MRL and MWL configuration:
8158 • If a Target does not support a new MRL/MWL value sent via the SETMRL/SETMWL CCCs, then
8159 it ignores the new value and returns the previously configured MRL/MWL value with the next
8160 GETMRL/GETMWL CCC
8161 • Clarifications on the use of the SETDASA and SETAASA CCCs, including:
8162 • Once a Dynamic Address is assigned to a Target with a Static Address (using SETDASA or
8163 SETAASA), the Target will not respond to the ENTDAA CCC, since it already has an assigned
8164 Dynamic Address
8165 • Dynamic Address can be changed or reset with subsequent use of RSTDAA or SETNEWDA
8166 CCCs
8167 • Clarifications on GETSTATUS indication of pending interrupt status
8168 • Moved Clock-to-Data Turnaround Delay details to Section 4.3.11.2 Timing Specification
8169 • Clarifications on the use of Target Reset, including:
8170 • Added timing details for the Target Reset Pattern
8171 • Added clarifications on when the Target Reset Pattern can be sent, as well as how the Controller
8172 handles a possible conflict between sending the Target Reset Pattern and receiving an IBI from a
8173 Target
8174 • For the configured reset action to be taken, the RSTACT CCC(s) and the Target Reset Pattern must
8175 now appear in the same SDR Frame. I.e., there can be no intervening STOP condition.
8176 • Added new table that shows the Required/Optional resets within the I3C Basic Target for various
8177 defined reset types
8178 • Whole-Target reset (i.e., the RSTACT CCC with Defining Byte 0x02) is not required
8179 • Whole-Target reset is not required to include a “full/chip” reset (however implementers may
8180 include this if desired)
8181 • New definitions for byte field format and implied time units for the RSTACT Direct Read CCC
8182 when a Target returns the expected reset time (for Defining Bytes 0x81 thru 0x83):
8183 • Implementers can optionally use the same format and time units for other RSTACT reset types
8184 • Added clarifications of how Controllers should interpret the reset time values returned by older
8185 I3C Basic Targets (i.e., those conforming to v1.1.1 and earlier of the I3C Basic Specification),
8186 since the time units were not defined in those versions of the spec
8187 • Several clarifications on Error Reset and Error Recovery methods
8188 • Added timing details to diagrams for the HDR Exit Pattern and the HDR Reset Pattern:
8189 • Note that the definition of the HDR Reset Pattern was also moved into Section 6.1 HDR Modes,
8190 since a Target is only required to support the pattern if it supports at least one of the HDR Modes
428 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 443 ---\nVersion 1.2 ANNEX B Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024
Annex B I3C Implementation Summary Templates
8191 I3C implementers should use the formats presented in this Annex to concisely express what I3C features their
8192 products do and do not implement. Use of these formats simplifies both identification of candidate I3C
8193 products that meet a system designer’s requirements, and comparison between different I3C products.
8194 Two formats are provided: Full Format and Concise Format. Both formats are appropriate for use in product
8195 data sheets and other technical marketing materials.
B.1 Full Format
8196 Product Name/Number: _________________
8197 Supports I3C Bus Operating Voltage(s): □ 1.5 V □ 1.1 V
8198 Implements I3C Optional Feature(s):
8199 □ F001 <Name of this optional feature>
8200 □ F002 < Name of this optional feature >
8201 □ F003 < Name of this optional feature >
8202 Example
8203 Product Name/Number: MyCompanyName A3445-88GGH
8204 Supports I3C Bus Operating Voltage(s): ✔ 1.5 V □ 1.1 V
8205 Implements I3C Optional Feature(s):
8206 □ F001 <Name of this optional feature>
8207 ✔ F002 < Name of this optional feature >
8208 □ F003 < Name of this optional feature >
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 429
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 444 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic ANNEX B Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
B.2 Concise Format
8209 Product: _________________
8210 I3C Bus Operating Voltage(s): _________________
8211 Optional Feature(s): _________________
8212 Examples
8213 Product: MyCompanyName A3445-88GGH
8214 1.5 V
8215 Optional Features: F003, F006, F009
8216 MyCompanyName A3445-88GGH: I3C 1.5 V; Optional Features F003, F006, F009
430 Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc.
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 445 ---\nVersion 1.2 ANNEX C Specification for I3C Basic
16-Dec-2024

Figure 188 I2C Legacy Controller FSM\n--- Page 465 ---\nVersion 1.2 ANNEX F Specification for I3C Basic

Annex F Typical I3C Basic Protocol Communications
8244 Figure 189 through Figure 193 illustrate a typical communication for each of the supported I3C Basic
8245 Protocols. While these figures do not exhaustively illustrate all possible I3C Basic communications, they do
8246 serve as useful introductions to the signaling and transmission formatting used in each I3C Basic Protocol.
F.1 Typical SDR Private Read
8247 Figure 189 illustrates example communication using I3C Single Data Rate (SDR) Mode. It shows the
8248 Controller reading a byte of data from the Target at Address 0x2B in SDR Mode.
8249 From the Bus Free Condition, the Controller issues a START by driving the SDA line Low while keeping the
8250 SCL line High. It then issues the Broadcast Address (7'h7E) followed by RnW (0 for Write). Then the
8251 Controller turns on a Pull-Up resistor and goes to Open Drain. All Targets ACK by pulling the SDA line Low
8252 (in the Figure, pink fill means the Target is in control of the SDA line at this time). The Controller then issues
8253 a Repeated START, then the Address of the Target (0x2B) it wants to read followed by RnW (1 for Read).
8254 The Controller then turns on a Pull-Up resistor and goes to Open Drain, allowing the Target to acknowledge
8255 by pulling the SDA line Low. At this point, the Controller continues to toggle the SCL line and release the
8256 SDA line, allowing the Target to drive SDA to send one byte of data (0x4A) followed by ‘T’. T = 1 informs
8257 the Controller that there is additional data, whereas T = 0 signals the end. Here there is additional data, so the
8258 Target drives SDA High until SCL goes High, at which time it releases SDA. The Controller has the option
8259 of holding SDA High with a weak Pull-Up, which signals to the Target that the Controller allows another
8260 byte to be transmitted, or to pull SDA Low (while SCL is High – hence a Repeated START), which would
8261 signal to the Target that the Controller has terminated the Read and is taking over.
8262 SDR Mode is backwards compatible with Legacy I2C Devices, because the High time of an SCL pulse is
8263 always less than 50 ns and therefore SCL will always appear to be Low because of the I2C 50 ns Spike Filter.

F.2 Typical Direct CCC in SDR Mode
8265 Figure 190 shows the Controller issuing a Direct CCC to a single Target. This particular command (GETPID)
8266 reads the Provisioned ID of a Target.
8267 From the Bus Free Condition, the Controller issues a START by driving the SDA line Low while keeping the
8268 SCL line High. It then issues the Broadcast Address (7'h7E) followed by RnW (0 for Write). Then the
8269 Controller turns on a Pull-Up resistor and goes to Open Drain. All Targets ACK by pulling SDA Low (in the
8270 Figure, pink fill means the Targets are in control of SDA at this time). The Controller then issues the Direct
8271 Common Command Code for GETPID (0x8D) followed by parity bit ‘T’ (odd parity = 1 for 0x8D) then the
8272 7-bit Dynamic Address of the Target (chosen arbitrarily here to be 0x2B) followed by a RnW bit (1 for Read).
8273 Then the Controller turns on a Pull-Up resistor and goes to Open Drain, allowing the Target at Address 0x2B
8274 to ACK by pulling SDA Low, which tells the Controller that the Target Acknowledges the command and will
8275 comply. (Alternatively, the Target may NACK by not pulling SDA Low, which would inform the Controller
8276 that the Target will not comply – in this case, that an error occurred.) Following the ACK the Target outputs
8277 its 48-bit PID one byte at a time, and then the Controller issues a Repeated START (this part of the waveform
8278 sequence is not shown in the Figure).

F.3 Typical Broadcast CCC in SDR Mode
8280 Figure 191 illustrates example SDR communication with a Broadcast CCC. The command used in this
8281 example sets the Maximum Read Length of all Targets to 43 bytes (0x002B).
8282 From the Bus Free Condition, the Controller issues a START by driving the SDA line Low while keeping the
8283 SCL line High. It then issues the Broadcast Address (7'h7E) followed by RnW (0 for Write). Then the
8284 Controller turns on a Pull-Up resistor and goes to Open Drain. All Targets ACK by pulling SDA Low (in the
8285 Figure, pink fill means the Targets are in control of SDA at this time). The Controller then issues the Broadcast
8286 Common Command Code for SETMRL (0x0A) followed by parity bit ‘T’ (odd parity = 1 for 0x0A), and
8287 then 2 data bytes (MSB first, and MSb first within each byte) to define the maximum number of bytes that
8288 can be read from a Target in a single read operation. Each data byte is followed by a ‘T’ bit (parity bit – odd
8289 parity). After this the Controller issues a Repeated START.

F.4 Typical HDR-DDR Read
8291 Figure 192 illustrates use of the HDR-DDR (Double Data Rate) Mode. It shows how the Controller can
8292 change the Mode from SDR (Single Data Rate) Mode to HDR-DDR Mode, and a sample of the HDR-DDR
8293 data format.
8294 From the Bus Free Condition, the Controller issues a START by driving the SDA line Low while keeping the
8295 SCL line High. The Controller then issues the Broadcast Address (7'h7E) followed by RnW (0 for Write).
8296 Then the Controller turns on a Pull-Up resistor and goes to Open Drain. All Targets ‘ACK’ by pulling SDA
8297 Low (in the Figure, pink fill means the Targets are in control of SDA at this time). The Controller then issues
8298 the Broadcast Common Command Code for ENTHDR0 (0x20), followed by parity bit ‘T’ (odd parity = 0 for
8299 0x20). At this point, the Bus is in HDR-DDR Mode. In the HDR-DDR protocol, the SDA line is sampled on
8300 every SCL edge (both Low-to-High and High-to-Low transitions of SCL). The HDR-DDR Word consists of
8301 a 2-bit Preamble, followed by two bytes of data, followed by two parity bits. The waveform for the 5-bit CRC
8302 and following traffic is not shown in the Figure.
8303 HDR-DDR Mode is backwards-compatible with Legacy I2C Devices, because the High time of an SCL pulse
8304 is always less than 50 ns and as a result SCL will always appear to be Low because of the I2C 50 ns Spike
8305 Filter.

F.5 Typical HDR-BT Read
8307 Figure 193 illustrates use of the HDR-BT (Bulk Transport) Mode. It shows how the Controller can change
8308 the Mode from SDR (Single Data Rate) Mode to HDR-BT Mode, and a sample of the HDR-BT data format
8309 using 1-lane configuration (i.e., Coding 0). Note that other ML lane configurations and Codings are possible.
8310 From the Bus Free Condition, the Controller issues a START by driving the SDA line Low while keeping the
8311 SCL line High. The Controller then issues the Broadcast Address (7'h7E) followed by RnW (0 for Write).
8312 Then the Controller turns on a Pull-Up resistor and goes to Open Drain. All Targets ‘ACK’ by pulling SDA
8313 Low (in the Figure, pink fill means the Targets are in control of SDA at this time). The Controller then issues
8314 the Broadcast Common Command Code for ENTHDR3 (0x23), followed by parity bit ‘T’ (odd parity = 0 for
8315 0x23). At this point, the Bus is in HDR-BT Mode. In the HDR-BT protocol, the SDA line is sampled on every
8316 SCL edge (both Low-to-High and High-to-Low transitions of SCL). Note that this example shows a Read,
8317 so the Controller sets up the SDA line appropriately such that the first bit (ADDR[0]) is sampled correctly
8318 for a Read transfer (i.e., RnW = 1'b1).
8319 In this example, the Controller starts the transfer by sending the HDR-BT Header Block in 1-lane form, and
8320 the Controller drives the SDA line to send the Address and RnW bit, to indicate the addressed Target (0x2B)
8321 as well as the direction of transfer; the Cmd0 byte (0x85) and additional Cmd1–Cmd3 bytes, to indicate
8322 which data to read; and the additional Control byte for transfer settings. The Controller then uses the
8323 Transition byte to set up SDA for the Target to accept, and the Target drives SDA Low to indicate acceptance.
8324 The Target then drives the SDA line (and optionally the SCL line, if supported) for one or more HDR-BT
8325 Data Blocks and the HDR-BT CRC Block that follows, with up to 32 bytes per Data Block. (The waveforms
8326 for the Data Blocks and CRC Block are not shown in the Figure; see Section 6.4.3.6 for reference.)
8327 HDR-BT Mode is backwards compatible with Legacy I2C Devices, because the High time of an SCL pulse
8328 is always less than 50 ns and as a result SCL will always appear to be Low because of the I2C 50 ns Spike
8329 Filter.

Annex G MIPI I3C Basic Specification Supplemental Patent
Licensing Terms
8331 This agreement (the “Agreement”) between MIPI Alliance Inc. (“MIPI”) and each MIPI member or other party
8332 that has manifest agreement to these terms (each a “Licensor” and collectively the “Licensors”) is effective as of
8333 the date the I3C Basic Specification (defined below) is first approved by the MIPI Board (the “Effective Date”).
8334 Capitalized terms used in this Agreement that are not expressly defined here have the meaning identified in the
8335 MIPI Membership Agreement or MIPI Bylaws, as applicable. For convenience, key definitions are reproduced in
8336 Attachment A. For the avoidance of doubt: (a) in connection with this Agreement, any reference to a “MIPI
8337 Specification” means the MIPI I3C Basic Specification as described in Section 2, and (b) any rights or obligations
8338 created under this Agreement are independent of any rights or obligations created under the MIPI Membership
8339 Agreement, Bylaws or any other agreements, and nothing in this Agreement is intended to alter rights or obligations
8340 established elsewhere.
8341 1. Background. Typically, MIPI specifications are implemented only by MIPI members. MIPI members
8342 make certain intellectual property licensing commitments to other members under the MIPI Membership
8343 Agreement, with different rules applying to “Mobile Terminals” and “Accessories,” in contrast to other types of
8344 implementations. The MIPI Board intends to make the MIPI I3C Basic Specification available for implementation
8345 by parties who are non-members of MIPI, however. Further, both MIPI members and non-members may use this
8346 specification inside and outside of Mobile Terminals or Accessories. The Licensors contributed to the development
8347 of the MIPI I3C Basic Specification, and desire to see it widely used. MIPI and the Licensors believe that making
8348 licenses available (as set forth in this Agreement) to both member and non-member implementers, for all types of
8349 implementations, will facilitate widespread adoption of the MIPI I3C Basic Specification, to the benefit of MIPI,
8350 the Licensors, and the broader community that MIPI serves.
8351 2. MIPI I3C Basic Specification. “MIPI I3C Basic Specification” means the specification titled “I3C Basic
8352 1.0” as approved by the MIPI Board, and all subsequent versions of such specification approved by the MIPI Board
8353 after the Effective Date. Any party implementing the MIPI I3C Basic Specification, whether or not a MIPI member,
8354 is an “Implementer.” For the avoidance of doubt: the MIPI I3C Basic Specification is distinct from the MIPI I3C
8355 Specification version 1.0 approved by the MIPI Board on Dec. 31, 2016. The terms of this Agreement apply
8356 exclusively to the MIPI I3C Basic Specification.
8357 3. License commitment.
8358 a. RAND-Z license obligation. For the MIPI I3C Basic Specification only, Licensor hereby agrees to
8359 grant, and to cause its Affiliates to grant, to any requesting Implementer a worldwide, non-exclusive,
8360 non-sublicensable license under the Necessary Claims of Licensor or its Affiliates, with zero royalties
8361 or other compensation, under terms and conditions that are reasonable and nondiscriminatory, to make,
8362 have made, use, import, offer to sell, lease, sell, promote and otherwise distribute Compliant Portions.
8363 Licensor shall not be obligated to license any part or function of a product in which a Compliant Portion
8364 is incorporated that is not itself a Compliant Portion.
8365 b. Reciprocity; defensive suspension. Licensor shall not be obligated to license any Implementer if
8366 that Implementer does not agree to make patent licenses available under any Necessary Claims of that
8367 Implementer and its Affiliates to Licensors and all other Implementers under terms substantially
8368 identical to the terms described in this Agreement. Further, a Licensor may suspend any license granted
8369 under this Agreement to any Implementer if that Implementer or its Affiliate initiates against any party
8370 litigation that alleges infringement of a Necessary Claim of Implementer or its Affiliate in connection
8371 with the MIPI I3C Basic Specification. Additionally, subject to Section 3.1(f) of the MIPI Membership
8372 Agreement as between MIPI Members, a Licensor may terminate, ab initio, any license granted
8373 pursuant to this Agreement to any Implementer that initiates litigation against the Licensor alleging
8374 infringement of any patent claim of the Implementer or its Affiliate. For the purposes of this Agreement,
8375 a party that files a suit which is defensive based on a patent infringement claim or suit by another party
8376 will not be deemed to have initiated litigation.
Copyright © 2016–2025 MIPI Alliance, Inc. 457
Public Release Edition\n--- Page 472 ---\nSpecification for I3C Basic ANNEX G Version 1.2
16-Dec-2024
8377 c. Circumvention or transfer. Licensor agrees that it has not transferred and will not transfer any
8378 patent having Necessary Claims solely for the purpose of circumventing the obligations described in
8379 this Agreement. In addition, Licensor agrees that any transfers by Licensor to a third party of a patent
8380 having relevant Necessary Claims, whether or not recognized as Necessary Claims at the time of
8381 transfer, shall be subject to (i) the terms and conditions of this Agreement, and (ii) the agreement that
8382 the third party shall grant licenses under said Necessary Claims to Implementers pursuant to the terms
8383 of this Agreement in like manner and to the same extent as the third party would be required to do if it
8384 had executed this Agreement. A transfer of ownership in a business entity which owns or has the right to
8385 license a patent having Necessary Claims shall be considered a transfer of such patent.
8386 4. Termination of obligation to license future versions. Each Licensor may terminate its participation in
8387 this Agreement at any time by providing written notice to the MIPI Managing Director; termination will be
8388 effective 30 days after such notice is actually received, subject to the survival points below. Promptly upon receipt
8389 of such notice, the MIPI Managing Director will alert the MIPI Board and will use reasonable efforts to notify all
8390 Licensors of such termination. After termination, the agreement to grant a license as provided in Section 3 shall
8391 survive in full force and effect only: (a) for versions of the MIPI I3C Basic Specification which the Board had
8392 approved before the effective date of termination; (b) for Necessary Claims relating to any version of the MIPI
8393 I3C Basic Specification approved after the effective date of termination that are used in a substantially similar
8394 manner and to a substantially similar extent with a substantially similar result as the Necessary Claims that were
8395 used in a prior version for which the Licensor is obligated to grant licenses under this Agreement; and (c) for those
8396 Necessary Claims that directly result from inclusion of Licensor-provided material in the draft version of the
8397 specification that existed immediately prior to Licensor’s withdrawal. Termination of this Agreement by one
8398 Licensor does not impact the Agreement among MIPI or the other Licensors, not does it not impact Licensor’s
8399 MIPI Membership Agreement, which will remain in full force and effect.
8400 5. Third party beneficiaries. All Implementers, whether or not they are MIPI members, are intended third
8401 party beneficiaries of this Agreement.
8402 6. Counterparts; additional Licensors. This Agreement may be signed in any number of counterparts. If
8403 additional parties manifest agreement to terms substantially identical to this Agreement, those parties will be
8404 deemed Licensors under this Agreement.
